# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between infrastructure mode and ad-hoc mode in IEEE 802.11 WLANs.**

**Model Answer**:
Infrastructure mode uses a central **Access Point (AP)** through which all wireless stations communicate, forming a **Basic Service Set (BSS)** or **Extended Service Set (ESS)**. Ad-hoc mode has no AP; stations form an **Independent Basic Service Set (IBSS)** and communicate directly with peers. Infrastructure mode is centralized, scalable, and supports internet connectivity, whereas ad-hoc mode is decentralized, requires no pre-existing infrastructure, and is suited for temporary peer-to-peer networks. *(Answer must mention BSS vs IBSS, presence/absence of AP, and the communication path difference to score full marks.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is an IBSS? Is 802.11 IBSS the same as a MANET?**

**Model Answer**:
An **IBSS (Independent Basic Service Set)** is the IEEE 802.11 term for an ad-hoc network in which stations communicate directly without an Access Point. No, **IBSS is not the same as a MANET**. An IBSS is a **single-hop** network — stations can only talk to peers within radio range. A **MANET (Mobile Ad-hoc NETwork)** is a **multi-hop** network that requires an additional routing protocol (e.g., AODV, DSR, OLSR) layered above 802.11 to forward traffic across multiple wireless hops. IBSS is the **physical/MAC layer foundation**, while MANET is a **complete networking architecture**.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the IEEE 802.11 wireless network architecture in detail. With neat diagrams, describe the Infrastructure mode and Ad-hoc mode. Compare their features, advantages, and limitations. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Infrastructure Mode (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Definition**: Infrastructure mode is the IEEE 802.11 operational mode in which all wireless stations (STAs) associate with a central **Access Point (AP)**. The AP manages the BSS, performs Layer-2 bridging between wireless and wired domains, and forwards all data frames between STAs.

**Key Components**:
1. **Station (STA)**: Any device containing an 802.11 wireless interface.
2. **Access Point (AP)**: A Layer-2 bridge that also performs beaconing, authentication, and encryption.
3. **Basic Service Set (BSS)**: The coverage area of a single AP, identified by a unique **BSSID** (the AP's MAC address).
4. **Distribution System (DS)**: The wired backbone (typically Ethernet) interconnecting APs.
5. **Extended Service Set (ESS)**: A collection of one or more BSSs interconnected via a DS, presenting a single logical network identified by an SSID.
6. **Portal**: The logical interface between the DS and a non-802.11 network (e.g., the internet).

**Operational Phases**:
- **Scanning**: Active (Probe Request/Response) or Passive (Beacon listening) on each supported channel.
- **Authentication**: Open System, WPA2-PSK, or WPA3-SAE.
- **Association**: STA receives an **Association Identifier (AID)** from the AP.
- **Data Transfer**: All unicast frames traverse STA → AP → Destination STA.
- **Reassociation**: Triggered when the STA moves to a new AP within the same ESS.

**Advantages**:
- Centralized security policy enforcement via the AP.
- Extended coverage via ESS with seamless roaming.
- Direct gateway connectivity to wired networks and the internet.
- Higher scalability — enterprise APs handle 50–200 clients per radio.

**Limitations**:
- AP is a **single point of failure** (mitigated by controller-managed APs).
- Halved throughput for direct STA-to-STA communication due to two-hop relay.
- Higher deployment cost and complexity.

#### Part (b) — Ad-hoc Mode & Comparison (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Definition**: Ad-hoc mode (also called **Independent BSS** or **IBSS**) is the IEEE 802.11 operational mode in which stations communicate directly in a peer-to-peer fashion without any infrastructure element. Each STA acts as both a client and (optionally) a router.

**Key Characteristics**:
- The **BSSID** is randomly generated (a 46-bit random value, locally administered bit set in the MAC address).
- All STAs in the IBSS participate in **beacon generation** using a randomized backoff to avoid collisions.
- Communication is **single-hop** by default; multi-hop requires external MANET routing protocols.
- The IBSS exists only as long as at least one STA is active; it dissolves when the last STA leaves.

**Comparison Table**:

| Feature | Infrastructure | Ad-hoc |
|---|---|---|
| AP Required | Yes | No |
| Topology | Star (centralized) | Mesh (distributed) |
| Service Set | BSS / ESS | IBSS |
| Frame Path | 2 logical hops | 1 direct hop |
| Internet Access | Easy via AP gateway | Requires special bridging |
| Security | Centralized | Decentralized / weak |
| Setup Time | Minutes to hours | Seconds (zero config) |
| Scalability | High | Low (<15 nodes) |
| Power Efficiency | Better (STA can sleep) | Worse (must remain active) |

**Advantages of Ad-hoc**:
- Zero-configuration, instant deployment.
- No dependency on fixed infrastructure.
- Highly resilient — no single point of failure.
- Ideal for emergency, military, and temporary networks.

**Limitations of Ad-hoc**:
- Poor scaling beyond ~10 nodes.
- Limited range (single hop).
- No centralized QoS or security policy.
- Higher power consumption at each node.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Do NOT confuse ESS with IBSS** — ESS is infrastructure mode extended across multiple APs; IBSS is purely ad-hoc.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse the DS with the internet** — the DS is a logical Layer-2 backbone, not a public network.
> 3. **Do NOT claim that ad-hoc mode is the same as MANET** — IBSS is single-hop; MANET is multi-hop.
> 4. Always draw a **labeled diagram** in comparison questions; missing diagrams typically cost 2–3 marks.
> 5. Avoid writing "ad-hoc is better" or "infrastructure is better" — answer depends on the scenario. The examiner expects a **contextual answer**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: With appropriate diagrams, explain BSS, IBSS, and ESS as defined in IEEE 802.11. Discuss how stations join a BSS, and explain why pure 802.11 ad-hoc networks are typically augmented with MANET routing protocols. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — BSS, IBSS, ESS Definitions and Joining Procedure (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**Basic Service Set (BSS)**: A BSS is the fundamental building block of an 802.11 infrastructure network. It consists of one **AP** and all STAs currently associated with that AP. The BSS is identified by a 48-bit **BSSID**, which is normally the MAC address of the AP. The geographic area covered by a single BSS is called the **Basic Service Area (BSA)**.

**Independent Basic Service Set (IBSS)**: An IBSS is an ad-hoc network comprising two or more STAs communicating directly without an AP. The BSSID is a randomly generated value with the **locally administered bit** (bit 1 of the first octet) set to 1. All STAs in the IBSS must use this same BSSID, the same SSID, and the same channel to communicate.

**Extended Service Set (ESS)**: An ESS consists of multiple BSSs interconnected through a **Distribution System (DS)**. All APs in the ESS advertise the same SSID, allowing STAs to roam between BSSs without changing their Layer-3 configuration. The ESS is identified by the SSID; individual BSSs are distinguished by their BSSIDs.

**Procedure for a Station to Join a BSS**:

1. **Scanning Phase**:
   - *Passive*: The STA tunes to each channel and listens for **Beacon frames** (sent every 100 ms by the AP). The STA selects the best AP based on RSSI and other criteria.
   - *Active*: The STA broadcasts a **Probe Request** frame on each channel. Any AP on that channel responds with a **Probe Response**.

2. **Authentication Phase**: The STA performs authentication with the chosen AP. For modern WPA2/WPA3 networks, this involves **4-way handshake** for WPA2-PSK or **SAE exchange** for WPA3.

3. **Association Phase**: The STA sends an **Association Request** containing its capabilities and listen interval. The AP responds with an **Association Response** containing the assigned **AID**.

4. **Data Exchange**: The STA is now a member of the BSS and can send/receive data frames.

5. **Reassociation** (mobility): When the STA moves to a new AP, it sends a **Reassociation Request** to the new AP, which communicates with the old AP via the DS to transfer context.

#### Part (b) — Why MANET Routing Augments Pure 802.11 IBSS (7 Marks)

**Model Solution**:

**The Fundamental Limitation**: Pure IEEE 802.11 IBSS supports only **single-hop communication**. If STA-A wants to reach STA-C, but STA-C is outside A's radio range, **802.11 has no built-in mechanism to forward the frame through an intermediate STA-B**. The frame is simply dropped.

**Why Multi-hop is Needed**:
- In real deployments (military, sensor networks, vehicular networks), nodes may be **physically dispersed** beyond the radio range of a single hop.
- Wireless conditions are **unreliable** — obstructions, interference, and multipath cause packet loss.
- A **mesh of cooperative relays** can extend coverage, improve robustness, and balance load.

**The Role of MANET Routing Protocols**:
MANET routing protocols operate at **Layer 3** and provide the multi-hop forwarding logic missing from 802.11. The two main families are:

1. **Reactive (On-Demand) Protocols**:
   - **AODV (Ad-hoc On-Demand Distance Vector)**: Discovers routes only when needed using Route Request (RREQ) / Route Reply (RREP) flooding.
   - **DSR (Dynamic Source Routing)**: Source includes the full route in every packet header.
   - **TORA (Temporally Ordered Routing Algorithm)**: Builds a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) toward destinations.

2. **Proactive (Table-Driven) Protocols**:
   - **OLSR (Optimized Link State Routing)**: Maintains a fresh topology map at every node using MPR (MultiPoint Relays) to reduce flooding overhead.
   - **DSDV (Destination-Sequenced Distance-Vector)**: A distance-vector protocol with sequence numbers to prevent loops.

**How They Integrate with 802.11**:
- Each MANET node runs an 802.11 IBSS interface as its **wireless NIC**.
- The MANET routing daemon listens to **link-layer notifications** (e.g., link up/down) to detect neighbor changes.
- Routing tables built by AODV/OLSR map **IP-layer destinations** to the **MAC address of the next hop**.
- When a packet arrives from the IP layer, the node looks up the next hop's MAC address and transmits it via the 802.11 IBSS interface. The intermediate node repeats the lookup, creating a **multi-hop chain**.

**Advantages of This Layered Approach**:
- **Reuses mature 802.11 hardware** — no proprietary radio needed.
- **Plug-and-play routing** — AODV/OLSR are application-independent.
- **Scalability** through protocol selection — proactive for stable, reactive for dynamic topologies.

**Conclusion**: Pure 802.11 ad-hoc mode provides only the **physical and MAC layer scaffolding** for direct peer communication. To realize the full vision of mobile ad-hoc networking — **anytime, anywhere, infrastructure-free connectivity** — MANET routing protocols are essential to bridge the multi-hop gap.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout for Question B**:
> 1. Do not say "BSSID and SSID are the same" — BSSID is the AP's MAC; SSID is the network name.
> 2. Do not skip the **scanning → authentication → association** sequence — examiners grade this strictly.
> 3. Do not write that AODV/OLSR are part of 802.11 — they are **Layer 3 protocols** layered on top.
> 4. Always mention the **locally administered bit** when explaining how IBSS randomly chooses a BSSID.
> 5. Avoid vague phrases like "the network becomes better" — use quantitative reasoning (e.g., "extends coverage from $R$ to $k \cdot R$ for $k$ hops").

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Infrastructure vs Ad-hoc Mode**

- ✅ IEEE 802.11 defines **two operational modes**: **Infrastructure** and **Ad-hoc**.
- ✅ **Infrastructure Mode** = AP present → forms **BSS** (single AP) or **ESS** (multiple APs via DS).
- ✅ **Ad-hoc Mode** = no AP → forms **IBSS** (Independent Basic Service Set).
- ✅ In infrastructure mode, **all communication goes through the AP** — two logical hops per unicast.
- ✅ In ad-hoc mode, **communication is direct** between STAs — one logical hop.
- ✅ **BSSID** = AP's MAC address (infrastructure) OR a randomly generated 48-bit value with the locally administered bit set (ad-hoc).
- ✅ **SSID** = the human-readable network name, 0 to 32 octets long.
- ✅ **Beacon interval** defaults to **100 ms** in both modes; in IBSS, beacon generation is distributed and randomized.
- ✅ **Association Identifier (AID)** ranges from 1 to **2007**, limiting BSS membership.
- ✅ Pure 802.11 IBSS is **single-hop**; multi-hop requires **MANET routing** (AODV, DSR, OLSR).
- ✅ **ESS** = multiple BSSs sharing one SSID, connected by a **Distribution System (DS)**, usually Ethernet.
- ✅ **Roaming** (BSS-Transition) is supported in infrastructure mode within an ESS — handled via Reassociation frames.
- ✅ Ad-hoc mode is preferred when **deployment speed, infrastructure independence, and resilience** outweigh **scalability and centralized control**.
- ✅ **Wi-Fi Direct** is a modern, standardized enhancement of ad-hoc mode with managed discovery and group formation.
- ✅ **Key formula**: Per-station IBSS throughput $\approx \dfrac{R_{raw}}{N(1+\alpha)}$, explaining the $\dfrac{1}{N}$ scaling penalty.
- ✅ **KTU keywords to use**: BSS, IBSS, ESS, DS, BSSID, SSID, AID, AP, STA, MANET, AODV, OLSR, beacon, TBTT, roaming, reassociation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IEEE 802.11 System Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IEEE 802.11 System Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **IEEE 802.11 System Architecture** is the foundational specification of the Wireless Local Area Network (WLAN) standard family, governing the Physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) sub-layers of the OSI data link layer for wireless communication in the 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz, and 6 GHz industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) radio bands. It formally defines the logical components, topology, frame exchange mechanisms, and channel access protocols that allow wireless stations (STAs) to interoperate within localized coverage domains.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 802.11 architecture is built on a strict hierarchy of service sets — the **Basic Service Set (BSS)** is the smallest deployable unit, the **Distribution System (DS)** interconnects BSSs, and the **Extended Service Set (ESS)** is the user-visible enterprise network. Every KTU question on this module assumes mastery of this hierarchy.

### Architectural Components (The Building Blocks)

| Component | Symbol | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Wireless Station** | STA | End-user device (laptop, smartphone, IoT sensor) |
| **Access Point** | AP | Bridge between wireless STAs and the Distribution System |
| **Basic Service Set** | BSS | A single AP plus all associated STAs in its coverage cell |
| **Independent BSS** | IBSS | Ad-hoc peer-to-peer network without an AP |
| **Distribution System** | DS | Backbone (typically Ethernet) linking multiple BSSs |
| **Extended Service Set** | ESS | One or more BSSs interconnected through a single DS |
| **Portal** | — | Gateway integrating 802.11 with 802.x wired LANs |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a shopping mall with multiple food courts.**
> - Each **food court** is a **BSS** (one Wi-Fi router serving several customers).
> - The **mall's internal walkways and elevators** form the **Distribution System (DS)** — they let you move between courts without exiting.
> - The **entire mall** is the **ESS** — from the customer's perspective, it's one continuous shopping experience under one Wi-Fi name.
> - A **group of friends sitting in the parking lot** sharing files directly without any router is an **IBSS / Ad-hoc network**.

### Standardized Metrics (Must Memorize)

- **Operating Frequency Bands:** **2.4 GHz**, **5 GHz**, **6 GHz** (Wi-Fi 6E)
- **Maximum Coverage Radius of a BSS:** approximately **100 meters indoors / 300 meters outdoors**
- **Maximum Data Rate (802.11ax / Wi-Fi 6):** up to **9.6 Gbps** theoretical
- **Fundamental MAC Coordination Function:** **DCF (Distributed Coordination Function)** — mandatory CSMA/CA-based contention method
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Layered 802.11 Protocol Stack

The IEEE 802.11 standard only specifies the **bottom two layers** of the OSI model — it deliberately leaves upper-layer concerns (mobility above the MAC, security above the MAC) to other standards like 802.11i (security) and 802.11r (fast roaming).

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            Upper Layers (TCP/IP)           │
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│            Logical Link Control (LLC)      │
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  MAC Sub-Layer (802.11)  ─►  Framing,      │
│                             Addressing,    │
│                             Channel Access │
├────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  PHY Sub-Layer (802.11a/b/g/n/ac/ax)       │
│  ─► Modulation, Spreading, RF Transmission │
└────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

### 2.2 The Five Topological Forms of an 802.11 Network

#### (a) Independent Basic Service Set (IBSS) — Ad-hoc Mode
- **No Access Point.** STAs communicate **directly peer-to-peer**.
- Useful for small, transient file-sharing scenarios.
- Cannot scale; limited to a few STAs.
- No integration with wired infrastructure.

#### (b) Infrastructure BSS — Managed Mode
- Contains **exactly one AP** + associated STAs.
- All communication between two STAs **must traverse the AP** (even if the STAs are physically adjacent).
- The AP acts as the central coordinator and bridge.

#### (c) Extended Service Set (ESS) — Enterprise Mode
- Multiple BSSs connected via a **Distribution System (DS)**.
- STAs can roam seamlessly between BSSs while maintaining Layer 3 connectivity (with protocols like Mobile IP).
- The DS is **typically Ethernet**, but it can be any IEEE 802 LAN.

#### (d) Mesh Basic Service Set (MBSS) — 802.11s
- STAs participate in forwarding each other's traffic.
- Self-healing, multi-hop wireless backhaul.
- Defines the **Mesh Deterministic Access** mechanism.

#### (e) Bridged Network (with Portal)
- The **Portal** is a logical point where the 802.11 ESS integrates with a non-802 LAN (e.g., legacy Ethernet).
- Performs frame format translation (802.11 ↔ 802.3).

### 2.3 Logical Services Provided by the 802.11 MAC

The MAC provides **nine services**, grouped into two categories:

#### Station Services (SS)
1. **Authentication** — verifies identity of the STA.
2. **Deauthentication** — terminates an authenticated relationship.
3. **Privacy (Encryption)** — WEP/WPA/WPA2/WPA3.
4. **MAC Service Data Unit (MSDU) Delivery** — guaranteed data delivery.

#### Distribution System Services (DSS)
5. **Association** — registers an STA with an AP to access the DS.
6. **Disassociation** — removes the registration.
7. **Reassociation** — transfers association from one AP to another (roaming).
8. **Distribution** — delivers frames across the DS.
9. **Integration** — translation between 802.11 frames and 802.3 frames at the portal.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every numerical relationship you must be able to recall or apply for KTU Module-1 derivations.

| Parameter | Symbol / Value | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slot Time (802.11b DSSS) | $\tau_{slot}$ | **20 µs** | Fundamental MAC time unit for backoff |
| SIFS | $t_{SIFS}$ | **10 µs** | Short Inter-Frame Space — used for ACK, CTS |
| PIFS | $t_{PIFS} = \tau_{slot} + 2 \times t_{SIFS}$ | **30 µs** | PCF Inter-Frame Space |
| DIFS | $t_{DIFS} = \tau_{slot} + 2 \times t_{SIFS}$ | **50 µs** | DCF Inter-Frame Space — used for data |
| EIFS | $t_{EIFS}$ | **≥ 92 µs** | Extended Inter-Frame Space (after error) |
| CW Minimum | $CW_{min}$ | **31** | Initial contention window size |
| CW Maximum | $CW_{max}$ | **1023** | Final contention window after retries |
| Backoff Counter | $BW \sim U[0, CW]$ | slots | Random draw after DIFS deferral |
| Throughput Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{L_{payload}+L_{overhead}}$ | ratio | Useful payload / total transmission time |
| Free-Space Path Loss | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | dB | d in km, f in MHz |
| Maximum Frame Body | $L_{max}$ | **2304 bytes** | Per 802.11 MSDU payload |
| RTS Threshold | $L_{RTS}$ | **20 bytes** | When RTS/CTS kicks in |
| Roaming Latency Goal | $t_{roam}$ | **< 50 ms** | 802.11r fast transition target |

> **Critical Caveat:** Notice that $t_{PIFS}$ and $t_{DIFS}$ share the same formula in the **DSSS 802.11b** profile. They differ only by an additional slot time. For 802.11a/g (OFDM PHY), PIFS = **16 µs** and DIFS = **34 µs**.

### 2.5 The Real-World Engineering Utility

The 802.11 architecture is the silent backbone of **smart campuses** (KTU colleges included), **industrial IoT** (smart factories), **vehicular telemetry**, and **last-mile residential broadband**. Understanding its hierarchy is essential for network engineers configuring **controllers, RADIUS servers, VLANs across APs**, and **mobility domains**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mapping the Station Roaming Decision Tree

When a STA moves through a building, it constantly re-evaluates which AP to associate with. Below is the full analytical flow that a Wi-Fi client follows. Every KTU question on "how a STA roams" requires this exact sequence.

> **Step 1 — Passive/Active Scanning:** The STA scans all 802.11 channels. Active scanning sends a **Probe Request**; Passive scanning listens for **Beacon frames** (default interval = **100 ms**).

> **Step 2 — Beacon/Probe Response Analysis:** For each candidate AP, the STA extracts the **Received Signal Strength Indicator (RSSI)** and **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**.

> **Step 3 — Decision Logic:** The STA computes a candidate score $S_{AP}$ for each AP:

$$
S_{AP} = w_1 \cdot \frac{RSSI_{AP}}{RSSI_{max}} + w_2 \cdot SNR_{AP} - w_3 \cdot C_{AP}
$$

- $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are vendor-specific weights (e.g., Cisco default $0.6, 0.3, 0.1$).
- $C_{AP}$ is the **channel utilization percentage** (0–100).
- The AP with the **highest $S_{AP}$** wins.

> **Step 4 — Authentication:** STA sends an **Authentication frame** to the chosen AP. The AP replies with **Authentication Response** (status code 0 = success).

> **Step 5 — Association:** STA sends an **Association Request**. AP allocates an **Association ID (AID)** in the **Association Response**.

> **Step 6 — State Transition:** STA enters **State 3 (Authenticated & Associated)** — it can now transmit data frames.

### 3.2 Derivation: Contention Window Backoff Progression

The DCF uses an **exponential backoff** algorithm. This is a guaranteed 14-mark KTU question every December. Below is the full analytical matrix.

> Let $i$ = number of failed transmission attempts for a given frame.
> Then the Contention Window size at attempt $i$ is given by:

$$
CW_i = \begin{cases} CW_{min} & i = 0 \\ \min\left(\, 2^{i}(CW_{min}+1) - 1 \,,\; CW_{max} \right) & i \geq 1 \end{cases}
$$

**Worked Example (CW progression for 802.11b):**

| Attempt $i$ | Computation | $CW_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial | **31** |
| 1 | $\min(2^1 \cdot 32 - 1,\; 1023) = \min(63, 1023)$ | **63** |
| 2 | $\min(2^2 \cdot 32 - 1,\; 1023) = \min(127, 1023)$ | **127** |
| 3 | $\min(255, 1023)$ | **255** |
| 4 | $\min(511, 1023)$ | **511** |
| 5 | $\min(1023, 1023)$ | **1023** |
| 6 | $\min(2047, 1023) = 1023$ | **1023** (saturated) |

The station then picks a random integer uniformly:

$$
BW_i \sim U[\,0,\; CW_i\,]
$$

and defers for exactly $BW_i$ slot times after sensing the channel idle for DIFS.

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Throughput in a Saturated BSS

Assume an isolated BSS where one AP serves one STA with a single 1500-byte MSDU. Using the DSSS PHY at 11 Mbps:

> Let:
> - $T_{DIFS} = 50\;\mu s$
> - $T_{SIFS} = 10\;\mu s$
> - $T_{slot} = 20\;\mu s$
> - $T_{PHY} = 192\;\mu s$ (long preamble DSSS)
> - $T_{ACK} = T_{PHY} + 14\text{ bytes} \times 8 / 11\text{Mbps} = 192 + 10.18 = 202.18\;\mu s$
> - $T_{DATA} = T_{PHY} + 1500 \times 8 / 11\text{Mbps} = 192 + 1090.91 = 1282.91\;\mu s$
> - $T_{prop} = 1\;\mu s$ (assumed negligible propagation)

> **Total Transmission Time (Basic Access Mode — no RTS/CTS):**

$$
T_{total} = T_{DIFS} + T_{DATA} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK} + T_{prop}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 50 + 1282.91 + 10 + 202.18 + 1 = 1546.09\;\mu s
$$

> **Application Throughput:**

$$
\eta_{app} = \frac{L_{payload}}{T_{total}} = \frac{1500 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{1546.09 \times 10^{-6}\text{ s}}
$$

$$
\eta_{app} = 7.76\;\text{Mbps}
$$

> **Effective MAC Efficiency:**

$$
\Theta_{MAC} = \frac{\eta_{app}}{R_{PHY}} = \frac{7.76}{11} = 0.705 = 70.5\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** The 802.11b PHY advertises 11 Mbps, but the actual upper-bound application-layer throughput (with no contention) is only **~7.76 Mbps**. This is one of the most frequently asked KTU questions to test whether students understand protocol overhead.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Frame Exchange Summary

| Phase | Frame Direction | Frame Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | STA → AP | Probe Request | Active scanning trigger |
| 2 | AP → STA | Probe Response | Carries BSSID, SSID, capabilities |
| 3 | STA → AP | Authentication | Open or Shared-Key auth |
| 4 | AP → STA | Auth Response | Status code (0 = success) |
| 5 | STA → AP | Association Req | Lists supported rates |
| 6 | AP → STA | Association Resp | Allocates AID |
| 7 | STA → AP | DATA | User traffic |
| 8 | AP → STA | ACK | MAC-layer acknowledgement |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — 802.11 Logical Architecture Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[USER DEVICE LAYER]:::top
    nodeB[STATION A]:::device
    nodeC[STATION B]:::device
    nodeD[STATION C]:::device
    nodeE[STATION D]:::device
    nodeF[STATION E]:::device
    nodeG[STATION F]:::device

    nodeH[ACCESS POINT AP1]:::ap
    nodeI[ACCESS POINT AP2]:::ap
    nodeJ[ACCESS POINT AP3]:::ap

    nodeK[DISTRIBUTION SYSTEM DS ETHERNET BACKBONE]:::ds
    nodeL[PORTAL GATEWAY]:::portal

    nodeM[802 X WIRED LAN INTERNET]:::wan

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE
    nodeA --> nodeF
    nodeA --> nodeG

    nodeB --- nodeH
    nodeC --- nodeH
    nodeD --- nodeI
    nodeE --- nodeI
    nodeF --- nodeJ
    nodeG --- nodeJ

    nodeH === nodeK
    nodeI === nodeK
    nodeJ === nodeK
    nodeK === nodeL
    nodeL === nodeM

    classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef device fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ap fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ds fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef portal fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef wan fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **STA groups** (top) represent end-user devices.
- **AP1, AP2, AP3** each anchor a separate **BSS**.
- The **DS** (center, pink) interconnects all three BSSs into a single **ESS**.
- The **Portal** (purple) bridges the wireless ESS to a wired 802.x LAN / Internet.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — MAC Frame Exchange (Successful Data Transfer)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant nodeS as STATION STA
    participant nodeM as MEDIUM WIRELESS CHANNEL
    participant nodeP as ACCESS POINT AP

    nodeS->>nodeM: Wait DIFS plus BACKOFF
    nodeS->>nodeP: RTS REQUEST TO SEND
    nodeP->>nodeM: Wait SIFS
    nodeP->>nodeS: CTS CLEAR TO SEND
    nodeS->>nodeP: DATA FRAME MSDU
    nodeP->>nodeM: Wait SIFS
    nodeP->>nodeS: ACK ACKNOWLEDGEMENT
    Note over nodeS,nodeP: Successful Unicast Transaction
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — State Transition of an 802.11 Station

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> State1: POWER ON

    State1: STATE 1 UNAUTHENTICATED UNASSOCIATED
    State2: STATE 2 AUTHENTICATED UNASSOCIATED
    State3: STATE 3 AUTHENTICATED ASSOCIATED

    State1 --> State2: AUTHENTICATION FRAME SUCCESS
    State2 --> State1: DEAUTHENTICATION
    State2 --> State3: ASSOCIATION REQ SUCCESS
    State3 --> State2: DISASSOCIATION
    State3 --> State3: REASSOCIATION TO NEW AP
    State3 --> [*]: POWER OFF
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Channel Access Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[NEW FRAME ARRIVES AT MAC]:::start
    nodeB{CHANNEL IDLE FOR DIFS}:::decision
    nodeC[START BACKOFF COUNTER BW ZERO TO CW]:::process
    nodeD{CHANNEL STILL IDLE FOR NEXT SLOT}:::decision
    nodeE[TRANSMIT FRAME]:::action
    nodeF[DECREMENT BW BY ONE]:::process
    nodeG[FREEZE COUNTER WAIT UNTIL CHANNEL IDLE AGAIN]:::process
    nodeH{ACK RECEIVED WITHIN TIMEOUT}:::decision
    nodeI[TRANSMISSION SUCCESS]:::success
    nodeJ[DOUBLE CW AND RETRY]:::retry

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -- YES --> nodeC
    nodeB -- NO --> nodeB
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeF
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeD
    nodeF --> nodeD
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeH
    nodeH -- YES --> nodeI
    nodeH -- NO --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeC

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef action fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef success fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef retry fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

**[Q1] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three differences between an Infrastructure BSS and an Independent BSS (IBSS).*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Presence of AP:** An Infrastructure BSS requires a dedicated Access Point; an IBSS has no AP.
2. **Communication Mode:** In a BSS, all STAs communicate *via* the AP even if they are adjacent; in an IBSS, STAs communicate directly peer-to-peer.
3. **Network Scope:** A BSS can be part of a larger ESS with a Distribution System; an IBSS is isolated and cannot connect to a DS.

---

**[Q2] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the terms ESS, DS, and Portal as used in the IEEE 802.11 architecture.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Extended Service Set (ESS):** A collection of one or more Basic Service Sets interconnected by a Distribution System, appearing as a single logical network to upper layers.
- **Distribution System (DS):** The backbone (typically IEEE 802 wired Ethernet) that transports frames between APs belonging to different BSSs in an ESS.
- **Portal:** The logical integration point where an 802.11 ESS connects to a non-802 LAN, performing any required frame-format translation between 802.11 and 802.3 MAC frames.

---

### **Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

> **Module-1 Internal Choice Rule:** KTU requires two alternatives in each slot. You are given **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the IEEE 802.11 logical architecture, clearly distinguishing between BSS, ESS, DS, and IBSS. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The IEEE 802.11 architecture defines a modular hierarchy for wireless LANs. The core components are:

- **STA (Station):** Any device containing an 802.11-conformant MAC and PHY interface.
- **BSS (Basic Service Set):** The fundamental building block — a single AP coordinating a set of STAs within its radio coverage area. All STAs in a BSS use the same MAC-layer coordination.
- **IBSS (Independent BSS):** A peer-to-peer ad-hoc network without an AP. STAs communicate directly within a small coverage radius.
- **DS (Distribution System):** A wired backbone (typically 802.3 Ethernet) interconnecting multiple BSSs.
- **ESS (Extended Service Set):** The user-visible enterprise network formed when multiple BSSs are joined via a DS.
- **Portal:** The gateway translating frames between the 802.11 ESS and an external 802.x LAN.

The logical architecture can be visualized as:

```
[STA]---[AP]--(BSS 1)
                  |
[STA]---[AP]--(BSS 2)---(DS)---[Portal]---[Wired LAN]
                  |
[STA]---[STA]-(IBSS)
```

The complete ESS is BSS 1 ∪ BSS 2 ∪ DS.

**(b)** Explain the MAC frame transmission procedure for successful data delivery using the DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) basic access method, including the role of DIFS, backoff, and ACK. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** is the mandatory contention-based channel access mechanism. Its procedure for a successful unicast transmission is:

1. **DIFS Deferral:** When a STA has a frame to send, it first senses the channel. If the channel is idle for **DIFS (DCF Inter-Frame Space)** continuously, it proceeds to the backoff phase. If the channel is busy, it waits until it becomes idle, then waits an additional DIFS.

2. **Random Backoff:** The STA selects a backoff counter:
$$
BW \sim U[0, CW]
$$
where $CW$ is the current contention window. The counter decrements by 1 every slot time (20 µs in 802.11b) **only while the channel remains idle**. If the channel becomes busy mid-countdown, the counter is **frozen** and resumes only after the channel is idle again for DIFS.

3. **Frame Transmission:** When the counter reaches zero, the STA transmits the DATA frame.

4. **SIFS and ACK:** After a **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space = 10 µs)** delay, the receiver transmits an ACK. SIFS is shorter than DIFS to ensure the ACK is not preempted by other contenders.

5. **Completion:** Upon receiving the ACK, the transmitter marks the frame as successfully delivered.

If no ACK is received within the timeout, the transmitter assumes a collision occurred, **doubles the contention window** (capped at $CW_{max} = 1023$), and retries.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the three logical states of an 802.11 station and the service primitives used to transition between them. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Every 802.11 STA exists in one of three logical states at any given time:

- **State 1 — Unauthenticated, Unassociated:** The STA has powered on but has no security credentials with any AP. It can send Probe Requests.
- **State 2 — Authenticated, Unassociated:** The STA has completed the Authentication exchange with an AP but has not yet been granted access to the DS.
- **State 3 — Authenticated, Associated:** The STA has both authenticated and associated with an AP. It can now transmit and receive user data frames.

The **service primitives** that drive state transitions are:

| Transition | Primitive | Direction | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | MLME-Authenticate | STA → AP | Validates identity |
| 2 → 1 | MLME-Deauthenticate | Either direction | Terminates auth |
| 2 → 3 | MLME-Associate | STA → AP | Registers with DS |
| 3 → 2 | MLME-Disassociate | Either direction | Removes DS registration |
| 3 → 3 (new AP) | MLME-Reassociate | STA → new AP | Roaming transfer |

**(b)** A wireless LAN uses 802.11b DSSS with a 1500-byte payload frame. Compute the maximum achievable throughput in Mbps at the application layer, assuming no contention, no RTS/CTS, and a propagation delay of 1 µs. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Given:**
> - PHY rate $R_{PHY} = 11\;\text{Mbps}$
> - $T_{DIFS} = 50\;\mu s$, $T_{SIFS} = 10\;\mu s$, $T_{PHY-preamble} = 192\;\mu s$
> - Payload $L = 1500\;\text{bytes}$, $T_{prop} = 1\;\mu s$

> **Step 1 — Data Frame Airtime:**

$$
T_{DATA} = T_{PHY} + \frac{8L}{R_{PHY}} = 192 + \frac{8 \times 1500}{11 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{DATA} = 192 + 1090.91 = 1282.91\;\mu s
$$

> **Step 2 — ACK Frame Airtime (14-byte ACK):**

$$
T_{ACK} = 192 + \frac{8 \times 14}{11 \times 10^6} = 192 + 10.18 = 202.18\;\mu s
$$

> **Step 3 — Total Cycle Time:**

$$
T_{total} = T_{DIFS} + T_{DATA} + T_{SIFS} + T_{ACK} + T_{prop}
$$

$$
T_{total} = 50 + 1282.91 + 10 + 202.18 + 1 = 1546.09\;\mu s
$$

> **Step 4 — Application Throughput:**

$$
\eta_{app} = \frac{8L}{T_{total}} = \frac{8 \times 1500}{1546.09 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\eta_{app} = \frac{12000}{1.54609 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.76\;\text{Mbps}
$$

> **Result:** The maximum application-layer throughput is **≈ 7.76 Mbps**, representing a MAC efficiency of only **70.5%**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DIFS with PIFS:** PIFS is used *only* for PCF contention-free polls; DIFS is used *only* for DCF data. Mixing them up = −2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting SIFS is shorter than DIFS:** This is *the* reason ACK never collides with data — students frequently miss this conceptual explanation.
> 3. **Ignoring PHY preamble time:** Many students compute $T_{DATA}$ without adding the 192 µs long-preamble overhead, inflating throughput incorrectly.
> 4. **Using $CW_{min} = 31$ as the *window itself*:** It is the maximum *value* of the initial counter; the actual window *size* in slots is $CW_{min}+1 = 32$.
> 5. **Mis-stating the Portal's role:** It is a *logical* entity, not a physical box — it exists as a service within an AP or a controller.

---

### **Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [x] **802.11 = Wireless LAN standard** specifying **PHY + MAC** layers only.
- [x] **BSS = 1 AP + its STAs.** **IBSS = ad-hoc, no AP.**
- [x] **DS = backbone (typically Ethernet)** connecting APs.
- [x] **ESS = multiple BSSs + 1 DS**, presented as one logical network.
- [x] **Portal** = gateway to non-802 LANs.
- [x] **Three STA states:** Unauth/Unassoc → Auth/Unassoc → Auth/Assoc.
- [x] **Five services: Authentication, Deauthentication, Privacy, MSDU Delivery** (Station Services) **+ Association, Disassociation, Reassociation, Distribution, Integration** (Distribution System Services).
- [x] **DCF uses CSMA/CA + binary exponential backoff.**
- [x] **DIFS > PIFS > SIFS** in duration — order matters for priority.
- [x] **DIFS = 50 µs**, **SIFS = 10 µs**, **Slot = 20 µs** (802.11b DSSS).
- [x] **CW progression:** $31 \to 63 \to 127 \to 255 \to 511 \to 1023$ (saturated).
- [x] **CW formula:** $CW_i = \min(2^i(CW_{min}+1) - 1,\; CW_{max})$.
- [x] **Throughput formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{L_{payload}}{T_{total}}$ where $T_{total} = T_{DIFS}+T_{DATA}+T_{SIFS}+T_{ACK}+T_{prop}$.
- [x] **Max MSDU payload = 2304 bytes**; max 1500 bytes typical Ethernet-compatible.
- [x] **Beacon interval = 100 ms** is the default AP broadcast.
- [x] **802.11b max application throughput ≈ 7.76 Mbps** at 11 Mbps PHY.
- [x] **Roaming uses Reassociation**, not a fresh Association from scratch.
- [x] **Hidden node problem** is solved by **RTS/CTS** (optional in DCF).
- [x] **Free-Space Path Loss:** $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.44$ dB.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocol Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Protocol Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Protocol Architecture** refers to the structured, layered framework of communication protocols that govern data exchange between heterogeneous devices in a wireless and mobile network. It defines the **functional hierarchy**, **service primitives**, and **peer-to-peer interaction rules** that enable reliable, secure, and efficient end-to-end communication across heterogeneous physical media (radio, infrared, fiber, copper).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a protocol architecture is the *logical blueprint* that maps the **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) Reference Model**, the **TCP/IP Internet Protocol Suite**, and **wireless-specific protocol stacks** (e.g., IEEE 802.11, 3GPP LTE/5G NR, Bluetooth) into a coherent operational stack.

Key constituent models that KTU examiners expect:

| Architecture Model | Layers | Application Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **OSI Reference Model (ISO/IEC 7498)** | 7 Layers | Theoretical/Universal Reference |
| **TCP/IP (Internet) Protocol Suite** | 4–5 Layers | Internet & Enterprise Networks |
| **Hybrid (Wired-Wireless) Architecture** | Modified TCP/IP + Wireless MAC | Mobile Computing |
| **3GPP Cellular Protocol Stack** | User/Control Plane separation | 4G/5G Mobile Networks |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Service" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are sending a gift to a friend in another country by post.
> - The gift is your **Data Payload**.
> - You wrap it in decorative paper → this is **Encapsulation**.
> - You address the outer cover → this is the **Network Layer header**.
> - You choose express courier or standard mail → this is the **Transport Layer (TCP reliable / UDP fast)**.
> - The postman hands it to a truck, then a plane, then another truck → these are the **Data Link (MAC)** and **Physical** layers.
> - At each step (hub), only the relevant outer wrapper is read; the inner gift stays untouched → this is **Layer Independence**.

In wireless mobile computing, the **radio air interface** simply replaces the "truck/plane" of the analogy — every other layer behaves identically.

## 1.3 Why Protocol Architecture Matters in Wireless/Mobile Computing

- **Heterogeneity**: A smartphone simultaneously runs Wi-Fi (802.11), LTE/5G (3GPP), and Bluetooth (IEEE 802.15) — each with its own lower-layer protocol, but all sharing the same upper-layer IP suite.
- **Mobility**: Protocol architecture must support **handover**, **location management**, and **roaming** across base stations / access points.
- **Quality of Service (QoS)**: Layered architecture enables independent optimization of throughput, delay, jitter, and packet loss.
- **Security**: Each layer contributes a security primitive (PHY-layer encryption, MAC-layer authentication, IPsec, TLS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 emphasizes understanding *why* a layered architecture is necessary in mobile computing — specifically how layers interact to mask the complexity of the unreliable, broadcast, time-varying wireless channel.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The OSI 7-Layer Reference Model — Layer-by-Layer Logic

The OSI model decomposes communication into **seven hierarchical layers**, where each layer *n* provides services to layer *n+1* and consumes services from layer *n-1*.

| # | Layer | PDU Name | KTU-Required Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | Application | Data | Interface to user applications (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS) |
| 6 | Presentation | Data | Data format translation (ASCII ↔ EBCDIC), encryption, compression |
| 5 | Session | Data | Dialog control, synchronization tokens (NetBIOS, RPC, SIP) |
| 4 | Transport | Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP) | End-to-end reliability, flow & congestion control |
| 3 | Network | Packet / Datagram | Logical addressing & routing (IP, ICMP, OSPF, BGP) |
| 2 | Data Link | Frame | Framing, MAC addressing, error detection (Wi-Fi MAC, Ethernet) |
| 1 | Physical | Bit | Bit transmission over radio/copper/fiber (modulation, coding) |

### Logical Step Decomposition

- **Step 1 — Service Provision:** Each layer offers a *Service Access Point (SAP)* through which the upper layer requests operations.
- **Step 2 — Encapsulation:** On the sender side, each layer appends its own header (and sometimes trailer) to the SDU received from above, transforming it into its own PDU.
- **Step 3 — Peer-to-Peer Protocol:** The header added by layer *n* on the sender is read *only* by layer *n* on the receiver — they are "peers."
- **Step 4 — Decapsulation:** At the receiver, each layer strips its corresponding header and passes the SDU upward.
- **Step 5 — Independence:** Changes in one layer (e.g., switching from Wi-Fi to 5G at Layer 1/2) require *no* modification in upper layers.

## 2.2 The TCP/IP (Internet) Protocol Suite — Practical 4/5-Layer Model

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Mandated Distinction:** OSI is a *reference* model; TCP/IP is the *implementation* model that actually runs on the Internet and on every smartphone.

| TCP/IP Layer | Maps to OSI | Key Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| Application | OSI 5–7 | HTTP, HTTPS, FTP, DNS, SMTP, DHCP, SNMP |
| Transport | OSI 4 | **TCP** (reliable), **UDP** (unreliable, low-latency) |
| Internet (Network) | OSI 3 | **IP** (IPv4/IPv6), ICMP, IGMP, ARP |
| Network Access / Link | OSI 1–2 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi (802.11), LTE MAC, PPP, ATM |
| *(sometimes)* Physical | OSI 1 | Radio, Fiber, Copper |

## 2.3 The Wireless-Mobile Protocol Architecture (KTU Hybrid View)

The course emphasizes a **hybrid reference architecture** that integrates wireless and mobile extensions onto the TCP/IP stack.

```
+---------------------------------------------------+
|        Application Layer (HTTP, FTP, SIP)          |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|     Transport Layer (TCP / UDP / SCTP / DCCP)     |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|   Network Layer (Mobile IP, IP, ICMP, Routing)    |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|   Logical Link Control (LLC) - IEEE 802.2         |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|        MAC Sublayer (CSMA/CA, TDMA, FDMA)         |
+---------------------------------------------------+
|   Physical Layer (OFDM, DSSS, FHSS, MIMO, mmWave) |
+---------------------------------------------------+
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Wireless-Specific Layers Inserted in TCP/IP Stack:**
> - **LLC Sublayer** — Provides flow control and error recovery independent of MAC technology.
> - **MAC Sublayer** — Handles channel access (CSMA/CA in Wi-Fi, scheduled access in LTE/NR).
> - **Physical Layer** — Modulation, coding, antenna diversity — directly subject to **fading, multipath, and interference**.

## 2.4 The 3GPP Cellular Protocol Architecture (4G/5G Context)

KTU Module 1 also requires familiarity with cellular protocol architecture, which has two distinct planes:

- **User Plane (U-Plane):** Carries actual user data (voice, video, packets).
- **Control Plane (C-Pane):** Carries signaling (mobility, session, authentication).

| Plane | Layer Stack | Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| User Plane | App → TCP/UDP → IP → PDCP → RLC → MAC → PHY | HTTP, TCP, IP, PDCP, RLC, MAC |
| Control Plane | App (NAS) → RRC → PDCP → RLC → MAC → PHY | NAS, RRC, PDCP, RLC, MAC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PDCP (Packet Data Convergence Protocol), RLC (Radio Link Control), MAC (Medium Access Control)** are the three radio-interface sublayers unique to 3GPP architectures — they replace the generic LLC/MAC of the Wi-Fi world.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Although Protocol Architecture is conceptually driven, several **quantitative metrics** accompany it:

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end}$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{i,proc} + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} T_{i,prop} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{i,queue}$ | seconds | Total latency across *n* protocol layers |
| $T_{prop}$ | $d / c$ where $d$=distance, $c$=speed of light ($3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s) | s | Physical-layer propagation delay |
| $T_{tx}$ | $L / R$ where $L$=frame length (bits), $R$=bit rate (bps) | s | Transmission (serialization) delay |
| $T_{queue}$ | $\frac{\rho \cdot T_{s}}{1-\rho}$ (M/M/1) — $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ | s | Queuing delay at MAC/Network layer |
| $\eta_{protocol}$ | Useful payload bits / Total transmitted bits | unitless (0 to 1) | Protocol efficiency (overhead penalty) |
| $R_{goodput}$ | $\frac{L_{app}}{T_{end\text{-}to\text{-}end}}$ | bps | Effective throughput seen by application |
| $S$ (Throughput) | $\frac{L}{T_{ack} + T_{data}}$ | bps | Stop-and-Wait ARQ efficiency analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Break Rule:** In every formula above, the vertical bar has been replaced with LaTeX `\vert` semantics. When writing these in your KTU answer sheet, NEVER embed raw `|` inside markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead to preserve table integrity.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **5G/6G Network Slicing:** Each slice has its own protocol stack variant for eMBB, URLLC, mMTC.
- **IoT Gateways:** Convert between LPWAN protocols (LoRaWAN, NB-IoT) and IP networks — protocol translation happens at the Network Access Layer.
- **Software-Defined Networking (SDN):** Decouples the Control Plane from the Data Plane — a direct architectural consequence of layered protocol design.
- **Cross-Layer Design (CLD):** Modern research breaks strict layering for performance — e.g., TCP-CLN, MobiStream — a KTU-favourite advanced discussion point.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Delay Across a Protocol Stack

Consider a packet traversing an *n*-layer wireless protocol stack. Let us derive the **total end-to-end delay** $T_{e2e}$.

### Step 1 — Identify Delay Components at Each Layer

For layer $i$, three delay components exist:

- **Processing delay** ($T_{i,proc}$): Time to examine header, route lookup, error check.
- **Transmission delay** ($T_{i,tx} = L_i / R_i$): Time to push all bits of the layer-$i$ PDU onto the medium.
- **Propagation delay** ($T_{i,prop} = d_i / v_i$): Time for the signal to physically traverse the medium.

### Step 2 — Aggregate at Each Hop

For $n$ layers per node and $h$ hops:

$$
T_{e2e} = \sum_{j=1}^{h} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{i,proc}^{(j)} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{i,tx}^{(j)} \;+\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_{i,prop}^{(j)} \;+\; T_{i,queue}^{(j)} \right)
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify for a Single-Hop Wi-Fi Transmission

Suppose one packet of application payload $L_{app}$ = 1500 bytes, traversing 5 TCP/IP layers:

- Application → Transport (adds 20 B TCP header)
- Transport → Internet (adds 20 B IP header)
- Internet → LLC (adds 4 B LLC header)
- LLC → MAC (adds 34 B 802.11 MAC header)
- MAC → PHY (adds preambles, training fields, FCS)

Total on-air bits $L_{total}$:

$$
L_{total} = (L_{app} + H_{tcp} + H_{ip} + H_{llc} + H_{mac} + H_{phy}) \times 8 \;\text{bits}
$$

Substituting $L_{app} = 1500$, $H_{tcp}=20$, $H_{ip}=20$, $H_{llc}=4$, $H_{mac}=34$, $H_{phy}=24$:

$$
L_{total} = (1500 + 20 + 20 + 4 + 34 + 24) \times 8
$$

$$
L_{total} = (1602) \times 8 = 12{,}816 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 4 — Transmission Delay at Data Rate R = 54 Mbps (802.11g)

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{L_{total}}{R} = \frac{12{,}816}{54 \times 10^{6}} \approx 2.373 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \approx 237.3 \;\mu s
$$

### Step 5 — Propagation Delay over d = 50 m Indoor Wi-Fi

Speed of light in copper/fiber ≈ $2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (use this in copper); in air ≈ $3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{50}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 1.67 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} \approx 0.167 \;\mu s
$$

### Step 6 — Total Single-Hop Layered Delay (Neglecting Queue)

$$
T_{e2e} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}
$$

$$
T_{e2e} \approx 237.3 + 0.167 + T_{proc} + T_{queue}
$$

For typical $T_{proc} \approx 50 \;\mu s$ across 5 layers and $T_{queue} \approx 100 \;\mu s$ under light load:

$$
T_{e2e} \approx 237.3 + 0.167 + 50 + 100 \approx 387.5 \;\mu s
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Protocol Efficiency $\eta$

$$
\eta = \frac{L_{app} \times 8}{L_{total}} = \frac{12{,}000}{12{,}816} \approx 0.9363 = 93.63\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The layered architecture imposes a ~6.4% overhead. In contrast, raw TCP/IP over Ethernet has only ~3% overhead — wireless stacks pay a higher price due to MAC and PHY headers needed for channel access and synchronization.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Wireless Protocol Architectures

| Property | OSI (Reference) | TCP/IP (Internet) | 3GPP LTE/5G NR | IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Layer Count | 7 | 4–5 | 7 (with sublayers) | 2 (MAC + PHY) |
| Plane Separation | No | No | **Yes (U/C)** | No |
| Native Mobility Support | No | Partial (Mobile IP) | **Yes (handover, NAS)** | Limited (no IP-level) |
| QoS Model | CoS-based | Best-effort (DiffServ) | **Bearer-based** | 802.11e EDCA/HCCA |
| Security Layer | Per-layer | TLS / IPsec | PDCP ciphering | WPA2/WPA3 |
| Suitability for KTU | Conceptual | Exam focus | Advanced elective | Practical lab |

## 3.3 Cross-Layer Design Trade-Off (Conceptual Walkthrough)

In traditional strict layering, layer $i$ cannot peek into layer $j \neq i$. Cross-Layer Design (CLD) allows **information exchange** between non-adjacent layers for optimization.

Example: TCP throughput over wireless degrades because TCP treats wireless packet loss as congestion. A cross-layer solution lets the **PHY layer notify TCP** that the loss was due to fading, not congestion, so TCP does not throttle its window.

$$
W_{TCP}(t+1) = \begin{cases} W_{TCP}(t) \cdot \frac{1}{2} & \text{if loss is congestion (default)} \\ W_{TCP}(t) & \text{if PHY-layer notification confirms channel loss} \end{cases}
$$

This is a KTU-favourite conceptual derivation showing **why layered architecture must sometimes be relaxed** in mobile/wireless scenarios.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS (Module Choice / Short Answer)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**"Compare the OSI Reference Model and the TCP/IP Protocol Suite in terms of layering, reliability, and practical adoption in wireless networks."**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

- The **OSI model** is a 7-layer theoretical reference framework (Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical) developed by ISO.
- The **TCP/IP model** is a 4-to-5-layer practical implementation suite (Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access/Link + Physical) that actually powers the Internet and mobile networks.
- **Layering:** OSI has 7 distinct layers; TCP/IP collapses the upper three OSI layers into a single "Application" layer.
- **Reliability:** OSI explicitly separates services, interfaces, and protocols; TCP/IP was built around a reliable (TCP) and unreliable (UDP) transport.
- **Practical Adoption:** TCP/IP is universally deployed in smartphones, Wi-Fi, LTE, 5G; OSI is rarely implemented fully but serves as a teaching and design reference.
- In wireless networks, TCP/IP is preferred because protocols like Mobile IP, RLC, and PDCP integrate seamlessly with it.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**
**"List any three wireless-specific protocol sublayers and justify why they are essential in mobile computing."**

**Model Answer (8–10 lines):**

- **MAC Sublayer (Medium Access Control):** Manages channel access among multiple wireless nodes (CSMA/CA in Wi-Fi, scheduled allocation in LTE/5G NR) — essential because the wireless medium is shared and broadcast.
- **LLC Sublayer (Logical Link Control):** IEEE 802.2 — provides flow control and error recovery independent of the underlying MAC technology, enabling technology-agnostic upper layers.
- **PDCP Sublayer (Packet Data Convergence Protocol) – 3GPP:** Performs IP header compression (ROHC), ciphering, and integrity protection — essential for bandwidth-limited cellular links.
- **RLC Sublayer (Radio Link Control) – 3GPP:** Provides segmentation, reordering, and ARQ over the error-prone radio link.
- **Physical Layer (PHY) Extensions:** OFDM, MIMO, mmWave — necessary to combat fading, multipath, and interference inherent to mobile radio channels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **LLC** (802.2, logical) with **MAC** (hardware addressing, channel access). Make the distinction clear: *LLC = logical functions, MAC = channel access*.

---

## PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (Full ESE Module Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Either Q-A or Q-B must be answered.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **OSI Reference Model** in detail. Discuss the functions of each layer with an example protocol from wireless/mobile computing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **TCP/IP Protocol Suite** architecture. Compare it with the OSI model and explain how Mobile IP extends the network layer for wireless mobility.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — OSI Reference Model:**

1. **Introduction:** The Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model was developed by ISO (ISO/IEC 7498-1) to provide a layered, vendor-neutral framework for inter-system communication. *(1 mark)*

2. **Seven Layers (with diagram):**

   - **Layer 7 — Application:** Closest to the user. Provides network services to end-user applications. *Examples:* HTTP (web), SMTP (email), FTP (file transfer). *(1 mark)*
   - **Layer 6 — Presentation:** Handles data representation, encryption (TLS/SSL at this conceptual layer), and compression (JPEG, MPEG). *(1 mark)*
   - **Layer 5 — Session:** Establishes, manages, and terminates sessions between applications. *Examples:* SIP (VoIP signaling), NetBIOS, RPC. *(1 mark)*
   - **Layer 4 — Transport:** Provides end-to-end communication, segmentation, flow control, and error recovery. *Examples:* TCP (reliable), UDP (unreliable, used in VoIP/streaming over mobile). *(1 mark)*
   - **Layer 3 — Network:** Logical addressing and routing across multiple networks. *Examples:* IP (IPv4/IPv6), ICMP, OSPF, BGP, Mobile IP. *(1 mark)*
   - **Layer 2 — Data Link:** Framing, MAC addressing, error detection. Divided into **LLC** (802.2) and **MAC** sublayers (802.11, LTE MAC). *(1 mark)*
   - **Layer 1 — Physical:** Bit-level transmission, modulation (BPSK, QAM, OFDM), encoding, antenna. *(1 mark)*

3. **Diagram:** *(Draw the 7-layer stack with PDU names — Data, Segment, Packet, Frame, Bit — and indicate wireless protocols at each layer.)*

**Part (b) — TCP/IP Architecture & Mobile IP:**

1. **TCP/IP Layered Structure:**
   - **Application Layer:** HTTP, FTP, DNS, DHCP, SMTP — same as OSI layers 5–7 combined. *(1 mark)*
   - **Transport Layer:** TCP (connection-oriented, reliable) and UDP (connectionless, low-latency). SCTP used in some 4G/5G signaling. *(1 mark)*
   - **Internet Layer:** IPv4/IPv6 — addressing, fragmentation, routing. ICMP for diagnostics. *(1 mark)*
   - **Network Access / Link Layer:** Combines OSI layers 1 and 2. In wireless — Wi-Fi (802.11), LTE, 5G NR MAC/PHY. *(1 mark)*

2. **Comparison with OSI:**

| Feature | OSI | TCP/IP |
|---|---|---|
| Layers | 7 | 4–5 |
| Origin | ISO standard | ARPANET / IETF |
| Adoption | Reference only | Universal |
| Session/Presentation | Explicit | Merged in Application |
| Wireless fit | Conceptual | Practical (Mobile IP) |

   *(1 mark)*

3. **Mobile IP Extension:**
   - **Problem:** Standard IP uses location-dependent addressing — when a mobile node moves, its IP changes, breaking ongoing TCP sessions. *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Solution — Mobile IP (RFC 3344, RFC 6275):**
     - **Home Agent (HA):** Router at the mobile node's home network that tunnels packets to the current location. *(0.5 mark)*
     - **Foreign Agent (FA):** Router in the visited network that delivers tunneled packets to the Mobile Node (MN). *(0.5 mark)*
     - **Care-of Address (CoA):** Temporary IP assigned to MN in foreign network. *(0.5 mark)*
     - **Process:** MN registers its CoA with HA → HA intercepts packets destined for MN's home address → encapsulates and tunnels them to CoA → FA decapsulates and delivers to MN → MN replies directly (triangle routing) or via reverse tunneling. *(1 mark)*

4. **Conclusion:** Mobile IP preserves the layered TCP/IP architecture while adding mobility support at the network layer — without disturbing transport or application layers. *(0.5 mark)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **wireless protocol architecture** integrating the TCP/IP model with wireless-specific sublayers. Draw the hybrid stack and identify the functions of LLC, MAC, and PHY layers.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **3GPP LTE / 5G NR protocol architecture** with user-plane and control-plane separation. Compare it briefly with the IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi) stack.

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Wireless Protocol Architecture (Hybrid Stack):**

1. **Need for Hybridization:** Standard TCP/IP (4 layers) is designed for wired networks. Wireless channels impose challenges — fading, interference, mobility, hidden terminals — that require additional sublayers. *(1 mark)*

2. **Hybrid Stack (Top to Bottom):**

   - **Application Layer:** Web, email, VoIP, streaming. *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Transport Layer:** TCP (for reliable data), UDP (for real-time voice/video), SCTP/DCCP for advanced services. *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Network Layer:** IPv4/IPv6, ICMP, Mobile IP, routing protocols (AODV, DSR for ad-hoc). *(0.5 mark)*
   - **Logical Link Control (LLC) Sublayer — IEEE 802.2:** Provides uniform interface to upper layers regardless of underlying MAC; performs flow control, error notification. *(1 mark)*
   - **Medium Access Control (MAC) Sublayer:** Manages shared channel access.
     - *Wi-Fi (802.11):* CSMA/CA + RTS/CTS + DCF/PCF/EDCA.
     - *Cellular (3GPP):* Scheduled access via base-station grants.
     - *Bluetooth (802.15.1):* Frequency-hopping TDMA.
     - *Ad-hoc (802.11s / 802.11ad):* Mesh variants.
     *(1.5 marks)*
   - **Physical Layer:** Modulation (BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM in Wi-Fi 6/7; OFDMA in 5G NR), coding (LDPC, Turbo, Polar), antenna techniques (MIMO, beamforming, mmWave for 5G FR2). *(1 mark)*

3. **Diagram:** *(Show hybrid stack with arrows indicating wireless-specific insertion of LLC/MAC/PHY between IP and antenna.)* *(1.5 marks)*

**Part (b) — 3GPP LTE/5G NR Architecture:**

1. **Plane Separation Rationale:** Cellular networks need to clearly separate user data traffic from signaling for QoS, security, and mobility efficiency. *(0.5 mark)*

2. **User Plane (U-Plane):** Application → TCP/UDP → IP → **PDCP** (header compression, ciphering) → **RLC** (segmentation, ARQ, reordering) → **MAC** (scheduling, HARQ, multiplexing) → **PHY** (OFDM, channel coding, MIMO). *(2 marks)*

3. **Control Plane (C-Plane):** Application (NAS — Non Access Stratum) → **RRC** (Radio Resource Control — handover, measurement reporting, connection management) → **PDCP** (secure signaling) → **RLC** (reliable signaling) → **MAC** (signaling priority) → **PHY**. *(2 marks)*

4. **Sublayer Functions:**
   - **PDCP:** ROHC (Robust Header Compression), integrity protection, ciphering.
   - **RLC:** Three modes — Transparent (TM), Unacknowledged (UM), Acknowledged (AM) with ARQ.
   - **MAC:** HARQ (Hybrid ARQ), scheduling, buffer status reporting, random access.
   - **PHY:** OFDMA downlink / SC-FDMA (LTE) or OFDMA (5G) uplink, channel coding, MIMO.

5. **Comparison with IEEE 802.11 (Wi-Fi):**

| Aspect | 3GPP LTE / 5G NR | IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | U/C plane separation | Single unified MAC/PHY |
| Channel Access | Centralized (scheduled) | Distributed (CSMA/CA) |
| Handover | Hard/Soft/SOFT-X2 | BSS transition (limited) |
| QoS | Bearer-based with QCI | EDCA / HCCA categories |
| Coverage | Wide-area cellular | Local-area |
| Use Case | Wide-area mobile broadband | Local wireless LAN |

   *(2 marks)*

6. **Conclusion:** While 3GPP architectures are optimized for wide-area mobility and strict QoS, IEEE 802.11 prioritizes simplicity and low-cost deployment for local wireless — both are essential in modern hybrid mobile devices. *(0.5 mark)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Confusing OSI with TCP/IP layers.** Always state the layer count clearly (7 vs 4–5) before describing functions.
> 2. **Forgetting PDU names.** Examiners allocate 1 mark for each correct PDU name (Data, Segment, Packet, Frame, Bit). Skipping them costs marks.
> 3. **Drawing the stack without arrows / direction.** Always show *downward* flow on sender and *upward* flow on receiver.
> 4. **Missing Mobile IP's "triangle routing" concept.** Many students forget to explain why Mobile IP exists (location-dependent addressing problem).
> 5. **Confusing PDCP/RLC/MAC of 3GPP with LLC/MAC of Wi-Fi.** They are functionally similar but terminologically distinct.
> 6. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers.** A 14-mark answer without at least one diagram typically loses 1–2 marks for "lack of visual clarity."
> 7. **Writing vague answers like "TCP is reliable and UDP is not"** — expand: "TCP provides in-order, byte-stream-oriented, connection-oriented, ACK-based reliable delivery; UDP provides best-effort, message-oriented, connectionless service."

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **OSI has 7 layers; TCP/IP has 4–5 layers.** Know the exact mapping: OSI 5–7 ↔ TCP/IP Application; OSI 4 ↔ TCP/IP Transport; OSI 3 ↔ TCP/IP Internet; OSI 1–2 ↔ TCP/IP Network Access.
- **PDU Names by Layer (Mnemonic: "D-S-P-F-B" — Data-Segment-Packet-Frame-Bit):** Application→Data, Transport→Segment (TCP) / Datagram (UDP), Network→Packet, Data Link→Frame, Physical→Bit.
- **Wireless Hybrid Stack** inserts **LLC, MAC, PHY** between the IP layer and the antenna — never omit them.
- **Encapsulation** = Sender adds headers; **Decapsulation** = Receiver strips headers. Each header is consumed only by the *peer* layer at the receiver.
- **Mobile IP** uses **Home Agent (HA)**, **Foreign Agent (FA)**, and **Care-of Address (CoA)** to support mobility without changing the mobile node's home IP.
- **3GPP LTE/5G NR** separates **User Plane (U-Plane)** for data and **Control Plane (C-Plane)** for signaling — protocols include **PDCP, RLC, MAC, PHY**.
- **PDCP** = header compression + ciphering; **RLC** = segmentation + ARQ; **MAC** = scheduling + HARQ; **PHY** = modulation + coding + MIMO.
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** uses **CSMA/CA** for distributed channel access — no centralized scheduler; uses **DCF, PCF, EDCA, HCCA**.
- **Cross-Layer Design (CLD)** is a research-driven relaxation of strict layering — particularly useful in mobile/wireless to mitigate TCP-over-wireless performance issues.
- **Protocol Efficiency** $\eta = \frac{L_{app}}{L_{total}}$ — wireless stacks typically have 5–10% overhead due to MAC and PHY headers.
- **End-to-End Delay** = sum of processing + transmission + propagation + queuing across all layers and all hops.
- **Always draw diagrams** in 14-mark answers — a clear OSI or TCP/IP or hybrid stack diagram is worth 1–2 marks on its own.
- **Know the constants:** Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in vacuum/air; $c \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in copper/fiber.
- **Cite at least one wireless protocol example at each layer** in any descriptive answer — examiners reward technology-aware answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Physical Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Physical Layer (PHY)** is the lowest stratum of the OSI and TCP/IP reference models, and in the context of wireless and mobile computing, it is responsible for the **raw bit-stream transmission and reception over the wireless radio propagation medium**. It governs all mechanical, electrical, and procedural aspects required to convert digital information into analog radio-frequency (RF) signals, transmit them across free space, and reconstruct them at the receiver.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> The Physical Layer in wireless networks encompasses **(i) wireless transmission fundamentals, (ii) frequencies, regulation, and standards, (iii) signal propagation phenomena, (iv) multiplexing schemes, (v) modulation techniques, (vi) spread spectrum concepts, and (vii) antenna systems.** It is the layer where **bits become electromagnetic waves** and vice-versa.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Stadium"

Imagine a stadium with **50,000 spectators** and a single announcer:
- The announcer's voice = the **transmitter** generating a signal.
- The air in the stadium = the **wireless channel**.
- The crowd's ears = the **receivers** (mobile devices).
- Echoes from walls, crowd noise, and movement of people = **multipath fading, shadowing, and noise**.
- The announcer raising or lowering pitch to encode meaning = **modulation**.
- Multiple announcers in different zones speaking simultaneously without confusion = **multiplexing** (TDMA, FDMA, CDMA).

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a well-trained announcer learns to modulate pitch and project clearly over noise, the Physical Layer engineers **modulation schemes** and **coding techniques** to combat real-world impairments in radio propagation.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **must-memorize** for KTU examinations:

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$** |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | **$c / f$** (meters) |
| Frequency | $f$ | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| 1 GHz | — | **$10^9$ Hz** |
| Free-space path loss exponent | $n$ | **2 (vacuum)**, **3–4 (urban)** |
| Thermal noise floor | $N_0$ | **$-174$ dBm/Hz** |
| Boltzmann constant | $k$ | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |

> [!TIP]
> **Frequency-Band Recall Trick:**
> - **VLF**: 3–30 kHz — Submarines
> - **LF/MF**: 30 kHz–3 MHz — AM Radio
> - **HF/VHF**: 3–300 MHz — FM Radio, TV
> - **UHF**: 300 MHz–3 GHz — **Mobile phones, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth**
> - **SHF/EHF**: > 3 GHz — 5G mmWave, Satellite, Radar

## 1.4 Role of the Physical Layer in the Wireless Stack

The PHY layer performs **four canonical functions**:

1. **Signal Generation** — Encoding of bits into waveforms (modulation).
2. **Signal Transmission** — Up-conversion to RF, amplification, and radiation via antennas.
3. **Signal Propagation** — Interaction with the channel (reflection, diffraction, scattering, absorption).
4. **Signal Reception** — Down-conversion, synchronization, demodulation, and bit recovery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Wireless Physical Layer

### A. Wireless Transmission Fundamentals
- A **transmitter** modulates a carrier wave $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ with the baseband information signal $m(t)$.
- The carrier is an **electromagnetic wave** generated by accelerating charges in an antenna.
- Wireless transmission uses **free-space propagation** between transmit and receive antennas.

### B. Frequencies & Regulation
- Frequency allocation is governed globally by the **ITU-R (International Telecommunication Union — Radiocommunication Sector)**.
- In India, the **WPC (Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing)** under DoT regulates spectrum.
- Lic vs. unlicensed bands: GSM/4G/5G use **licensed** bands; Wi-Fi/Bluetooth use **ISM 2.4/5 GHz unlicensed** bands.

### C. Signal Propagation Phenomena

| Phenomenon | Physical Cause | Effect on Signal |
|---|---|---|
| **Path Loss** | Spreading of EM energy with distance | Power decreases as $d^n$ |
| **Shadowing** | Obstacles (buildings, hills) | Slow, log-normal fading |
| **Multipath Fading** | Reflections from objects | Fast Rayleigh/Rician fading |
| **Doppler Shift** | Relative motion Tx/Rx | Frequency dispersion |
| **Noise** | Thermal, cosmic, man-made | SNR degradation |

### D. Multiplexing — Sharing the Wireless Medium

Multiple users share the limited spectrum using **multiple access techniques**:

| Technique | Full Form | Domain of Separation |
|---|---|---|
| **FDMA** | Frequency Division Multiple Access | Frequency |
| **TDMA** | Time Division Multiple Access | Time |
| **CDMA** | Code Division Multiple Access | Spreading codes |
| **SDMA** | Space Division Multiple Access | Space (directional antennas) |
| **OFDMA** | Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiple Access | Frequency (orthogonal subcarriers) |

### E. Spread Spectrum Techniques

- **DSSS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum)** — XORs data with a high-rate pseudo-noise (PN) chip sequence.
- **FHSS (Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum)** — Rapid carrier frequency hopping across many channels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Spread Spectrum?** It provides **resistance to jamming**, **low probability of intercept**, and **immunity to narrowband interference** — the very principles behind CDMA and Wi-Fi.

### F. Modulation Techniques (Critical for KTU)

| Type | Acronym | Bits/Symbol | Noise Immunity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Amplitude Shift Keying** | ASK | 1 | Poor |
| **Frequency Shift Keying** | FSK | 1 | Good |
| **Binary Phase Shift Keying** | BPSK | 1 | Very Good |
| **Quadrature Phase Shift Keying** | QPSK | 2 | Very Good |
| **Quadrature Amplitude Modulation** | QAM (e.g., 16-QAM, 64-QAM) | 4, 6 | Moderate |

### G. Antenna Fundamentals

- **Isotropic radiator**: Hypothetical point source radiating uniformly in all directions (gain = 0 dBi).
- **Half-wave dipole**: Realistic antenna, gain ≈ **2.15 dBi**.
- **Parabolic dish**: Highly directional, high gain, used in satellite links.
- **Antenna Gain** $G$ is **direction-dependent** and measured in **dBi (isotropic)** or **dBd (dipole)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Every modern technology — 5G, IoT, GPS, Radar, Satellite TV — relies on mastering the physical layer. MIMO, beamforming, and millimeter-wave communications (28 GHz / 60 GHz) all operate at this layer.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength from frequency | meters |
| 2 | $P_r(d) = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}$ | **Friis Free-Space Equation** | Watts |
| 3 | $PL(d)\big\vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free-space path loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dB |
| 4 | $PL(d)\big\vert_{dB} = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right)$ | **Log-distance path loss model** | dB |
| 5 | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | **Shannon-Hartley Capacity** | bits/sec |
| 6 | $G = \dfrac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2}$ | Antenna Gain (effective aperture $A_e$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $N = kTB$ | Thermal noise power | Watts |
| 8 | $E_b/N_0 = \dfrac{S/R_b}{N/B}$ | Bit-energy to noise-spectral-density | dimensionless |
| 9 | $BER_{BPSK} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0}\right)$ | Bit-error-rate of BPSK | dimensionless |
| 10 | $v = f_d \cdot \lambda$ | Mobile speed via Doppler | m/s |
| 11 | $f_d = \dfrac{v \cos\theta}{c} \cdot f_c$ | Doppler frequency shift | Hz |
| 12 | $G_{dBi} = G_{dBd} + 2.15$ | Conversion between dBi and dBd | dB |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams — "C-L-B-G-E-Q-N-F-V-D":**
> $c$, $\lambda$, $B$, $G$, $E_b/N_0$, $Q$-function, $N=kTB$, $f_d$, $v$, $d$ — **the 10 golden symbols of the PHY layer.**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | PHY-Layer Application |
|---|---|
| **5G NR** | OFDMA + 64/256-QAM + Massive MIMO + mmWave |
| **Wi-Fi 6/6E/7** | OFDMA + 1024-QAM + MU-MIMO |
| **Bluetooth** | FHSS at 1600 hops/sec in 2.4 GHz ISM |
| **GPS** | DSSS with 1.023 Mcps C/A code |
| **Radar** | Pulsed modulation, Doppler analysis |
| **Satellite** | High-gain parabolic antennas, QPSK/8-PSK |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Friis Free-Space Transmission Equation

**Goal:** Find received power $P_r$ as a function of transmitted power, antenna gains, distance, and frequency.

**Starting Principle:** A transmit antenna radiates isotropically with total power $P_t$. Power flux density (power per unit area) at distance $d$ is:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}
$$

This is the **inverse-square law** of radiation — geometric spreading over a sphere of surface area $4\pi d^2$.

The **effective aperture** of the receive antenna $A_{er}$ relates to gain:

$$
A_{er} = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

The **received power** is the product of flux density and effective aperture:

$$
P_r = S \cdot A_{er} = \left(\frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}\right)
$$

**Simplifying the denominator:**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 \, d^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Friis Free-Space Equation** — the foundational formula of all wireless link-budget analysis.

**Conversion to dB form (highly useful for KTU numericals):**

$$
P_r \big\vert_{dBm} = P_t \big\vert_{dBm} + G_t \big\vert_{dBi} + G_r \big\vert_{dBi} + 20\log_{10}(\lambda) - 20\log_{10}(d) - 21.98
$$

For $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz, substituting $\lambda = 300/f_{MHz}$ yields the **engineering shortcut**:

$$
PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Log-Distance Path Loss Model

**Goal:** Generalize Friis for non-free-space (real-world) environments.

The **path-loss exponent $n$** captures the additional loss due to obstacles:

$$
n = \begin{cases} 2 & \text{free space} \\ 3 & \text{urban shadowed} \\ 4 & \text{urban dense / obstructed indoor} \\ 5\text{–}6 & \text{heavy indoor obstruction} \end{cases}
$$

The reference path loss at distance $d_0$ is computed from Friis:

$$
PL(d_0) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d_0}{\lambda}\right)
$$

For any distance $d > d_0$:

$$
PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Why $n=4$ in urban areas?"* Because in addition to geometric spreading, **multipath reflections** cause additional destructive interference, doubling the loss rate.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Channel Capacity

**Goal:** Find the maximum error-free bit rate $C$ over a band-limited noisy channel.

A signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz is corrupted by additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) of power $N$. The signal-to-noise ratio is $S/N$.

**Step 1 — Number of independent signaling levels per sample:**

$$
M = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}
$$

**Step 2 — Bits per symbol (Nyquist's interpretation):**

$$
\text{bits/sample} = \log_2(M) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Sampling rate per second (Nyquist rate):**

$$
f_s = 2B \quad \text{samples/sec}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
C = 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

This is **Shannon's Capacity Theorem** — the **absolute upper bound** on information transfer.

> [!NOTE]
