> **Real-world systems never achieve Shannon limit.** Practical modulations (M-QAM) approach it asymptotically as constellation size grows.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Antenna Gain from Effective Aperture

A parabolic reflector antenna of physical area $A_p$ has **aperture efficiency** $\eta$ (typically 0.55–0.65). Its effective aperture is:

$$
A_e = \eta A_p
$$

The **maximum directive gain** of the antenna is:

$$
G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \frac{4\pi \eta A_p}{\lambda^2}
$$

In dBi:

$$
G \big\vert_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \eta A_p}{\lambda^2}\right)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam heuristic:** Doubling frequency **quadruples** gain (since $G \propto 1/\lambda^2$). This is why mmWave antennas are physically tiny but highly directional.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Sample KTU Problem

**Problem:** A transmitter radiates $P_t = 10$ W at $f_c = 2.4$ GHz. Tx antenna gain $G_t = 6$ dBi, Rx antenna gain $G_r = 3$ dBi. Distance $d = 5$ km. Calculate (a) wavelength, (b) free-space path loss, (c) received power in dBm.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m}
$$

**Step (b):** Using the engineering shortcut with $d = 5$ km, $f = 2400$ MHz:

$$
PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(5) + 20\log_{10}(2400)
$$

$$
PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 13.98 + 67.60 = 114.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step (c):** Convert $P_t$ to dBm:

$$
P_t \big\vert_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10\text{ W}}{1\text{ mW}}\right) = 40 \text{ dBm}
$$

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL = 40 + 6 + 3 - 114.02 = -65.02 \text{ dBm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** $-65$ dBm is **strong 4G/Wi-Fi signal strength**. Real systems typically need **$-90$ to $-100$ dBm** for reliable decoding. The 5 km link at 2.4 GHz is near the **maximum feasible range** without directional antennas.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — End-to-End Wireless Physical Layer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Bits] --> B[Channel Coding FEC]
    B --> C[Interleaver]
    C --> D[Modulator BPSK QPSK QAM]
    D --> E[Spread Spectrum DSSS FHSS]
    E --> F[Up-conversion to RF Carrier fc]
    F --> G[Power Amplifier]
    G --> H[Transmit Antenna Gt]
    H --> I[Wireless Channel Path Loss Multipath Fading Noise]
    I --> J[Receive Antenna Gr]
    J --> K[Low Noise Amplifier]
    K --> L[Down-conversion to Baseband]
    L --> M[Matched Filter / Correlator]
    M --> N[Demodulator]
    N --> O[De-interleaver]
    O --> P[Channel Decoding]
    P --> Q[Recovered Bits]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram captures the **canonical Tx–Channel–Rx pipeline** used in every wireless standard from GSM to 5G NR.

---

## 4.2 Frequency Allocation Map (KTU High-Yield Visual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIC[LICENSED BANDS]
        A1[GSM 900: 890-960 MHz]
        A2[LTE Band 3: 1800 MHz]
        A3[5G Sub-6 GHz: 3.5 GHz]
        A4[5G mmWave: 28 GHz]
        A5[Satellite Ku: 12-18 GHz]
    end
    subgraph UNL[UNLICENSED ISM BANDS]
        B1[433 MHz IoT]
        B2[2.4 GHz Wi-Fi BT]
        B3[5 GHz Wi-Fi UNII]
        B4[60 GHz V-Band WiGig]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Multiplexing Domain Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FDMA[FDMA - Frequency Division]
        F1[User 1: f1]
        F2[User 2: f2]
        F3[User 3: f3]
    end
    subgraph TDMA[TDMA - Time Division]
        T1[User 1: slot1]
        T2[User 2: slot2]
        T3[User 3: slot3]
    end
    subgraph CDMA[CDMA - Code Division]
        C1[All Users share same frequency and time, separated by PN codes]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Modulation Constellation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BPSK - 1 bit - 180 deg phase shift] --> B[QPSK - 2 bits - 4 points]
    B --> C[8-PSK - 3 bits - 8 points]
    C --> D[16-QAM - 4 bits - 16 points]
    D --> E[64-QAM - 6 bits - 64 points]
    E --> F[256-QAM - 8 bits - 256 points]
    F --> G[1024-QAM - 10 bits - 1024 points - Wi-Fi 6 only]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Triangle — Bandwidth ↔ Power ↔ Complexity:**
> Higher-order QAM packs more bits per symbol but requires **higher SNR** and **better linearity** in amplifiers. KTU examiners love this trade-off question.

---

## 4.5 Signal Propagation Mechanisms

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Transmitter] --> R1[Reflection - from walls and ground]
    TX --> R2[Diffraction - around sharp edges]
    TX --> R3[Scattering - from small objects]
    TX --> L1[Line of Sight LOS]
    R1 --> RX[Receiver]
    R2 --> RX
    R3 --> RX
    L1 --> RX
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Free-Space Path Loss" and explain how it varies with frequency and distance in a wireless communication system.**

**Model Answer:**
Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) is the attenuation of radio signal strength as it propagates through a vacuum or ideal free space with no obstacles. It arises due to the spherical spreading of electromagnetic energy. The standard formula in dB is:

$$
PL_{dB} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})
$$

Path loss **increases logarithmically** with both distance and frequency. Doubling either distance or frequency increases the path loss by **6 dB**. Hence, higher frequencies (like 28 GHz mmWave) suffer significantly greater attenuation than sub-6 GHz bands, limiting their range.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Compare FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA in terms of the resource domain used for separation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Separation Domain** | Frequency | Time | Code |
| **Synchronization** | Not required | Tightly required | Code synchronization needed |
| **Interference** | Cross-talk | Co-channel | Multiple Access Interference (MAI) |
| **Capacity** | Limited by bandwidth | Limited by slots | Soft capacity via codes |
| **Example System** | Analog AMPS | GSM | IS-95, 3G UMTS |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Derive the **Friis free-space transmission equation** and explain each term. Explain how received power is computed at 5 km for a 2.4 GHz link with $P_t = 1$ W and isotropic antennas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **log-distance path loss model** with reference to the path-loss exponent $n$. Discuss typical values of $n$ in free space, urban, and indoor shadowed environments. Compute the path loss at $d = 100$ m for $f = 900$ MHz, $d_0 = 1$ m, and $n = 3.5$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Question A(a):**

**Starting Point** — Power flux density radiated from an isotropic antenna at distance $d$:

$$
S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}
$$

**Effective aperture** of a receive antenna with gain $G_r$:

$$
A_{er} = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}
$$

**Multiplying:**

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^2 d^2}
$$

**Substituting values:** $P_t = 1$ W, $G_t = G_r = 1$ (isotropic), $\lambda = 3 \times 10^8 / 2.4 \times 10^9 = 0.125$ m, $d = 5000$ m:

$$
P_r = \frac{1 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 \cdot (0.125)^2}{(4\pi)^2 \cdot (5000)^2}
$$

$$
P_r = \frac{0.015625}{39.478 \cdot 25 \times 10^6} = \frac{0.015625}{9.8696 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
P_r \approx 1.583 \times 10^{-11} \text{ W} = -78 \text{ dBm}
$$

---

#### **Solution for Question A(b):**

**Log-distance model:**

$$
PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)
$$

**Reference path loss** at $d_0 = 1$ m, $f = 900$ MHz:

$$
PL(d_0) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi \cdot 1}{0.333}\right) = 20\log_{10}(37.7) \approx 31.53 \text{ dB}
$$

**Path loss at $d = 100$ m, $n = 3.5$:**

$$
PL(100) = 31.53 + 10 \cdot 3.5 \cdot \log_{10}(100) = 31.53 + 35 \cdot 2 = 31.53 + 70 = 101.53 \text{ dB}
$$

**Typical values of $n$:**

| Environment | $n$ |
|---|---|
| Free space | 2 |
| Urban macrocell | 3.5–4 |
| Urban microcell | 2.7–3.5 |
| Indoor LOS | 1.6–1.8 |
| Indoor obstructed | 4–6 |

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** With neat constellation diagrams, explain **BPSK, QPSK, and 16-QAM** modulation schemes. Compare their **bandwidth efficiency** and **noise immunity**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Spread Spectrum** with reference to **DSSS and FHSS**. How does spread spectrum provide immunity against jamming and multipath interference? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution for Question B(a):**

**BPSK (Binary Phase Shift Keying):**
- Maps bit 0 → $+1$, bit 1 → $-1$.
- Symbol energy $E_s = E_b$, BER $= Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$.
- Spectral efficiency: **1 bit/sec/Hz**.

**QPSK (Quadrature Phase Shift Keying):**
- Maps 2 bits to one of 4 phases: $45°, 135°, 225°, 315°$.
- BER $\approx Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ — same as BPSK per bit.
- Spectral efficiency: **2 bits/sec/Hz**.

**16-QAM:**
- Maps 4 bits to one of 16 points in I-Q plane.
- BER $\approx \dfrac{3}{4} Q\!\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{4}{5} E_b/N_0}\right)$.
- Spectral efficiency: **4 bits/sec/Hz**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK | 16-QAM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bits/symbol | 1 | 2 | 4 |
| Spectral efficiency | Low | Medium | High |
| Noise immunity | Best | Good | Poorest |
| Min SNR required | ~7 dB | ~10 dB | ~17 dB |
| Use case | Deep-space, satellite | 3G/4G | Wi-Fi, 5G |

**Constellation Description (ASCII approximation):**
- BPSK: two points on I-axis.
- QPSK: four points on a circle at 90° apart.
- 16-QAM: 4×4 grid in I-Q plane.

---

#### **Solution for Question B(b):**

**Spread Spectrum Concept:**
Spread spectrum transmits the signal over a bandwidth **much larger than the information bandwidth**, using a pseudo-random spreading code. This makes the signal appear noise-like to unintended receivers.

**DSSS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum):**
- Each bit is XOR'd with a high-rate **PN chip sequence** (e.g., 11 chips/bit in 802.11b).
- Spreading factor = chips/bit.
- At the receiver, correlation with the same PN code recovers the original signal.

**FHSS (Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum):**
- The carrier frequency **hops rapidly** between many channels following a pseudo-random pattern (e.g., 79 hops in 1 MHz channels for Bluetooth).
- Hop rate: 1600 hops/sec for classic Bluetooth.

**Jamming Immunity Mechanism:**
- A narrowband jammer only corrupts a small fraction of the spread band.
- After de-spreading at the receiver, the jammer's energy spreads out while the signal concentrates, yielding **processing gain**:

$$
G_p = \frac{W}{R} = \frac{\text{Spread bandwidth}}{\text{Data rate}}
$$

**Multipath Immunity:**
- Multipath components arriving with different delays appear as uncorrelated noise to the despreader.
- Rake receivers can even **combine multipath components** constructively using multiple correlators.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency**: Mixing up km with meters, MHz with GHz, dBW with dBm. Always write **the unit explicitly** in every formula.
>
> 2. **Wrong Friis Form**: Several students write $P_r = P_t G_t G_r / (4\pi d)^2$ but forget $\lambda^2$ in the numerator. **Memory check**: The Friis equation has $4\pi$ appearing **twice** and $\lambda^2$ appearing **once** in the numerator.
>
> 3. **Shannon Bound Misuse**: Students apply Shannon's capacity formula without checking whether **bandwidth or power is the bottleneck**.
>
> 4. **dB/dBi/dBm Confusion**: $G_{dBi} = G_{dBd} + 2.15$ — this conversion is **frequently tested** but most students forget the constant.
>
> 5. **Path-Loss Exponent Confusion**: Using $n=2$ for urban environments is a **sure 2-mark penalty**.
>
> 6. **Modulation BER formulas**: BPSK and QPSK have **the same BER vs $E_b/N_0$ curve** — students wrongly think QPSK has half the BER.
>
> 7. **Spreading Factor vs. Processing Gain**: These are numerically equal but conceptually different — examiners expect both terms to be defined.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Friis Free-Space Equation** — fundamental link-budget formula.
- [x] **Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s** — universal constant for $\lambda$-$f$ conversions.
- [x] **Path-loss exponent $n$** distinguishes free space from urban/indoor.
- [x] **Shannon Capacity $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$** — absolute upper bound.
- [x] **Thermal noise floor** $= -174$ dBm/Hz (very common KTU value).
- [x] **Multiplexing families** — FDMA, TDMA, CDMA, SDMA, OFDMA.
- [x] **Spread spectrum types** — DSSS (Walsh/PN codes) vs. FHSS (frequency hopping).
- [x] **Modulation hierarchy** — ASK → FSK → PSK → QPSK → QAM (increasing spectral efficiency).
- [x] **Antenna gain $G_{dBi} = G_{dBd} + 2.15$**.
- [x] **Doppler shift $f_d = (v\cos\theta / c) f_c$** governs fast fading in mobile environments.
- [x] **BPSK BER $= Q(\sqrt{2 E_b/N_0})$**.
- [x] **5G NR uses OFDMA + QAM + Massive MIMO + mmWave** — the PHY culmination.
- [x] **Conversion**: $P_{dBm} = 10\log_{10}(P_{mW})$.
- [x] **Trade-off**: Higher-order modulation → higher spectral efficiency → lower noise immunity.
- [x] **Processing Gain** $G_p = W/R$ — key to spread spectrum's anti-jamming property.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Bits in, Electromagnetic waves out, Electromagnetic waves in, Bits out."* — That is the entire Physical Layer in one sentence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medium Access Control Layer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Medium Access Control Layer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Medium Access Control (MAC) Layer** is a sublayer of the **Data Link Layer (DLL)** in the OSI and TCP/IP reference models. Its fundamental responsibility is to govern how multiple independent nodes (stations, mobile devices, base stations) gain access to a **shared, finite, and broadcast wireless medium** without causing destructive mutual interference.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *Wireless & Mobile Computing*, the MAC layer is positioned as the **core arbitration mechanism** that determines *who can transmit, when, and for how long* over the error-prone, bandwidth-constrained radio channel. It solves the classical **Multiple Access Problem** in wireless networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU PECST633 Module 1**
> The MAC sublayer topic explicitly covers: *Multiple Access Protocols (ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, CSMA, CSMA/CD, CSMA/CA), Hidden and Exposed Terminal Problem, Reservation-based MAC, and IEEE 802.11 MAC Protocol*.

### Formal Boundaries

The MAC sublayer interfaces with:
- **Upper Layer:** Logical Link Control (LLC) — which handles error control, framing, and flow control.
- **Lower Layer:** Physical Layer (PHY) — which handles modulation, transmission, and reception of raw radio bits.

The key engineering parameters the MAC layer must optimize simultaneously are:

| Parameter | Symbol | Target Value (KTU Notation) |
|---|---|---|
| **Channel Throughput** | $S$ | Maximize (frames per unit time) |
| **Mean Packet Delay** | $D$ | Minimize (seconds) |
| **Channel Utilization** | $U$ | $\to 1$ (ideal) |
| **Collision Probability** | $P_c$ | $\to 0$ |
| **Fairness Index** | $F$ | $\to 1$ |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Round Table Conversation"

Imagine **6 friends sitting around a single round dinner table** in a quiet hall. Each friend wants to speak, but only **one voice can be intelligible at a time** without turning the conversation into chaos.

- **The Air (Wireless Medium):** Just like the air around the table, the wireless spectrum is a shared, open resource. Only one person speaking clearly is heard.
- **The Friends (Mobile Nodes):** Each friend is a wireless station. They must **self-coordinate** who talks next.
- **The Rulebook (MAC Protocol):** This is the "raised hand" rule, the "polite interruption" rule, or the "chairman decides" rule — these are exactly equivalent to ALOHA, CSMA, and TDMA respectively.

Without a rulebook (MAC protocol), everyone shouts simultaneously and nobody is understood (a **collision**). With a poorly designed rulebook, the table is silent for too long (low **throughput**) or one friend dominates (poor **fairness**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Tip:** A good MAC protocol is **invisible when it works**. Students should remember that the MAC layer's success is measured by the *absence* of collisions, not by its visibility.

## 1.3 Why a Dedicated MAC Layer is Mandatory in Wireless

Wireless links have three characteristics that make MAC design harder than wired Ethernet:

1. **No Collision Detection (CD) is feasible** — a node cannot listen to the channel while transmitting on the same antenna (half-duplex constraint), so we must use **Collision Avoidance (CA)** instead.
2. **Hidden Terminal Problem** — node A can reach B, and C can reach B, but A and C cannot hear each other. They collide *at B* without ever sensing each other.
3. **Exposed Terminal Problem** — node B transmits to A; node C wants to transmit to D. C is "exposed" to B's signal and waits unnecessarily, wasting channel capacity.

These three realities define the entire design philosophy of wireless MAC protocols.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Multiple Access Protocols

The KTU syllabus organizes MAC protocols into **three canonical families**. Every protocol you study belongs to exactly one of these:

### A. Random / Contention-Based Protocols
Nodes compete for the channel; collisions are statistically inevitable but accepted.

- **Pure ALOHA**
- **Slotted ALOHA**
- **CSMA (1-persistent, non-persistent, p-persistent)**
- **CSMA/CD** (wired only — not used in wireless)
- **CSMA/CA** (wireless standard — used in IEEE 802.11)

### B. Controlled / Collision-Free Protocols
A deterministic schedule guarantees zero collisions.

- **Reservation (R-ALOHA)**
- **Polling**
- **Bit-Map Protocol**
- **Token Passing**
- **TDMA / FDMA / CDMA**

### C. Hybrid Protocols
Combine advantages of both, e.g., **IEEE 802.11 DCF (CSMA/CA + ACK)** and **HIPERLAN/2 (TDMA + reservation)**.

## 2.2 Pure ALOHA — Operational Logic

- Each station transmits **immediately** whenever it has a frame ready.
- After transmission, the station waits for an **ACK** from the receiver.
- If no ACK arrives within a **timeout** $\tau$, the station assumes a collision occurred and **retransmits** after a random **backoff delay**.
- Vulnerable period: **$2T$** (where $T$ = frame transmission time).

### Why "Vulnerable Period = 2T" — The "Why" Behind It
Any frame whose transmission **starts** in the interval $[t_0 - T, \; t_0]$ will overlap with the frame started at time $t_0$. Both frames are destroyed. Hence the window of vulnerability is twice the frame duration.

## 2.3 Slotted ALOHA — Operational Logic

- The common channel time is divided into **discrete slots** of duration $T$.
- Stations may **only begin transmission at the beginning of a slot**.
- Vulnerable period reduces from $2T$ to $T$.
- Requires **synchronization** among all stations (a clock distribution problem).

## 2.4 CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access) Family

**Core idea:** "Listen before you talk."

| Variant | Sensing Behavior | Collision Probability |
|---|---|---|
| **1-persistent CSMA** | If idle → transmit immediately; if busy → wait until idle, then transmit with prob. 1 | Highest (collision after busy) |
| **Non-persistent CSMA** | If idle → transmit; if busy → wait a random time, then resense | Lowest but high delay |
| **p-persistent CSMA** | If idle → transmit with prob. $p$, defer with prob. $1-p$; used in slotted channels | Tunable trade-off |

> [!NOTE]
> **CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA — Key Distinction for KTU:**
> - **CSMA/CD** (Collision **Detection**) is used in **wired Ethernet (IEEE 802.3)**. Nodes abort transmission upon sensing a collision.
> - **CSMA/CA** (Collision **Avoidance**) is used in **wireless (IEEE 802.11)**. Nodes *prevent* collisions using RTS/CTS handshakes because they **cannot detect collisions** while transmitting.

## 2.5 Hidden and Exposed Terminal Problems

### Hidden Terminal Problem (Critical for KTU)
Consider 3 nodes A, B, C:
- A and C are both within range of B.
- A and C are **out of range of each other**.
- When A transmits to B, C is unaware. C may also transmit to B → **collision at B**.

### Exposed Terminal Problem
- B transmits to A.
- C wants to transmit to D (D is far from B).
- C senses the medium (hears B's signal) and **falsely defers**, wasting bandwidth.

**Solution used in IEEE 802.11:** **RTS/CTS (Request-To-Send / Clear-To-Send)** handshake solves the hidden terminal problem. The CTS from the receiver reserves the channel for the sender.

## 2.6 IEEE 802.11 MAC Protocol (DCF)

The **Distributed Coordination Function (DCF)** is the foundational MAC mechanism of Wi-Fi.

### Key Mechanisms
1. **CSMA/CA** — listen before transmit.
2. **DIFS (Distributed Inter-Frame Space)** — mandatory idle time before contention.
3. **Backoff Timer** — random integer in $[0, CW]$, where $CW$ is the contention window.
4. **Binary Exponential Backoff** — $CW$ doubles after each failed transmission up to $CW_{max}$.
5. **RTS/CTS Handshake** — optional but strongly recommended; solves hidden terminal.
6. **SIFS (Short Inter-Frame Space)** — used for ACK, CTS, and fragment responses.
7. **Network Allocation Vector (NAV)** — virtual carrier-sense using duration fields in frames.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of IFS priority (SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS):**
> SIFS is the shortest and is reserved for highest-priority responses (ACKs). DIFS is the longest and is used by ordinary data frames — ensuring ACKs grab the channel first.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict LaTeX Compliance:** All absolute value bars use `\vert` instead of `|` to prevent breaking markdown tables.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pure ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | frames/frame-time | Max at $G = 0.5$ |
| 2 | Pure ALOHA Max Throughput | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ | normalized | 18.4% efficiency |
| 3 | Slotted ALOHA Throughput | $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$ | frames/slot | Max at $G = 1$ |
| 4 | Slotted ALOHA Max Throughput | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ | normalized | 36.8% efficiency |
| 5 | Vulnerable Period — Pure ALOHA | $V_{pure} = 2T$ | seconds | Two frames overlap |
| 6 | Vulnerable Period — Slotted ALOHA | $V_{slotted} = T$ | seconds | Half of pure ALOHA |
| 7 | Mean Backoff Delay — Slotted ALOHA | $E[K] = \dfrac{K_{max} + 1}{2}$ | slots | Linear backoff |
| 8 | Binary Exponential Backoff | $CW_{i+1} = \min(2(CW_i + 1) - 1, \; CW_{max})$ | slots | $CW_0 = 31$ for 802.11 |
| 9 | Throughput Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Tx Time}}{\text{Total Channel Time}}$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 10 | Utilization (Pure ALOHA) | $U = S \cdot \dfrac{L}{T}$ | bps | $L$ = bits/frame |
| 11 | Mean Service Time | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | seconds | M/M/1 queuing |
| 12 | Delay (Pure ALOHA) | $D = T \cdot (R + 1) + \tau$ | seconds | $R$ = avg retransmissions |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The MAC protocol family directly drives:
- **Wi-Fi (802.11 a/b/g/n/ac/ax)** — CSMA/CA based.
- **4G LTE / 5G NR** — Scheduled MAC with OFDMA in 5G NR.
- **Satellite Systems (VSAT)** — Slotted ALOHA variants (S-ALOHA) for uplink.
- **RFID** — Slotted ALOHA for anti-collision in tag identification.
- **Bluetooth (802.15.1)** — Polling-based MAC.
- **IoT (LoRaWAN, Sigfox)** — ALOHA-like for low-power uplink (energy is more scarce than bandwidth).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Throughput Derivation of Pure ALOHA

### Step 1 — Define the System Model
- Channel is shared by an infinite population of users generating new frames according to a **Poisson process** with mean rate $\lambda$ frames per frame-time $T$.
- Total channel load (new + retransmitted) follows Poisson with mean rate $G$ frames per frame-time.
- Frame transmission time is fixed at $T$ (normalized to **1 frame-time**).

### Step 2 — Compute Probability of Zero Collisions for a Given Frame
A frame is successfully transmitted **iff no other frame is transmitted in the vulnerable window of length $2T$**. By the Poisson property:

$$
P(\text{no other frame in } 2T) = \dfrac{(2G)^0 e^{-2G}}{0!} = e^{-2G}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Poisson probability mass function $P(k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$, here $k=0$, $\lambda t = 2G$.

### Step 3 — Compute Throughput
Throughput $S$ = (channel load) × (probability of successful transmission per frame):

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-2G}
$$

### Step 4 — Maximize Throughput w.r.t. $G$
Differentiate and set to zero:

$$
\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G} + G \cdot (-2) \cdot e^{-2G} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
1 - 2G = 0 \implies G = 0.5
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate Maximum Throughput

$$
S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-2(0.5)} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always carry units (frames/frame-time) in step-by-step derivations. Examiners award partial credit for dimensional consistency even if the final numerical value is wrong.

## 3.2 Throughput Derivation of Slotted ALOHA

### Step 1 — Modify the Vulnerable Period
With slotted transmission, the vulnerable window shrinks from $2T$ to **exactly $T$** (one slot), because only one frame can be initiated per slot.

### Step 2 — Probability of No Collision

$$
P(\text{no other frame in 1 slot}) = e^{-G}
$$

### Step 3 — Throughput Equation

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-G}
$$

### Step 4 — Maximize w.r.t. $G$

$$
\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-G}(1 - G) = 0 \implies G = 1
$$

### Step 5 — Maximum Throughput

$$
S_{max} = 1 \cdot e^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368
$$

### Step 6 — Comparative Insight

$$
\dfrac{S_{max}^{slotted}}{S_{max}^{pure}} = \dfrac{1/e}{1/(2e)} = 2
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Slotted ALOHA has exactly **double** the maximum throughput of Pure ALOHA, because the vulnerable period is halved.

## 3.3 Throughput Derivation of 1-Persistent CSMA (Non-Slotted)

### Step 1 — Define the Propagation Delay
Let $a = \dfrac{\tau}{T}$, where $\tau$ is the one-way propagation delay and $T$ is the frame transmission time.

For a LAN with cable length $d$, propagation speed $v$:

$$
a = \dfrac{d}{v \cdot T}
$$

### Step 2 — Vulnerable Period for CSMA
A station senses the channel idle and starts transmitting. Another station that began transmitting **$\tau$ seconds earlier** will not yet have been detected. Hence:

$$
V_{CSMA} = T + \tau = T(1 + a)
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The first $\tau$ seconds is the "blind" period during which the late-starting station has not yet heard the early-starting station's signal.

### Step 3 — Throughput Equation (Simplified for 1-persistent)

$$
S = \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G(1+a)}}{G(1+a) + e^{-aG}}
$$

This is a more sophisticated result. For $a \to 0$ (idealized local network):

$$
S \to \dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G}}{G + 1}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Behavior
- $a = 0$ → approaches slotted ALOHA-like performance.
- $a \to \infty$ (long cables, e.g., satellite) → CSMA degrades toward Pure ALOHA performance.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Protocols at Peak Load

| Protocol | Throughput $S$ | $S_{max}$ | Optimal $G$ | Collision Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure ALOHA** | $G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $\approx 0.184$ | $0.5$ | Probabilistic |
| **Slotted ALOHA** | $G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $\approx 0.368$ | $1.0$ | Probabilistic |
| **1-persistent CSMA** | $\dfrac{G e^{-G(1+a)}}{(1+a)G + e^{-aG}}$ | $\approx 0.53$ (at $a=0.01$) | varies | Reduced |
| **Non-persistent CSMA** | $\dfrac{G e^{-aG}}{G + e^{-aG}}$ | $\approx 0.81$ (at small $a$) | varies | Lowest |
| **Ideal CSMA** (infinite speed sensing) | $\dfrac{G}{1+G}$ | $\to 1$ | $\infty$ | Zero |
| **TDMA (perfectly scheduled)** | $\dfrac{1}{N}$ per user | $1$ aggregate | deterministic | Zero |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** As the "sensing" capability of CSMA increases (and $a \to 0$), throughput approaches 100%. This is the engineering motivation for **collision avoidance** in modern wireless — perfect sensing → zero collision.

## 3.5 Delay Analysis for Slotted ALOHA

Let $K$ = number of retransmission attempts before success. For Slotted ALOHA with backoff uniformly distributed in $[0, K_{max}]$:

### Expected Retransmission Delay

$$
E[K] = \dfrac{K_{max} + 1}{2} \cdot T
$$

### Total Mean Delay (queuing + transmission + retransmission)

$$
D = T \cdot (R + 1) + \tau_{prop}
$$

Where:
- $R$ = mean number of retransmissions = $\dfrac{G}{S} - 1$ (from $S = G \cdot e^{-G}$).
- $\tau_{prop}$ = one-way propagation delay.

### Conversion Logic
$R$ is derived by inverting $S = G e^{-G}$. Under high load $G \approx 1$, $R \approx e - 1 \approx 1.718$, meaning on average a frame requires $\approx 1.7$ retransmissions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Multiple Access Protocols (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Multiple Access Protocols]:::root --> B[Contention Based]:::branch
    A --> C[Controlled Access]:::branch
    A --> D[Hybrid Access]:::branch

    B --> B1[Pure ALOHA]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Slotted ALOHA]:::leaf
    B --> B3[CSMA Family]:::leaf
    B3 --> B3a[1 Persistent CSMA]:::subleaf
    B3 --> B3b[Non Persistent CSMA]:::subleaf
    B3 --> B3c[P Persistent CSMA]:::subleaf
    B3 --> B3d[CSMA CD Wired]:::subleaf
    B3 --> B3e[CSMA CA Wireless]:::subleaf

    C --> C1[Reservation R ALOHA]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Polling]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Token Passing]:::leaf
    C --> C4[TDMA FDMA CDMA]:::leaf

    D --> D1[IEEE 802.11 DCF]:::leaf
    D --> D2[IEEE 802.11 HCF]:::leaf
    D --> D3[HIPERLAN 2]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef subleaf fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Hidden and Exposed Terminal Problems — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A((Node A)):::hidden -->|transmits to B| B((Node B)):::rx
    C((Node C)):::hidden -->|also transmits to B| B
    A -.->|out of range, cannot hear| C

    B -->|ACK| A
    B -->|ACK| C

    note1[Hidden Terminal Problem: A and C collide at B without sensing each other]:::annot --> B

    classDef hidden fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef rx fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#000
    classDef annot fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#854d0e,color:#000
```

## 4.3 CSMA/CA with RTS/CTS — Frame Exchange Sequence (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Src as Source Node S
    participant Chan as Wireless Medium
    participant Dst as Destination Node D
    participant Hdn as Hidden Node H

    Src->>Chan: Sense channel for DIFS idle
    Src->>Chan: Wait random backoff slots
    Src->>Dst: RTS (Request To Send)
    Note over Dst: D replies after SIFS
    Dst->>Src: CTS (Clear To Send)
    Note over Hdn: H overhears CTS, sets NAV
    Src->>Dst: DATA frame
    Dst->>Src: ACK after SIFS
    Note over Hdn: H remains silent due to NAV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Sequence:** The NAV (Network Allocation Vector) field broadcast in the CTS frame tells every hidden node the exact duration of the impending data transmission, forcing them into virtual silence. This is **collision avoidance**, not detection.

## 4.4 IEEE 802.11 DCF Timing Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
gantt
    title IEEE 802.11 DCF Channel Access Timeline
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s

    section Frame Exchange
    DIFS Wait (Source)            :a1, 0, 5
    Backoff Slots (random)         :a2, after a1, 7
    RTS Transmission               :a3, after a2, 2
    SIFS + CTS                     :a4, after a3, 2
    SIFS + Data Frame              :a5, after a4, 10
    SIFS + ACK                     :a6, after a5, 2

    section Contention
    Other Nodes Defer (NAV set)   :crit, b1, after a4, 14
```

## 4.5 Binary Exponential Backoff State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle: Frame Ready
    Idle --> Sensing: Wait DIFS
    Sensing --> Transmit: Channel Idle
    Sensing --> Backoff0: Channel Busy
    Backoff0 --> Transmit: CW = 31 slots, counter hits 0
    Transmit --> Success: ACK received
    Transmit --> Fail: Timeout, no ACK
    Success --> [*]
    Fail --> Backoff1: CW = 63 slots
    Backoff1 --> Transmit: Counter hits 0
    Transmit --> Fail2: Another failure
    Fail2 --> Backoff2: CW = 127 slots
    Backoff2 --> Transmit: Counter hits 0
    Backoff2 --> Drop: CW = 1023 (max), retry limit exceeded
    Drop --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The doubling sequence $31 \to 63 \to 127 \to 255 \to 511 \to 1023$ is a **highly tested** sequence in KTU exams. Memorize the formula $CW_i = 2^{i+5} - 1$ for retry $i$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"Why is CSMA/CD not used in wireless networks? Explain with an engineering justification."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

CSMA/CD stands for **Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection**, used in wired Ethernet. It is unsuitable for wireless networks due to three primary reasons:

1. **Half-Duplex Radio Constraint:** A wireless node cannot listen to the channel and transmit on the same antenna simultaneously, so it **cannot detect a collision while transmitting** — making collision detection physically impossible.
2. **Signal Strength Disparity:** The locally transmitted signal is often $10^5$ to $10^6$ times stronger than any remote colliding signal, drowning out the collision signature.
3. **Hidden Terminal Problem:** Even if sensing were perfect, two nodes out of radio range of each other cannot detect each other's transmissions, so collisions are undetectable by either source.

Hence wireless systems use **CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance)** — a preventive mechanism based on RTS/CTS handshakes — instead.

*(Award 1 mark each point, total 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**"Distinguish between Hidden Terminal and Exposed Terminal problems in wireless MAC."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hidden Terminal | Exposed Terminal |
|---|---|---|
| **Scenario** | A→B and C→B simultaneously; A and C cannot sense each other | B→A transmitting; C→D waits unnecessarily |
| **Problem Type** | False sense of safety → **collision at receiver** | False sense of busy → **wasted bandwidth** |
| **Affected Metric** | Throughput loss due to collision | Throughput loss due to unnecessary deferral |
| **Solution in 802.11** | RTS/CTS handshake (CTS reserves channel) | Use of directional antennas / tuned RTS threshold |
| **Severity** | More critical (data loss) | Less critical (delay only) |

*(Award 1.5 marks for definitions and 1.5 marks for contrast. Total 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B question is internally split with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Two alternative question choices are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**"Derive the throughput equation of Pure ALOHA. Show that the maximum throughput is $\frac{1}{2e}$. State two disadvantages of Pure ALOHA."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Load $G$:** (1 mark)
Let $G$ = average number of transmission attempts (new + retransmitted) per frame-time $T$.

**Step 2 — Vulnerable Period:** (1 mark)
A frame of duration $T$ is vulnerable to collision from any frame that begins transmission within the window $[t_0 - T, \; t_0 + T]$, of total duration $2T$.

**Step 3 — Probability of No Collision:** (1.5 marks)

$$
P_{success} = e^{-2G}
$$

(Poisson distribution with $k = 0$ over interval $2G$.)

**Step 4 — Throughput Equation:** (1 mark)

$$
S = G \cdot e^{-2G}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximize:** (1.5 marks)

$$
\dfrac{dS}{dG} = e^{-2G}(1 - 2G) = 0 \implies G = 0.5
$$

**Step 6 — Substituting:** (1 mark)

$$
S_{max} = 0.5 \cdot e^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184
$$

**Two Disadvantages:** (Bonus 1 mark if attempted)
1. Low channel utilization (only 18.4% efficiency).
2. No mechanism to detect/prevent collisions; high retry overhead.

---

**Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]*

**"Explain the IEEE 802.11 MAC protocol with a clearly labeled timing diagram showing DIFS, SIFS, RTS, CTS, DATA, and ACK. Justify why SIFS < DIFS."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Protocol Overview:** (1 mark)
IEEE 802.11 MAC uses **DCF (Distributed Coordination Function)** based on **CSMA/CA** with binary exponential backoff and optional RTS/CTS handshake.

**Step 2 — Inter-Frame Spaces:** (2 marks)

$$
\underbrace{SIFS}_{\approx 10\,\mu s} \;<\; \underbrace{PIFS}_{\approx 30\,\mu s} \;<\; \underbrace{DIFS}_{\approx 50\,\mu s}
$$

- **SIFS (Short IFS):** Used by ACK, CTS, and frame fragments — highest priority.
- **PIFS (PCF IFS):** Used by point coordinator in PCF mode.
- **DIFS (DCF IFS):** Used by ordinary data frames — lowest priority.

**Step 3 — Sequence of Operations:** (2 marks)

1. Source senses channel; if idle for **DIFS**, enters backoff.
2. Backoff counter decrements per idle slot; when 0, transmits **RTS**.
3. After **SIFS**, destination replies with **CTS**.
4. After **SIFS**, source sends **DATA**.
5. After **SIFS**, destination sends **ACK**.

**Step 4 — Why SIFS < DIFS:** (1 mark)
SIFS must be shorter than DIFS so that ACK/CTS responses can grab the channel **before** any other node that is only waiting for DIFS — guaranteeing uninterrupted frame exchange.

**Step 5 — Hidden Terminal Mitigation:** (1 mark)
The CTS frame contains a **duration field** that all overhearing nodes store in their **NAV (Network Allocation Vector)**, forcing silence for the entire upcoming DATA transmission.

*(Total: 7 marks distributed as above.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]*

**"Explain CSMA and its variants. Compare the throughput of 1-persistent, non-persistent, and slotted ALOHA using appropriate equations."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — CSMA Principle:** (1 mark)
**Carrier Sense Multiple Access (CSMA)** requires each station to **listen to the channel** before transmitting. If the channel is sensed **busy**, the station defers; if **idle**, it transmits (subject to its persistence policy).

**Step 2 — Variants:** (2 marks)

- **1-persistent CSMA:** Transmit immediately when channel becomes idle (probability = 1). Aggressive, high collision probability.
- **Non-persistent CSMA:** If busy, wait a random time and re-sense. Less collision, higher delay.
- **p-persistent CSMA:** (slotted) Transmit with probability $p$, defer with $1-p$. Tunable trade-off.

**Step 3 — Throughput Comparison:** (3 marks)

| Protocol | Throughput $S$ | $S_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Pure ALOHA | $G \cdot e^{-2G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$ |
| Slotted ALOHA | $G \cdot e^{-G}$ | $\dfrac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$ |
| 1-persistent CSMA | $\dfrac{G \cdot e^{-G(1+a)}}{G(1+a) + e^{-aG}}$ | $\approx 0.53$ |
| Non-persistent CSMA | $\dfrac{G \cdot e^{-aG}}{G + e^{-aG}}$ | $\approx 0.81$ (small $a$) |

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** (1 mark)
As channel sensing becomes more effective ($a \to 0$), throughput improves progressively from ALOHA → Slotted ALOHA → 1-persistent CSMA → Non-persistent CSMA.

---

**Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]*

**"What is the Hidden Terminal Problem? How does IEEE 802.11 solve it? Explain with a message sequence diagram."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Hidden Terminal Problem:** (1.5 marks)
A **hidden terminal** is a node whose transmissions **cannot be sensed** by another node, but whose signals **collide at a common receiver**. Example: Nodes A and C are both in range of B but not of each other.

**Step 2 — Impact:** (1 mark)
When A transmits to B, C is unaware. C transmits to B at the same time. Frames collide at B, both lost. Throughput collapses.

**Step 3 — IEEE 802.11 Solution — RTS/CTS:** (2 marks)

$$
\underbrace{RTS}_{S \to D} \;\to\; \underbrace{CTS}_{D \to \text{all}} \;\to\; \underbrace{DATA}_{S \to D} \;\to\; \underbrace{ACK}_{D \to S}
$$

The CTS frame is broadcast (overheard by C, the hidden node) and contains a **NAV (duration) field** that reserves the medium for the entire DATA exchange duration.

**Step 4 — Message Sequence Diagram:** (2 marks)

```
Source S          Destination D          Hidden C
   | -- RTS --------> |                    |
   |                  | -- CTS ----------> | (overhears CTS, sets NAV)
   | <-- DATA ------->|                    | (silent due to NAV)
   | <--- ACK --------|                    |
```

**Step 5 — Conclusion:** (0.5 marks)
RTS/CTS reserves the channel at the **receiver's** broadcast radius, not just the sender's — solving the hidden terminal problem without requiring A and C to be in direct range.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in MAC Layer Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $S$ (Throughput) with $G$ (Load):** Students often differentiate $S$ with respect to the wrong variable. Always maximize **throughput**, not load. Examiner's tip: clearly state $\frac{dS}{dG} = 0$ before solving.
>
> 2. **Using $|x|$ or absolute value bars in formulas:** Markdown table syntax breaks when raw `|` appears. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX expressions.
>
> 3. **Omitting the vulnerable-period justification:** Writing just the formula $S = G e^{-2G}$ without explaining *why* $2G$ appears costs 1–1.5 marks. Always show the Poisson assumption and the $2T$ window.
>
> 4. **Writing CSMA/CD instead of CSMA/CA** in a wireless context — this is an **automatic 0.5 mark deduction** even if the rest is correct.
>
> 5. **Confusing SIFS and DIFS order:** Writing SIFS > DIFS is a frequent error. Remember: **SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS**, with shorter IFS = higher priority.
>
> 6. **Skipping the unit/dimension in the final answer:** Always write "frames per frame-time" or normalized throughput — examiners reward dimensional awareness.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the $\times 2$ improvement justification in Slotted ALOHA:** Don't just say "it doubles" — explain that the vulnerable period halves from $2T$ to $T$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **MAC Layer Purpose:** Arbitrate shared medium access among multiple nodes; sits between LLC (above) and PHY (below).
- **Three MAC Families:** Contention (ALOHA, CSMA), Controlled (TDMA, polling), Hybrid (802.11 DCF).
- **Pure ALOHA:** $S = G e^{-2G}$; $S_{max} = \frac{1}{2e} \approx 0.184$; vulnerable period $2T$.
- **Slotted ALOHA:** $S = G e^{-G}$; $S_{max} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.368$; vulnerable period $T$ (halved).
- **Slotted is exactly 2× better than Pure ALOHA** at peak load — memorize this ratio.
- **CSMA = Listen Before Talk.** CSMA/CD (wired, detect) vs CSMA/CA (wireless, avoid).
- **1-persistent CSMA** is greedy — high collision. **Non-persistent CSMA** is patient — low collision but high delay. **p-persistent CSMA** is the tunable middle ground (slotted channels).
- **Hidden Terminal:** Node collision at receiver, source nodes cannot sense each other. **Solved by RTS/CTS** in IEEE 802.11.
- **Exposed Terminal:** Unnecessary deferral due to overheard transmission. Less critical; can be mitigated by directional antennas.
- **IEEE 802.11 DCF** uses CSMA/CA + binary exponential backoff + RTS/CTS (optional) + NAV (virtual carrier sense).
- **IFS Priority:** $SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS$. Shortest IFS = highest priority response (ACK/CTS).
- **Binary Exponential Backoff Sequence:** $CW_0 = 31$, $CW_1 = 63$, $CW_2 = 127$, $CW_3 = 255$, $CW_4 = 511$, $CW_5 = 1023$ (max).
- **CSMA throughput increases** as the propagation parameter $a = \tau/T$ decreases (smaller cells, faster propagation).
- **Real-world deployments:** ALOHA in satellite & RFID; CSMA/CA in Wi-Fi; scheduled MAC in LTE/5G NR (OFDMA).
- **Engineering Mantra:** *"The MAC layer's success is measured by the absence of collisions."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HIPERLAN-1
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** HIPERLAN-1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIPERLAN-1 (High Performance Radio Local Area Network, Type 1)** is the first European standard for **Wireless Local Area Networks (WLANs)**, standardized by the **ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute)** under project **RES-10** and published as **ETSI EN 300 652** in **1996**. It operates in the **5.15 GHz – 5.30 GHz** radio band, supports raw data rates from **1 Mbps up to 23.5 Mbps**, and is designed to provide both **asynchronous (best-effort)** and **time-bounded (QoS-guaranteed)** services in **ad-hoc** and **infrastructure-based** network topologies.

The standard defines a **3-layer protocol architecture** consisting of the **Physical Layer (PHY)**, **Medium Access Control Layer (MAC)**, and a **Convergence Layer (CL)** that adapts HIPERLAN services to upper-layer network infrastructures such as Ethernet, IP, ATM, and FireWire (IEEE 1394).

### Key Standardized Parameters (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> | Parameter | Specification |
> |---|---|
> | **Standard Body** | **ETSI (RES-10)** |
> | **Frequency Band** | **5.15 GHz – 5.30 GHz** |
> | **Channel Spacing** | **23.5294 MHz** (5 channels total) |
> | **Data Rates** | **1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 23.5 Mbps** |
> | **Modulation** | **GMSK (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying)** with BT = 0.5 |
> | **Multiple Access Scheme** | **EY-NPMA** (Elimination Yield – Non-Preemptive Priority Multiple Access) |
> | **MAC Addressing** | **48-bit** (IEEE 802-compatible) |
> | **Coverage Range** | **~50 m** indoors / **~800 m** open outdoor |
> | **Handover Support** | **Yes** (mobility up to ~36 km/h) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a **classroom where many students want to ask the teacher a question**, but only one student can speak at a time. The teacher sets up three strict rules:
> 1. **Priority Check** — PhD students raise a yellow flag, undergraduates raise a white flag. Yellow always wins.
> 2. **Elimination Round** — Once a question is posed, students secretly flip a coin (range 0–9). Those who flip low numbers (0–4) keep their hand raised; the rest sit down (eliminated).
> 3. **Yield Round** — Among survivors, the one who picks the smallest number "yields" and speaks first.
>
> This is essentially how **EY-NPMA** works in HIPERLAN-1 — a **3-phase priority-based contention protocol** that guarantees both fairness and service differentiation. The "frequency" is the shared channel, and the "students" are wireless nodes competing for it.

### Why This Analogy Works
- The **Channel** = The teacher's attention (only one user at a time).
- **Nodes** = Students in the classroom.
- **EY-NPMA** = Priority + Elimination + Yield = Smart hand-raising protocol.
- **GMSK** = The students speak in a clear, recognizable tone (constant envelope, robust modulation) so the teacher (receiver) hears them despite classroom noise.

## 1.3 Scope & Position in the WLAN Family

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIPERLAN-1** is the **forerunner** of the modern European WLAN ecosystem. It pre-dates IEEE 802.11 in commercial adoption but introduced **QoS-aware MAC** concepts years before IEEE 802.11e. It later spawned **HIPERLAN-2 (ETSI BRAN)** which is physically similar to IEEE 802.11a (OFDM-based, 5 GHz).

**Historical Timeline for Context:**
- **1991–1992:** ETSI RES-10 group formed.
- **1996:** HIPERLAN/1 standard approved.
- **2000:** HIPERLAN/2 published (OFDM, 54 Mbps).
- **2003 onwards:** IEEE 802.11g/n dominated; HIPERLAN commercially faded but remained academically influential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Protocol Stack Architecture

HIPERLAN-1 is defined by a clean, three-layer reference model:

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 3 — Convergence Layer (CL)**
> - Functions: Service adaptation, segmentation/reassembly, fragmentation, QoS mapping.
> - Profiles: **Ethernet (CL-E), IP (CL-I), ATM (CL-A), FireWire (CL-F), PPP (CL-P), UMTS (CL-U)**.
>
> **Layer 2 — Medium Access Control (MAC)**
> - Functions: Channel access (EY-NPMA), addressing (48-bit MAC ID), encryption (DES-based), power saving, mobility management.
> - Topology support: Ad-hoc (decentralized) and infrastructure (centralized via Forwarder/Controller).
>
> **Layer 1 — Physical Layer (PHY)**
> - Modulation: **GMSK** (Gaussian-filtered MSK) with normalized bandwidth **BT = 0.5**.
> - Bit rate derivation: 1 bit/symbol → 1 Mbps; 2 bits/symbol → 2 Mbps; etc.
> - Transmitter power: **+10 dBm (10 mW)** typical; up to **+20 dBm** (100 mW).

## 2.2 The EY-NPMA MAC Protocol (Heart of HIPERLAN-1)

The MAC is the **single most important topic** for KTU exams. EY-NPMA is a **3-phase contention-based** access method that combines **TDMA-like priority enforcement** with **CSMA-style distributed arbitration**.

### Phase 1 — Priority Phase
Each node selects one of **5 user priorities** (0–4) before contending. Nodes broadcast a short **priority assertion** signal. The **highest priority survivor** wins the right to proceed. This guarantees **deterministic latency for high-priority traffic**.

### Phase 2 — Elimination Phase
Survivors from the priority phase pick a random **elimination number** from the set **{0, 1, …, 9}** and wait for that many **elimination slots** (each slot ≈ **256 µs**). Nodes whose slot arrives **without hearing any other node** survive; others drop out.

### Phase 3 — Yield Phase
The last surviving node(s) — those who heard no other transmission for the longest duration — **yield the channel first** (i.e., begin transmitting). This resolves ties among priority-and-elimination winners.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The three phases must always be cited in this exact order — **Priority → Elimination → Yield**. Reversing them is a common 1-mark deduction.

## 2.3 MAC Frame Format (Logical)

| Field | Size (Bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **LLC Header** | 3 | Logical Link Control (IEEE 802.2) |
| **MAC Header** | 28 | Address, priority, lifetime, length |
| **Data Payload** | 0 – 2312 | User data |
| **FCS** | 4 | Frame Check Sequence (CRC-32) |

**Largest MAC Service Data Unit (MSDU)** = **2312 bytes** (matches Ethernet MTU).

## 2.4 Network Topology Variants

> [!NOTE]
> **Ad-hoc (Decentralized) Mode:**
> - All nodes are peers.
> - No central controller.
> - Forwarding is multi-hop via HIPERLAN-1 relay nodes.
>
> **Infrastructure (Centralized) Mode:**
> - A **Forwarder** node acts as the access point.
> - A **Controller** provides configuration & coordination.
> - Optional **Gatekeeper** for connection to fixed networks.
> - Supports **mobility and handover** at walking pace (~36 km/h) up to vehicle speed.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All key formulas and derived quantities for HIPERLAN-1 problems:

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Channel spacing** $B_c$ | $B_c = \dfrac{150 \text{ MHz}}{5 \text{ (odd multiples)}}$ — *but defined as* $B_c = 23.5294$ **MHz** | **MHz** |
| **Carrier frequencies** $f_n$ | $f_n = 5.1767 + 0.2064 \cdot n$, for $n = 0,1,2,3,4$ | **GHz** |
| **Raw symbol rate** | $R_s = 1.176470$ **Msymbols/s** | **Msps** |
| **Bit rate at rate index $i$** | $R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2(M_i)$ | **Mbps** |
| **MSK bandwidth parameter** | $BT = 0.5$ (Gaussian filter 3-dB bandwidth × bit period) | dimensionless |
| **Elimination slot duration** | $T_e = 256 \;\mu s$ | **µs** |
| **Maximum MSDU length** | $L_{\max} = 2312$ **bytes** | **bytes** |
| **Transmit power (nominal)** | $P_t = +10$ **dBm** (10 mW) | **dBm** |
| **Indoor range** | $r_{\text{in}} \approx 50$ **m** | **meters** |
| **Outdoor range** | $r_{\text{out}} \approx 800$ **m** | **meters** |
| **MAC address length** | $\vert MAC\vert = 48$ **bits** | **bits** |
| **Number of priority levels** | $P = 5$ (priorities 0–4) | levels |
| **Elimination range** | $\{0, 1, \dots, 9\}$ i.e. **10 values** | values |
| **Path-loss exponent (indoor)** | $n \approx 3$ | dimensionless |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although HIPERLAN-1 is obsolete commercially, its concepts live on in:
- **IEEE 802.11e** (EDCA — Enhanced Distributed Channel Access) uses priority-based contention.
- **IEEE 802.11s** (mesh networking) borrows HIPERLAN's ad-hoc/relay model.
- **5G NR unlicensed (NR-U)** listen-before-talk (LBT) schemes use EY-NPMA-like priority arbitration.
- **Industrial IoT** MAC protocols still draw on the deterministic-priority ideas pioneered by EY-NPMA.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: HIPERLAN-1 Carrier Frequency Set

The 5 GHz ISM-adjacent band allocated to HIPERLAN spans **150 MHz**, divided into **5 channels** centered on the **5 odd multiples of 23.5294 MHz above 5.1767 GHz**.

**Given:**
- Lowest center frequency: $f_0 = 5.1767$ **GHz**
- Frequency step: $\Delta f = 0.2064$ **GHz** (which equals $\dfrac{150 \text{ MHz}}{5} - 23.5294$ MHz spacing — *historical ETSI definition*)
- Number of channels: $N = 5$

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_n &= f_0 + n \cdot \Delta f, \quad \text{for } n \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\} \\[6pt]
f_0 &= 5.1767 + (0)(0.2064) = 5.1767 \text{ GHz} \\[4pt]
f_1 &= 5.1767 + (1)(0.2064) = 5.3831 \text{ GHz} \\[4pt]
f_2 &= 5.1767 + (2)(0.2064) = 5.5895 \text{ GHz} \\[4pt]
f_3 &= 5.1767 + (3)(0.2064) = 5.7959 \text{ GHz} \\[4pt]
f_4 &= 5.1767 + (4)(0.2064) = 6.0023 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The exact centre frequencies reported by ETSI in **EN 300 652** are:
> **5.17678 GHz, 5.19968 GHz, 5.22258 GHz, 5.24548 GHz, 5.26838 GHz**, with a uniform step of **22.9 MHz** above 5.17678 GHz.
> The simplified formula $f_n = 5.1767 + 0.2064n$ is a teaching approximation; use the **23.5294 MHz spacing** for exam precision.

## 3.2 Derivation: Achievable Data Rates from GMSK Symbols

HIPERLAN-1 uses **GMSK** with a Gaussian pre-filter having $BT = 0.5$. Although GMSK is technically a **constant-envelope binary modulation**, ETSI achieves higher rates by **reducing the filter bandwidth** and using **quasi-constant envelope multi-level signaling**, treating the system as having $M_i$ symbols per symbol period.

**Given symbol rate:**
$$
R_s = 1.17647 \text{ Msymbols/s} = \dfrac{1}{0.85 \;\mu s}
$$

**Generic rate formula:**
$$
R_b = R_s \cdot \log_2(M_i)
$$

**Worked rate table:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M = 2 &: \; R_b = 1.17647 \cdot \log_2(2) = 1.17647 \cdot 1 \approx 1.18 \text{ Mbps} \\[4pt]
M = 3 &: \; R_b = 1.17647 \cdot \log_2(3) = 1.17647 \cdot 1.585 \approx 1.864 \text{ Mbps} \\[4pt]
M = 4 &: \; R_b = 1.17647 \cdot \log_2(4) = 1.17647 \cdot 2 = 2.353 \text{ Mbps} \\[4pt]
M = 8 &: \; R_b = 1.17647 \cdot \log_2(8) = 1.17647 \cdot 3 = 3.529 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

The full ETSI-specified data rate set — **1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 23.5 Mbps** — is obtained by varying both the **modulation level** and the **channel coding rate** (rate-1/2 or rate-1/3 convolutional coding).

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Effective Throughput

The maximum MSDU size is **2312 bytes = 18,496 bits**. At the highest physical rate of **23.529 Mbps**, the minimum transmission time is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{frame}} &= \dfrac{L_{\max}}{R_b} = \dfrac{18{,}496 \text{ bits}}{23.529 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} \\[6pt]
T_{\text{frame}} &= 7.86 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 786 \;\mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**Adding MAC/PHY overhead** (≈ 100 µs preamble + headers), the **maximum effective throughput** becomes:

$$
\eta_{\max} = \dfrac{L_{\max}}{T_{\text{frame}} + T_{\text{overhead}}} \approx \dfrac{18{,}496 \text{ bits}}{886 \;\mu s} \approx 20.88 \text{ Mbps}
$$

This represents an **~89% MAC efficiency** at peak load — excellent for the era.

## 3.4 Derivation: EY-NPMA Worst-Case Channel Access Delay

Worst case for the lowest priority user (priority 0) being starved by 4 higher-priority users is bounded by the sum of the three phase time-outs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{access,max}} &= T_{\text{priority}} + T_{\text{elimination,max}} + T_{\text{yield}} \\[6pt]
&= 4 \cdot T_{\text{slot}} + (10 - 1) \cdot T_e + T_{\text{yield}} \\[6pt]
&= 4 \cdot 64 \;\mu s + 9 \cdot 256 \;\mu s + 64 \;\mu s \\[6pt]
&= 256 \;\mu s + 2304 \;\mu s + 64 \;\mu s \\[6pt]
&= 2624 \;\mu s \approx 2.62 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

This **deterministic upper bound** is why HIPERLAN-1 was attractive for **time-bounded multimedia** applications.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: When EY-NPMA Wins

| Scenario | CSMA/CA (802.11) | EY-NPMA (HIPERLAN-1) | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mixed voice + data | Voice can starve | Priority arbitration | **EY-NPMA** |
| Heavy burst load | Backoff resets | Yield phase resolves quickly | **EY-NPMA** |
| High mobility, short frames | DCF overhead large | Deterministic 2.6 ms bound | **EY-NPMA** |
| Pure best-effort, cheap HW | Simpler, single phase | 3 phases = complex radio | **CSMA/CA** |
| Global interoperability | Wi-Fi ecosystem | ETSI-only | **CSMA/CA** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HIPERLAN-1 Protocol Stack vs OSI Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Upper["Upper Layer Applications / Network"]
        APP["Application Layer (VoIP, Video, File Transfer)"]
    end

    subgraph CL["Convergence Layer - Layer 3"]
        CLE["CL-Ethernet"]
        CLI["CL-IP"]
        CLA["CL-ATM"]
        CLF["CL-FireWire"]
    end

    subgraph MACL["Medium Access Control Layer - Layer 2"]
        EYN["EY-NPMA Channel Access"]
        FRM["Framing and MAC Addressing - 48-bit"]
        SEC["Security - DES Encryption"]
        MOB["Mobility and Handover Management"]
    end

    subgraph PHYL["Physical Layer - Layer 1"]
        GMSK["GMSK Modulation BT = 0.5"]
        CHAN["RF Channel 5.15 to 5.30 GHz"]
        ANT["Antenna Interface"]
    end

    APP --> CLE
    APP --> CLI
    APP --> CLA
    APP --> CLF
    CLE --> EYN
    CLI --> EYN
    CLA --> EYN
    CLF --> EYN
    EYN --> FRM
    FRM --> SEC
    SEC --> MOB
    MOB --> GMSK
    GMSK --> CHAN
    CHAN --> ANT
```

## 4.2 EY-NPMA Three-Phase Contention Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Node wants to transmit"]) --> prio["Phase 1: Priority Assertion"]
    prio --> priowin{"Highest priority wins?"}
    priowin -- Yes --> elim["Phase 2: Elimination Phase"]
    priowin -- No --> wait1["Defer and retry"]
    wait1 --> prio
    elim --> elimdecide{"Picked lowest elimination number and heard no other node?"}
    elimdecide -- No, eliminated --> wait1
    elimdecide -- Yes, survived --> yield["Phase 3: Yield Phase"]
    yield --> yielddecide{"Last node to hear silence?"}
    yielddecide -- No --> wait1
    yielddecide -- Yes --> tx["Transmit Frame"]
    tx --> ack["Wait for ACK"]
    ack --> done(["Channel Idle, return to listening"])
```

## 4.3 HIPERLAN-1 Network Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AdHoc["Ad-Hoc Topology"]
        A1["Node A"] <--> A2["Node B"]
        A2 <--> A3["Node C"]
        A3 <--> A1
        A2 <--> A4["Node D"]
    end

    subgraph Infra["Infrastructure Topology"]
        F1["Forwarder Node - acts as AP"]
        F2["Controller Node - configuration"]
        G1["Gatekeeper - to fixed network"]
        N1["Mobile Node 1"]
        N2["Mobile Node 2"]
        N3["Mobile Node 3"]
        F1 --- N1
        F1 --- N2
        F1 --- N3
        F1 --- F2
        F1 --- G1
        G1 --> extnet["External Network Ethernet IP ATM"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Architecture — HIPERLAN-1 Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UserData["User Data Path"]
        UD["Application Payload"]
    end

    subgraph CLBlock["Convergence Layer"]
        SGMT["Segmentation and Reassembly"]
        FRAG["Fragmentation Logic"]
        QOSMAP["QoS to Priority Mapping - 5 levels"]
    end

    subgraph MACBlock["MAC Sublayer"]
        MACADDR["48-bit MAC Address Filter"]
        EYNCTRL["EY-NPMA State Machine - Priority Elimination Yield"]
        FRMENG["Frame Engine - Header and FCS"]
        DESENC["DES Encryption Engine"]
        POWSAVE["Power Save Coordinator"]
    end

    subgraph PHYBlock["Physical Layer"]
        GMSKMOD["GMSK Modulator BT 0.5"]
        GAUSFLT["Gaussian Low Pass Filter"]
        RFUP["5.15 to 5.30 GHz Upconverter"]
        ANTBLK["Omnidirectional Antenna"]
    end

    UD --> SGMT --> FRAG --> QOSMAP --> MACADDR --> EYNCTRL --> FRMENG --> DESENC --> GMSKMOD --> GAUSFLT --> RFUP --> ANTBLK
    POWSAVE -.-> EYNCTRL
```

## 4.5 Convergence Layer Profile Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLProfiles["Convergence Layer Profiles"]
        PE["CL-E - Ethernet 10 Mbps Bridge"]
        PI["CL-I - Internet Protocol v4 v6"]
        PA["CL-A - ATM with AAL5"]
        PF["CL-F - FireWire IEEE 1394"]
        PP["CL-P - Point to Point Protocol"]
        PU["CL-U - UMTS Interworking"]
    end
    PE --> MAC["HIPERLAN MAC"]
    PI --> MAC
    PA --> MAC
    PF --> MAC
    PP --> MAC
    PU --> MAC
    MAC --> PHY["HIPERLAN PHY 5 GHz GMSK"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> **Define HIPERLAN-1. List any three of its specifications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> **HIPERLAN-1** (High Performance Radio Local Area Network – Type 1) is an ETSI standard (EN 300 652, 1996) for wireless LANs operating in the **5.15 GHz – 5.30 GHz** band.
>
> **Three specifications:**
> 1. **Modulation:** GMSK with $BT = 0.5$, supporting data rates from **1 Mbps to 23.5 Mbps**.
> 2. **MAC:** Uses **EY-NPMA** (Elimination-Yield Non-Preemptive Multiple Access) for priority-based, deterministic channel access.
> 3. **Coverage:** Up to **50 m indoors** and **800 m outdoors**; supports mobility up to ~36 km/h via handover between Forwarder nodes.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 3 specifications = 3 marks)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
> **What is the role of the Convergence Layer in HIPERLAN-1?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> The **Convergence Layer (CL)** in HIPERLAN-1 is the **top layer** of the protocol stack. Its roles are:
> 1. **Service Adaptation:** Maps HIPERLAN services onto various upper-layer networks such as **Ethernet, IP, ATM, FireWire, PPP, and UMTS** through standardized profiles (CL-E, CL-I, CL-A, etc.).
> 2. **Segmentation and Reassembly (SAR):** Breaks large MSDUs into smaller MAC frames and reassembles them.
> 3. **QoS Mapping:** Translates application QoS requirements (delay, jitter, loss) into one of the **5 MAC user priorities**.

*(1 mark per role = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Blueprint]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **three phases of the EY-NPMA protocol** in HIPERLAN-1 with a suitable diagram. Discuss how it supports **time-bounded services**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **HIPERLAN-1 protocol architecture** with a neat block diagram. Compare **ad-hoc** and **infrastructure** topologies. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution A(a) — 7 Marks

**Phase 1 — Priority Phase (2 marks):**
Each contending node selects one of **5 user priorities** (0 = lowest, 4 = highest) based on its traffic class. Nodes transmit a short **priority signal** before data. Only nodes with the highest priority proceed; others defer. This guarantees that **voice/video traffic (priority 3–4)** always wins arbitration over **best-effort data (priority 0–1)**.

**Phase 2 — Elimination Phase (2 marks):**
Priority survivors pick a **uniformly random elimination number** $k \in \{0, 1, \dots, 9\}$. They wait for $k \cdot 256 \;\mu s$. The node whose slot arrives with no other transmission survives. This quickly reduces contenders (e.g., 10 nodes → ~1 survivor within 2.3 ms).

**Phase 3 — Yield Phase (1 mark):**
Survivors monitor the channel. The node that hears **silence for the longest time** is the first to **yield** — i.e., it begins transmitting. This resolves ties deterministically.

**Time-bounded Service Support (2 marks):**
Because the protocol has a **deterministic upper bound** (≈ **2.6 ms** worst-case channel access time), HIPERLAN-1 can guarantee **maximum latency** for real-time traffic — making it suitable for **voice (≤ 100 ms delay budget)** and **video (≤ 40 ms jitter budget)** applications.

```
        ┌─────────────┐
        │ PRIORITY    │  ← Highest priority wins
        └──────┬──────┘
               ↓
        ┌─────────────┐
        │ ELIMINATION │  ← Random slot 0–9, lowest survives
        └──────┬──────┘
               ↓
        ┌─────────────┐
        │ YIELD       │  ← Last silence wins
        └──────┬──────┘
               ↓
        ┌─────────────┐
        │ TRANSMIT    │
        └─────────────┘
```

### Model Solution A(b) — 7 Marks

**Protocol Architecture (4 marks):**
HIPERLAN-1 has three layers:

- **Physical Layer (PHY):** GMSK modulation at 5.15–5.30 GHz, 1–23.5 Mbps.
- **MAC Layer:** EY-NPMA, 48-bit addressing, DES encryption, mobility/handover.
- **Convergence Layer:** Service adaptation (CL-E, CL-I, CL-A, CL-F, CL-P, CL-U).

**Ad-hoc vs Infrastructure (3 marks):**

| Feature | Ad-hoc | Infrastructure |
|---|---|---|
| **Controller?** | None | Forwarder + Controller + Gatekeeper |
| **Centralized scheduling?** | No (fully distributed EY-NPMA) | Optional via Controller |
| **Handover** | Peer-to-peer via relay | Between Forwarders (AP-like) |
| **External network** | Not directly connected | Via Gatekeeper |
| **Use case** | Temporary meetings, emergency | Office/campus WLAN |

---

### Question B (Option 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Blueprint]**  
> **(a)** Explain the **physical layer specifications** of HIPERLAN-1 — modulation, frequency band, data rates, and transmitter power. Show the derivation of the **carrier frequency set** for the 5 channels. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a functional block diagram, describe how a HIPERLAN-1 node processes an **outgoing MSDU** from the application layer to the antenna. Highlight the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution B(a) — 7 Marks

**PHY Specifications (4 marks):**
- **Frequency band:** 5.15 GHz – 5.30 GHz (150 MHz total, 5 channels).
- **Modulation:** **GMSK (Gaussian MSK)** with **$BT = 0.5$**.
- **Data rates:** 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, **23.5 Mbps** (using different symbol alphabets and punctured convolutional codes).
- **TX power:** **+10 dBm (10 mW)** typical, up to **+20 dBm** (100 mW).
- **Sensitivity:** Around **–85 dBm** at 1 Mbps; –68 dBm at 23.5 Mbps.

**Carrier Frequency Derivation (3 marks):**
$$
f_n = 5.17678 + (n \cdot 0.0229) \text{ GHz}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_0 &= 5.17678 \text{ GHz}, \; f_1 = 5.19968 \text{ GHz}, \; f_2 = 5.22258 \text{ GHz} \\[4pt]
f_3 &= 5.24548 \text{ GHz}, \; f_4 = 5.26838 \text{ GHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

Channel spacing is **22.9 MHz**, equal to the **symbol rate** $\frac{1}{0.85 \;\mu s} \approx 1.176$ Msps. This places the channels on the **odd multiples** of the symbol rate above the band edge — a clever spectral design.

### Model Solution B(b) — 7 Marks

**Outgoing MSDU Processing Flow (1 mark per block, 7 blocks):**

1. **Application Layer** → generates MSDU (≤ **2312 bytes**).
2. **Convergence Layer – Segmentation** → splits MSDU into MAC-sized units.
3. **Convergence Layer – QoS Mapping** → assigns one of **5 user priorities**.
4. **MAC – Framing** → prepends **28-byte MAC header**, appends **4-byte FCS**.
5. **MAC – EY-NPMA Controller** → arbitrates channel access via 3-phase contention.
6. **MAC – DES Encryption** → encrypts payload for confidentiality.
7. **PHY – GMSK Modulator** → converts bits to GMSK symbols, upconverts to **5.2 GHz** RF, sends to antenna.

The **end-to-end latency** for a small packet under low contention is roughly **T_access ≈ 1 ms + T_frame ≈ 1 ms = 2 ms**, demonstrating HIPERLAN-1's suitability for interactive applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Reversing EY-NPMA phases** — Always write **Priority → Elimination → Yield** (PYE). Any other order costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing HIPERLAN-1 with HIPERLAN-2** — HIPERLAN-2 uses **OFDM**, not GMSK, and operates at **54 Mbps**. Mixing them up is an instant 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong frequency band** — It is **5 GHz (not 2.4 GHz)**. Saying 2.4 GHz will mark you as having studied 802.11, not HIPERLAN.
> 4. **Forgetting the convergence layer** — A common omission. Always mention **CL profiles (E, I, A, F)** if asked about architecture.
> 5. **Calling MAC addressing 64-bit** — It is **48-bit** (same as IEEE 802). Confusing this loses a mark.
> 6. **Not specifying "GMSK with BT = 0.5"** — Just saying "GMSK" is half-credit; BT = 0.5 is the **Gaussian filter bandwidth-time product** and is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — HIPERLAN-1**
>
> ☐ **Standard:** ETSI EN 300 652, finalized **1996**, project **RES-10**.
> ☐ **Frequency:** **5.15–5.30 GHz**, 5 channels, **22.9 MHz spacing**, carriers on odd multiples of symbol rate.
> ☐ **Modulation:** **GMSK** with **BT = 0.5**, symbol rate **1.176 Msps**.
> ☐ **Data rates:** **1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9, 12, 18, 23.5 Mbps**.
> ☐ **MAC:** **EY-NPMA** in three phases — **Priority → Elimination → Yield**.
> ☐ **5 user priorities** (0–4) for QoS.
> ☐ **Elimination slot = 256 µs**, 10 values {0,…,9}.
> ☐ **Worst-case channel access ≤ 2.62 ms** (time-bounded support).
> ☐ **MAC address: 48-bit.**
> ☐ **Max MSDU = 2312 bytes.**
> ☐ **TX power: +10 dBm typical.**
> ☐ **Range: 50 m indoor, 800 m outdoor.**
> ☐ **Topology:** Ad-hoc (peer) and Infrastructure (Forwarder + Controller + Gatekeeper).
> ☐ **Convergence Layer profiles:** CL-E, CL-I, CL-A, CL-F, CL-P, CL-U.
> ☐ **Mobility:** Handover supported up to ~36 km/h.
> ☐ **Distinguished from HIPERLAN-2:** HIPERLAN-2 = OFDM, 5 GHz, 54 Mbps, connection-oriented.
> ☐ **Historical relevance:** Predecessor of 802.11e EDCA priority ideas; inspiration for 5G NR-U LBT.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bluetooth # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bluetooth** is a short-range, low-power, low-cost **Wireless Personal Area Network (WPAN)** technology that operates in the unlicensed **2.4 GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) band**, using **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** with **1600 hops per second** across **79 RF channels** (in Classic Bluetooth) to establish ad-hoc connections between devices. Bluetooth Special Interest Group (SIG) defines it as an open specification for short-range wireless voice and data communication between mobile and stationary electronic devices. ### Key Standard Specifications - **Operating Frequency Band:** 2.400 GHz – 2.4835 GHz (ISM Band) - **Modulation:** Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying (GFSK) for Basic Rate - **Channel Spacing:** 1 MHz between adjacent hops - **Range:** Typically **10 meters** (Class 2), up to **100 meters** (Class 1) - **Original Data Rate:** **1 Mbps** (Basic Rate BR), **2/3 Mbps** (Enhanced Data Rate EDR) > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — "The Digital Handshake"** > Imagine you walk into a room full of strangers speaking different languages. To find a friend, you and your friend agree on a secret handshake rhythm. Every few seconds, you change your handshake pattern so that no eavesdropper can follow. **Bluetooth does exactly this**: two devices agree on a hopping pattern (the rhythm) and rapidly switch between 79 different frequencies 1600 times per second. Even if someone tries to listen on one frequency, they only catch a 1/79th fraction of the conversation — making the link secure and noise-resistant. ## 1.2 Historical Context & Evolution | Version | Year | Key Feature | |---|---|---| | Bluetooth 1.0 / 1.1 | 1999 / 2001 | First commercial release, 1 Mbps | | Bluetooth 1.2 | 2003 | Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH) | | Bluetooth 2.0 + EDR | 2004 | Enhanced Data Rate up to 3 Mbps | | Bluetooth 3.0 + HS | 2009 | High Speed via 802.11 radio | | Bluetooth 4.0 (BLE) | 2010 | Bluetooth Low Energy — IoT focus | | Bluetooth 5.0 | 2016 | 2x speed, 4x range, 8x broadcast capacity | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architectural Entities — Piconet & Scatternet ### 2.1.1 Piconet The fundamental unit of a Bluetooth network. - **Master Device:** The device that initiates the connection. Controls the clock and hopping pattern. **Maximum 1 master per piconet.** - **Slave Devices:** Up to **7 active slaves** per master simultaneously. Each slave has a 3-bit Active Member Address (AM_ADDR). - **Parked Slaves:** Additional slaves can be parked (up to 255), placed in a low-power state. Only 7 can be *active* at a time. ### 2.1.2 Scatternet When two or more piconets are interconnected, they form a scatternet. - A device can be a **master in one piconet** and a **slave in another**, acting as a bridge. - Maximum configuration theoretically supports thousands of devices through interconnected piconets. ## 2.2 Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS) Bluetooth uses FHSS to combat interference and improve security. ### Hopping Rate Calculation $$f_{hop} = 1600 \text{ hops/second}$$ $$T_{slot} = \frac{1}{f_{hop}} = \frac{1}{1600} = 625 \text{ μs}$$ ### Hop Frequency Formula Each hop occupies a 1 MHz wide channel. The carrier frequency of the k-th channel is: $$f_k = f_1 + k \text{ MHz}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, 78$$ Where $f_1 = 2402$ MHz (the lowest hopping frequency). ### Adaptive Frequency Hopping (AFH) Bluetooth 1.2 introduced AFH which classifies channels into: - **Good channels:** Used normally. - **Bad channels:** Excluded from hopping sequence (e.g., to avoid Wi-Fi interference in 2.4 GHz). ## 2.3 Connection Establishment States Bluetooth devices progress through these states: 1. **Standby:** Default, low-power idle state. 2. **Inquiry:** Device scans for other devices in range by broadcasting an **ID packet** on inquiry hopping sequence (32 dedicated frequencies). 3. **Page:** Master invites a specific slave to join the piconet using its **BD_ADDR**. 4. **Connected:** Data transfer can begin using ACL (Asynchronous Connection-Less) or SCO (Synchronous Connection-Oriented) links. ## 2.4 Link Types | Link Type | Full Form | Purpose | Symmetry | |---|---|---|---| | **ACL** | Asynchronous Connection-Less | Data traffic (best effort) | Asymmetric | | **SCO** | Synchronous Connection-Oriented | Voice traffic (reserved slots) | Symmetric | ## 2.5 Protocol Stack The Bluetooth protocol stack follows the OSI model: - **Radio Layer:** Physical transmission (2.4 GHz, FHSS) - **Baseband Layer:** Hopping, link control, error correction - **LMP (Link Manager Protocol):** Authentication, encryption, power control - **HCI (Host Controller Interface):** Command interface to baseband - **L2CAP (Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol):** Multiplexing, segmentation - **RFCOMM:** Serial port emulation - **OBEX:** Object exchange - **Application Layer:** Profiles (e.g., HSP, A2DP, HID) ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Bluetooth enables: - **Wireless audio:** Headsets, speakers (A2DP profile) - **IoT sensors:** BLE-based heart rate monitors, beacons - **Personal area networking:** Phone-to-laptop file transfer - **Medical devices:** Wireless ECG, glucose monitors - **Industrial:** Asset tracking, machine-to-machine communication ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these formulas — they are the most tested numerical concepts in KTU exams.** | Parameter | Formula | Value/Unit | |---|---|---| | Hopping Rate | $f_{hop}$ | **1600 hops/s** | | Slot Duration | $T_{slot} = 1 / f_{hop}$ | **625 μs** | | Carrier Frequency | $f_k = 2402 + k$ (MHz) | $k = 0..78$ | | Total Hop Channels | Classic BT: 79, BLE: 40 | channels | | Baseband Data Rate (BR) | 1 symbol/slot | **1 Mbps** | | Max Active Slaves | $2^3 - 1$ | **7 slaves** | | Max Parked Slaves | $2^8 - 1$ | **255 slaves** | | Voice Channels (SCO) | Reserved slots | Up to **3** | | Range (Class 1/2/3) | Power dependent | 100m / 10m / 1m | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Slot Duration from Hopping Rate Given Bluetooth hops 1600 times per second, we derive the duration of each hop slot. ### Given Hopping rate $f_{hop} = 1600$ hops/second. ### Step 1 Each hop occupies one time slot. The total time available is 1 second. $$T_{total} = 1 \text{ second}$$ ### Step 2 If 1600 hops occur in 1 second, the duration of a single slot is the reciprocal: $$T_{slot} = \frac{T_{total}}{N_{hops}} = \frac{1 \text{ s}}{1600}$$ ### Step 3 Convert to microseconds (since μs is the practical Bluetooth unit): $$T_{slot} = \frac{1}{1600} \text{ s} = 0.000625 \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{T_{slot} = 625 \text{ μs}}$$ ### Real-World Interpretation A Bluetooth packet can occupy 1, 3, or 5 slots.
