$$
C_{MIMO} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{min}} B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{P_i \lambda_i}{\sigma^2}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — High-SNR Approximation:**
At high SNR with equal power allocation, the capacity grows linearly with $\min(N_t, N_r)$:

$$
C_{MIMO} \approx N_{min} \cdot B \log_2\!\left(\frac{\text{SNR}}{N_t}\right)
$$

This **linear growth** (vs. logarithmic growth in SISO) is the fundamental reason SDMA-via-MIMO is the dominant capacity-boosting technique in 4G/5G/6G.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SDMA Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["User Equipment Pool"] --> node2["Channel Estimation Module"]
    node2 --> node3["AoA DoA Estimator MUSIC ESPRIT"]
    node3 --> node4["Spatial Signature Database"]
    node4 --> node5["Beamforming Weight Calculator MVDR Capon"]
    node5 --> node6["Smart Antenna Array N elements"]
    node6 --> node7["Beam 1 User A Sector North"]
    node6 --> node8["Beam 2 User B Sector East"]
    node6 --> node9["Beam 3 User C Sector South"]
    node7 --> node10["Same Frequency Same Time Slot Different Space"]
    node8 --> node10
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11["Interference Mitigation Null Steering"]
    node11 --> node12["Increased System Capacity N equals 1 Reuse"]
```

### 4.2 SDMA Operating Principle — Cell Sectorization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cellA["Cell A Sectorized 120 degrees"]
        antA["Base Station Antenna"] --> beamA1["Beam Alpha 0 to 120 deg"]
        antA --> beamA2["Beam Beta 120 to 240 deg"]
        antA --> beamA3["Beam Gamma 240 to 360 deg"]
    end
    beamA1 --> u1["User 1 Co Channel Reuse Enabled"]
    beamA2 --> u2["User 2 No Interference"]
    beamA3 --> u3["User 3 Spatial Separation"]
```

### 4.3 SDMA Layered Relationship with FDMA / TDMA / CDMA

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    layer1["Layer 1 Frequency Domain FDMA"] --> layer2["Layer 2 Time Domain TDMA"]
    layer2 --> layer3["Layer 3 Code Domain CDMA"]
    layer3 --> layer4["Layer 4 Space Domain SDMA"]
    layer1 --> layer5["Combined Multiple Access FDMA plus TDMA plus SDMA in LTE 5G"]
    layer2 --> layer5
    layer4 --> layer5
```

### 4.4 Adaptive Beamforming Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inp["Incoming Signals from K Users"] --> corr["Correlation Matrix Rxx Estimation"]
    corr --> inv["Matrix Inversion Rxx inverse"]
    inv --> wcalc["Compute Optimal Weights w equals Rxx inverse a theta0"]
    wcalc --> wapply["Apply Weights to Antenna Elements"]
    wapply --> out["Steered Beam Toward Desired User"]
    wapply --> nulls["Nulls Toward Interferers"]
    out --> outSig["Enhanced Desired Signal"]
    nulls --> cancelSup["Suppressed Interference"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Design Note (KTU Board-Friendly):**
> The four block diagrams above map the *functional architecture* of SDMA. They correspond directly to the typical 14-mark answers expected in KTU ESE for the question *"Explain Space Division Multiple Access with neat block diagram"* (Dec 2022 / June 2023 pattern).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1. Define Space Division Multiple Access (SDMA). How does it differ from FDMA and TDMA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
SDMA is a multiple access technique that allows simultaneous use of the same frequency band by multiple users by separating them in *space* (i.e., by their geographic location or angular position) rather than in frequency or time. It typically employs **directional antennas, sectorized cells, or smart antenna arrays** to confine each user's signal to a unique spatial region.

| Scheme | Separation Domain |
|---|---|
| FDMA | Frequency |
| TDMA | Time |
| SDMA | Space |

Unlike FDMA and TDMA, which require partitioning of the *frequency–time plane*, SDMA exploits the *spatial dimension*, enabling frequency reuse factor **N = 1** in cellular systems.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary Exam]**

**Q2. List two advantages and one disadvantage of SDMA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **Increased system capacity** through spatial reuse of frequencies.
2. **Improved signal quality** due to higher antenna gain and reduced co-channel interference.

**Disadvantage:**
1. **Complex antenna hardware and signal processing** — requires adaptive beamforming, channel estimation, and tracking algorithms, increasing cost and computational load.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 ESE]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the principle of Space Division Multiple Access (SDMA) in detail. With a neat block diagram, describe how a smart antenna system implements SDMA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss how SDMA is integrated with FDMA/TDMA in modern cellular systems (LTE / 5G NR). What is meant by *frequency reuse factor N = 1*? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Principle and Block Diagram (7 marks):**

The principle of SDMA is to **reuse the same frequency and time slot** by isolating users *spatially*. This is implemented using a **smart antenna array** at the base station.

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. **Channel Estimation:** The base station continuously estimates the channel impulse response from each user.
2. **Direction-of-Arrival (DoA) Estimation:** Algorithms like **MUSIC** or **ESPRIT** compute the angular location of each user.
3. **Beamforming Weight Calculation:** Using the MVDR (Minimum Variance Distortionless Response) criterion, optimal complex weights are computed:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \frac{\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0)}{\mathbf{a}^H(\theta_0) \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0)}
$$

4. **Beam Steering:** The weights steer the array's main lobe toward the desired user and place nulls on interferers.
5. **Simultaneous Multi-User Reception:** Multiple users are received simultaneously on the *same frequency and time slot*.

The block diagram follows the *Adaptive Beamforming Signal Flow* shown in Section 4.4 above.

**(b) SDMA + FDMA/TDMA Integration (7 marks):**

In LTE and 5G NR, the radio resources are organized as a **time-frequency grid** (FDMA × TDMA). On top of this grid, **SDMA is overlaid** as an additional spatial layer:

- The **eNodeB / gNodeB** schedules multiple UEs on the *same* PRBs (Physical Resource Blocks) in the *same* subframe.
- A **spatial layer** is created for each UE using precoding vectors in the MIMO codebook.
- The **MU-MIMO (Multi-User MIMO)** scheduler is essentially an SDMA scheduler running on top of OFDMA.

**Frequency Reuse Factor N = 1:**
In classical cellular design, the *same* frequency cannot be reused in adjacent cells due to co-channel interference (CCI), forcing a reuse factor of $N = 3$, $N = 4$, or $N = 7$. With SDMA (via beamforming and null steering), each cell can use **every channel in every cell** because the beams spatially isolate users from neighboring cells. Hence **N = 1** — *universal reuse* — is achievable.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Derive the array factor expression for a Uniform Linear Array (ULA) of $N$ isotropic elements with spacing $d$. Show that the half-power beamwidth is inversely proportional to $N$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of *smart antennas* and describe any two beamforming algorithms used in SDMA. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) ULA Array Factor Derivation (7 marks):**

(Insert the derivation from Section 3.1 here — the geometric delay, phase shift, steering vector, geometric series sum, and magnitude form.)

**Showing HPBW ∝ 1/N:**

From the magnitude form:

$$
\vert AF(\theta) \vert = \left\vert \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{N}{2}(k d \sin\theta + \beta)\right)}{\sin\!\left(\frac{1}{2}(k d \sin\theta + \beta)\right)} \right\vert
$$

The first null occurs when the numerator is zero, i.e., $\frac{N \psi}{2} = \pi$, giving $\psi = \frac{2\pi}{N}$. This corresponds to the angular position of the first null. The half-power point lies approximately halfway between the main-lobe peak and the first null, yielding:

$$
\theta_{3dB} \propto \frac{1}{N}
$$

Therefore, **doubling the number of array elements halves the beamwidth**, increasing spatial resolution and SDMA capacity.

**(b) Smart Antennas and Beamforming Algorithms (7 marks):**

A **smart antenna** is an antenna system whose radiation pattern is dynamically adjusted based on the signal environment to improve the performance of wireless communication systems.

**Algorithm 1 — MVDR (Minimum Variance Distortionless Response) Beamformer:**
Also known as the Capon beamformer, it minimizes the total output power subject to a unit-gain constraint in the desired direction:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{w}} \; \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{R}_{xx} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^H \mathbf{a}(\theta_0) = 1
$$

The optimal weight vector is:

$$
\mathbf{w}_{MVDR} = \frac{\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0)}{\mathbf{a}^H(\theta_0) \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0)}
$$

This produces a beam with maximum SIR.

**Algorithm 2 — MUSIC (Multiple Signal Classification):**
A *subspace-based* DoA estimation method. It exploits the orthogonality between the signal subspace and the noise subspace of the correlation matrix $\mathbf{R}_{xx}$. The MUSIC pseudo-spectrum is:

$$
P_{MUSIC}(\theta) = \frac{1}{\mathbf{a}^H(\theta) \mathbf{E}_n \mathbf{E}_n^H \mathbf{a}(\theta)}
$$

where $\mathbf{E}_n$ contains the noise-subspace eigenvectors. Peaks of $P_{MUSIC}(\theta)$ yield precise DoA estimates, even for closely-spaced users — a critical requirement for SDMA.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SDMA with MIMO:** Many students write "SDMA = MIMO". Correct framing: *MIMO is an implementation of SDMA using spatial multiplexing*. Always clarify the hierarchy.
> 2. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** A 7-mark sub-question on SDMA *requires* a labelled diagram. Skipping it typically costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Beamforming Equation:** Students often describe beamforming verbally but omit the **MVDR weight formula** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0) / [\mathbf{a}^H(\theta_0) \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1} \mathbf{a}(\theta_0)]$ — this is a high-yield KTU board expectation.
> 4. **Mixing up Reuse Factors:** Stating "$N = 1$ is universal reuse" without explaining *why* (null-steering / SDMA enables it) loses marks.
> 5. **Omitting Units in HPBW Formula:** The 70$\lambda/D$ formula yields *degrees*; the student should explicitly state the units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] SDMA separates users **spatially**, not by frequency or time.
- [ ] Implemented via **directional antennas, sectorization, smart antennas, or MIMO**.
- [ ] SDMA is **overlaid** on FDMA/TDMA/CDMA — not a standalone access scheme in classical systems.
- [ ] **Frequency reuse factor $N = 1$** (universal reuse) is the canonical capacity benefit.
- [ ] **HPBW** of parabolic antenna: $\theta_{3dB} \approx 70\lambda / D$ degrees.
- [ ] **Antenna gain:** $G = 4\pi A_e / \lambda^2$; reported in dBi.
- [ ] **ULA Array Factor:** $\vert AF(\theta) \vert = \vert \sin(N\psi/2) / \sin(\psi/2) \vert$, where $\psi = kd\sin\theta + \beta$.
- [ ] **MVDR beamformer:** $\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{a}(\theta_0) / [\mathbf{a}^H(\theta_0)\mathbf{R}_{xx}^{-1}\mathbf{a}(\theta_0)]$.
- [ ] **MUSIC algorithm** is the standard DoA estimator for SDMA channel profiling.
- [ ] **MIMO capacity** grows *linearly* with $\min(N_t, N_r)$ at high SNR — this is why 5G uses Massive MIMO.
- [ ] Two core SDMA flavours: **switched-beam** (low complexity) vs. **adaptive beamforming** (high performance).
- [ ] **5G NR / Wi-Fi 6/7 / Satellite spot-beams** are real-world deployments of SDMA.
- [ ] Beam tracking is essential for **mobile users** — beam must follow the user as they move.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA)** is a fundamental channel access method used in wireless communication systems in which the available radio spectrum is partitioned into a set of non-overlapping frequency bands (sub-channels), and each individual user is assigned a unique, dedicated frequency band for the entire duration of their communication session. The fundamental principle of FDMA relies on the orthogonality property of sinusoidal signals at different frequencies, allowing multiple users to transmit simultaneously without mutual interference, provided their frequency bands are separated by adequate **guard bands**.

In FDMA systems, the total system bandwidth $B_{total}$ is divided into $N$ narrowband channels, each of bandwidth $B_c$, with $B_g$ denoting the guard band placed between adjacent channels to prevent adjacent-channel interference (ACI) caused by imperfections in real-world filters and oscillators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST633, Module 3):** FDMA is a foundational multiple access technique. Students must understand channel allocation, guard bands, the relationship between system capacity and bandwidth, and the comparative advantages/disadvantages relative to TDMA, CDMA, and SDMA.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a multi-lane highway where each lane is reserved exclusively for one specific type of vehicle (cars, trucks, buses). All vehicles travel **simultaneously** but in **separate lanes**, never colliding. The "lanes" of the radio spectrum are the **frequency bands**, and the "vehicles" are the user signals. The small grass medians between the highway lanes correspond to **guard bands**, which prevent vehicles from veering into the wrong lane due to driver error (analogous to filter imperfections and oscillator drift in real RF systems).

A simpler analogy: think of a radio with multiple preset stations (e.g., 92.7 MHz, 95.3 MHz, 98.6 MHz). Each station broadcasts continuously on its own fixed frequency, and your receiver simply tunes to one frequency at a time. FDMA works the same way — many transmitters broadcast simultaneously, and a receiver picks the right frequency band.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Number of simultaneous users (N)** in an FDMA system: dimensionless integer.
- **System Bandwidth ($B_{total}$)**: typically expressed in **MHz** or **kHz**.
- **Channel Bandwidth ($B_c$)**: typically **30 kHz** (classic AMPS 1G cellular), **200 kHz** (GSM uses 200 kHz carrier spacing with TDMA overlaid, but the FDMA layer uses 200 kHz).
- **Guard Band ($B_g$)**: typically a few **kHz**.
- **Carrier Frequency Separation ($\Delta f$)**: the center-to-center distance between adjacent FDMA channels.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"narrowband FDMA"** refers to systems where each user's signal bandwidth is small compared to the coherence bandwidth of the channel, while **"wideband FDMA"** implies the opposite. The classic AMPS cellular system is the canonical example of narrowband FDMA.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle of FDMA — The "Why" and "How"

The operational logic of an FDMA system can be broken down into five sequential steps:

1. **Spectrum Partitioning:** The total available spectrum $B_{total}$ allocated to the service provider is mathematically divided into $N$ equal sub-bands. The governing equation is:
$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{total}}{B_c + B_g} \right\rfloor$$
where $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ denotes the floor function because we cannot have fractional users.

2. **Guard Band Insertion:** To the immediate left and right of each user's allocated band, a small unused band $B_g$ is reserved. This **guard band** is the engineering compromise that protects against:
   - Adjacent channel interference (ACI) due to imperfect bandpass filters
   - Doppler shifts in high-mobility scenarios
   - Oscillator frequency drift in practical hardware

3. **Assignment of Carriers:** Each user $i$ (where $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$) is assigned a unique carrier frequency $f_i$, with the relationship:
$$f_i = f_0 + (i-1) \cdot (B_c + B_g)$$
where $f_0$ is the lowest carrier frequency in the allocation.

4. **Continuous Transmission:** A given FDMA user, once assigned a channel, transmits **continuously** for the duration of the call. There is no time-sharing. This is the defining characteristic that distinguishes FDMA from TDMA.

5. **Duplexing via FDD:** Since most communication is bidirectional, FDMA systems typically employ **Frequency Division Duplexing (FDD)**, where the **downlink** (BS → MS) and **uplink** (MS → BS) use **separate frequency bands**. The pair of frequencies (uplink + downlink) assigned to a user is called a **duplex channel**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Description / Units |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Number of FDMA channels | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B_{total} - 2B_{guard}}{B_c} \right\rfloor$ | $B_{total}$ in Hz, $N$ is dimensionless |
| 2 | Alt. form (with guard band between channels) | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B_{total}}{B_c + B_g} \right\rfloor$ | Common form for KTU problems |
| 3 | Carrier of $i^{th}$ user | $f_i = f_0 + (i-1) \cdot (B_c + B_g)$ | Hz |
| 4 | Channel capacity (Shannon per channel) | $C = B_c \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/sec |
| 5 | Total system capacity | $C_{total} = N \cdot B_c \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/sec |
| 6 | Required guard band | $B_g \approx 2 \cdot f_{Doppler,max} + 2 \cdot \Delta f_{osc}$ | Hz |
| 7 | AMPS 1G example | $B_{total} = 25 \text{ MHz},\ B_c = 30 \text{ kHz},\ B_g \approx 10\text{–}17 \text{ kHz}$ | Yields 832 channels (with reuse) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on Pipes:** All vertical bars in formulas above use $\vert$ notation (not the literal `|` character) so as not to break the markdown table structure.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

FDMA was the dominant multiple access scheme in the **first generation (1G) analog cellular systems**:
- **AMPS (Advanced Mobile Phone System)** — North American standard using **30 kHz** channels within a **25 MHz** allocation.
- **TACS (Total Access Communication System)** — European variant.
- **NMT (Nordic Mobile Telephone)** — Scandinavian system.

Even in modern 4G LTE and 5G NR systems, FDMA principles persist at the resource-element level through **OFDMA (Orthogonal FDMA)**, where users are allocated subsets of orthogonal subcarriers. Therefore, mastering FDMA is foundational for understanding all subsequent generations of mobile technology.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Number of FDMA Channels in a Given Bandwidth

**Problem Setup:**
A wireless operator is allocated a total spectrum of $B_{total} = 25$ MHz for downlink transmission. Each user requires a channel bandwidth of $B_c = 30$ kHz. To prevent adjacent channel interference, a guard band of $B_g = 10$ kHz is placed between adjacent channels. Determine the maximum number of simultaneous FDMA users $N$ the system can support.

**Step 1: Identify the system parameters**

The total available spectrum is $B_{total} = 25$ MHz $= 25 \times 10^6$ Hz.
The per-user channel bandwidth is $B_c = 30$ kHz $= 30 \times 10^3$ Hz.
The guard band between channels is $B_g = 10$ kHz $= 10 \times 10^3$ Hz.

**Step 2: Compute the effective bandwidth consumed per user**

Each user occupies a channel of width $B_c$, plus a guard band of width $B_g$ to its right (the next user's channel to the left already has its own guard band). The total spectrum consumed per user is therefore:

$$B_{eff} = B_c + B_g$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$B_{eff} = 30 \text{ kHz} + 10 \text{ kHz} = 40 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3: Compute the maximum number of channels**

The number of users that can be packed into the spectrum is the ratio of total bandwidth to effective bandwidth per user:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{total}}{B_c + B_g} \right\rfloor$$

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{25 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}{40 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz}} \right\rfloor$$

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{25{,}000{,}000}{40{,}000} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 4: Perform the division**

$$\frac{25{,}000{,}000}{40{,}000} = \frac{25{,}000}{40} = 625$$

Therefore:

$$N = \lfloor 625 \rfloor = 625 \text{ users}$$

**Step 5: Compute the residual bandwidth**

The spectrum actually used is:

$$B_{used} = N \cdot (B_c + B_g) = 625 \times 40 \text{ kHz} = 25{,}000 \text{ kHz} = 25 \text{ MHz}$$

The remaining unused spectrum is:

$$B_{residual} = B_{total} - B_{used} = 25 \text{ MHz} - 25 \text{ MHz} = 0 \text{ Hz}$$

So the spectrum is **perfectly utilized** in this idealized case.

### 3.2 Derivation: FDMA System Capacity Using Shannon's Theorem

**Problem Setup:**
Each of the $N = 625$ FDMA channels described above operates with a signal-to-noise ratio of $\text{SNR} = 30$ dB. Compute (a) the per-channel capacity and (b) the total system capacity.

**Step 1: Convert SNR from dB to linear scale**

The conversion formula from decibels to linear scale is:

$$\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{\frac{\text{SNR}_{dB}}{10}}$$

$$\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{\frac{30}{10}} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 2: Apply Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem**

For a single FDMA channel of bandwidth $B_c$ with AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise), the maximum achievable data rate is:

$$C = B_c \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$

Substituting values:

$$C = 30 \times 10^3 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000)$$

**Step 3: Compute the log term**

$$1 + 1000 = 1001$$

$$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}$$

**Step 4: Compute per-channel capacity**

$$C = 30{,}000 \cdot 9.967 \text{ bps}$$

$$C \approx 299{,}000 \text{ bps} \approx 299 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 5: Compute total system capacity**

$$C_{total} = N \cdot C = 625 \times 299 \text{ kbps}$$

$$C_{total} = 186{,}875 \text{ kbps} \approx 186.875 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Final Answer:** Each FDMA channel supports approximately **299 kbps**, and the total FDMA system capacity is approximately **186.875 Mbps**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: FDMA vs. Other Multiple Access Schemes

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA | OFDMA |
|-----------|------|------|------|-------|
| Resource Domain | Frequency | Time | Code (Spread Spectrum) | Frequency (orthogonal subcarriers) |
| Transmission Mode | Continuous | Burst | Continuous | Burst on assigned subcarriers |
| Guard Band Required | **Yes** (frequency) | **Yes** (time slot) | Not between users (uses codes) | Minimal (orthogonality) |
| Synchronization | Moderate (frequency) | Strict (time) | Strict (code) | Very strict |
| Power Control | Less critical | Less critical | **Critically required** | Moderate |
| Complexity | Low | Moderate | High | High |
| Spectral Efficiency | Moderate | Higher | High | Highest |
| Susceptibility to Narrowband Interference | High | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Example System | AMPS (1G) | GSM (2G) | IS-95 (2G CDMA) | LTE / 5G NR |

> [!NOTE]
> The above matrix is a high-frequency KTU comparison question. Memorize the **trade-off axes** (resource domain, guard band requirement, power control strictness) rather than the exact numbers.

### 3.4 Worked Example: Finding Carrier Frequency of the $i^{th}$ User

**Given:** $f_0 = 870$ MHz, $B_c = 30$ kHz, $B_g = 10$ kHz.

**Find:** The carrier frequency of the **$50^{th}$** user.

**Step 1: Write the carrier frequency equation**

$$f_i = f_0 + (i-1) \cdot (B_c + B_g)$$

**Step 2: Substitute $i = 50$**

$$f_{50} = 870 \text{ MHz} + (50 - 1) \cdot (30 + 10) \text{ kHz}$$

$$f_{50} = 870 \text{ MHz} + 49 \cdot 40 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3: Compute the offset**

$$49 \cdot 40 \text{ kHz} = 1960 \text{ kHz} = 1.96 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4: Compute the final carrier frequency**

$$f_{50} = 870 \text{ MHz} + 1.96 \text{ MHz} = 871.96 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final Answer:** The 50th user's carrier frequency is **$f_{50} = 871.96$ MHz**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FDMA Frequency Allocation Block Diagram (Conceptual)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the conceptual frequency allocation and architecture of an FDMA system. Each user occupies a unique frequency slot, separated by guard bands, and bidirectional communication is achieved using FDD with separate uplink and downlink bands.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Total Spectrum B_total 25 MHz"]:::top
    node2["Guard Band Upper 10 kHz"]:::guard
    node3["User 1 Channel 30 kHz f0"]:::ch1
    node4["Guard Band 10 kHz"]:::guard
    node5["User 2 Channel 30 kHz f1"]:::ch2
    node6["Guard Band 10 kHz"]:::guard
    node7["User 3 Channel 30 kHz f2"]:::ch3
    node8["Guard Band 10 kHz"]:::guard
    node9["User N Channel 30 kHz fN-1"]:::chN
    node10["Guard Band Lower 10 kHz"]:::guard
    
    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6 --> node7 --> node8 --> node9 --> node10

    classDef top fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef guard fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#900,stroke-width:1px,color:#FFF
    classDef ch1 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ch2 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ch3 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef chN fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 FDMA Transmitter & Receiver Block Architecture

This Mermaid diagram depicts the functional block-level architecture of an FDMA system, showing how the voice/data signal is modulated onto the assigned carrier, transmitted through the channel, and demodulated at the receiver with a bandpass filter isolating the correct frequency.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Voice or Data Source"]:::src --> B["Audio Lowpass Filter"]:::proc
    B --> C["FM Modulator in FDD or AM in AMPS"]:::proc
    C --> D["Mixer with Carrier fi from Synthesizer"]:::proc
    D --> E["Power Amplifier PA"]:::proc
    E --> F["Antenna Transmit"]:::ant
    F -. Wireless Channel .-> G["Antenna Receive"]:::ant
    G --> H["Low Noise Amplifier LNA"]:::proc
    H --> I["Bandpass Filter Centered at fi"]:::filter
    I --> J["Mixer with Local Oscillator"]:::proc
    J --> K["FM or AM Demodulator"]:::proc
    K --> L["Audio Lowpass Filter"]:::proc
    L --> M["Speaker or Data Sink"]:::src

    classDef src fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#006,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ant fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef filter fill:#98FB98,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 FDD-Based FDMA Duplex Channel Pairing

This diagram shows how a single FDMA user actually occupies **two** frequency channels — one for uplink (MS → BS) and one for downlink (BS → MS) — separated by a **duplex distance** $f_{dd}$ to prevent the near-far self-interference between the user's own transmitter and receiver.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DL["DOWNLINK BAND 869 to 894 MHz BS to MS"]
        D1["DL Channel 1 f_dl1"]:::dl
        D2["DL Channel 2 f_dl2"]:::dl
        D3["DL Channel 3 f_dl3"]:::dl
    end
    subgraph DD["DUPLEX DISTANCE f_dd = 45 MHz"]
        DDnode["Separate UL and DL bands to avoid self-interference"]:::note
    end
    subgraph UL["UPLINK BAND 824 to 849 MHz MS to BS"]
        U1["UL Channel 1 f_ul1"]:::ul
        U2["UL Channel 2 f_ul2"]:::ul
        U3["UL Channel 3 f_ul3"]:::ul
    end

    D1 -. paired with .-> U1
    D2 -. paired with .-> U2
    D3 -. paired with .-> U3

    classDef dl fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ul fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#900,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the duplex distance?** If uplink and downlink were on the same frequency (TDD instead of FDD), the user's own transmitter would drown out the weak received signal. FDD with a wide duplex spacing ($f_{dd} = 45$ MHz in AMPS) makes filtering practical.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Define Frequency Division Multiple Access (FDMA). Mention two of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
FDMA is a multiple access technique in which the available radio spectrum is divided into a number of non-overlapping frequency bands, and each user is assigned a unique frequency band for the entire duration of the call. Multiple users transmit simultaneously on different frequency channels, separated by guard bands to prevent mutual interference.

**Two Key Characteristics (1.5 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Continuous transmission:** A user transmits continuously on the assigned channel; there is no time-sharing.
2. **Narrowband channels:** Each user gets a small slice of the spectrum (e.g., 30 kHz in AMPS), making the symbol period long relative to delay spread.
3. *(any one of the following acceptable)* Requires **guard bands** between adjacent channels; Uses **FDD** for duplexing; Low complexity in user equipment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is a guard band in FDMA? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A guard band is an unused portion of the spectrum inserted between two adjacent FDMA channels.

**Why necessary — any two of the following (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Adjacent Channel Interference (ACI) Prevention:** Real-world bandpass filters have finite roll-off (skirts), so they cannot perfectly isolate one channel from its neighbor. The guard band absorbs the leakage.
2. **Oscillator Drift:** Practical oscillators exhibit frequency drift due to temperature, aging, and Doppler shifts in mobile environments. The guard band provides tolerance.
3. **Doppler Effect Compensation:** In high-mobility scenarios, the received frequency shifts, and the guard band prevents overlap.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question adapted to Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the operational principle of FDMA with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of guard bands and the FDD duplexing scheme used in FDMA systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AMPS cellular operator is allocated a total downlink spectrum of **25 MHz**. Each user channel occupies **30 kHz**, and a guard band of **10 kHz** is placed between adjacent channels. Compute:
   (i) The maximum number of simultaneous FDMA users.
   (ii) The carrier frequency of the **75th** user, given the lowest carrier is at **870 MHz**.
   (iii) The total system capacity (in Mbps) if each channel operates with **SNR = 20 dB**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FDMA Operational Principle, Guard Bands, and FDD (7 marks):**

**Operational Principle (3 marks):**
In FDMA, the available radio spectrum is divided into $N$ non-overlapping frequency bands, each assigned exclusively to one user. The division is given by $N = (B_{total}) / (B_c + B_g)$. Each user transmits a continuously modulated carrier (typically FM in analog systems like AMPS). Multiple users coexist in the spectrum simultaneously because their frequency bands do not overlap (in the ideal case). At the receiver, a bandpass filter centered at the desired user's frequency isolates that user's signal.

**Role of Guard Bands (2 marks):**
Guard bands are unused frequency slots placed between adjacent FDMA channels. They serve three critical engineering purposes:
- Compensate for imperfect filter roll-off and out-of-band emissions
- Accommodate oscillator frequency drift
- Mitigate Doppler shift in mobile scenarios

**FDD Duplexing (2 marks):**
Since mobile communication is bidirectional, FDMA systems use **Frequency Division Duplexing (FDD)**. The uplink (MS → BS) and downlink (BS → MS) are assigned to **separate frequency bands**, separated by a wide **duplex distance** (e.g., 45 MHz in AMPS). Each user is given a **pair** of frequencies — one for uplink, one for downlink. The wide duplex separation enables cheap, practical filters to isolate transmit and receive paths in the user's handset, preventing the strong transmitted signal from drowning out the weak received signal.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum number of users (3 marks):**

Using the FDMA channel-counting formula:

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{total}}{B_c + B_g} \right\rfloor$$

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{25 \times 10^6}{(30 + 10) \times 10^3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{25{,}000{,}000}{40{,}000} \right\rfloor$$

$$N = 625 \text{ users}$$

**(ii) Carrier frequency of 75th user (2 marks):**

Using $f_i = f_0 + (i-1)(B_c + B_g)$:

$$f_{75} = 870 \text{ MHz} + (75 - 1) \times 40 \text{ kHz}$$

$$f_{75} = 870 \text{ MHz} + 74 \times 40 \text{ kHz} = 870 \text{ MHz} + 2960 \text{ kHz}$$

$$f_{75} = 870 \text{ MHz} + 2.96 \text{ MHz} = 872.96 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) Total system capacity (2 marks):**

Convert SNR: $\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{20/10} = 100$.

Per-channel capacity:

$$C = B_c \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 30{,}000 \cdot \log_2(101)$$

$$\log_2(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

$$C \approx 30{,}000 \times 6.658 \approx 199{,}750 \text{ bps} \approx 199.75 \text{ kbps}$$

Total system capacity:

$$C_{total} = N \times C = 625 \times 199.75 \text{ kbps} \approx 124{,}844 \text{ kbps} \approx 124.84 \text{ Mbps}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question adapted to Module 3]**

**(a)** Compare FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA across at least **five** parameters. State two advantages and two disadvantages of FDMA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wireless system uses FDMA with the following parameters: $B_{total} = 12.5$ MHz, $B_c = 25$ kHz, $B_g = 5$ kHz. Compute the number of channels, the total system capacity at SNR = 1000 (linear), and the fraction of spectrum used as guard band. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison of FDMA, TDMA, CDMA + Advantages/Disadvantages of FDMA (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (5 marks — 1 mark per row):**

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|-----------|------|------|------|
| Resource Domain | Frequency | Time | Code (PN sequence) |
| Transmission | Continuous | Burst | Continuous (spread) |
| Guard Band | Required (frequency) | Required (time slot) | Not needed (codes separate) |
| Synchronization | Frequency only | Strict time | Strict code phase |
| Power Control | Less critical | Less critical | Critically required |

**Two Advantages of FDMA (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Simplicity:** FDMA is the simplest multiple access scheme to implement; user equipment only needs a frequency synthesizer and a bandpass filter.
2. **No stringent timing or power control:** Unlike TDMA (which needs slot synchronization) and CDMA (which needs tight power control to avoid near-far problem), FDMA is more forgiving.

**Two Disadvantages of FDMA (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. **Wastage of bandwidth:** Guard bands reduce spectral efficiency. A channel is wasted even when the user is silent (no voice activity factor exploited).
2. **Inflexibility:** A channel assigned to a user cannot be reassigned mid-call, leading to poor utilization for bursty data traffic.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Number of channels (3 marks):**

$$N = \left\lfloor \frac{B_{total}}{B_c + B_g} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{12.5 \times 10^6}{(25 + 5) \times 10^3} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{12{,}500{,}000}{30{,}000} \right\rfloor$$

$$N = \lfloor 416.67 \rfloor = 416 \text{ channels}$$

**(ii) Total system capacity (2 marks):**

$$\log_2(1 + 1000) = \log_2(1001) \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$

Per-channel capacity:

$$C = 25{,}000 \times 9.967 \approx 249{,}175 \text{ bps} \approx 249.18 \text{ kbps}$$

Total capacity:

$$C_{total} = 416 \times 249.18 \text{ kbps} \approx 103{,}659 \text{ kbps} \approx 103.66 \text{ Mbps}$$

**(iii) Fraction of spectrum used as guard band (2 marks):**

Total guard band used: $B_{g,total} = N \cdot B_g = 416 \times 5 \text{ kHz} = 2080 \text{ kHz} = 2.08 \text{ MHz}$.

Fraction:

$$\eta_g = \frac{B_{g,total}}{B_{total}} = \frac{2.08 \text{ MHz}}{12.5 \text{ MHz}} = 0.1664 = 16.64\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Unit conversions:** Always convert MHz to kHz (or Hz) before dividing bandwidths. Mixing units is the **#1 cause of lost marks** in FDMA numerical problems.
> 2. **Sign convention in Shannon:** Use $\log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ where SNR is in **linear** scale, not dB. Convert dB → linear **first**.
> 3. **Guard band placement:** A guard band of $B_g$ between $N$ channels results in $(N-1) \cdot B_g$ total guard spectrum (not $N \cdot B_g$), since the outermost edges of the allocation may not need internal guard bands. Read the problem wording carefully.
> 4. **Failing to draw the diagram:** KTU examiners explicitly state "neat block diagram" in 7-mark questions. Skipping the diagram can cost **2 marks** even if the written content is perfect.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FDMA = Frequency Division Multiple Access** — multiple users share the spectrum by being assigned **distinct, non-overlapping frequency bands**.
- **Continuous transmission:** A user transmits on its assigned channel without time-sharing.
- **Channel-counting formula:** $N = \lfloor B_{total} / (B_c + B_g) \rfloor$.
- **Guard band** $B_g$ is mandatory to mitigate adjacent channel interference, oscillator drift, and Doppler shifts.
- **FDD (Frequency Division Duplex)** is the typical duplexing mode in FDMA: uplink and downlink on separate frequency bands separated by a **duplex distance** (e.g., 45 MHz in AMPS).
- **Carrier frequency of user $i$:** $f_i = f_0 + (i-1)(B_c + B_g)$.
- **AMPS** is the canonical 1G FDMA system: $B_{total} = 25$ MHz, $B_c = 30$ kHz, $B_g \approx 10$ kHz $\Rightarrow$ 625 channels.
- **Capacity** is governed by Shannon's theorem applied per channel: $C_{total} = N \cdot B_c \cdot \log_2(1+\text{SNR})$.
- **Advantages:** simple implementation, no tight power/timing control needed, well-suited for analog voice.
- **Disadvantages:** spectrum wasted in guard bands, channel held even during silence, inflexible for bursty data.
- **Modern descendant:** OFDMA in 4G/5G is a sophisticated, orthogonal-subcarrier version of FDMA — the foundational principle persists.
- **Common KTU mistake to avoid:** Forgetting unit consistency, confusing SNR in dB vs. linear, omitting the block diagram in 7-mark questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)** is a channel-access method for shared-medium networks that allows multiple users to share the same **frequency band** by allocating **disjoint, periodic time slots** to each user within a repeating structure called a **frame**. Each mobile station transmits its burst of data only inside its assigned slot and remains silent (idle) during all other slots, while base station control ensures precise slot synchronization across all subscribers.

In TDMA, the entire **bandwidth** (BW) of the channel is given to a single user — but only for a fraction of the time. The transmission is therefore **non-contiguous in time but contiguous in frequency**, distinguishing TDMA clearly from FDMA (which is contiguous in time but disjoint in frequency).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Round-Table Discussion 🍽️

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine **8 friends sitting around a round table**, but only **one shared microphone**. The moderator announces: *"Each person gets exactly 30 seconds to speak, in fixed order: Alice → Bob → Carol → ... → back to Alice."* Even though the microphone is *physically* used by everyone, **at any given instant only ONE person is speaking**. The "air-time" is divided, not the microphone itself. That is TDMA in a nutshell.

In radio terms:
- The **microphone** = the shared carrier frequency
- The **30-second window** = a TDMA **time slot**
- The **full cycle (Alice → Bob → ...)** = a TDMA **frame**
- The **moderator's stopwatch** = the **base station clock** providing synchronization

### 1.3 Salient Features at a Glance

| Parameter | TDMA Characteristic |
|---|---|
| Frequency domain | Shared by all users (single carrier band) |
| Time domain | Divided into N disjoint periodic slots |
| Number of users per carrier | **N** = slots per frame |
| Transmission mode | **Bursty** (user transmits only in own slot) |
| Duplexing support | Both **TDD** and **FDD** |
| Power consumption | Lower (transmitter ON only during slot) |
| Handover complexity | Simpler (idle slots allow monitoring) |
| Synchronization need | **Critical** (slot alignment mandatory) |

### 1.4 Standard Systems that Use TDMA

- **GSM** (Global System for Mobile Communications) — 8 slots/frame
- **IS-54 / IS-136** (North American Digital Cellular)
- **PDC** (Personal Digital Cellular — Japan)
- **TETRA** (Terrestrial Trunked Radio)
- **DAMPS** and **DECT** (cordless phones)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 emphasis: questions on TDMA are typically linked with **GSM frame hierarchy** and **comparison with FDMA/CDMA** in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Heart of TDMA

A TDMA channel is organized as a **periodic sequence of frames**. Each frame is partitioned into **N non-overlapping time slots**, and each user is statically or dynamically assigned one or more slots per frame.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Time-axis quantization:** The continuous timeline is sliced into equal-length frames of duration $T_f$.
2. **Frame sub-division:** Each frame is sub-divided into **N slots**, so the slot duration is:
$$T_s = \frac{T_f}{N}$$
3. **Slot assignment:** Base station assigns slot index $k \in \{1, 2, ..., N\}$ to mobile station $MS_k$.
4. **Burst transmission:** During slot $k$, only $MS_k$ transmits; all others remain silent (receiver ON for downlink).
5. **Guard time ($T_g$):** A short silent interval between slots absorbs propagation-delay jitter and timing misalignment.
6. **Training/Midamble sequence:** A known bit pattern inside each burst helps the receiver perform **channel estimation** and **equalization** (essential because each user sees a different channel impulse response).
7. **Uplink/Downlink separation:** Achieved either via **TDD** (alternate frames for UL/DL on same frequency) or **FDD** (paired frequencies, half slots UL / half slots DL).
8. **Frame repetition:** The cycle repeats every $T_f$ seconds; the user's effective data rate is therefore reduced by a factor of **N**.

## 2.2 Anatomy of a TDMA Burst (Slot Internals)

Each TDMA time slot is not 100 % payload. A typical burst structure contains:

| Field | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Guard bits | $T_g$ | Prevent overlap from propagation delay |
| Training sequence | $T_t$ | Channel estimation / equalization |
| Tail bits | $T_{tail}$ | Help convolutional decoder flushing |
| Information bits | $T_{info}$ | **Useful payload** |
| Stealing bits | $T_{st}$ | Indicate if slot carries signalling |

The **useful fraction** of a slot is therefore:

$$T_{info} = T_s - T_g - T_t - T_{tail} - T_{st}$$

## 2.3 GSM Frame Hierarchy (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The GSM hierarchy is **deepest** among TDMA systems and is a recurring KTU exam topic:

$$\text{Hyperframe} = 2048 \text{ Superframes} = 2{,}715{,}648 \text{ TDMA frames}$$
$$\text{Superframe} = 1326 \text{ TDMA frames} \approx 6.12 \text{ s}$$
$$\text{Multiframe} = 26 \text{ or } 51 \text{ frames}$$
$$\text{TDMA Frame} = 8 \text{ time slots} = 4.615 \text{ ms}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim** — most KTU numericals are direct applications.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frame duration | $T_f = N \cdot T_s$ | seconds | N = slots/frame |
| 2 | Slot duration | $T_s = T_f \,/\, N$ | seconds | |
| 3 | Bits per frame | $B_f = R \cdot T_f$ | bits | R = channel bit rate |
| 4 | Effective user rate | $R_u = R \,/\, N$ | bps | One slot per user |
| 5 | Frame efficiency | $\eta = T_{info} \,/\, T_s$ | dimensionless | $0 \le \eta \le 1$ |
| 6 | Overall TDMA efficiency | $\eta_{TDMA} = T_{info} \cdot N \,/\, T_f$ | dimensionless | |
| 7 | Number of users | $N = \lfloor T_f \,/\, (T_{info}+T_g) \rfloor$ | integer | Floor function |
| 8 | Guard time minimum | $T_g \ge 2 \cdot d_{max} \,/\, c$ | seconds | $d_{max}$ = max cell radius, $c$ = speed of light $\approx 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 9 | TDD guard | $T_{TDD} \ge 2 \cdot d_{max} \,/\, c$ | seconds | Switch-point gap |
| 10 | Capacity (users/carrier) | $N \cdot (\text{channels/carrier})$ | users | Multi-carrier extension |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary condition:** $T_g$ must be strictly greater than the maximum round-trip propagation delay within the cell to prevent inter-slot collision (called **co-channel time overlap**).

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why Industry Uses TDMA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Digital-friendly:** Slots can carry any digital service (voice, SMS, data) — perfect for 2G/3G evolution.
> 2. **Dynamic capacity:** Slots can be **re-allocated on demand** (e.g., GPRS assigns 1–8 slots to a single user for higher data rate).
> 3. **Battery savings:** Mobile transmitter is OFF for $(N-1)/N$ of the time.
> 4. **Interference-limited reuse:** Adjacent cells can use the same carrier if their TDMA frame timing is offset — a concept central to GSM frequency reuse patterns.
> 5. **Easy handoff:** During idle slots, the mobile scans neighbouring base stations — supporting **Mobile-Assisted Handover (MAHO)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Numerical Problem 1 — Frame & Slot Duration (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A TDMA system supports **8 users per frame**. The total channel bit rate is **R = 270.833 kbps** and the frame duration is **T_f = 4.615 ms**. Compute the slot duration, bits per frame, bits per slot, and the effective per-user rate.

### Step 1 — Frame duration in bits

Convert rate and time into total bits carried by one frame:

$$B_f = R \cdot T_f$$

Substitute $R = 270{,}833$ bits/s and $T_f = 4.615 \times 10^{-3}$ s:

$$B_f = 270{,}833 \times 4.615 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$B_f = 270{,}833 \times 0.004615$$

$$B_f = 1250 \text{ bits per frame}$$

### Step 2 — Bits per time slot

Divide total bits per frame by number of slots N = 8:

$$B_s = \frac{B_f}{N} = \frac{1250}{8}$$

$$B_s = 156.25 \text{ bits per slot}$$

In GSM this is exactly **156.25 bits/slot**, of which **148 bits carry information** (the rest are training, tail, and guard).

### Step 3 — Slot duration in seconds

$$T_s = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{4.615 \times 10^{-3}}{8}$$

$$T_s = 576.875 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \approx 577 \,\mu s$$

### Step 4 — Effective per-user data rate

A user occupies only **1 slot in 8**:

$$R_u = \frac{R}{N} = \frac{270{,}833}{8}$$

$$R_u = 33{,}854 \text{ bps} \approx 33.85 \text{ kbps}$$

This matches GSM's gross full-rate speech channel rate before vocoding.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 2 — Guard Time from Cell Radius

**Problem:** A TDMA cell has a maximum radius $d_{max}$ = **35 km**. Compute the **minimum guard time** $T_g$ required to prevent uplink slot collisions between mobile stations at opposite ends of the cell.

### Step 1 — Maximum one-way propagation delay

$$T_p = \frac{d_{max}}{c} = \frac{35 \times 10^{3}}{3 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$T_p = 116.67 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 116.67 \,\mu s$$

### Step 2 — Round-trip difference between two extremes

A user at the cell edge experiences $T_p$ delay; a user at the base experiences $\approx 0$ delay. The difference is the one-way **relative delay**:

$$\Delta T = T_p = 116.67 \,\mu s$$

### Step 3 — Minimum guard time (must exceed $\Delta T$)

$$T_g \ge \Delta T = 116.67 \,\mu s$$

GSM actually uses $T_g \approx 30 \,\mu s$ because it constrains cell radius via **Timing Advance (TA)** commands, which instruct the mobile to pre-transmit by $T_p$ seconds. This **avoids wasting a full $T_p$ as guard time** — a clever KTU-favourite trick.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 3 — Frame Efficiency

**Problem:** A TDMA slot has total duration $T_s$ = **577 μs**. Of this, **guard time** = 30 μs, **training** = 26 μs, **tail** = 3 μs × 2 = 6 μs, and **stealing** bits = 2 × 3 μs = 6 μs. Compute the frame efficiency.

### Step 1 — Compute payload duration

$$T_{info} = T_s - T_g - T_t - T_{tail} - T_{st}$$

$$T_{info} = 577 - 30 - 26 - 6 - 6$$

$$T_{info} = 509 \,\mu s$$

### Step 2 — Compute efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{T_{info}}{T_s} = \frac{509}{577}$$

$$\eta = 0.8821 \approx 88.21\%$$

### Step 3 — Overall TDMA efficiency (N = 8)

$$\eta_{TDMA} = \frac{N \cdot T_{info}}{T_f} = \eta = 88.21\%$$

So roughly **11.79 % of every frame is overhead** — a typical KTU question expects recognition that this is the cost TDMA pays for synchronization and equalization support.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 4 — Capacity Planning

**Problem:** A cellular operator has **25 MHz of total spectrum**, each TDMA channel occupies **200 kHz**, and each frame supports **8 users**. Compute total simultaneous users per cell-site sector (assume frequency reuse factor is reused later — single-sector first).

### Step 1 — Number of carrier frequencies

$$N_{carrier} = \frac{BW_{total}}{BW_{channel}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$N_{carrier} = 125 \text{ carriers}$$

### Step 2 — Users per carrier

Each carrier carries 8 TDMA users, so:

$$U_{per\_carrier} = N = 8$$

### Step 3 — Total users in allocated band

$$U_{total} = N_{carrier} \cdot U_{per\_carrier} = 125 \times 8 = 1000 \text{ users}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This raw capacity is then divided by the **frequency reuse cluster size** $K$ to yield **per-cell capacity** $U_{cell} = U_{total} / K$. For GSM, typical $K = 7$ (or 3, 4, 12 depending on reuse pattern).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Domain of separation | Frequency | Time | Code |
| Number of users/carrier | 1 | N (slots) | M (codes) |
| Symbol rate vs user | Full $R$ | $R/N$ | Full $R$ (spread) |
| Guard needed | Guard band | Guard time | Power control |
| Synchronization | Frequency only | **Time** (strict) | Time + code |
| Handover | Hard | MAHO-supported | Soft |
| Susceptibility to multipath | Low | Equalizer needed | RAKE receiver used |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TDMA Frame Structure — Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the **logical architecture** of a TDMA frame, mapping how base-station timing, slot allocation, and burst contents interact. (Mermaid cannot natively render a Gantt-style time axis, so we use a block-flow that captures the same hierarchy.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[BS Master Clock 13 MHz] --> A1[Frame Generator T sub f equals 4.615 ms]
    A1 --> A2[Slot Counter 0 to 7]
    A2 --> A3{Active Slot Index}
    A3 -- slot 0 --> B0[Mobile Station 1 Transmit Burst]
    A3 -- slot 1 --> B1[Mobile Station 2 Transmit Burst]
    A3 -- slot 2 --> B2[Mobile Station 3 Transmit Burst]
    A3 -- slot 3 --> B3[Mobile Station 4 Transmit Burst]
    A3 -- slot 4 --> B4[Mobile Station 5 Transmit Burst]
    A3 -- slot 5 --> B5[Mobile Station 6 Transmit Burst]
    A3 -- slot 6 --> B6[Mobile Station 7 Transmit Burst]
    A3 -- slot 7 --> B7[Mobile Station 8 Transmit Burst]
    B0 --> C0[Burst Anatomy T guard T training T tail T info T steal]
    B1 --> C0
    B2 --> C0
    B3 --> C0
    B4 --> C0
    B5 --> C0
    B6 --> C0
    B7 --> C0
    C0 --> D0[Channel Estimator uses Training Sequence]
    C0 --> D1[Equalizer removes ISI]
    C0 --> D2[Decoder Viterbi for convolutional code]
    D0 --> E0[Recovered User Bitstream]
    D1 --> E0
    D2 --> E0
    E0 --> F0[Speech Codec or Data Sink]

    style A0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C0 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E0 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 TDD vs FDD Duplexing inside TDMA

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FDD_Path[FDD Mode - Two Frequencies]
        F1[Uplink Frequency f1] --> F2[Slots 0 to 3 UL]
        F2 --> F3[Slots 4 to 7 UL]
        G1[Downlink Frequency f2] --> G2[Slots 0 to 3 DL]
        G2 --> G3[Slots 4 to 7 DL]
    end

    subgraph TDD_Path[TDD Mode - Single Frequency]
        H1[Frame N Uplink slots] --> H2[Switch Gap Guard]
        H2 --> H3[Frame N plus 1 Downlink slots]
        H3 --> H4[Switch Gap Guard]
        H4 --> H1
    end

    style FDD_Path fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TDD_Path fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 GSM Multiframe/Superframe Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Hyperframe 2048 Superframes equals 3 hours 28 min] --> L2[Superframe 1326 TDMA Frames]
    L2 --> L3[Multiframe Type 26 Traffic frames]
    L2 --> L4[Multiframe Type 51 Control frames]
    L3 --> L5[TDMA Frame 4.615 ms 8 Slots]
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6[Time Slot 577 microseconds]
    L6 --> L7[Burst Payload plus Training plus Guard]

    style L1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style L5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style L6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style L7 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> Reading the diagrams top-down gives the entire GSM timing pyramid that KTU frequently tests. Memorize the multipliers: **1 frame = 8 slots; 1 multiframe = 26 frames; 1 superframe = 1326 frames; 1 hyperframe = 2048 superframes**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define TDMA. State **two** advantages and **two** disadvantages of TDMA over FDMA.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA) is a multiple-access technique in which several users share the same frequency band by transmitting in **disjoint, periodically recurring time slots** within a TDMA frame. Each user occupies the full channel bandwidth but only for a fraction of the total time.

**Two advantages over FDMA (1 mark):**
1. Higher spectral efficiency because one carrier serves **N** users via time-sharing.
2. Mobile transmitter is OFF during $(N-1)/N$ of the frame → **battery saving**.
3. (Bonus) Flexible dynamic slot allocation — supports variable-rate services like GPRS/EDGE.

**Two disadvantages over FDMA (1 mark):**
1. Requires **precise time synchronization**; guard times reduce efficiency.
2. Complex burst-by-burst **equalization** is needed because the channel varies slot-to-slot.
3. (Bonus) Higher peak-to-average power due to bursty transmission.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why is a **training sequence** embedded inside every TDMA burst? What happens if it is removed?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Why training sequence is needed (2 marks):**
The TDMA channel is **time-varying and frequency-selective** due to multipath fading. Because each burst belongs to a different user who may be moving at a different Doppler rate, the receiver cannot rely on long-term channel statistics. The training sequence is a **known bit pattern** that allows the receiver to:
1. Estimate the channel impulse response (CIR).
2. Set the coefficients of the **equalizer** (Viterbi/DFE).
3. Synchronize bit timing and perform AGC adjustment.

**Effect of removal (1 mark):**
Without training, the receiver cannot equalize properly, leading to **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** and **burst error propagation**. Voice quality collapses, and BER rises sharply, especially for high-mobility users.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students routinely lose marks by **(a)** confusing the **hyperframe multiplier (2048)** with multiframe multiplier (26), **(b)** omitting units in numerical answers, **(c)** forgetting that **timing advance** reduces effective guard time, and **(d)** drawing the TDMA frame without labelling **guard bits, training bits, tail bits, and stealing bits**. Always include these to earn full marks.

---

### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **hierarchical frame structure of GSM** (Hyperframe → Superframe → Multiframe → Frame → Slot → Burst). State the duration and number of slots at each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TDMA system has **channel rate R = 270.833 kbps**, frame duration **T_f = 4.615 ms**, and **8 slots per frame**. Compute (i) bits per frame, (ii) bits per slot, (iii) slot duration, (iv) effective per-user rate. If **148 bits per slot** are information bits, calculate the **frame efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) GSM Frame Hierarchy (7 marks):**

GSM organizes time into a strict hierarchy:

$$\text{Hyperframe} = 2048 \text{ Superframes}$$
$$\text{Superframe} = 1326 \text{ TDMA Frames} \approx 6.12 \text{ s}$$
$$\text{Multiframe} = 26 \text{ frames (traffic)} \text{ or } 51 \text{ frames (control)}$$
$$\text{TDMA Frame} = 8 \text{ time slots}, \quad T_f = 4.615 \text{ ms}$$
$$\text{Time Slot} = 156.25 \text{ bits}, \quad T_s = 577 \,\mu s$$
$$\text{Burst} = 148 \text{ info} + 26 \text{ training} + 6 \text{ tail} + 8.25 \text{ guard bits}$$

**Explanation (per level):**
1. **Burst (0.577 ms):** The smallest transmission unit; carries 148 user bits plus overhead.
2. **Slot:** A burst positioned in one of 8 slot positions within a frame.
3. **TDMA Frame (4.615 ms):** 8 slots multiplexed together; one user is mapped to one slot.
4. **Multiframe:** Two types — 26-frame multiframe for traffic (TCH/SACCH), 51-frame multiframe for control (BCCH, CCCH).
5. **Superframe:** Combines 51 traffic multiframes + 26 control multiframes (26+51 = 1326 frames total).
6. **Hyperframe:** 2048 superframes; used to cipher voice — encryption repeats every hyperframe.

**Block diagram:**

```
Hyperframe (2048 Superframes)
   └── Superframe (1326 Frames, ~6.12 s)
         ├── Multiframe-T (26 Frames)
         │     └── TDMA Frame (8 Slots, 4.615 ms)
         │           └── Slot (156.25 bits, 577 µs)
         │                 └── Burst (info + training + guard)
         └── Multiframe-C (51 Frames)
               └── ... (same structure)
```

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Bits per frame:**

$$B_f = R \cdot T_f = 270{,}833 \times 4.615 \times 10^{-3} = 1250 \text{ bits/frame}$$

**(ii) Bits per slot:**

$$B_s = \frac{B_f}{N} = \frac{1250}{8} = 156.25 \text{ bits/slot}$$

**(iii) Slot duration:**

$$T_s = \frac{T_f}{N} = \frac{4.615 \text{ ms}}{8} = 0.5769 \text{ ms} \approx 577 \,\mu s$$

**(iv) Effective per-user rate:**

$$R_u = \frac{R}{N} = \frac{270{,}833}{8} = 33{,}854.13 \text{ bps} \approx 33.85 \text{ kbps}$$

**Frame efficiency (using 148 info bits per 156.25 total bits):**

$$\eta = \frac{148}{156.25} = 0.9472 = 94.72\%$$

(Equivalent by time: $\eta = 509/577 = 88.21\%$ — small discrepancy because GSM includes some spare bits reserved for future use. Use whichever derivation the question implies.)

---

### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**(a)** Compare **FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA** across **any seven** technical parameters in a table. Discuss why GSM adopted TDMA in the 2G era. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **guard time** in TDMA. A TDMA cell has a maximum radius of **30 km**. Calculate the **minimum guard time** needed to prevent uplink time-slot collision. If the system uses **Timing Advance (TA)** to pre-compensate, explain how it changes the answer. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) FDMA vs TDMA vs CDMA Comparison (7 marks — 1 mark per row + ½ mark for conclusion):**

| Parameter | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Domain of sharing | Frequency | Time | Code |
| Users per carrier | 1 | N (slots) | M (codes) |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Low (guard bands) | Medium | High (no guard) |
| Synchronization need | Frequency only | Strict time | Time + code |
| Multipath handling | Simple | Equalizer needed | RAKE receiver |
| Handover type | Hard | Hard, but MAHO-aided | **Soft** |
| Power control | Less critical | Less critical | **Strictly required** |
| Privacy | Low | Medium | **High** (pseudo-random code) |
| Capacity | Hard-limited by spectrum | Limited by slots | **Soft** (interference-limited) |

**Why GSM chose TDMA (1 mark):**
GSM (1991 era) needed a fully digital system with:
1. Mature digital signal processing for equalization (feasible at 270 kbps).
2. Support for both voice and **SMS/data** without re-architecting the air interface.
3. Efficient use of scarce 900 MHz spectrum by **multiplexing 8 users per 200 kHz carrier**.
4. Reasonable battery life (transmitter OFF 7/8 of the time).
5. Smooth migration path to **GPRS** (multiple slots per user) and **EDGE** (higher-order modulation).

**(b) Guard Time Concept & Calculation (7 marks):**

**Concept (3 marks):**
Guard time is a small silent interval between two consecutive TDMA slots. It absorbs:
- Difference in propagation delay between a near-mobile and a far-mobile.
- Synchronization inaccuracy at the mobile station.
- Multi-path-induced pulse spreading.

If $T_g$ is too small, the burst from user $i$ spills over into the slot of user $i+1$ → **inter-slot interference** (uplink collision at base station).

**Calculation (2 marks):**

Maximum propagation delay:

$$T_p = \frac{d_{max}}{c} = \frac{30 \times 10^{3}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 100 \,\mu s$$

Minimum guard time:

$$T_g \ge T_p = 100 \,\mu s$$

**Timing Advance effect (2 marks):**
Instead of waiting $100 \,\mu s$ inside every slot, GSM instructs each mobile via a **Timing Advance (TA)** command to transmit **$T_p$ seconds early**. This pre-compensation cancels the propagation delay at the base station receiver. Therefore:

- **Without TA:** $T_g \ge T_p = 100 \,\mu s$ → wastes 100 μs every slot.
- **With TA:** $T_g$ can be reduced to the **timing-jitter margin only**, typically **~30 μs** in GSM.

This is why GSM slots are only **577 μs long** instead of the **~700 μs** they would need without TA — a 14 % efficiency gain that scales across the entire network.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for TDMA**

- **Definition:** TDMA = multiple users share one frequency band by transmitting in **disjoint time slots** within a periodic frame.
- **Frame = N slots**; each user gets **at least one slot** per frame.
- **Slot duration:** $T_s = T_f / N$.
- **Per-user rate:** $R_u = R / N$.
- **Bits/frame:** $B_f = R \cdot T_f$.
- **Guard time minimum:** $T_g \ge d_{max} / c$ — reduced by Timing Advance.
- **Burst anatomy:** Guard → Training → Info → Tail → Steal (memorize order).
- **Frame efficiency:** $\eta = T_{info}/T_s$ (typically 80–95 %).
- **GSM specifics (memorize):**
  - 8 slots/frame, 4.615 ms/frame, 577 μs/slot, 156.25 bits/slot, 148 info bits.
  - Multiframe = 26 (traffic) or 51 (control).
  - Superframe = 1326 frames; Hyperframe = 2048 superframes.
  - Channel rate = 270.833 kbps → user rate ≈ 33.85 kbps.
- **Duplexing modes:** TDD (single freq, time-switched) vs FDD (paired freq).
- **Advantages:** digital-friendly, battery-saving, dynamic slot allocation, MAHO support.
- **Disadvantages:** strict synchronization, equalizer complexity, guard-time overhead.
- **Comparison anchors:** FDMA = freq-domain; TDMA = time-domain; CDMA = code-domain.
- **Soft handover → CDMA; Hard/MAHO → TDMA** — examiners love this distinction.
- **Timing Advance = unique TDMA trick** to reduce guard time without reducing cell size.
- **Hyperframe** is used for ciphering — never confuse with superframe/multiframe durations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Code Division Multiple Access (CDMA)** is a **spread-spectrum multiple access technique** in which multiple users share the **same frequency band simultaneously** and transmit at the **same time** by assigning each user a **unique, quasi-orthogonal spreading code**. Information from each user is *spread* over a wide bandwidth using its code, transmitted, and then *despread* at the receiver by correlating with the same code. Signals of other users appear as low-level pseudo-random noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight:**
> CDMA is fundamentally different from FDMA (separate frequencies) and TDMA (separate time slots). In CDMA, **all users occupy the same band at the same time**, and they are separated only by their **codes**. This is the principle behind 2G/3G cellular systems (IS-95, WCDMA, CDMA2000).

**Notation used throughout this module:**

- $R_b$ — Information bit rate (bps)
- $R_c$ — Chip rate of spreading code (chips/sec)
- $W$ — Spread bandwidth (Hz), approximately $W \approx R_c$
- $G_p$ — Processing Gain (dimensionless)
- $E_b$ — Energy per information bit (Joules)
- $I_0$ — Interference spectral density (W/Hz)

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cocktail Party" Model

Imagine a large **cocktail party** in a single hall (one room = one frequency band) with everyone speaking at the same time. Now suppose:

- **English speakers** listen for English, **French speakers** for French, **German speakers** for German.
- All these languages are spoken simultaneously in the same room, but each listener only understands their own language; the other languages sound like **unintelligible background babble**.

CDMA works exactly like this:

| Element | Real System | Cocktail Party Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| Common medium | One shared frequency band | One common room |
| User separation | Unique spreading code | Native language |
| Data signal | Original voice/data | Conversation content |
| Spread signal | Code × data (high rate chips) | Conversation heard by all |
| Despreading | Correlate with own code | Listener understands own language |
| Other users' signals | Treated as noise | Other languages = background noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "spread spectrum"?** The bandwidth of the transmitted signal is deliberately **spread (widened)** by a factor equal to the *Processing Gain*. This spread provides **resistance to jamming, multipath fading, and interception**, and allows many users to coexist in the same band.

---

## 1.3 Why CDMA — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The CDMA Advantage (from the KTU prescribed text framework):**
> 1. **High spectral efficiency** — all users reuse every frequency in every cell.
> 2. **Soft capacity** — no hard limit on the number of users; capacity degrades gracefully.
> 3. **Inherent privacy** — without the correct code, the signal is unintelligible.
> 4. **Multipath resistance** — RAKE receiver exploits multipath components.
> 5. **Soft handover** — a mobile can communicate with two base stations simultaneously.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Spreading & Despreading Principle

### Step-by-step logical breakdown:

- **Step 1 — Source data:** User's narrowband data bit stream at rate $R_b$ bps occupies bandwidth $\approx R_b$ Hz.
- **Step 2 — Spreading:** The data is multiplied (mod-2 added, i.e., XOR) with a **pseudo-random (PN) spreading code** running at a much higher chip rate $R_c$. The resulting signal has bandwidth $\approx R_c \gg R_b$.
- **Step 3 — Transmission:** All users transmit their spread signals simultaneously on the **same RF carrier frequency**.
- **Step 4 — Reception:** The intended receiver multiplies the composite received signal by a *synchronized replica* of the original spreading code. This **despreads** the wanted signal back to its original bandwidth $R_b$, while leaving other users' signals still spread (and thus appearing as low-power wideband noise).
- **Step 5 — Filtering:** A narrow bandpass filter at the data bandwidth rejects the wideband noise, recovering the original data with high signal-to-noise ratio.

### Mathematical representation of spreading:

Let the data bit be $b(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$ and the spreading code be $c(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$. The transmitted spread signal for user $k$ is:

$$s_k(t) = b_k(t) \cdot c_k(t)$$

At the receiver, despreading produces:

$$r_k(t) = s_k(t) \cdot c_k(t) = b_k(t) \cdot c_k(t) \cdot c_k(t) = b_k(t) \cdot c_k^2(t) = b_k(t)$$

since $c_k^2(t) = 1$ for any bipolar code. For other users $j \neq k$:

$$r_k(t) = s_j(t) \cdot c_k(t) = b_j(t) \cdot c_j(t) \cdot c_k(t)$$

Because the codes are **orthogonal** (or quasi-orthogonal for asynchronous CDMA), this cross-product behaves like noise.

---

## 2.2 Processing Gain ($G_p$)

The **Processing Gain** is the most fundamental CDMA parameter. It quantifies how much the bandwidth is expanded.

### Definition:

$$G_p = \frac{\text{Spread Bandwidth}}{\text{Information Rate}} = \frac{W}{R_b} = \frac{R_c}{R_b}$$

It also equals the number of chips per information bit. For example, IS-95 uses $R_c = 1.2288$ Mcps and $R_b = 9.6$ kbps, giving:

$$G_p = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 128 \;(21 \text{ dB})$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every key equation** the examiner can ask in a Part-A or Part-B question on CDMA. Memorize the formulas, the boundary conditions, and the engineering use.

| # | Formula | Symbols | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_p = \dfrac{W}{R_b} = \dfrac{R_c}{R_b}$ | $W$=bandwidth, $R_b$=bit rate, $R_c$=chip rate | dimensionless (dB: $10 \log_{10} G_p$) | Spreading factor; anti-jamming margin |
| 2 | $E_b / I_0 = \dfrac{G_p}{(M-1)}$ (single cell, reverse link) | $M$ = number of users | dimensionless (dB) | Minimum required $E_b/I_0$ sets capacity |
| 3 | $M = 1 + \dfrac{W/R_b}{(E_b / I_0)_{req}}$ | $M$ = number of users | users | **Single-cell CDMA capacity** (Hard limit) |
| 4 | $M = 1 + \dfrac{G_p / (E_b/I_0)_{req}}{1+f}$ | $f$ = other-cell interference factor (typ. 0.55–0.77) | users | **Multi-cell reverse-link capacity** |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{G_p}{E_b / I_0} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1+f} \cdot \eta$ | $\eta$ = voice activity factor ($\approx 0.4$ for voice) | users | Refined reverse-link capacity |
| 6 | $E_b / N_0 = \dfrac{W/R}{N_0}$ (no interferers) | $N_0$ = noise PSD | dimensionless | Baseline SNR after despreading |
| 7 | $\text{Capacity loss factor} = (1+f) \cdot \eta^{-1}$ | $f, \eta$ as above | dimensionless | Quantifies inefficiency of reverse link |
| 8 | $\text{Hadamard size: } H_{2n} = \begin{bmatrix} H_n & H_n \\ H_n & -H_n \end{bmatrix}$ | Recursive construction | — | Generates Walsh codes for forward link |
| 9 | $\text{Spreading ratio (chips/bit)} = G_p$ | as above | chips/bit | Visualizing "how much wider" the signal is |
| 10 | $\text{Forward-link orthogonality: } \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i(j) W_k(j) = 0 \; \text{for } i \neq k$ | $W_i$ = Walsh code of length $N$ | — | All codes perfectly orthogonal in same cell |

> [!NOTE]
> **About the `|` rule:** In the table above, all the dividing lines (such as in $E_b/I_0$ and $W/R_b$) are written using the slash `/` inside a LaTeX math block `$...$`. The slash is a clean alternative to `\vert` inside a markdown table — but the formal latex way to write absolute value style fractions inside tables is `$E_b / I_0$` or `$\frac{E_b}{I_0}$`. The vertical pipe `|` symbol has been **deliberately avoided** so the markdown table renders correctly.

---

## 2.4 Spreading Codes — Walsh, PN, and Gold

CDMA uses two broad categories of codes:

### (a) Walsh Codes (used in **forward link / synchronous**)

- Length = $N = 2^n$ chips, where $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$
- Generated recursively from a **Hadamard matrix** $H_N$.
- **Strictly orthogonal** when perfectly time-synchronized.
- Used in IS-95 forward (pilot, sync, paging, traffic) channels.

### (b) Pseudo-Noise (PN) Codes (used in **reverse link / asynchronous**)

- Long, pseudo-random sequences (e.g., $m$-sequences, Gold codes, Kasami codes).
- Provide **good autocorrelation** and low **cross-correlation**.
- Cannot be perfectly orthogonal ⇒ introduce self-interference.

### Comparison Table:

| Property | Walsh Codes | PN / Gold Codes |
|---|---|---|
| Orthogonality | Strict (synchronous) | Quasi-orthogonal (async) |
| Length | Short (64–256 typical) | Very long ($2^{42}-1$ in IS-95) |
| Used in | Forward link | Reverse link |
| Cross-correlation | Zero (sync) | Low but non-zero |
| Spectral properties | Concentrated | Noise-like |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

CDMA is the foundation of:

- **IS-95 / cdmaOne** (2G) — the first commercial CDMA cellular system.
- **CDMA2000 1x / 1xEV-DO** (3G).
- **WCDMA / UMTS** (3G, used globally outside the US).
- **GPS** — each satellite transmits on the same frequency using distinct Gold codes (CDMA on a global scale!).
- **3G / 4G transitional** systems and some IoT (e.g., LoRa uses chirp spread spectrum, related principle).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Processing Gain from First Principles

We start with a binary data signal $b(t)$ of bit duration $T_b = 1/R_b$. It is multiplied by a spreading code $c(t)$ of chip duration $T_c = 1/R_c$. The transmitted signal is:

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t) \quad \text{where } c(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$$

The bandwidth of $b(t)$ is approximately $1/T_b = R_b$. The bandwidth of $s(t)$ is dominated by the faster-changing $c(t)$, hence approximately $1/T_c = R_c$.

> **Step 1 — Express the bandwidth expansion ratio:**

$$\text{Bandwidth expansion} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{1/T_c}{1/T_b} = \frac{T_b}{T_c}$$

> **Step 2 — Identify with the number of chips per bit:**

The number of chips per information bit is:

$$N_c = \frac{T_b}{T_c} = \frac{R_c}{R_b}$$

> **Step 3 — Define Processing Gain:**

$$G_p \;\equiv\; \frac{\text{Transmitted Bandwidth}}{\text{Information Bandwidth}} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = N_c$$

> **In dB:**

$$G_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_c}{R_b}\right)$$

> **Example (IS-95 traffic channel):** $R_c = 1.2288$ Mcps, $R_b = 9.6$ kbps.

$$G_p = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 128 \quad \Rightarrow \quad G_p \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}(128) \approx 21.07 \text{ dB}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Single-Cell CDMA Capacity (Reverse Link)

> **Starting assumption:** All $M$ users transmit with **perfect power control** so the desired signal is received at power $S$. Each of the $(M-1)$ other users is an interferer of power $S$ (chip-synchronous analysis).

> **Step 1 — Energy per information bit:**

$$E_b = S \cdot T_b = \frac{S}{R_b}$$

> **Step 2 — Interference spectral density $I_0$:**

The total interference power from $(M-1)$ users is $(M-1) \cdot S$, spread over bandwidth $W$:

$$I_0 = \frac{(M-1) \cdot S}{W}$$

> **Step 3 — Form the $E_b / I_0$ ratio:**

$$\frac{E_b}{I_0} = \frac{S / R_b}{(M-1) S / W} = \frac{W}{R_b (M-1)} = \frac{G_p}{M-1}$$

> **Step 4 — Solve for $M$:**

$$M - 1 = \frac{G_p}{E_b / I_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad M = 1 + \frac{G_p}{E_b / I_0} = 1 + \frac{W/R_b}{E_b / I_0}$$

> **Result (KTU high-yield equation):**

$$\boxed{\,M = 1 + \frac{W / R_b}{(E_b/I_0)_{\text{required}}}\,}$$

> **Step 5 — Numerical example (IS-95 reverse link):**

Take $W = 1.2288$ MHz, $R_b = 9.6$ kbps, and the required $E_b/N_0 \approx 7$ dB $\approx 5$ (linear). Then:

$$G_p = \frac{1.2288 \times 10^6}{9.6 \times 10^3} = 128$$

$$M = 1 + \frac{128}{5} = 1 + 25.6 \approx 26.6 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 26 \text{ users per cell (voice only, no voice activity factor).}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multi-Cell CDMA Capacity (Reverse Link, with Other-Cell Interference and Voice Activity)

In a real cellular system, users in **neighbouring cells** also produce interference. Let $f$ be the **other-cell-to-own-cell interference ratio** (typical $f \approx 0.55$ for omni-directional antennas, $\approx 0.77$ for 3-sectored). Also, voice users are silent $\approx 60\%$ of the time; let the **voice activity factor** be $\eta \approx 0.4$.

> **Step 1 — Re-express interference:**

The total interference power in the original formula becomes:

$$I_{\text{total}} = (M-1) S + f \cdot M S \approx M S (1 + f)$$

(assuming $M \gg 1$, and multiplying $M$ by the average activity $\eta$ in the next step).

> **Step 2 — Include voice activity factor $\eta$:**

With voice activity, average interference is reduced by $\eta$:

$$I_{\text{total}} \approx M S (1 + f) \eta$$

> **Step 3 — Write the refined $E_b/I_0$:**

$$\frac{E_b}{I_0} = \frac{S / R_b}{M S (1+f) \eta / W} = \frac{G_p}{M (1+f) \eta}$$

> **Step 4 — Solve for $M$:**

$$\boxed{\,M = \frac{G_p}{(E_b / I_0)_{\text{req}} \, (1+f) \, \eta}\,}$$

> **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

Using $G_p = 128$, $(E_b/I_0) = 5$, $f = 0.6$, $\eta = 0.4$:

$$M = \frac{128}{5 \times 1.6 \times 0.4} = \frac{128}{3.2} = 40 \text{ users per cell}$$

> **Step 6 — Compare with single-cell result (26 users).** The reduction of interferer count by the voice activity factor more than compensates the increase from other-cell interference, yielding a **realistic engineering capacity** of about 40 voice users per cell for IS-95.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Forward-Link Capacity

> **Step 1 — Forward link uses synchronous Walsh codes ⇒ orthogonal.** In a single cell, all other in-cell users contribute **zero** interference (orthogonal codes).
> **Step 2 — Only other-cell interference matters.** This gives:

$$\frac{E_b}{I_0} = \frac{G_p}{f \cdot M}$$

> **Step 3 — Solve for $M$:**

$$M_{\text{forward}} = \frac{G_p}{f \cdot (E_b / I_0)_{\text{req}}}$$

> **Step 4 — Numerical check:** $M_f = 128 / (0.6 \times 5) \approx 42.7 \Rightarrow 42$ users.
