Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 GEO Orbit Geometry — Mermaid Block Diagram 🛰️ ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Earth
R_E = 6378 km] --> B[Equatorial Plane
Inclination = 0 deg] B --> C[Sub-Satellite Point
Fixed Longitude on Equator] C --> D[GEO Satellite
Altitude h = 35786 km] D --> E[Orbital Radius
r = 42164 km] D --> F[Orbital Velocity
v = 3.07 km per sec] D --> G[Orbital Period
T = 86164 s] D --> H[Footprint Coverage
Angle = 17.34 deg] H --> H1[Coverage Zone A
Min Elevation 0 deg] H --> H2[Coverage Zone B
Practical Elevation 5 to 10 deg] H --> H3[Coverage Zone C
Optimal Elevation gt 20 deg] H1 --> I[Ground Earth Station
Antenna Fixed Pointing] H2 --> I H3 --> I G --> J[Appears Stationary
to Earth Observer] ``` ### 4.2 GEO Communication System — End-to-End Functional Architecture 📡 ```mermaid flowchart LR TX[Ground Transmitter
Uplink Frequency e.g. 6 GHz] --> UP[Uplink Path
FSPL approx 200 dB] UP --> SAT[GEO Satellite
Transponder] SAT --> DOWN[Downlink Path
FSPL approx 196 dB] DOWN --> RX[Ground Receiver
Downlink Frequency e.g. 4 GHz] RX --> DEMOD[Demodulator and
Decoder] DEMOD --> USER[End User
TV, VSAT, Telemetry] SAT --> ATT[Attitude Control
Station-Keeping] SAT --> SOLAR[Solar Array
Power Subsystem] SAT --> TTC[Telemetry Tracking
Command Subsystem] ``` ### 4.3 Three-Satellite Global Coverage Concept 🌍 ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GLOBAL[Global GEO Constellation - 3 Satellites] S1[GEO Sat 1
Longitude 0 deg] --> F1[Footprint 1
Africa Europe Americas] S2[GEO Sat 2
Longitude 120 deg E] --> F2[Footprint 2
Asia Australia Pacific] S3[GEO Sat 3
Longitude 240 deg E or 120 deg W] --> F3[Footprint 3
Americas Pacific] end F1 --> COV[Near-Global Coverage
Excluding Polar Regions] F2 --> COV F3 --> COV ``` ### 4.4 GEO vs LEO vs MEO — Orbit Classification Table | Parameter | LEO (Low Earth Orbit) | MEO (Medium Earth Orbit) | GEO (Geostationary Orbit) | |---|---|---|---| | Altitude | 160 – 2,000 km | 2,000 – 35,786 km | **35,786 km** | | Orbital Period | 90 – 130 min | ~5 – 12 hours | **23 h 56 min ≈ 24 h** | | Signal Delay (one-way) | ~5 – 20 ms | ~50 – 100 ms | **~119 – 139 ms** | | Footprint per Sat | Small (region) | Medium (continent) | **Very large (~42% Earth)** | | Satellites for Global Coverage | 50 – 800 (e.g., Starlink, Iridium) | 10 – 15 (e.g., GPS ~31) | **3 (e.g., Inmarsat, Intelsat)** | | Doppler Effect | Significant | Moderate | **Negligible** | | Antenna Tracking | Required (complex) | Required (simpler) | **Not required (fixed)** | | Example Systems | Starlink, Iridium | GPS, GLONASS, Galileo | **INSAT, GOES, Inmarsat** | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Define Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) and state any two of its essential conditions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A GEO is a circular orbit in the Earth's equatorial plane at an altitude of **35,786 km**, where the satellite's orbital period equals Earth's sidereal rotation period (≈ 86,164 s ≈ 24 h), making the satellite appear **stationary** relative to a ground observer. **Essential conditions (any two):** 1. The orbit must be **circular** (eccentricity $e = 0$). 2. The orbit must lie in the **equatorial plane** (inclination $i = 0°$). 3. The **orbital period must match Earth's sidereal rotation period** ($T \approx 23 \ \text{h} \ 56 \ \text{min}$). --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **State any three advantages and any three disadvantages of using GEO satellites for communication.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Advantages:** 1. No need for tracking antennas — satellite is **fixed in sky** — simpler ground equipment. 2. A **single GEO satellite** covers roughly **42% of Earth's surface** (one-third with overlap for 3 sats). 3. **Negligible Doppler shift** since relative motion between satellite and ground station is zero. 4. Long lifetime (15+ years) due to stable station-keeping. **Disadvantages:** 1. **High propagation delay** (~240 ms round-trip) — unsuitable for real-time, voice, or gaming. 2. **High free-space path loss** (~200 dB) — requires high transmit power and large antennas. 3. Cannot serve **high-latitude / polar regions** effectively (elevation angle becomes very low). 4. Launch cost is very high due to the required altitude. --- ### 📝 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Full Module Questions with Internal Choice) > **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]** > **(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expression for the orbital radius of a Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) satellite. Show all algebraic steps clearly. > **(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the GEO orbital radius, altitude above Earth's surface, and orbital velocity. Given: $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}^2$, $T_E = 86{,}164$ s, $R_E = 6{,}378$ km. #### ✅ Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:** - **Step 1 (1 mark):** Equate centripetal force to gravitational force: $$m \omega^2 r = \frac{\mu m}{r^2}$$ - **Step 2 (1 mark):** Cancel $m$ and multiply both sides by $r^2$: $$\omega^2 r^3 = \mu$$ - **Step 3 (1 mark):** Use $\omega = 2\pi / T$: $$\frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{T^2} = \mu$$ - **Step 4 (1 mark):** Solve for $r^3$: $$r^3 = \frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2}$$ - **Step 5 (1 mark):** Take cube root to obtain orbital radius: $$r = \left(\frac{\mu T^2}{4\pi^2}\right)^{1/3}$$ - **Step 6 (2 marks):** Substitute $T = T_E = 86{,}164$ s and substitute into equation, showing the cancellation logic: $$r^3 = \frac{(3.986 \times 10^{14}) \cdot (86{,}164)^2}{4\pi^2} = 7.4985 \times 10^{22} \ \text{m}^3$$ $$r = (7.4985 \times 10^{22})^{1/3} = 4.2164 \times 10^7 \ \text{m} = 42{,}164 \ \text{km}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:** - **Altitude (2 marks):** $$h = r - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \ \text{km}$$ - **Orbital Velocity (3 marks):** Using $v = \sqrt{\mu / r}$: $$v = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{4.2164 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{9.4535 \times 10^6} = 3{,}074 \ \text{m/s} = 3.07 \ \text{km/s}$$ - **Final summary (2 marks):** - Orbital radius = **42,164 km** - Altitude = **35,786 km** - Orbital velocity = **3.07 km/s** --- > **Q.B. [Internal Choice — Alternative 14-Mark Question, Dec 2023]** > **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the three conditions required for a satellite to be geostationary. Describe the physical meaning of each. > **(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the maximum and minimum slant range for a GEO satellite and compute the corresponding round-trip propagation delays. Take $r = 42{,}164$ km, $R_E = 6{,}378$ km, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. #### ✅ Model Solution: **Part (a) — The Three Conditions [7 marks]:** - **Condition 1 — Circular orbit (e = 0) (2 marks):** A satellite traces an ellipse around Earth. For the satellite to remain at a constant distance from Earth's center (and thus appear stationary), the orbit must be a **circle**, meaning eccentricity $e = 0$. Otherwise, the satellite would move closer and farther from Earth during its orbit, appearing to oscillate in the sky. - **Condition 2 — Equatorial plane (i = 0°) (2.5 marks):** The Earth's spin axis is tilted 23.5° relative to the orbital plane of most satellites. For the satellite's ground track to be a **fixed point** on Earth (rather than a figure-eight curve like a Molniya orbit), the orbital plane must coincide with Earth's equatorial plane, i.e., inclination $i = 0°$. - **Condition 3 — Earth-synchronous period (T = T_E) (2.5 marks):** The orbital period must equal Earth's sidereal rotation period (23 h 56 min ≈ 86,164 s). This way, as Earth rotates once beneath the satellite, the satellite completes exactly one orbit, and the relative motion is zero — the satellite appears frozen in the sky. **Part (b) — Slant Range and Delay [7 marks]:** - **Minimum slant range** (Earth station at sub-satellite point, zenith $\theta = 90°$) **(2 marks):** $$d_{min} = h = r - R_E = 42{,}164 - 6{,}378 = 35{,}786 \ \text{km}$$ - **Maximum slant range** (Earth station at edge of footprint, $\theta = 0°$) **(3 marks):** $$d_{max} = \sqrt{r^2 - R_E^2} = \sqrt{(42{,}164)^2 - (6{,}378)^2}$$ $$d_{max} = \sqrt{1{,}777{,}807{,}696 - 40{,}678{,}884} = \sqrt{1{,}737{,}128{,}812} \approx 41{,}679 \ \text{km}$$ - **Round-trip delays (2 marks):** - Min RTD = $2 \times 35{,}786{,}000 / 3 \times 10^8 = 0.2386$ s ≈ **238 ms** - Max RTD = $2 \times 41{,}679{,}000 / 3 \times 10^8 = 0.2779$ s ≈ **278 ms** --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Using 24 hours instead of 86,164 s (sidereal day)** — Many students write $T = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400$ s, which is the **solar day**, NOT the sidereal day. Always use **86,164 seconds** for orbital mechanics calculations. Marks deducted: **2 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to subtract $R_E$** — Students often confuse orbital radius (42,164 km) with altitude (35,786 km). The altitude is what is normally quoted for "GEO at ~36,000 km." > 3. **Writing $G$ and $M_E$ separately** — KTU prefers the combined constant $\mu = GM_E = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s². Always use $\mu$. > 4. **Ignoring units in final answer** — Always state km vs m. KTU examiners are strict about unit consistency. > 5. **Forgetting to compute round-trip delay = 2 × one-way** — A common 1-mark deduction in delay-based questions. --- ### 🔥 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ GEO altitude = **35,786 km** (memorize!) - ✅ GEO orbital radius = **42,164 km** - ✅ Orbital period = **86,164 s** (sidereal day — NOT 86,400 s) - ✅ Orbital velocity = **3.07 km/s** - ✅ Standard gravitational parameter $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² - ✅ Earth's radius $R_E = 6{,}378$ km - ✅ One-way delay range: **119 ms (min) to 139 ms (max)** - ✅ Round-trip delay: **~240 ms to ~278 ms** - ✅ Three GEO conditions: **circular + equatorial + earth-synchronous period** - ✅ Coverage angle = **17.34°** → **3 GEO sats** cover nearly all of Earth (excluding poles) - ✅ Kepler's Third Law: $T^2 \propto r^3$ — apply it whenever asked for GEO altitude - ✅ Path loss (uplink/downlink) at GEO ≈ **200 dB** (for ~6 GHz band) — high-power satellite needed - ✅ Doppler shift in GEO is **negligible** (zero relative motion) — major advantage - ✅ Typical GEO applications: **DTH TV (INSAT, Tata Sky), VSAT, weather monitoring, Inmarsat maritime, GOES weather satellites** - ✅ **GEO is unsuitable for polar regions** — minimum elevation angle becomes negative above ~81° latitude - ✅ Typical KTU keywords to spot the topic: *"derive GEO altitude," "calculate orbital period," "slant range," "propagation delay," "kepler's third law"*

topics.title: Low Earth Orbit (LEO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Low Earth Orbit (LEO)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Low Earth Orbit (LEO)** satellite system is a class of non-geostationary satellite constellation in which the spacecraft orbit the Earth at altitudes typically ranging between **500 km and 2000 km** above the Earth's mean sea level. At these altitudes, the orbital period lies between approximately **90 minutes and 120 minutes**, and the satellites travel at velocities of roughly **7.5 km/s** relative to the Earth's surface.

Unlike a single Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO) satellite, a LEO system is deployed as a *constellation* — a coordinated network of many satellites (typically 40 to 100+) distributed across multiple orbital planes to provide **continuous global or near-global coverage** through coordinated, fast-moving footprints.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECST633 – Module 3):**
> LEO forms the third tier of the satellite-orbit classification (GEO → MEO → LEO) studied in satellite-based communication. It is the *only* tier in which the orbital period, propagation delay, and handover frequency become comparable to — or shorter than — a typical mobile phone call duration.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Swarm of Lighthouses"

Imagine the Earth as a dark ocean, and a GEO satellite as a single huge lighthouse mounted very far away — it stays still, but its beam is dim by the time it reaches the water. Now imagine **hundreds of small but bright flashlights** strapped to fast drones that sweep across the sky in tight, low circles. Even though each drone is only visible for a few minutes from any one ship, there is *always* at least one drone overhead because the swarm is so dense and moves so fast.

That is precisely a LEO constellation: instead of one distant, stationary platform, many close, fast, low-flying satellites hand the user over from one to the next almost as quickly as a cell tower handover in a terrestrial cellular network.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Memorize for KTU)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Earth's mean radius | $R_E$ | **6371 km** |
| Earth's standard gravitational parameter | $\mu = GM$ | **3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²** |
| Speed of light (vacuum) | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| GEO altitude | $h_{GEO}$ | **35 786 km** |
| LEO altitude band | $h_{LEO}$ | **500 – 2000 km** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners expect the value **μ = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²** and **R_E = 6371 km** whenever a numerical orbital calculation is attempted. Forgetting these is the single most common cause of unit-conversion errors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why a LEO Constellation Exists — The "Why"

A single LEO satellite cannot provide *continuous* service to a fixed point on the ground because:
- It moves at ~7.5 km/s and crosses the sky in 5–10 minutes.
- It disappears below the horizon long before a user finishes a phone call.

Therefore, to provide **uninterrupted coverage**, the LEO system requires:
1. A *constellation* of multiple satellites.
2. **Inter-satellite links (ISLs)** or a *bent-pipe* architecture to relay traffic between satellites and gateways.
3. A **handover mechanism** that hands a user's call/data from one satellite to the next within seconds.

### 2.2 The "How" — Operational Architecture of a LEO System

A LEO system operates in five logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Uplink from User Terminal (UT):** The mobile user transmits on the assigned uplink frequency. Because the satellite is close (≤ 2000 km), the path loss is small and a small handset antenna suffices.
- **Step 2 — On-board Processing / Bent-Pipe:** Either the satellite amplifies and shifts the frequency (bent-pipe, e.g., Globalstar) or it demodulates, switches, and re-modulates (on-board processing, e.g., Iridium).
- **Step 3 — Crosslink or Downlink to Gateway:** The signal is routed to a ground gateway via an inter-satellite link, or relayed to a gateway Earth station within the same footprint.
- **Step 4 — Handover:** When the active satellite descends below the minimum elevation angle $E_{min}$ (typically 8°–25°), the system triggers a *satellite handover* to a successor satellite that is rising above $E_{min}$.
- **Step 5 — Downlink to the Called Party / Internet:** The traffic reaches the destination via the gateway and terrestrial backhaul.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the *cheat-sheet* expected for KTU 2024 ESE numerical problems. Memorize the formulas, their units, and their boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | KTU Typical Value (LEO) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Geocentric radius | $r = R_E + h$ | m or km | 6871 – 8371 km |
| 2 | Orbital velocity | $v = \sqrt{\mu / r}$ | m/s | ~7.5 km/s |
| 3 | Orbital period (Kepler III) | $T = 2\pi \sqrt{r^{3} / \mu}$ | s | ~90 – 120 min |
| 4 | Earth central half-angle (max visibility) | $\cos \varepsilon = R_E / (R_E + h)$ | degrees / rad | 20° – 30° |
| 5 | Footprint diameter on Earth | $D_{fp} = 2 R_E \varepsilon$ (ε in rad) | km | 4000 – 7000 km |
| 6 | Max. slant range | $d_{max} = \sqrt{h^{2} + 2 R_E h}$ | km | ~3200 km at 780 km altitude |
| 7 | One-way propagation delay | $t_{ow} = d / c$ | s | ~10 ms |
| 8 | Free-space path loss | $L_{fs} = 20 \log_{10}(4 \pi d / \lambda)$ | dB | ~155 – 175 dB at L-band |
| 9 | Coverage fraction per satellite | $f = (1 - \cos \varepsilon)/2$ | dimensionless | ~0.06 – 0.10 |
| 10 | Theoretical min. satellites for full coverage | $N_{min} = 1/f$ | integer | ~12 – 16 (ideal) |

> **Note on the table:** The symbol `\vert` is intentionally avoided in headers; all quantities are scalar.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of LEO Systems

- **Handheld mobile-satellite telephony** (Iridium, Globalstar).
- **Global IoT and asset tracking** (e.g., Astrocast, Swarm).
- **Low-latency broadband internet** (Starlink, OneWeb, Kuiper) — because $d \le 2000$ km, the one-way latency drops to ~10–25 ms, which is *comparable to terrestrial fiber* and **20–25 times lower** than GEO.
- **Earth observation and remote sensing** (Planet Labs, ICEYE).
- **Disaster-recovery communications** when terrestrial infrastructure is destroyed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Orbital Velocity and Period (Kepler's Third Law)

**Starting principle:** The satellite of mass $m$ is held in a circular orbit by the gravitational pull of Earth, which supplies exactly the centripetal force.

$$F_{grav} = F_{centripetal}$$

$$\frac{G M m}{r^{2}} = \frac{m v^{2}}{r}$$

**Step 1 — Cancel one $m$ and one $r$:**

$$\frac{G M}{r} = v^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the standard gravitational parameter $\mu = GM$:**

$$v^{2} = \frac{\mu}{r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

**Step 3 — Orbital period from circumference / speed:**

$$T = \frac{2 \pi r}{v} = \frac{2 \pi r}{\sqrt{\mu / r}} = 2 \pi r \sqrt{\frac{r}{\mu}} = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{r^{3}}{\mu}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check for an Iridium-like LEO at $h = 780$ km:**

$r = R_E + h = 6371 + 780 = 7151 \text{ km} = 7.151 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}$

$$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.151 \times 10^{6})^{3}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$$= 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{3.658 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}} = 2 \pi \sqrt{9.178 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$= 2 \pi \times 957.99 \text{ s} = 6020 \text{ s} \approx 100.3 \text{ min}$$

This matches Iridium's published value of ~100 min — confirming the formula.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Footprint Diameter and Coverage Fraction

**Step 1 — Earth central half-angle $\varepsilon$:**

From the geometry of a satellite at altitude $h$, the half-angle of Earth visible from the satellite satisfies:

$$\cos \varepsilon = \frac{R_E}{R_E + h}$$

**Step 2 — Footprint radius on Earth's surface** (in radians, then converted):

$$R_{fp} = R_E \cdot \varepsilon \quad \text{[with } \varepsilon \text{ in radians]}$$

$$D_{fp} = 2 R_E \varepsilon = 2 R_E \arccos\!\left(\frac{R_E}{R_E + h}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation at $h = 1000$ km:**

$\cos \varepsilon = 6371 / 7371 = 0.8643 \;\Rightarrow\; \varepsilon = 30.3^{\circ} = 0.5287 \text{ rad}$

$$D_{fp} = 2 \times 6371 \times 0.5287 = 6735 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Fraction of Earth's surface covered by one LEO satellite:**

The spherical cap area visible from the satellite has area $2 \pi R_E^{2} (1 - \cos \varepsilon)$; total Earth area is $4 \pi R_E^{2}$. Therefore:

$$f = \frac{2 \pi R_E^{2}(1 - \cos \varepsilon)}{4 \pi R_E^{2}} = \frac{1 - \cos \varepsilon}{2}$$

For $h = 1000$ km: $f = (1 - 0.8643)/2 = 0.0678 \;\Rightarrow\;$ one LEO satellite covers **~6.78 %** of Earth's surface.

**Step 5 — Theoretical minimum number of satellites for 100 % coverage:**

$$N_{min} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{2}{1 - \cos \varepsilon}$$

For $h = 1000$ km: $N_{min} = 1 / 0.0678 \approx 14.7 \;\Rightarrow\;$ at least **15 satellites** in the best-case geometric arrangement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Real LEO constellations (Iridium = 66, Globalstar = 48) use **2 to 4 times more** satellites than the theoretical $N_{min}$ to (a) tolerate satellite failures, (b) maintain coverage at higher latitudes, and (c) avoid gaps at the minimum-elevation cut-off.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Slant Range and Round-Trip Delay

**Step 1 — Slant range using the law of cosines on the Earth–satellite triangle:**

$$d^{2} = R_E^{2} + (R_E + h)^{2} - 2 R_E (R_E + h) \cos \gamma$$

where $\gamma$ is the Earth-central angle between the sub-satellite point and the ground station.

**Step 2 — Maximum slant range occurs at $\gamma = \varepsilon$** (the geometric horizon). Substituting $\cos \varepsilon = R_E / (R_E + h)$:

$$d_{max}^{2} = R_E^{2} + (R_E + h)^{2} - 2 R_E \cdot R_E$$

$$= (R_E + h)^{2} - R_E^{2} = h^{2} + 2 R_E h$$

$$\boxed{d_{max} = \sqrt{h^{2} + 2 R_E h}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example, $h = 780$ km:**

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{780^{2} + 2 \times 6371 \times 780} = \sqrt{608\,400 + 9\,938\,760}$$

$$= \sqrt{10\,547\,160} = 3248 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — One-way propagation delay at $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:**

$$t_{ow} = \frac{3\,248\,000 \text{ m}}{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} = 10.83 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Round-trip delay (UT → satellite → UT, single hop):**

$$t_{rt} = 2 \times 10.83 = 21.66 \text{ ms}$$

For comparison, GEO at the same 8° elevation gives a one-way delay of **~138 ms** — i.e., LEO delivers data **~13 × faster** than GEO. This is why modern broadband constellations prefer LEO.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — LEO vs. MEO vs. GEO (KTU Favourite Comparison)

| Parameter | LEO | MEO | GEO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Altitude | 500 – 2000 km | 5000 – 25 000 km | 35 786 km |
| Orbital period | 90 – 120 min | 5 – 12 h | 24 h (geostationary) |
| One-way delay (max) | ~10 – 25 ms | ~50 – 100 ms | ~250 – 280 ms |
| Path loss at L-band | Low | Medium | High |
| Satellites for global coverage | 40 – 100+ | 10 – 20 | 3 (theoretical) |
| Handover frequency | High (≈ every 5–10 min) | Medium (≈ every 1–2 h) | None (stationary) |
| Handheld terminal feasible? | **Yes** | Marginal | **No** (large antenna) |
| Typical examples | Iridium, Starlink | GPS, GLONASS, Galileo | Inmarsat, INTELSAT |
| Cost per satellite | Low | Medium | High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a LEO Communication System

The diagram below traces the **functional flow** of a typical call through a LEO constellation, the way KTU expects in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER TERMINAL UT HANDHELD OR IOT MODULE] --> B[UPLINK UHF L BAND OR S BAND]
    B --> C[ACTIVE LEO SATELLITE AT ALTITUDE H]
    C --> D{ON BOARD PROCESSING}
    D -- YES --> E[DEMODULATE SWITCH RE MODULATE]
    D -- NO --> F[TRANSPONDER BENT PIPE FREQUENCY SHIFT]
    E --> G[INTER SATELLITE LINKS ISL KA BAND]
    F --> G
    G --> H[NEIGHBOUR LEO SATELLITES]
    H --> I[GROUND GATEWAY EARTH STATION]
    I --> J[TERRESTRIAL BACKHAUL TO PSTN OR INTERNET]
    J --> K[CALLED PARTY OR SERVER]
    C --> L[BEAM HANDOVER MANAGER]
    H --> L
    L --> M[SUCCESSOR LEO SATELLITE RISING ABOVE MIN ELEVATION]
    M --> B
```

> **Reading guide for the diagram:**
> - Block `A` is the user; block `K` is the destination.
> - Decision node `D` separates *on-board processing* (Iridium-style, lower latency) from *bent-pipe* (Globalstar-style, simpler satellite).
> - Block `L` is the **handover manager** — the most critical subsystem unique to LEO (not present in GEO).

### 4.2 Comparative Orbit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[EARTH R_E 6371 KM] --> O1[GEO H 35786 KM T 24 H]
    E1 --> O2[MEO H 20000 KM T 12 H]
    E1 --> O3[LEO H 1000 KM T 100 MIN]
    O1 --> P1[3 SATS GLOBAL COVERAGE]
    O2 --> P2[10 TO 20 SATS GLOBAL COVERAGE]
    O3 --> P3[40 TO 100 PLUS SATS CONSTELLATION]
    P1 --> N1[NO HANDOVER FIXED FOOTPRINT]
    P2 --> N2[OCCASIONAL HANDOVER]
    P3 --> N3[CONTINUOUS HANDOVER EVERY FEW MINUTES]
```

> **Reading guide:** The lower the orbit, the **larger** the constellation count and the **faster** the handover — a fundamental trade-off KTU repeatedly tests.

### 4.3 Constellation Topology — Polar Walker (Iridium-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PLANE1[ORBITAL PLANE 1 INCLINATION 86 DEG] --> S1[SAT 1]
    PLANE1 --> S2[SAT 2]
    PLANE1 --> S3[SAT 3]
    PLANE1 --> S4[SAT 4]
    PLANE1 --> S5[SAT 5]
    PLANE1 --> S6[SAT 6]
    PLANE1 --> S7[SAT 7]
    PLANE1 --> S8[SAT 8]
    PLANE1 --> S9[SAT 9]
    PLANE1 --> S10[SAT 10]
    PLANE1 --> S11[SAT 11]
    PLANE1 --> CONN1[INTER PLANE ISL]
    CONN1 --> PLANE2[ORBITAL PLANE 2 INCLINATION 86 DEG]
    CONN1 --> PLANE3[ORBITAL PLANE 3 INCLINATION 86 DEG]
    CONN1 --> PLANE4[ORBITAL PLANE 4 INCLINATION 86 DEG]
    CONN1 --> PLANE5[ORBITAL PLANE 5 INCLINATION 86 DEG]
    CONN1 --> PLANE6[ORBITAL PLANE 6 INCLINATION 86 DEG]
    PLANE2 --> P2S[SATELLITES 11 PER PLANE]
    PLANE3 --> P3S[SATELLITES 11 PER PLANE]
    PLANE4 --> P4S[SATELLITES 11 PER PLANE]
    PLANE5 --> P5S[SATELLITES 11 PER PLANE]
    PLANE6 --> P6S[SATELLITES 11 PER PLANE]
```

> **Reading guide:** Iridium's 66-satellite Walker-star constellation is the canonical LEO reference architecture. 6 planes × 11 satellites × polar inclination gives seamless polar coverage.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellites. List any three advantages of LEO over GEO satellite systems.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Definition (1 mark):* A LEO satellite is one that orbits the Earth at an altitude between **500 km and 2000 km** above the Earth's surface, with an orbital period of approximately **90 to 120 minutes**.
- *Advantages (any 3 × 2/3 mark each, rounded):*
  1. **Lower propagation delay** (~10–25 ms one-way vs. ~250 ms for GEO), enabling real-time voice and low-latency broadband.
  2. **Lower free-space path loss**, allowing smaller, hand-held user terminals.
  3. **Smaller launch cost per satellite**, since less fuel is needed to reach LEO than GEO.
  4. **Smaller footprint delay spread** and better signal strength at the user terminal.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Why is satellite handover necessary in a LEO system? Mention its typical frequency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Reason (2 marks):* Unlike GEO satellites, LEO satellites move rapidly across the sky and remain visible to a ground user for only 5–10 minutes. Once the active satellite descends below the minimum elevation angle ($E_{min}$, typically 8°–25°), the link would be lost; therefore, the user's call/data must be transferred to the *successor* LEO satellite that is rising above $E_{min}$. This process is called *satellite handover*.
- *Frequency (1 mark):* A handover occurs approximately every **5 to 10 minutes** in a LEO constellation such as Iridium.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Derive the expression for the orbital period of a satellite in terms of the Earth's gravitational parameter $\mu$ and the orbital radius $r$. Calculate the orbital period for a LEO satellite at an altitude of 1000 km. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operation of a LEO satellite system. Compare its latency with that of a GEO system. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Derivation of orbital period — 7 marks**

- *Step 1 (1 mark):* Equate gravitational force to centripetal force for a satellite of mass $m$ in circular orbit:

$$F_{grav} = F_{centripetal} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{G M m}{r^{2}} = \frac{m v^{2}}{r}$$

- *Step 2 (1 mark):* Cancel $m$ and one $r$:

$$\frac{G M}{r} = v^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

- *Step 3 (1 mark):* Orbital period from circumference:

$$T = \frac{2 \pi r}{v} = \frac{2 \pi r}{\sqrt{\mu / r}} = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{r^{3}}{\mu}}$$

- *Step 4 (1 mark):* Substitute $r = R_E + h = 6371 + 1000 = 7371$ km $= 7.371 \times 10^{6}$ m.

- *Step 5 (1 mark):* Apply Kepler's third law:

$$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.371 \times 10^{6})^{3}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

- *Step 6 (1 mark):* Compute step by step:

$$(7.371 \times 10^{6})^{3} = 4.004 \times 10^{20} \text{ m}^{3}$$

$$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4.004 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}} = 2 \pi \sqrt{1.0045 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \pi \times 1002.27 = 6298 \text{ s}$$

- *Step 7 (1 mark):* Convert to minutes:

$$T \approx 105 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ h } 45 \text{ min}$$

**(b) LEO architecture and latency comparison — 7 marks**

- *Architecture (4 marks):* A LEO system consists of (i) **constellation of satellites** in multiple low-altitude orbital planes, (ii) **ground gateways** that connect the constellation to terrestrial networks, (iii) **user terminals** (handheld phones, IoT modules, broadband dishes), and (iv) optionally **inter-satellite links (ISLs)** for direct satellite-to-satellite routing.

The user uplink is received by the *currently overhead* satellite, processed (or bent-piped), and routed via ISL to a satellite that is *currently over a gateway*; the gateway terminates the call into the PSTN/Internet. A **handover manager** triggers satellite-to-satellite handover every few minutes as the active satellite moves out of view. (Refer to the functional architecture diagram in Section 4.1.)

- *Latency (3 marks):*
  - LEO max one-way slant range at 1000 km altitude:

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{h^{2} + 2 R_E h} = \sqrt{(1000)^{2} + 2 \times 6371 \times 1000} = 3860 \text{ km}$$

$$t_{ow}^{LEO} = \frac{3.86 \times 10^{6}}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 12.87 \text{ ms}$$

- GEO max slant range ≈ 41 679 km (at 0° elevation), giving one-way ≈ **138 ms** (round trip ≈ 277 ms).
- **Comparison:** LEO latency is roughly **10× lower** than GEO, making LEO suitable for latency-sensitive applications (VoIP, gaming, financial trading) where GEO is unsuitable.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** A LEO satellite constellation is designed for global coverage. The orbital altitude is 1500 km. Calculate (i) the orbital period, (ii) the maximum Earth central half-angle, (iii) the maximum slant range, and (iv) the theoretical minimum number of satellites required for full single-coverage. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain why the Iridium constellation uses 66 satellites in 6 polar planes of 11 satellites each, even though the theoretical minimum is far smaller. Discuss the role of inter-satellite links in this design. (7 marks)

##### **Model Solution**

**(a) Calculations for $h = 1500$ km — 7 marks**

- *Step 1 (1 mark):* $r = R_E + h = 6371 + 1500 = 7871$ km $= 7.871 \times 10^{6}$ m.

- *(i) Orbital period (2 marks):*

$$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{r^{3}}{\mu}} = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(7.871 \times 10^{6})^{3}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}}$$

$(7.871 \times 10^{6})^{3} = 4.876 \times 10^{20}$ m³

$$T = 2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{4.876 \times 10^{20}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}}} = 2 \pi \sqrt{1.2233 \times 10^{6}} = 2 \pi \times 1106.05$$

$$T = 6950 \text{ s} \approx 115.8 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ h } 56 \text{ min}$$

- *(ii) Earth central half-angle (1 mark):*

$$\cos \varepsilon = \frac{R_E}{R_E + h} = \frac{6371}{7871} = 0.8094$$

$$\varepsilon = \arccos(0.8094) = 35.97^{\circ} = 0.628 \text{ rad}$$

- *(iii) Maximum slant range (1 mark):*

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{h^{2} + 2 R_E h} = \sqrt{(1500)^{2} + 2 \times 6371 \times 1500}$$

$$= \sqrt{2.25 \times 10^{6} + 1.9113 \times 10^{7}} = \sqrt{2.1363 \times 10^{7}} = 4622 \text{ km}$$

- *(iv) Theoretical minimum number of satellites (2 marks):*

$$f = \frac{1 - \cos \varepsilon}{2} = \frac{1 - 0.8094}{2} = 0.0953$$

$$N_{min} = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{0.0953} \approx 10.5 \;\Rightarrow\; 11 \text{ satellites (theoretical lower bound)}$$

**(b) Why Iridium uses 66 satellites — 7 marks**

- *Practical coverage (3 marks):* The theoretical $N_{min} = 11$ assumes satellites can be *ideally arranged* with full spherical coverage, but in reality (i) coverage must extend to the **minimum elevation angle $E_{min} = 8.2°$** for the user's antenna, which *reduces* the effective coverage area per satellite; (ii) the constellation must serve **high-inclination / polar users** that demand denser coverage at the poles; (iii) at least one or two in-orbit **spares** are needed for failed satellites; and (iv) **seam coverage** between adjacent planes must overlap. The empirical count for Iridium became 6 planes × 11 satellites = **66 satellites**, providing ~3–4× redundancy over the theoretical minimum.

- *Inter-satellite links (4 marks):* Each Iridium satellite carries **four Ka-band ISLs** — two to the satellite *ahead* and *behind* in the same orbital plane, and two to satellites in *adjacent* planes. These ISLs allow traffic to be routed *entirely in space*, hopping from satellite to satellite until the packet reaches the satellite currently over the destination gateway. This (i) reduces the number of ground gateways required, (ii) lowers the end-to-end delay by avoiding multiple Earth-bound hops, and (iii) makes the system resilient to gateway outages, since any one of many gateways can serve as the egress point.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places KTU students lose marks on LEO questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Kepler's formula** — students plug $r$ in km instead of metres and then *forget* to convert, producing an answer that is off by a factor of 1000³. Always convert $r$ to **metres** before substituting into $T = 2\pi \sqrt{r^{3}/\mu}$ because $\mu$ is in **m³/s²**.
> 2. **Using GEO formulas for LEO** — applying $h = 35\,786$ km or $T = 24$ h to a LEO problem; this is an instant 0.
> 3. **Forgetting the "theoretical" qualifier** in $N_{min} = 1/f$. The formula gives the *geometric lower bound*; the *practical* number is 3–4× higher. Examiners deduct 1 mark if the qualifier is missing.
> 4. **Confusing satellite handover with cellular handover.** In a LEO system, *both* occur (the user may also be moving in a car), but the **satellite handover is the dominant event**, and students who omit it lose 2 marks.
> 5. **No diagram = −1 to −2 marks** in any 14-mark architecture question. Always draw the block diagram even if it is rough.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Altitude band of LEO:** 500 – 2000 km. **Orbital period:** 90 – 120 min. **Orbital speed:** ~7.5 km/s.
- **Kepler's third law:** $T = 2\pi \sqrt{r^{3}/\mu}$, with $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s² and $R_E = 6371$ km. Always use **metres** inside the radical.
- **Footprint half-angle:** $\cos \varepsilon = R_E / (R_E + h)$. At $h = 1000$ km, $\varepsilon \approx 30.3°$, giving a footprint diameter of ~6735 km.
- **Coverage fraction per satellite:** $f = (1 - \cos \varepsilon)/2$. The theoretical minimum number of satellites is $N_{min} = 1/f$; practical constellations use 3–4× this.
- **Max slant range:** $d_{max} = \sqrt{h^{2} + 2 R_E h}$. For $h = 780$ km, $d_{max} \approx 3248$ km, giving one-way delay ≈ 10.83 ms and round-trip ≈ 21.66 ms.
- **Latency advantage:** LEO's one-way delay is **~10–25 ms** vs. GEO's **~250 ms** — about an order of magnitude lower.
- **Handover:** LEO requires *continuous* satellite handover every 5–10 minutes; this is the single most distinctive operational feature.
- **Constellation size:** Iridium = 66 (6 × 11), Globalstar = 48, Starlink = thousands.
- **Inter-satellite links (ISLs):** Enable in-space routing; reduce gateway dependence and end-to-end delay.
- **KTU 2024 outcome mapping:** This topic primarily serves **CO3** — *"Analyse the architecture of wireless and mobile communication systems including satellite constellations."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medium Earth Orbit (MEO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Medium Earth Orbit (MEO)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 MODULE 3 — MEDIUM EARTH ORBIT (MEO) SATELLITES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> A **Medium Earth Orbit (MEO)** satellite is one that orbits the Earth at an altitude between **2,000 km and 35,786 km** above the Earth's mean sea level. MEO lies **above Low Earth Orbit (LEO)** and **below Geostationary Earth Orbit (GEO)**, occupying the "middle band" of non-geostationary orbits used for global mobile, navigation, and timing services.

The upper boundary of MEO is set by **35,786 km** because that is the precise altitude at which a circular orbit yields an orbital period equal to Earth's rotational period (24 hours) — this defines GEO. The lower boundary (~**2,000 km**) marks the practical edge above LEO's atmospheric drag zone, above which satellites can remain aloft for years without significant orbital decay.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Lanes" Intuition

Imagine the space around Earth as three concentric circular highways stacked vertically:

- **Inner Lane (LEO, 160–2,000 km):** Fast, low, small cars. Many needed (hundreds) for full coverage, but quick signal travel.
- **Middle Lane (MEO, 2,000–35,786 km):** Commercial flights. Fewer planes needed (around 24–30), broader visibility, slightly slower signal.
- **Outer Lane (GEO, 35,786 km):** A parked satellite hovering above one spot. Only three satellites cover the entire planet, but signals travel slowly.

> **MEO is the sweet spot used by Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSS)** — systems like **GPS (USA, ~20,200 km)**, **GLONASS (Russia, ~19,100 km)**, **Galileo (EU, ~23,222 km)**, and **BeiDou (China, ~21,150 km)** — because it offers excellent Earth coverage with only a small constellation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants for MEO (Earth-Centered)**
> - Earth's Standard Gravitational Parameter: **μ = GM = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²**
> - Earth's Mean Radius: **R_E = 6,371 km**
> - Sidereal Day: **T_E = 86,164 s** (≈ 23 h 56 min)
> - Geostationary Altitude: **35,786 km** (above mean sea level)
> - Speed of Light (vacuum): **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**

---

## 2. Architectural & Operational Classifications of MEO

MEO satellites are further classified by their orbital geometry, which directly governs coverage behavior.

| MEO Sub-Class | Inclination | Coverage Strength | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Polar MEO** | i ≈ 90° | Uniform global, strong polar coverage | Earth observation, science |
| **Inclined MEO (Walker Delta)** | i = 50°–60° | Excellent mid-latitude + acceptable polar | GNSS (GPS, GLONASS, Galileo) |
| **Equatorial MEO** | i ≈ 0° | Concentrated near the equator | Regional communications |
| **Highly Elliptical (HEO/Molniya)** | e ≈ 0.7, i = 63.4° | Long dwell over high latitudes | Russia, Northern regions |

> [!TIP]
> **Walker Notation (KTU High-Yield):** Constellations are described as `i: t/p/f`, where `i` = inclination, `t` = total satellites, `p` = number of orbital planes, `f` = relative phasing between planes. Example: GPS = **55° : 24/6/1** (24 satellites, 6 planes, phase offset of 1).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 3. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 3.1 Orbital Mechanics Governing MEO

Every MEO satellite obeys **Kepler's Third Law of Planetary Motion**, which links orbital radius `r` to the orbital period `T`.

For a circular orbit around Earth (mass `M_E`), gravitational attraction provides the centripetal force:

$$\frac{G M_E m}{r^2} = \frac{m v^2}{r}$$

Solving for orbital period `T = 2πr/v` gives the canonical form:

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}$$

where `μ = G·M_E = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²` and `r = R_E + h` (Earth radius + altitude).

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Reasoning Behind the Period Formula

- A higher altitude means a **larger circle** to traverse.
- A higher altitude also means **weaker gravitational pull**, so the satellite moves **slower**.
- These two effects combine such that `T` grows roughly as `r^{3/2}` — a non-linear climb.

### 3.3 Other Useful MEO Quantities

The **orbital angular velocity** `ω` and **linear orbital speed** `v` are:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r^3}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{GM_E}{r}}$$

The **propagation delay** (one-way) between satellite and ground station:

$$t_{prop} = \frac{d}{c}$$

where `d` is the slant range.

The **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** in dB:

$$L_{FSPL}\ (dB) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

### 3.4 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Orbital Period | $T = 2\pi\sqrt{r^3/\mu}$ | seconds (s) | Kepler's Third Law |
| 2 | Orbital Radius | $r = R_E + h$ | km | h = altitude above MSL |
| 3 | Orbital Speed | $v = \sqrt{\mu/r}$ | m/s | Circular orbit |
| 4 | Angular Velocity | $\omega = \sqrt{\mu/r^3}$ | rad/s | Useful for tracking |
| 5 | One-way Delay | $t_{prop} = d/c$ | seconds | d = slant range |
| 6 | Max Slant Range | $d_{max} = \sqrt{r^2 - R_E^2}$ (when elevation = 0°) | km | Horizon geometry |
| 7 | FSPL | $32.45 + 20\log d + 20\log f$ | dB | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 8 | Walker Notation | `i : t/p/f` | — | i=inclination, t=total, p=planes, f=phase |
| 9 | Coverage Time (MEO GPS) | ~5 hrs per satellite | hours | Overlap with next pass |
| 10 | Doppler Shift | $\Delta f = f_c \cdot v_{rel}/c$ | Hz | Critical for GNSS receivers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU problems, the most frequently tested quantities are (1) orbital period `T`, (2) propagation delay `t_prop`, (3) slant range at minimum elevation, and (4) FSPL.

### 3.5 Engineering Significance of MEO

MEO occupies the **operational sweet spot** for global mobile and positioning systems because:

- **24–30 satellites** suffice for **continuous global coverage** (versus 50–800 for LEO, 3 for GEO).
- **High elevation angles** (typically > 30°) reduce multipath and atmospheric attenuation.
- **Lower launch cost** than GEO (less energy to reach MEO altitude).
- **Lower path loss** than GEO (~10–20 dB less), enabling smaller user terminals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 4. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

### 4.1 Worked Example 1: Orbital Period of a GPS Satellite

**Given:** GPS orbital altitude `h = 20,200 km`, Earth's radius `R_E = 6,371 km`, `μ = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s²`.

**Find:** Orbital period `T` in hours.

**Step 1 — Compute orbital radius `r`:**

$$r = R_E + h = 6371 + 20200 = 26571\ \text{km}$$

Convert to meters: $r = 2.6571 \times 10^7\ m$.

**Step 2 — Apply Kepler's Third Law:**

$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{r^3}{\mu}}$$

Compute $r^3$:

$$r^3 = (2.6571 \times 10^7)^3 = (2.6571)^3 \times 10^{21}$$

$$(2.6571)^3 = 2.6571 \times 2.6571 \times 2.6571$$

$$2.6571 \times 2.6571 = 7.0602$$

$$7.0602 \times 2.6571 = 18.761$$

So $r^3 = 18.761 \times 10^{21} = 1.8761 \times 10^{22}\ m^3$.

**Step 3 — Divide by μ:**

$$\frac{r^3}{\mu} = \frac{1.8761 \times 10^{22}}{3.986 \times 10^{14}} = 4.7067 \times 10^{7}\ s^2$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{4.7067 \times 10^7} = \sqrt{4.7067} \times 10^{3.5} = 2.1697 \times 3162.28 = 6860.2\ s$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 2π:**

$$T = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 6860.2 = 43,108\ s$$

**Step 6 — Convert to hours:**

$$T = \frac{43,108}{3600} \approx 11.97\ \text{hours} \approx 12\ \text{hours}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** GPS satellites complete one full orbit in **~11 hours 58 minutes**, meaning they pass over the same location **twice per sidereal day**. This is the origin of the famous "12-hour orbit" quote in GNSS textbooks.

---

### 4.2 Worked Example 2: Slant Range at Minimum Elevation Angle

**Given:** A MEO satellite at altitude `h = 20,200 km`. A ground station sets a minimum usable elevation angle `ε_min = 5°` above the horizon.

**Find:** The maximum slant range `d_max` and one-way propagation delay.

**Geometric Setup (Law of Cosines on the triangle Earth-center → satellite → ground station):**

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{r^2 - R_E^2 \cdot \cos^2(\varepsilon_{min})} - R_E \cdot \sin(\varepsilon_{min}) \times \frac{1}{1}$$

The clean textbook formula is:

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{r^2 - R_E^2 \cos^2\varepsilon} - R_E \sin\varepsilon$$

Wait — let's use the **standard textbook geometry** directly:

$$d^2 = R_E^2 + r^2 - 2 R_E r \cos\psi$$

where `ψ` is the Earth-central angle between the ground station and the satellite sub-point.

When elevation `ε = 5°`:

$$\psi_{max} = \arccos\left(\frac{R_E}{r} \cdot \cos\varepsilon_{min}\right)$$

Step-by-step:

- $\frac{R_E}{r} = \frac{6371}{26571} = 0.2398$
- $\cos(5°) = 0.9962$
- $0.2398 \times 0.9962 = 0.2388$
- $\psi_{max} = \arccos(0.2388) = 76.18°$

Convert to radians: $76.18° \times \pi/180 = 1.330\ rad$.

Apply law of cosines:

$$d_{max}^2 = (6371)^2 + (26571)^2 - 2(6371)(26571)\cos(76.18°)$$

$$\cos(76.18°) = 0.2388$$

$$d_{max}^2 = 40,588,641 + 706,019,241 - 2(169,228,941)(0.2388)$$

$$= 746,607,882 - 80,830,948 = 665,776,934\ km^2$$

$$d_{max} = \sqrt{665,776,934} \approx 25,803\ km$$

**One-way delay:**

$$t_{prop} = \frac{d_{max}}{c} = \frac{25,803 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.0860\ s \approx 86\ \text{ms}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Rounded Comparison:** A GEO satellite has one-way delay ~120 ms (at 5° elevation). MEO gives ~86 ms — the user-perceptible improvement in voice/data latency.

---

### 4.3 Worked Example 3: Free-Space Path Loss Comparison (MEO vs GEO)

**Given:** Carrier frequency `f_c = 1.575 GHz` (GPS L1 band).

**For MEO** (`d = 25,803 km`):

$$L_{MEO} = 32.45 + 20\log(25803) + 20\log(1575)$$

$$20\log(25803) = 20 \times 4.412 = 88.24\ dB$$

$$20\log(1575) = 20 \times 3.197 = 63.94\ dB$$

$$L_{MEO} = 32.45 + 88.24 + 63.94 = 184.63\ dB$$

**For GEO** (`d ≈ 40,000 km` at 5° elevation):

$$L_{GEO} = 32.45 + 20\log(40000) + 20\log(1575)$$

$$20\log(40000) = 20 \times 4.602 = 92.04\ dB$$

$$L_{GEO} = 32.45 + 92.04 + 63.94 = 188.43\ dB$$

**Difference:** $\Delta L \approx 3.8\ dB$ — small but meaningful for link-budget design.

---

### 4.4 Worked Example 4: Constellation Count for Global MEO Coverage

For continuous global coverage from MEO, the minimum number of satellites `N_min` is approximated by the area the Earth presents divided by each satellite's "footprint" area:

$$N_{min} \approx \frac{4\pi R_E^2}{\pi d_{max}^2 \cdot \cos^2(\varepsilon_{min})/(\dots)}$$

The simpler engineering rule of thumb, derived from dividing Earth's surface into spherical caps visible above the elevation mask, gives:

$$N_{min} \approx \frac{4}{\sin^2(\psi_{max}/2)}$$

With $\psi_{max} = 76.18°$:

$$\sin(38.09°) = 0.617$$

$$N_{min} \approx \frac{4}{0.617^2} = \frac{4}{0.3807} = 10.5 \rightarrow \text{at least 11 satellites}$$

But for **continuous quadruple coverage** (4 satellites visible at all times for 3D positioning), this grows to ~24 — exactly the GPS constellation size.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 5. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 5.1 MEO Position Within the Three-Tier Satellite Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Earth-Centered Orbits] --> B[LEO 160 to 2000 km]
    A --> C[MEO 2000 to 35786 km]
    A --> D[GEO 35786 km]

    B --> B1[ISS 400 km]
    B --> B2[Iridium 780 km]
    B --> B3[Starlink 550 km]

    C --> C1[GPS 20200 km]
    C --> C2[GLONASS 19100 km]
    C --> C3[Galileo 23222 km]
    C --> C4[BeiDou 21150 km]

    D --> D1[INMARSAT 35786 km]
    D --> D2[Direct TV 35786 km]
```

### 5.2 Walker Delta Constellation Geometry (GPS-like)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SpaceSegment[GPS Walker Delta Constellation 55 deg 24 slash 6 slash 1]
        P1[Plane 1 RAAN 0 deg]
        P2[Plane 2 RAAN 60 deg]
        P3[Plane 3 RAAN 120 deg]
        P4[Plane 4 RAAN 180 deg]
        P5[Plane 5 RAAN 240 deg]
        P6[Plane 6 RAAN 300 deg]
    end

    E[Earth R_E 6371 km] --> SpaceSegment

    P1 --> S1A[S1 0 deg phase]
    P1 --> S1B[S2 90 deg phase]
    P1 --> S1C[S3 180 deg phase]
    P1 --> S1D[S4 270 deg phase]

    P2 --> S2A[S1 offset 60 deg]
    P2 --> S2B[S2 offset 60 deg]
    P2 --> S2C[S3 offset 60 deg]
    P2 --> S2D[S4 offset 60 deg]

    P3 --> S3A[S1 offset 120 deg]
    P4 --> S4A[S1 offset 180 deg]
    P5 --> S5A[S1 offset 240 deg]
    P6 --> S6A[S1 offset 300 deg]

    Ground[Ground User min elevation 5 deg] --> Visibility
    Visibility{At least 4 sats visible}
```

### 5.3 Functional Block Architecture: MEO GNSS Receiver Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[MEO Satellite Constellation 24 to 30 sats] --> A2[L Band Downlink 1.1 to 1.6 GHz]
    A2 --> A3[Receiver Antenna Omni L1 L2 L5]
    A3 --> A4[LNA + BPF]
    A4 --> A5[Downconverter to IF]
    A5 --> A6[ADC Sampling]
    A6 --> A7[Acquisition PRN Code Search]
    A7 --> A8[Tracking DLL plus PLL]
    A8 --> A9[Navigation Bit Decode]
    A9 --> A10[Pseudorange Computation]
    A9 --> A11[Doppler Extraction]
    A10 --> A12[Position Velocity Time Solver]
    A11 --> A12
    A12 --> A13[User Position ECEF lat lon alt]
```

### 5.4 MEO Link-Budget Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    TX[Satellite EIRP dBW] --> FreeSpaceLoss[FSPL 32.45 plus 20 log d plus 20 log f]
    FreeSpaceLoss --> OtherLosses[Atmospheric plus Rain plus Scintillation]
    OtherLosses --> RXAntenna[Receiver G/T dB per K]
    RXAntenna --> CN0[Carrier to Noise C/N0]
    CN0 --> BER[BER plus Link Margin]
    BER --> Decision[Coverage Decision]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 6. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define Medium Earth Orbit (MEO). State its altitude range and give two examples of MEO satellite systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Medium Earth Orbit (MEO) satellite orbits the Earth at an altitude between **2,000 km and 35,786 km** above the mean sea level — the band between LEO and GEO. The upper limit corresponds to the geostationary altitude.

Two examples:
1. **GPS (NAVSTAR)** — 24 satellites at ~20,200 km altitude in 55° inclined planes.
2. **Galileo (EU)** — 30 satellites at ~23,222 km in 56° inclined planes.

(1 mark – altitude range, 1 mark – definition, 1 mark – examples.) ✅

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**A MEO satellite is at altitude 20,200 km. Compute the approximate orbital period using Kepler's Third Law. Use μ = 3.986 × 10¹⁴ m³/s².**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$r = R_E + h = 6371 + 20200 = 26571\ km = 2.6571 \times 10^7\ m$$

$$T = 2\pi\sqrt{r^3/\mu} = 2\pi\sqrt{(2.6571 \times 10^7)^3 / 3.986 \times 10^{14}}$$

$$T = 2\pi \times 6860 \approx 43,108\ s \approx 11.97\ h$$

**∴ Orbital period ≈ 12 hours.** (1 mark each: r computation, formula setup, final answer.)

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain in detail the architecture of the GPS MEO constellation, including orbital parameters, Walker notation, and reasons for choosing 55° inclination. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A MEO satellite at altitude 23,222 km operates at carrier frequency 1.575 GHz. Compute (i) the orbital period, (ii) the maximum slant range at 5° elevation, (iii) the free-space path loss, and (iv) the one-way propagation delay. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**(a) GPS Constellation Architecture (7 marks):**

- **Altitude:** ~20,200 km (semi-major axis = 26,560 km). **(0.5 mark)**
- **Walker Notation:** `55° : 24/6/1` — meaning 24 satellites in 6 orbital planes, inclination 55°, phase parameter 1. **(1 mark)**
- **Plane Spacing:** 60° apart in Right Ascension of Ascending Node (RAAN). **(0.5 mark)**
- **Satellites per Plane:** 4 satellites, evenly spaced 90° apart in argument of latitude. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Orbital Period:** ~11 h 58 min (half a sidereal day) → each satellite traces the same ground track twice per day. **(1 mark)**

**Why 55° inclination (1.5 marks):**
- Optimizes coverage over the **densest population belts** (mid-latitudes 30°–60° N/S).
- Avoids polar overcrowding (90° inclination would waste capacity over Antarctica/Arctic).
- Ensures **≥ 4 satellites visible above 5° elevation** at any time anywhere on Earth.
- Compromise between equatorial focus (low i) and polar uniformity (high i).

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Orbital Period (2 marks):**
- $r = 6371 + 23222 = 29593\ km = 2.9593 \times 10^7\ m$
- $r^3 = (2.9593)^3 \times 10^{21} = 25.91 \times 10^{21} = 2.591 \times 10^{22}$
- $r^3 / \mu = 2.591 \times 10^{22} / 3.986 \times 10^{14} = 6.499 \times 10^{7}$
- $\sqrt{} = 8062\ s$
- $T = 2\pi \times 8062 = 50,651\ s \approx 14.07\ hours$

**(ii) Max Slant Range at 5° elevation (2 marks):**
- $\cos\varepsilon = 0.9962$, $R_E/r = 6371/29593 = 0.2153$
- $R_E \cos\varepsilon / r = 0.2145$
- $\psi_{max} = \arccos(0.2145) = 77.62°$
- Using law of cosines: $d^2 = R_E^2 + r^2 - 2R_E r \cos\psi$
- $d^2 = 40.59 \times 10^6 + 875.74 \times 10^6 - 2 \times 6371 \times 29593 \times 0.2145$
- $d^2 = 916.33 \times 10^6 - 80.92 \times 10^6 = 835.41 \times 10^6\ km^2$
- $d_{max} \approx 28,903\ km$

**(iii) FSPL at 1.575 GHz (1.5 marks):**
- $L = 32.45 + 20\log(28903) + 20\log(1575)$
- $20\log(28903) = 89.22\ dB$
- $20\log(1575) = 63.94\ dB$
- $L = 32.45 + 89.22 + 63.94 = 185.61\ dB$

**(iv) One-way Delay (1.5 marks):**
- $t_{prop} = d/c = 28903 \times 10^3 / 3 \times 10^8 = 0.0963\ s \approx 96.3\ ms$

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Module Choice

**(a)** Compare LEO, MEO, and GEO satellite systems across **altitude, orbital period, number of satellites needed, propagation delay, path loss, and typical applications.** Present in tabular form with at least six parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Kepler's Third Law from Newton's Law of Gravitation for a circular satellite orbit. Explain why the period increases with orbital radius. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1 per row + 1 for synthesis):**

| Parameter | LEO | MEO | GEO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Altitude | 160–2,000 km | 2,000–35,786 km | 35,786 km |
| Orbital Period | 90–120 min | 2–24 h | 24 h (sidereal) |
| Satellites for Global Coverage | 50–800 | 10–30 | 3 |
| One-way Delay | 5–20 ms | 50–100 ms | ~240 ms |
| FSPL (at 1.5 GHz) | ~150–165 dB | ~180–190 dB | ~190–200 dB |
| Handoffs | Frequent (every 10 min) | Moderate (~hourly) | None (stationary) |
| Applications | IoT, imaging, broadband | GNSS, mobile data | TV, weather, fixed telecom |

**(b) Kepler's Third Law Derivation (7 marks):**

- Equate gravitational force to centripetal force for circular orbit: **(1 mark)**

$$\frac{GM_E m}{r^2} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$

- Cancel `m` and one `r`: **(1 mark)**

$$\frac{GM_E}{r} = v^2$$

- Substitute $v = 2\pi r / T$: **(1 mark)**

$$\frac{GM_E}{r} = \left(\frac{2\pi r}{T}\right)^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^2}{T^2}$$

- Rearrange: **(1 mark)**

$$T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{GM_E}$$

- Define $\mu = GM_E$: **(0.5 mark)**

$$\boxed{T^2 = \frac{4\pi^2 r^3}{\mu}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = 2\pi\sqrt{r^3/\mu}$$

**Why T increases with r (2.5 marks):**
- A satellite at higher altitude traverses a **larger circumference** (`2πr`).
- At higher altitude, gravity is **weaker** ($F \propto 1/r^2$), so the satellite moves **slower**.
- Since `T = (distance)/speed`, both effects make `T` grow — but distance grows linearly while speed decreases as $1/\sqrt{r}$. Net result: `T ∝ r^{3/2}`.
- Numerical check: doubling r multiplies T by $2^{3/2} \approx 2.83$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting Earth Radius** — Students often use `h` directly as `r` in Kepler's formula. Always: $r = R_E + h = 6371 + h\ (km)$. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 2. **Unit Mismatch in μ** — `μ = 3.986 × 10¹⁴` requires `r` in **meters**, not km. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Converting Period Incorrectly** — Always convert `T` to hours by dividing by 3600. **(Lose 0.5 mark)**
> 4. **Walker Notation Order** — Format is `i : t/p/f`. Reversing gives wrong marks in descriptive answers.
> 5. **Confusing LEO with MEO Applications** — MEO is dominated by **GNSS**, not broadband. Examiners penalize misclassification.

---

### 7. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MEO**
> - **Altitude band:** 2,000 km – 35,786 km (above LEO, below GEO). ✅
> - **Canonical formula:** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{r^3/\mu}$, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}\ m^3/s^2$. ✅
> - **GPS period:** ~12 hours (twice per sidereal day). ✅
> - **Walker notation:** `i : t/p/f`. GPS = `55° : 24/6/1`. ✅
> - **GNSS examples:** GPS (20,200 km), GLONASS (19,100 km), Galileo (23,222 km), BeiDou (21,150 km). ✅
> - **One-way delay:** ~50–100 ms (between LEO's 5–20 ms and GEO's 240 ms). ✅
> - **FSPL rule:** Add 32.45 + 20log d + 20log f (d in km, f in MHz). ✅
> - **Slant range formula:** $d^2 = R_E^2 + r^2 - 2R_E r \cos\psi$, where $\psi$ = Earth-central angle. ✅
> - **Minimum visibility:** ≥ 4 satellites above 5° elevation for 3D positioning. ✅
> - **Inclination 55°:** Optimal for population belts, balances polar and equatorial coverage. ✅
> - **Doppler shift:** $\Delta f = f_c v_{rel}/c$ — critical for GNSS receiver acquisition. ✅
> - **FSPL difference vs GEO:** ~4 dB lower path loss than GEO at L-band. ✅

> **Final Tip:** In KTU ESE, expect at least one Part-A 3-mark question on MEO altitude + an example, and a Part-B 7+7 mark question pairing constellation architecture with one numerical computation (period or slant range).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Routing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Routing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Routing in Mobile & Wireless Networks?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Routing** is the process of selecting a path (or route) for traffic across one or more networks, ensuring that data packets travel efficiently, reliably, and correctly from a **source node** to a **destination node** through a sequence of intermediate nodes (routers/hosts).

In **Mobile Ad Hoc Networks (MANETs)** — which form the heart of this KTU Module 3 — routing becomes extraordinarily challenging because **no fixed infrastructure exists**. Every node is simultaneously a host and a router, the topology changes constantly, and links can be asymmetric, lossy, or temporarily broken.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Blind Postal Service"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like this:** Imagine a city where the streets themselves are shifting, where post offices frequently change locations, and where every citizen carrying a letter must also help deliver other citizens' letters. There are no fixed postal addresses. To send a letter, you hand it to a neighbor who happens to know the next best hop toward the destination. **Routing in MANETs is exactly this.** Each node must dynamically discover "who is my next reliable neighbor toward the destination?" — and keep updating this answer every few seconds.

## 1.2 Why Traditional Routing Fails in MANETs

Traditional Internet routing protocols (RIP, OSPF, BGP) assume:

1. A **stable, hierarchical topology**.
2. **Low mobility** and infrequent topology changes.
3. **Trusted, fixed infrastructure**.

In MANETs, all three assumptions collapse. Therefore, MANETs demand **entirely new routing protocols** built specifically for mobility, energy constraints, and unreliable wireless links.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 (Routing) focuses exclusively on **ad hoc, mobile, and dynamic routing protocols** — including **DSDV, DSR, AODV, TORA, ZRP**, plus **cluster-based** and **location-based** routing.

## 1.3 Core Routing Challenges in Mobile Ad Hoc Networks

| # | Challenge | Description |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Mobility** | Nodes move → links break/appear dynamically |
| 2 | **Bandwidth Limitation** | Wireless channel is shared and scarce (**typically 1–54 Mbps**) |
| 3 | **Energy Constraints** | Battery-powered devices → limited lifetime |
| 4 | **Error-Prone Channel** | High bit error rate (**BER ≈ 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁵**), fading, interference |
| 5 | **Hidden & Exposed Terminal Problem** | Wireless collisions due to radio range |
| 6 | **No Central Authority** | Distributed decision-making is mandatory |
| 7 | **Scalability** | Flat routing tables grow O(N²) with N nodes |
| 8 | **Security** | Open medium → vulnerable to spoofing, blackhole, wormhole attacks |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of MANET Routing Protocols

The single most-tested diagram on this KTU Module is the **classification of routing protocols**. Every classification axis below is a guaranteed KTU Part-A (3-mark) or Part-B (7-mark) question.

### A. By **Routing Information Update Strategy** (The Main KTU Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three families** dominate the KTU syllabus:
> 1. **Proactive (Table-Driven)**
> 2. **Reactive (On-Demand)**
> 3. **Hybrid**

---

### B. Proactive (Table-Driven) Routing Protocols

**Operating Principle:** Every node **continuously maintains a routing table** with routes to **every other node**, regardless of whether traffic is required or not. Tables are updated periodically or whenever topology changes occur.

**Analogy:** Like a taxi driver who constantly consults a live GPS map for the *entire* city, even when no passenger is inside.

**Sub-Protocols in KTU Syllabus:**
- **DSDV** (Destination-Sequenced Distance Vector) — based on Bellman-Ford
- **OLSR** (Optimized Link State Routing) — based on Link State

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Routes are immediately available → **low latency** for the first packet.
> **Disadvantage:** Heavy control overhead → wastes bandwidth and battery.

---

### C. Reactive (On-Demand) Routing Protocols

**Operating Principle:** Routes are computed **only when a source node demands** a path to a destination. Two phases:
1. **Route Discovery** — flood the network with a `RREQ` (Route Request)
2. **Route Maintenance** — monitor the active path; recompute on failure

**Analogy:** Like asking directions **only when you are actually lost**, not carrying an entire city map pre-loaded.

**Sub-Protocols in KTU Syllabus:**
- **DSR** (Dynamic Source Routing)
- **AODV** (Ad hoc On-Demand Distance Vector)
- **TORA** (Temporally Ordered Routing Algorithm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage:** Low overhead when traffic is sparse.
> **Disadvantage:** High latency for the *first* packet due to route discovery.

---

### D. Hybrid Routing Protocols

**Operating Principle:** Combines proactive behavior locally + reactive behavior globally. The network is divided into **zones** (or clusters).

**Key Protocol:** **ZRP** (Zone Routing Protocol) — proactive intra-zone, reactive inter-zone.

---

### E. Other Classification Axes (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Axis | Categories |
|------|------------|
| **Network Topology** | Flat routing, Hierarchical routing, Cluster-based routing, Geographic (location-based) routing |
| **Communication Model** | Unicast, Broadcast, Multicast, Geocast |
| **Cast Type** | Single-path, Multi-path |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are tested in KTU numericals and short-answer questions.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Meaning |
|------------------|-----------|---------|
| **Routing Overhead** | $O_{routing} = \dfrac{N_{ctrl}}{N_{total}}$ | Ratio of control packets to total packets transmitted |
| **End-to-End Delay** | $D_{e2e} = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{prop}^{i} + T_{trans}^{i} + T_{queue}^{i})$ | Sum of propagation, transmission, and queueing delays across $n$ hops |
| **Throughput** | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{delivered}}{T_{total}}$ (bits/sec) | Successful packets delivered per unit time |
| **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $PDR = \dfrac{P_{recv}}{P_{sent}} \times 100\%$ | Reliability metric |
| **Path Optimality** | $\rho = \dfrac{L_{shortest}}{L_{actual}}$ | Ratio of shortest path length to actual path length (≤ 1) |
| **Bellman-Ford Update (DSDV base)** | $D_{x}^{y} = \min\{c(x,v) + D_{v}^{y}\}$ for all neighbors $v$ | Distance vector update rule |
| **Route Stability** | $S_{route} = \dfrac{T_{valid}}{T_{active}}$ | Fraction of time the route remained usable |
| **Hop Count Limit** | $TTL_{max} = N - 1$ (for $N$ nodes) | Maximum possible hops in a network of $N$ nodes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Alert:** All absolute values use `\vert` not `|` to preserve the markdown table. For example, $D_{x}^{y} = \min\{\vert c(x,v) + D_{v}^{y} \vert\}$ is rendered using `\vert` in the source.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of MANET Routing |
|--------|------------------------------|
| **Military (Battlefield)** | Tactical networks — soldiers, vehicles, drones forming ad hoc mesh |
| **Disaster Recovery** | When cell towers collapse after earthquakes/tsunamis |
| **Vehicular Ad Hoc Networks (VANETs)** | Car-to-car communication for safety/autonomous driving |
| **IoT / Smart Cities** | Low-power wireless sensor mesh routing (RPL, 6LoWPAN) |
| **Underwater / Space Networks** | Extreme environments with no infrastructure |
| **Pandemic-era Temporary Hospitals** | Rapid deployment of wireless patient monitoring |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DSDV — Destination Sequenced Distance Vector (Proactive)

### 3.1.1 Theoretical Foundation

DSDV is based on the classical **Bellman-Ford algorithm**, augmented with a **sequence number** assigned by the destination to **eliminate routing loops** and **prevent count-to-infinity problems**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Most-Asked Algorithm:** DSDV, AODV, and DSR are the "big three" for 14-mark Part-B questions.

### 3.1.2 Data Structures Maintained at Each Node

1. **Routing Table Entry** for every destination:
   - Destination address
   - Next hop
   - Metric (hop count)
   - Sequence number (assigned by destination)
   - Install time
2. **Settling Time** — used to suppress fluctuations before advertising a new route.

### 3.1.3 Operational Steps

**Step 1 — Initialization**
Each node creates a routing entry for itself with **sequence number = 0** (even) and metric = 0.

**Step 2 — Periodic Update (Full Dump)**
Every node broadcasts its **entire routing table** to all neighbors every $T_{period}$ seconds (typically **15 seconds**).

**Step 3 — Triggered Update (Incremental)**
If the table changes substantially, the node broadcasts **only the changed entries** (smaller packet).

**Step 4 — Sequence Number Logic**
- Destination node always advertises a route with an **even sequence number**.
- On detecting a broken link, the destination increments the sequence number to an **odd number** to signal route invalidity.
- Receiving node updates its table **only if** the incoming sequence number is **greater** than the one stored.

### 3.1.4 DSDV Update Rule — Exhaustive Derivation

Let:
- $N_i$ = node $i$
- $D_x^{y}$ = distance from node $x$ to destination $y$
- $c(x, v)$ = cost (typically 1 hop) of link from $x$ to neighbor $v$
- $S_y$ = current sequence number for destination $y$

When node $x$ receives an update from neighbor $v$ advertising distance $D_v^{y}$ with sequence number $S_y^{new}$:

$$
D_x^{y} = 
\begin{cases}
\min_{v \in \text{Neighbors}(x)}\{ c(x,v) + D_v^{y}\}, & \text{if } S_y^{new} > S_y^{old} \text{ (fresh route)} \\
\min\{ D_x^{y,old}, \; c(x,v) + D_v^{y}\}, & \text{if } S_y^{new} = S_y^{old} \text{ and new metric is better} \\
\text{mark unreachable}, & \text{if odd sequence received (broken link)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**

Consider the 4-node MANET:
- Links: A–B, B–C, C–D, A–D
- Each link cost = 1 hop.

Initially, every node's table has itself at distance 0, seq=0.

**Iteration 1 (after first periodic broadcast):**
- Node A receives B's table: B knows path to C (1 hop) and to D (2 hops). 
- A computes its table as:

$$
D_A^{B} = 1,\quad D_A^{C} = 2,\quad D_A^{D} = 1
$$

All with sequence number = 0 (fresh).

**Iteration 2 (link B–C breaks):**
- Node B detects broken link → advertises B→C with **odd seq = 1** (invalid).
- Node A notices that A's stored path A→C was via B. A must now check for alternative routes.
- A can still reach C via D? Let's verify: A→D (1) → D→C (1) = 2 hops.
- A updates: $D_A^{C} = 2$ via next-hop = D, seq remains even.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because sequence numbers come from the **destination**, and any new route gets a **higher even number**, routing loops are mathematically impossible — this is the elegant guarantee of DSDV.

---

## 3.2 DSR — Dynamic Source Routing (Reactive)

### 3.2.1 Core Innovation

Unlike DSDV where each hop decides independently, DSR includes the **entire route** in **every packet's header**. This is called **source routing**.

### 3.2.2 Two-Phase Operation

**Phase 1 — Route Discovery (RREQ Flooding)**

1. Source node $S$ wants to reach destination $D$.
2. $S$ broadcasts a **Route Request (RREQ)** containing: [Source ID, Destination ID, Unique Request ID, Route Record = empty].
3. Every intermediate node appends its own ID to the Route Record before rebroadcasting.
4. When $D$ receives RREQ, it sends back a **Route Reply (RREP)** containing the **complete path** $S \to n_1 \to n_2 \to \dots \to D$.
5. RREP travels back along the **reverse path** (unidirectional links need a fresh RREQ).

**Phase 2 — Route Maintenance**

- While forwarding data, each node is responsible for confirming that the next hop successfully received the packet (e.g., using link-layer ACK or passive acknowledgment).
- On failure, the node sends a **Route Error (RERR)** back to the source.
- Source either: (i) uses an alternate cached route, or (ii) initiates fresh route discovery.

### 3.2.3 DSR Route Cache

Every node maintains a **Route Cache** — a database of learned routes. On receiving an RREQ, if a node already has a path to the destination, it can reply immediately without forwarding the RREQ further → reduces overhead.

---

## 3.3 AODV — Ad hoc On-Demand Distance Vector (Reactive)

### 3.3.1 Hybrid Heritage

AODV is the **practical fusion** of DSR's on-demand discovery + DSDV's sequence-number mechanism. It is the **most-deployed reactive protocol** in real MANETs and the **single most-tested reactive protocol in KTU exams**.

### 3.3.2 Data Structures

1. **Routing Table** at each node — only entries for **active destinations**.
2. **Sequence Numbers** — per destination, set & incremented only by the destination.

### 3.3.3 AODV Operational Flow

**Step 1:** Source $S$ increments its own sequence number and broadcasts a **RREQ** with: [Source Address, Source Sequence #, Broadcast ID, Destination Address, Destination Sequence #, Hop Count = 0].

**Step 2:** Intermediate node $n$ on first receiving the RREQ:
- Sets up a **reverse path** entry back to $S$ (for forwarding RREP later).
- Increments hop count and rebroadcasts if it has no fresher route.

**Step 3:** When RREQ reaches destination $D$ (or any node with a fresh enough route):
- $D$ unicasts a **RREP** back along the reverse path.
- RREP contains $D$'s current sequence number and hop count = 0.

**Step 4:** Intermediate nodes forwarding RREP:
- Set up a **forward path** entry toward $D$.
- Increment hop count.

**Step 5:** Source $S$ receives RREP → installs route → begins data transmission.

### 3.3.4 AODV Route Maintenance — RERR

If a link breaks, the upstream node:
1. Marks all affected destinations as invalid.
2. Increments their sequence numbers by 1.
3. Broadcasts a **Route Error (RERR)** to all active precursors (neighbors using these routes).

### 3.3.5 Detailed Comparison Matrix (KTU Favourite Table)

| Parameter | **DSDV** | **DSR** | **AODV** |
|-----------|----------|---------|----------|
| Type | Proactive | Reactive | Reactive |
| Algorithm Basis | Bellman-Ford (DV) | Source Routing | DV + Sequence Numbers |
| Route Stored In | Each node's table | Packet header + cache | Each node's table |
| Route Discovery | Never (always known) | On-demand RREQ | On-demand RREQ |
| Sequence Numbers | Yes (per destination) | No | Yes (per destination) |
| Loop-Free | Yes | Yes (header-based) | Yes (sequence-based) |
| Multiple Routes | No | Yes (cache) | No |
| Routing Overhead | High (periodic) | Low | Low |
| Latency for 1st Packet | Low (instant) | High | High |
| Scalability | Poor (O(N²) tables) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Energy Efficiency | Low | High | High |
| Suitable Topology | Small, low mobility | Small, low traffic | Medium, moderate mobility |

---

## 3.4 TORA — Temporally Ordered Routing Algorithm

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** TORA is reactive + provides **multiple loop-free paths** using a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** rooted at the destination.

### TORA Phases

1. **Route Creation** — uses the **"height" metric** (relative to destination). Each node picks a higher "height" than its upstream neighbor toward the destination.
2. **Route Maintenance** — uses **QRY/CLR** messages in a controlled localized manner (does **not** flood the entire network).
3. **Route Erasure** — invalid routes are erased using **broadcast** of CLR with null height.

---

## 3.5 ZRP — Zone Routing Protocol (Hybrid)

### Zone Concept

For every node $N$, the **zone radius $r$** (in hops) defines the local zone of $N$.

- **Intra-zone routing** → uses a **proactive** protocol (typically intra-zone routing protocol = IARP).
- **Inter-zone routing** → uses a **reactive** protocol (IARP).

### Operation

1. Node $S$ wants to reach $D$.
2. If $D$ is **within $S$'s zone (≤ r hops)** → proactive table already has the route → **no discovery needed**.
3. If $D$ is **outside** → $S$ uses **bordercasting** — sends RREQ only to peripheral nodes (zone boundary), not entire network.
4. Border nodes recursively check: is $D$ in *my* zone? If yes, reply directly; if no, bordercast again.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tunable Parameter:** Choosing $r$ optimally balances proactive overhead vs reactive latency. Small $r$ → behaves reactively. Large $r$ → behaves proactively.

---

## 3.6 Location-Based Routing

### 3.6.1 Concept

Each node knows **its own geographic position** (via GPS) and the **position of the destination** (via a location service). No routing tables.

### 3.6.2 Greedy Perimeter Stateless Routing (GPSR)

**Two modes:**

1. **Greedy Forwarding Mode** — node forwards packet to the neighbor **closest to the destination** (Euclidean distance).

$$
n_{next} = \arg\min_{v \in \text{Neighbors}(x)} \vert \text{dist}(v, D) \vert
$$

2. **Perimeter Mode (Recovery)** — if greedy fails (local maximum / void), switches to **right-hand rule** traversal of the planar graph (Gabriel Graph or Relative Neighborhood Graph) to "walk around" the void.

> [!WARNING]
> **Watch out:** Greedy routing can fail in a "void" (no neighbor is closer to destination) — perimeter mode is the recovery mechanism. This is a guaranteed KTU short-answer question.

---

## 3.7 Cluster-Based Routing

### 3.7.1 Architecture

- Network is divided into **clusters**.
- Each cluster has:
  - **Cluster Head (CH)** — coordinates intra-cluster routing
  - **Gateway nodes** — connect to other clusters
  - **Ordinary members** — communicate via CH

### 3.7.2 Advantages

- **Scalability** — routing tables only contain cluster heads, reducing from O(N) to O(√N) effectively.
- **Stability** — cluster membership can be maintained despite internal mobility.

**Protocols:** **LEACH** (Low-Energy Adaptive Clustering Hierarchy) — energy-aware CH rotation; **CBRP** (Cluster Based Routing Protocol).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MANET Routing Protocols] --> B[Proactive Table-Driven]
    A --> C[Reactive On-Demand]
    A --> D[Hybrid]
    A --> E[Hierarchical or Cluster-Based]
    A --> F[Geographic or Location-Based]

    B --> B1[DSDV]
    B --> B2[OLSR]
    B --> B3[WRP]

    C --> C1[DSR]
    C --> C2[AODV]
    C --> C3[TORA]
    C --> C4[ABR]

    D --> D1[ZRP]
    D --> D2[ZHLS]

    E --> E1[CBRP]
    E --> E2[LEACH]
    E --> E3[HSR]

    F --> F1[GPSR]
    F --> F2[LAR]
    F --> F3[DREAM]
```

## 4.2 DSDV Periodic Update Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant N1 as Node 1
    participant N2 as Node 2
    participant N3 as Node 3
    participant N4 as Node 4 Destination

    Note over N1,N4: Every T_period seconds
    N1->>N2: Broadcast full routing table
    N2->>N1: Ack and update own table
    N1->>N3: Broadcast full routing table
    N3->>N1: Ack and update own table

    Note over N4: Topology change detected
    N4->>N2: Triggered update new seq even
    N2->>N1: Forward triggered update
    Note over N1: Updates only if new seq greater
```

## 4.3 AODV Route Discovery — Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Source Node
    participant A as Intermediate Node A
    participant B as Intermediate Node B
    participant D as Destination Node

    S->>A: RREQ broadcast hop count 0
    A->>B: RREQ rebroadcast hop count 1
    B->>D: RREQ rebroadcast hop count 2
    D-->>B: RREP unicast reverse path
    B-->>A: RREP forward reverse path
    A-->>S: RREP deliver to source
    Note over S: Install route and start sending data packets
    S->>A: DATA packet
    A->>B: DATA forward
    B->>D: DATA deliver
```

## 4.4 DSR Packet Header Structure (Source Routing Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[DSR Packet Header] --> Q[Source Address]
    P --> R[Destination Address]
    P --> S[Route Record Field]
    S --> S1[Hop 1 ID]
    S --> S2[Hop 2 ID]
    S --> S3[Hop 3 ID]
    S --> S4[Dot Dot Dot]
    S --> S5[Final Hop ID]
    P --> T[Payload]
```

## 4.5 Zone Routing Protocol (ZRP) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Source Node S] --> Z1[Zone Radius r equals 2 hops]
    Z1 --> I1[Intra-Zone Proactive IARP]
    Z1 --> I2[Inter-Zone Reactive IERP]

    I1 --> M1[Direct route if destination inside zone]
    I2 --> M2[Bordercasting to peripheral nodes]
    M2 --> M3[Check recursively at each border node]
    M3 --> M4[Reply if destination inside zone else re-bordercast]
```

## 4.6 GPSR Greedy vs Perimeter Mode Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Packet at Node X for Destination D] --> B{Is any neighbor closer to D than X?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Greedy Mode: forward to nearest neighbor]
    B -- No --> D[Local Maximum detected]
    D --> E[Switch to Perimeter Mode]
    E --> F[Traverse planar graph using right hand rule]
    F --> G{Recovered closer neighbor found?}
    G -- Yes --> C
    G -- No --> F
```

## 4.7 Cluster-Based Routing Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cluster1
        CH1[Cluster Head 1] --- M1a[Member A]
        CH1 --- M1b[Member B]
        CH1 --- M1c[Member C]
        GW1[Gateway 1]
    end

    subgraph Cluster2
        CH2[Cluster Head 2] --- M2a[Member D]
        CH2 --- M2b[Member E]
        GW2[Gateway 2]
    end

    GW1 --- GW2
    CH1 --- CH2
```

---
