# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define MANET. List any four challenges in routing for MANETs. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **Mobile Ad Hoc Network (MANET)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of mobile devices connected by wireless links, where nodes act as both hosts and routers.

**Four Routing Challenges:**
1. **Mobility** — frequently changing topology causing link breakages.
2. **Limited bandwidth** — shared, scarce wireless channel.
3. **Energy constraints** — battery-powered nodes.
4. **Absence of central authority** — distributed decision-making required.
*(Any four accepted)*

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between proactive and reactive routing protocols with one example each. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Proactive | Reactive |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| Route Availability | Always maintained | Computed on demand |
| Latency | Low | High for first packet |
| Overhead | High (periodic) | Low |
| Example | **DSDV** | **AODV** / DSR |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of routing protocols** in MANETs with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The classification is along three primary axes (covered in Section 4.1). The student should present:

1. **Proactive / Table-Driven** — maintain routes continuously. Examples: DSDV, OLSR.
2. **Reactive / On-Demand** — discover routes only when needed. Examples: DSR, AODV, TORA.
3. **Hybrid** — combine both. Example: ZRP.
4. Additionally classify by topology: **Flat, Hierarchical, Cluster-Based, Location-Based**.

Key points for full marks:
- Definition of each class (1 mark each × 3 = 3 marks)
- Suitable examples (1 mark)
- Comparison table of when each is preferred (2 marks)
- Neat flowchart / diagram (1 mark)

**(b)** Describe the **DSDV protocol** in detail. Explain how sequence numbers prevent routing loops with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**DSDV Description (4 marks):**
- Proactive protocol based on Bellman-Ford algorithm.
- Each node maintains a routing table with: Destination, Next Hop, Metric (hop count), Sequence Number, Install Time.
- Two types of updates: **Periodic (full dump)** and **Triggered (incremental)**.
- Uses **settling time** to dampen route fluctuations.

**Sequence Number Mechanism (3 marks):**
- Every destination advertises its own route with an **even sequence number**.
- A new route advertisement uses an incremented (still even) sequence number.
- A broken link is advertised with an **odd sequence number**, marking the route invalid.
- Receiving node updates its table **only if** the new sequence number is **greater than or equal to** the stored one (with tie-breaking by better metric).

**Example:**
- Initially, A→D advertised as [seq=0, hop=2].
- Link A–B breaks; B advertises A→D as [seq=1 (odd), hop=∞] → marked invalid.
- D is still reachable via B–C–D? Let A→C→D be [seq=2 (even), hop=2]. A accepts because seq=2 > stored seq=0.
- No loop possible since all updates originate from the destination itself with monotonically increasing sequence numbers.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **AODV protocol** in detail with route discovery and route maintenance phases. Compare AODV with DSR. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**AODV Description (4 marks):**
- **Reactive protocol** combining DSR's on-demand nature with DSDV's sequence numbers.
- **Three message types:** RREQ, RREP, RERR.
- **Route Discovery:** Source broadcasts RREQ with source seq #, broadcast ID, destination seq #. Reverse path is set up at each intermediate node. Destination (or node with fresh route) unicasts RREP back.
- **Route Maintenance:** On link failure, upstream node increments dest seq #, broadcasts RERR to active precursors.

**Comparison AODV vs DSR (3 marks):**

| Feature | AODV | DSR |
|---------|------|-----|
| Routing info | Stored in tables | Stored in packet header + cache |
| Sequence numbers | Yes | No |
| Multiple routes | No | Yes (from cache) |
| Overhead per packet | Lower (only destination) | Higher (entire path) |
| Scalability | Better for large nets | Better for small nets |

**(b)** Describe **ZRP** and **location-based (GPSR) routing** in MANETs. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ZRP — Zone Routing Protocol (4 marks):**
- Hybrid protocol using a configurable zone radius $r$ (in hops).
- **Intra-zone:** uses proactive protocol (IARP) — maintains routes within the zone.
- **Inter-zone:** uses reactive protocol (IERP) — initiates route discovery via **bordercasting** (only to peripheral nodes).
- **Trade-off:** Smaller $r$ → more reactive; Larger $r$ → more proactive.

**GPSR — Greedy Perimeter Stateless Routing (3 marks):**
- Location-based; requires **GPS** at each node and a **location service** for destinations.
- **Greedy mode:** forward to neighbor with minimum distance to destination using
$$n_{next} = \arg\min_{v \in \mathcal{N}(x)} \vert \text{dist}(v, D) \vert$$
- **Perimeter mode (recovery):** when greedy fails (local maximum), uses **right-hand rule** on a planarized graph (Gabriel Graph) to bypass the void.
- **Advantage:** O(1) routing table size at each node → highly scalable.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Routing Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing DSR and AODV** — both reactive but DSR uses *source routing in headers* while AODV uses *hop-by-hop table-based routing with sequence numbers*. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for mixing these.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention Sequence Numbers** when describing DSDV or AODV — losing **2 marks** easily.
> 3. **Writing "DSDV uses distance vector" without naming Bellman-Ford** — incomplete answer.
> 4. **Drawing the classification diagram without examples** — must include at least one protocol per category.
> 5. **Not stating why proactive protocols have higher overhead** — must mention *periodic updates even when no traffic*.
> 6. **Confusing "reactive" with "responsive to all traffic"** — clarify: reactive = computes route *only on demand*.
> 7. **Skipping the route maintenance phase** in 7-mark AODV/DSR questions — this is a separate 2-mark sub-topic.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision — Must Memorize for KTU Module 3:**

- ☐ **MANET** = infrastructure-less, mobile, multi-hop, self-configuring wireless network.
- ☐ **Three families:** Proactive (DSDV, OLSR), Reactive (DSR, AODV, TORA), Hybrid (ZRP).
- ☐ **DSDV** uses **Bellman-Ford + Sequence Numbers**; loops mathematically impossible.
- ☐ **Sequence Numbers:** even = valid route, odd = broken link; higher = fresher.
- ☐ **DSR** = **source routing** (entire path in packet header); uses **Route Cache**.
- ☐ **AODV** = DSDV's sequence numbers + DSR's on-demand discovery.
- ☐ **AODV messages:** RREQ (flood), RREP (unicast reverse), RERR (broadcast on failure).
- ☐ **TORA** = reactive + multi-path DAG; uses QRY/CLR messages.
- ☐ **ZRP** zone radius $r$ tunes proactivity vs reactivity; uses **bordercasting**.
- ☐ **GPSR** = Greedy forwarding + Perimeter mode recovery (right-hand rule).
- ☐ **Cluster-based** protocols use CH + Gateway + Members for scalability (LEACH, CBRP).
- ☐ **Location-based** protocols need GPS + location service.
- ☐ **Routing Overhead formula:** $O_{routing} = N_{ctrl}/N_{total}$.
- ☐ **PDR formula:** $PDR = P_{recv}/P_{sent} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **Greedy next-hop formula:** $n_{next} = \arg\min_{v \in \mathcal{N}(x)} \vert \text{dist}(v, D) \vert$.
- ☐ **Bellman-Ford update rule** is the foundation of DSDV.
- ☐ **Hidden terminal problem** is solved (partially) using RTS/CTS, not at routing layer.
- ☐ **Count-to-infinity problem** is prevented in DSDV by sequence numbers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Localization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Localization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Localization** is the process of determining the **physical/geographical position** of a mobile device, sensor node, or user equipment (UE) in a wireless network — typically expressed as a set of 2D coordinates $(x, y)$ or 3D coordinates $(x, y, z)$, sometimes augmented with orientation, velocity, and timestamp.

In the context of KTU's PECST633 syllabus, localization is studied as a **layered location-sensing problem** combining signal processing, geometric computation, statistical estimation, and radio propagation theory. The core objective is to convert one or more **measurable signal parameters** (time, angle, power, signature) emitted between a *target node* and a set of *reference nodes* (called **beacons, anchors, or base stations**) into a **position estimate** with quantifiable accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU cares about this topic:** The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) **Enhanced 911 (E-911) mandate** (USA, 1996) and equivalent ITU/3GPP location-services (LCS) specifications legally require that every emergency mobile call be locatable to within **50 m – 100 m**. Localization is therefore not just a research curiosity — it is a regulated, deployable, and commercially critical capability behind Google Maps, Uber, ride-hailing, fleet tracking, IoT asset tracking, AR/VR, and disaster response.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Lighthouse and the Blindfolded Sailor"

Imagine a sailor **blindfolded** on a dark sea, holding a stopwatch. There are **three lighthouses** at known fixed coordinates flashing synchronized light signals. The sailor hears/observes each flash and notes how long the light took to reach him.

- If he **knows the time** light took → he can compute **distance** (because light travels at known speed $c$).
- With **three distances** from three known points → he can **triangulate** his position as the unique intersection of three circles.
- Replace the lighthouses with **Wi-Fi access points**, **cellular BTS towers**, **BLE beacons**, or **GPS satellites**, and the stopwatch with an internal radio clock — that, in essence, is **wireless localization**.

The "blindfold" represents the fact that the device has **no inherent sense of its own coordinates** in the physical world; it must derive them entirely from radio observables.

### 1.3 Classification of Localization — A Taxonomy

Localization schemes can be classified along **four orthogonal axes** which examiners frequently test:

| Axis | Category A | Category B |
|---|---|---|
| **Where computation runs** | *Network-based* (location computed at server) | *Device-based / Handset-based* (computed at mobile) |
| **What is measured** | *Range-based* (ToA, TDoA, AoA, RSS) | *Range-free* (Cell-ID, fingerprinting, hop-count) |
| **Outdoor vs Indoor** | *Outdoor* (GPS, cellular E-OTD) | *Indoor* (Wi-Fi, BLE, UWB, RFID, VLC) |
| **Active vs Passive** | *Active* (device cooperates) | *Passive* (silently tracked, e.g., RFID tags) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU syllabus highlight:** Module 3 specifically stresses *ranging techniques* (ToA, TDoA, AoA, RSS-based) and *position estimation methods* (trilateration, triangulation, multilateration, fingerprinting). Students must be able to **distinguish measurement principle from estimation principle** — they are not the same thing.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics — The "Four Aces" of Localization

Every localization algorithm is graded against four standard metrics:

1. **Accuracy** — How close the estimate $\hat{p}$ is to the true position $p$. Expressed as **Mean Euclidean Error** in meters:
$$\text{Accuracy} = \sqrt{(x - \hat{x})^2 + (y - \hat{y})^2}$$
2. **Precision** — Repeatability of the estimate under repeated measurements (variance / standard deviation).
3. **Complexity** — Computational cost, energy consumption, hardware requirements.
4. **Scalability** — Performance degradation as the number of nodes increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** "Accuracy vs Precision" is the single most-asked conceptual confusion question in KTU viva and Part A. *Accuracy = closeness to truth (bias), Precision = closeness to itself (variance).* A dart-thrower hitting the same spot on the board 10 times has high **precision**; only if that spot is the bullseye does she have high **accuracy**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Localization Pipeline — Four Universal Stages

Regardless of the technique chosen (GPS, Wi-Fi, BLE, UWB, RFID), **every** localization system passes through these four stages:

> **Stage 1 — Signal Observation:** The target node and reference nodes exchange or passively receive radio signals; one or more *observables* are recorded.
> **Stage 2 — Ranging / Measurement:** A mathematical model converts raw observables (time delay, angle, power) into **distance estimates** $d_i$ (or angles $\theta_i$).
> **Stage 3 — Position Estimation:** Geometric/statistical algorithms (trilateration, triangulation, ML regression) combine the $d_i$ to yield $\hat{p} = (\hat{x}, \hat{y})$.
> **Stage 4 — Refinement & Filtering:** Recursive filters (Kalman, Particle, MMSE) smooth the estimate and improve robustness against NLOS, multipath, and clock drift.

## 2.2 Detailed Survey of Ranging Techniques

### A. Time of Arrival (ToA)

**Physical principle:** If signal propagation speed is known ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for RF), distance is simply:
$$d = c \cdot \Delta t$$
where $\Delta t$ is the one-way propagation time. **Critical requirement:** transmitter and receiver clocks must be **tightly synchronized** (sub-nanosecond for meter-level accuracy).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why clock sync is brutal:** A clock error of just **1 microsecond** produces a range error of $\approx 300$ m. This is why ToA is used with **GPS satellites** (carrying atomic clocks) but is impractical for plain Wi-Fi without Time-Synchronization protocols like PTP or FTSP.

**Two-way ToA (Round-Trip Time, RTT)** removes the sync requirement by measuring the *round-trip* time at the same node.

### B. Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA)

Instead of absolute times, **relative** time differences between arrivals at multiple base stations are measured. The locus of constant $\Delta t$ between two BSs is a **hyperbola** with the BSs as foci. Three BSs produce two hyperbolas → their intersection is the target's location.

- **Advantage:** Target device need not be synchronized; only BSs need tight sync.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires **high-bandwidth backhaul** between BSs to share timestamps.

### C. Angle of Arrival (AoA)

The direction from which the signal arrives is measured using **directional antennas** or **antenna arrays** (MUSIC, ESPRIT algorithms). Two angle measurements from two BSs yield the position via **triangulation** (line intersection).

$$\theta_i = \arctan\!\left(\frac{y - y_i}{x - x_i}\right)$$

### D. Received Signal Strength (RSS / RSSI)

Distance is inferred from path-loss. The **log-distance path loss model** is:
$$PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$
where:
- $PL(d)$ = path loss at distance $d$ (dB)
- $PL(d_0)$ = path loss at reference distance $d_0$ (usually 1 m)
- $n$ = path-loss exponent (free space $n=2$; urban $\approx 3$–$4$; indoor with obstructions $n=4$–$6$)
- $X_\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ = log-normal shadowing noise

Inverting gives the **RSS-distance relation**:
$$d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\,\dfrac{PL(d) - PL(d_0)}{10n}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheap but noisy:** RSSI is the cheapest method (no extra hardware, every Wi-Fi chip reports it) but the **least accurate** because multipath, fading, body shadowing, and orientation dramatically distort the path-loss model.

### E. Cell-ID / Cell of Origin (COO)

The simplest method — the device is located at the coordinates of the serving cell tower. Accuracy is bound by **cell radius** (50 m in dense urban, up to several km in rural).

### F. Fingerprinting

A two-phase **machine-learning** approach:
- **Offline phase (training):** A radio map (Wi-Fi RSSI, BLE beacons) is collected at known calibration points $\{(x_k, y_k, \mathbf{s}_k)\}_{k=1}^{K}$ where $\mathbf{s}_k$ is the signal signature vector.
- **Online phase (inference):** A live signature $\mathbf{s}$ is matched against the radio map using **k-NN, weighted k-NN, SVM, or deep neural networks** to predict $(\hat{x}, \hat{y})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU favorite:** Fingerprinting is widely cited as the gold standard for indoor Wi-Fi localization (Horus, RADAR systems). Expect either a 7-mark sub-question or a viva question on the offline-online split.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Distance from ToA | RF, acoustic, optical |
| 2 | $c_{\text{RF}} \approx 3\times 10^8$ m/s | Speed of light / RF | ToA, TDoA conversions |
| 3 | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0) + X_\sigma$ | Log-distance path loss | RSSI ranging |
| 4 | $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(PL(d)-PL(d_0))/10n}$ | Inverted RSS-distance | Estimating range |
| 5 | $\theta_i = \arctan\!\big((y-y_i)/(x-x_i)\big)$ | AoA geometry | Triangulation |
| 6 | $\sqrt{(x-x_i)^2 + (y-y_i)^2} = d_i$ | Circle equation (Trilateration) | Position from 3 ranges |
| 7 | $\big\vert\!\sqrt{(x-x_2)^2+(y-y_2)^2} - \sqrt{(x-x_1)^2+(y-y_1)^2}\big\vert = \vert d_2 - d_1 \vert = c \cdot \Delta t_{21}$ | Hyperbola (TDoA) | Position from time-diff |
| 8 | $\text{MEE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\sqrt{(x_i-\hat{x}_i)^2+(y_i-\hat{y}_i)^2}$ | Mean Euclidean Error | Accuracy metric |
| 9 | $\sigma^2 \geq \dfrac{c^2}{8\pi^2 \beta^2 \text{SNR}}$ | CRLB (ToA variance) | Theoretical accuracy bound |
| 10 | $n_{\text{free space}}=2$ | Path-loss exponent | Free-space path loss |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU trick:** Formula (3) is sometimes written with the alternative parameterization using **transmit power $P_t$** and **received power $P_r$**: $\;P_r(d) = P_t - PL(d)$. Substituting the log-distance model gives $P_r(d) = P_t - PL(d_0) - 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0)$, and solving for $d$ recovers formula (4). Examiners may switch between notations.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility & Engineering Relevance

- **Emergency Services (E-911 / E-112):** Legal obligation to locate every emergency caller.
- **Navigation & LBS (Location-Based Services):** Google Maps, Uber, Swiggy, Zomato dispatch logic.
- **IoT & Asset Tracking:** Cold-chain monitoring, fleet management, livestock tracking, container logistics.
- **Smart Cities:** Parking spot detection, pedestrian flow analytics, intelligent traffic lights.
- **Health Care:** Wandering-patient tracking in geriatric wards, hospital equipment locating.
- **AR/VR & Gaming:** Pokémon Go, indoor AR navigation, real-world games.
- **Military & Defense:** Soldier tracking, blue-force tracking, drone swarm coordination.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Log-Distance Path Loss Model (RSS Ranging)

We begin from the **free-space Friis transmission equation**, which states that the received power $P_r$ decays as the inverse square of distance:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \, \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

Taking the logarithm (in dB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r\,[\text{dBm}] &= P_t\,[\text{dBm}] + G_t\,[\text{dBi}] + G_r\,[\text{dBi}] \\
&\quad - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Group the constant terms (everything except $d$) into a single reference $PL(d_0)$ measured at distance $d_0$:

$$PL(d) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right) = PL(d_0) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$

This is the **free-space** case with $n = 2$. Empirically, real environments show steeper decay due to obstructions, multipath, and absorption. We generalize by replacing the constant 20 with $10n$ and adding the shadowing term $X_\sigma$:

$$
\boxed{\,PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma\,}
$$

**Solving for distance** (taking the exponential):

$$
\begin{aligned}
10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) &= PL(d) - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma \\
\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) &= \frac{PL(d) - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma}{10\,n} \\
\frac{d}{d_0} &= 10^{\,(PL(d) - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma)/(10\,n)} \\
d &= d_0 \cdot 10^{\,(PL(d) - PL(d_0) - X_\sigma)/(10\,n)}
\end{aligned}
$$

In expectation (averaging over $X_\sigma = 0$):

$$\boxed{\,\hat{d} = d_0 \cdot 10^{\,(PL(d) - PL(d_0))/(10\,n)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption exposed:** The RSS-to-distance inversion is **monotonically one-to-one** *only* in expectation. In practice, a *single* RSSI sample can produce nonsensical estimates (e.g., negative distances or wildly different $d$ values for the same physical spot) because $X_\sigma$ has a standard deviation of typically **6–12 dB**. This is why RSS-based localization needs **averaging, filtering, or fingerprinting** to be useful.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Trilateration Position Estimation

**Problem statement:** Given three anchors at known positions $A_1 = (x_1, y_1)$, $A_2 = (x_2, y_2)$, $A_3 = (x_3, y_3)$ and three measured ranges $d_1, d_2, d_3$ from a target node at unknown position $(x, y)$, find $(x, y)$.

Each range gives a circle:
$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2, \qquad i = 1,2,3$$

Expanding each equation:
$$x^2 - 2 x x_i + x_i^2 + y^2 - 2 y y_i + y_i^2 = d_i^2$$

**Subtract equation 1 from equations 2 and 3** (this eliminates $x^2 + y^2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x_1^2 - x_2^2) + (y_1^2 - y_2^2) + 2x(x_2 - x_1) + 2y(y_2 - y_1) &= d_1^2 - d_2^2 \\
(x_1^2 - x_3^2) + (y_1^2 - y_3^2) + 2x(x_3 - x_1) + 2y(y_3 - y_1) &= d_1^2 - d_3^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Rewrite in matrix form $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ where $\mathbf{x} = [x,\, y]^T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(x_2 - x_1)\, x + 2(y_2 - y_1)\, y &= d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 \\
2(x_3 - x_1)\, x + 2(y_3 - y_1)\, y &= d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Define the matrix and vectors:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} 2(x_2 - x_1) & 2(y_2 - y_1) \\ 2(x_3 - x_1) & 2(y_3 - y_1) \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{b} = \begin{bmatrix} d_1^2 - d_2^2 + x_2^2 - x_1^2 + y_2^2 - y_1^2 \\ d_1^2 - d_3^2 + x_3^2 - x_1^2 + y_3^2 - y_1^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Closed-form solution (when $A$ is invertible, i.e., anchors not collinear):**

$$\boxed{\,\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = A^{-1} \mathbf{b}\,}$$

For *more than three* anchors (over-determined system), the **Least Squares (LS)** solution is preferred:

$$\boxed{\,\hat{\mathbf{x}} = (A^T A)^{-1} A^T \mathbf{b}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric insight:** Three anchors not on a single line always produce a unique solution (the intersection of two independent circles). However, range measurement noise means the three circles generally **do not** meet at a single point — they form a curvilinear triangular "lens" region. The LS solution picks the centroid that *best* fits in the squared-error sense.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Trilateration

**Given:** Anchors $A_1 = (0, 0)$, $A_2 = (10, 0)$, $A_3 = (0, 10)$ and measured ranges $d_1 = 7.07$, $d_2 = 7.07$, $d_3 = 5$.

**Step 1 — Build matrix $A$:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} 2(10-0) & 2(0-0) \\ 2(0-0) & 2(10-0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 20 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Build vector $\mathbf{b}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_1 &= 7.07^2 - 7.07^2 + 10^2 - 0 + 0 - 0 = 100 \\
b_2 &= 7.07^2 - 5^2 + 0 - 0 + 10^2 - 0 = 50 - 25 + 100 = 125
\end{aligned}
$$

So $\mathbf{b} = [100,\, 125]^T$.

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 0 \\ 0 & 20 \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 125 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.05 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.05 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 100 \\ 125 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 \\ 6.25 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Result:** Target is at $\boxed{(5,\, 6.25)}$. Sanity check: distance to $A_1 = \sqrt{25 + 39.06} = 8.01$ (vs measured 7.07 — error $\approx 0.94$ m, attributable to the noisy $d_3 = 5$ measurement).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of the Cramer-Rao Lower Bound (CRLB) for ToA Accuracy

The CRLB sets the **theoretical minimum variance** achievable by any unbiased estimator. For a ToA estimator observing a signal of bandwidth $B$ in AWGN with SNR, the variance bound is:

$$\text{var}(\hat{t}) \geq \frac{1}{8\pi^2 \beta^2 \cdot \text{SNR}}$$

where $\beta$ is the **root-mean-square (RMS) bandwidth** of the signal, defined as:

$$\beta^2 = \frac{\int f^2 \,\vert S(f)\vert^2 \, df}{\int \vert S(f)\vert^2 \, df}$$

Converting to distance variance ($\sigma_d^2 = c^2 \cdot \text{var}(\hat{t})$):

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_d^2 \geq \frac{c^2}{8\pi^2 \beta^2 \cdot \text{SNR}}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering takeaway — why UWB is so accurate:** Ultra-Wideband (UWB) signals have $\beta$ in the **GHz** range. With $\beta = 1$ GHz and SNR = 10 (10 dB), $\sigma_d \geq \dfrac{3\times 10^8}{2\pi \cdot 10^{9} \cdot \sqrt{10}} \approx 1.5$ cm. This is why UWB (used in Apple AirTags, Samsung SmartTag+, car key fobs) achieves **10–30 cm** accuracy while narrowband Wi-Fi RSS gives **5–15 m**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Techniques at a Glance

| Technique | Accuracy | Hardware Cost | Sync Req. | NLOS Robustness | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **GPS** | 3–10 m outdoor | High (dedicated receiver) | Yes (atomic clocks) | Poor indoors | Outdoor navigation |
| **ToA (one-way)** | 1–10 m | High | Very tight | Poor | GPS, acoustic |
| **TDoA** | 1–5 m | Medium | BS-only | Medium | Cellular (E-OTD) |
| **AoA** | 1–3 m (LOS) | High (antenna arrays) | None | Very poor | 5G mmWave, beamforming |
| **RSSI** | 5–15 m | Very low | None | Poor | Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee |
| **Cell-ID** | 100 m – 10 km | None | None | Excellent | Cellular fallback |
| **Fingerprinting** | 1–5 m | Low | None | Good | Indoor Wi-Fi/BLE |
| **UWB** | 10–30 cm | Medium | Tight | Medium | RTLS, AirTags |
| **VLC (Visible Light)** | cm-level | Low (LEDs) | None | Excellent | Indoor, hospital |

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Localization System Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete data-flow of a generic localization pipeline — from raw radio observables to a filtered, accurate position estimate.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Target Node: Mobile Device or Sensor] -->|Emits / Receives RF Signal| B[Stage 1: Signal Observation]
    B -->|Raw IQ samples / timestamps / power| C[Stage 2: Ranging Engine]
    C --> D{Choose Measurement Type}
    D -->|ToA / TDoA| E[Time-based Range Estimator]
    D -->|AoA| F[Angle Estimation via MUSIC/ESPRIT]
    D -->|RSSI| G[Log-Distance Path-Loss Inversion]
    D -->|Fingerprinting| H[ML Matcher: kNN/SVM/DNN]
    E --> I[Range Vector d1 d2 d3 dn]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> J[Direct Position Estimate]
    I --> K[Stage 3a: Trilateration / LS Solver]
    K --> L[Stage 3b: NLOS Detection and Mitigation]
    L --> M[Stage 4: Kalman / Particle Filter]
    J --> M
    M --> N[Final Position Estimate: x_hat y_hat z_hat]
    N --> O[Application Layer: Maps / E-911 / IoT Tracking]
    
    P[Reference Anchors: BTS / WiFi AP / BLE / GPS Sat] -.->|Known coordinates + signals| B
    Q[Environment Database: Floorplan / RF Map] -.->|Prior knowledge| H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show data-flow; dashed arrows show out-of-band configuration (anchor positions, RF map). The branch at "Choose Measurement Type" shows that the same Stage 2 output (range/angle/signature) feeds into Stage 3, but the *type of output* determines whether Stage 3 is geometric (trilateration) or data-driven (fingerprinting). Stage 4 is universal — Kalman filtering always improves time-correlated estimates.

---

## 4.2 Comparative Flowchart — Range-Based vs Range-Free Localization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Localization Method] --> S{Range Required?}
    S -->|YES: Range-based| T[Ranging Techniques]
    S -->|NO: Range-free| U[Proximity/Hop Methods]
    
    T --> T1[ToA: c * delta_t]
    T --> T2[TDoA: c * delta_t_diff]
    T --> T3[AoA: arctan geometry]
    T --> T4[RSSI: log-distance]
    
    T1 --> V1[Trilateration - 3 circles]
    T2 --> V2[Hyperbolic Multilateration]
    T3 --> V3[Triangulation - 2 lines]
    T4 --> V4[Centroid / Weighted LS]
    
    U --> U1[Cell-ID]
    U --> U2[DV-Hop]
    U --> U3[APIT]
    U --> U4[Fingerprinting ML]
    
    V1 --> W[Position Estimate]
    V2 --> W
    V3 --> W
    V4 --> W
    U1 --> W
    U2 --> W
    U3 --> W
    U4 --> W
    
    W --> X[Accuracy Bound: CRLB]
```

## 4.3 Trilateration Geometry — Block-Level Functional View

The following diagram is a **functional block mapping** (rather than a physical drawing) of how three circles intersect to produce a position.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Anchors
        A1[Anchor A1: x1 y1, Range d1]
        A2[Anchor A2: x2 y2, Range d2]
        A3[Anchor A3: x3 y3, Range d3]
    end
    
    A1 --> C1[Circle 1 centered at A1, radius d1]
    A2 --> C2[Circle 2 centered at A2, radius d2]
    A3 --> C3[Circle 3 centered at A3, radius d3]
    
    C1 --> I1[Intersection of Circle 1 and Circle 2: two candidate points]
    C2 --> I1
    I1 --> I2[Intersection of line above with Circle 3: unique point]
    C3 --> I2
    
    I2 --> P[Position Estimate: x_hat, y_hat]

```

## 4.4 Fingerprinting Workflow — Offline vs Online Phases

```mermaid
flowchart TB

```

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Localization Questions:**
> - ✗ **Confusing ToA with TDoA:** ToA needs absolute sync between TX and RX; TDoA needs sync *only among base stations*. Examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this confusion.
> - ✗ **Forgetting $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s:** Always state the propagation speed used in numerical answers; otherwise marks are cut even if the formula is correct.
> - ✗ **Confusing $n=2$ (free space) with empirical $n \in [2, 6]$:** Real environments have $n > 2$. Writing $n = 2$ universally loses 2 marks.
> - ✗ **Mixing trilateration (circles) with triangulation (angles):** These are *different* techniques. Trilateration uses *distances*, triangulation uses *angles*.
> - ✗ **Skipping the "3 anchors minimum" justification:** Trilateration needs $\geq 3$ non-collinear anchors; triangulation needs $\geq 2$ angles; TDoA needs $\geq 3$ BSs. State this explicitly.
> - ✗ **Ignoring NLOS in RSSI derivations:** RSS-to-distance inversion assumes LOS. Always mention that NLOS introduces a positive bias that *overestimates* distance.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]`

**Q: Differentiate between Time of Arrival (ToA) and Time Difference of Arrival (TDoA) localization techniques. Which requires stricter synchronization and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | ToA | TDoA |
|---|---|---|
| **What is measured** | Absolute propagation time $\Delta t$ | Difference of arrival times $\Delta t_{ij} = t_i - t_j$ at multiple BSs |
| **Clock synchronization** | Between **transmitter and receiver** (both target and anchors) | Only among the **base stations**; target device need not be synchronized |
| **Geometric locus** | Circle (range) | Hyperbola (range difference) |
| **Minimum anchors** | 3 (non-collinear) | 3 BSs (to get 2 independent hyperbolas) |
| **Hardware complexity** | High (precise clocks at every node) | Medium (precise clocks only at BSs) |

**Synchronization comparison:** **ToA requires stricter synchronization** because the absolute one-way propagation time $\Delta t$ is directly multiplied by $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. A clock skew of just 1 microsecond translates to a 300 m range error. In TDoA, the unknown clock offset of the *target* cancels out when taking differences, so only BS-to-BS sync matters, which is achievable via **wired backhaul (e.g., IEEE 1588 PTP)** or **GPS-disciplined oscillators**. **(3 marks — 1 for definition each, 1 for synchronization comparison with justification.)**

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**Q: What is fingerprinting in indoor localization? Explain the offline (training) and online (estimation) phases briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Fingerprinting is a *range-free, pattern-matching* localization technique that infers a device's position by comparing its live **signal signature** (e.g., RSSI vector from multiple Wi-Fi APs) against a pre-collected **radio map** of the environment. **(1 mark)**

**Offline (Training) phase:** A surveyor walks through the area, divides it into a grid of calibration points $\{(x_k, y_k)\}_{k=1}^{K}$, and at each point records the signal-strength vector $\mathbf{s}_k = (s_1^{(k)}, s_2^{(k)}, \dots, s_N^{(k)})$ from $N$ access points. The pairs $\{(x_k, y_k, \mathbf{s}_k)\}$ form the **radio map**. ML models (k-NN, SVM, neural network) are trained on this dataset. **(1 mark)**

**Online (Estimation) phase:** A user at unknown location measures a live signature $\mathbf{s}$. This is fed to the trained model, which returns the **estimated coordinates** $(\hat{x}, \hat{y})$. The match may be a nearest-neighbor lookup (k-NN), a weighted average, or a regression output (DNN). **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern, Module 3 ESE]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]: Explain the log-distance path loss model used in RSSI-based ranging. Derive the relation between received power and distance, and discuss how the path-loss exponent $n$ varies with environment.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Log-distance path loss model [3 marks]:**

The RSSI-based ranging technique exploits the fact that the average received signal power decays with distance. The empirically validated **log-distance path loss model** states:

$$PL(d)\,[\text{dB}] = PL(d_0) + 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_\sigma$$

where:
- $PL(d)$ = total path loss (dB) between transmitter and receiver separated by distance $d$
- $PL(d_0)$ = known path loss at the reference distance $d_0$ (typically 1 m)
- $n$ = path-loss exponent (dimensionless, environment-dependent)
- $X_\sigma$ = zero-mean Gaussian random variable $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ modeling **shadow fading** (obstructions, multipath)

**(ii) Derivation of received power vs distance [2 marks]:**

The received power is related to transmitted power and path loss by:

$$P_r\,[\text{dBm}] = P_t\,[\text{dBm}] - PL(d)\,[\text{dB}]$$

Substituting the path-loss equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_r(d) &= P_t - PL(d_0) - 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) - X_\sigma \\
&= P_r(d_0) - 10\,n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) - X_\sigma
\end{aligned}
$$

Inverting to recover distance from a measured $P_r$:

$$d = d_0 \cdot 10^{\,(P_r(d_0) - P_r - X_\sigma)/(10\,n)}$$

Or, in expectation (averaging over $X_\sigma$):

$$\boxed{\,\hat{d} = d_0 \cdot 10^{\,(P_r(d_0) - P_r)/(10\,n)}\,}$$

**(iii) Path-loss exponent $n$ in different environments [2 marks]:**

| Environment | Path-loss exponent $n$ | Comments |
|---|---|---|
| Free space (vacuum / outdoor LoS) | **2** | Theoretical Friis value |
| Open rural area | 2 to 2.5 | Few obstructions |
| Urban cellular (with buildings) | 3 to 4 | Reflections, scattering |
| Indoor LOS (same room) | 1.5 to 2 | Waveguide effects |
| Indoor NLOS (through walls) | 4 to 6 | Heavy attenuation |
| Factory / warehouse with metal | 5 to 6 | Severe multipath |

> **Note:** A larger $n$ implies faster power decay with distance, hence **shorter effective range** for a given transmit power.

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]: With the help of a block diagram, explain the trilateration technique. A target node receives signals from three anchor nodes placed at coordinates $(0,0)$, $(10,0)$, and $(0,10)$ with measured distances $d_1 = \sqrt{50}$, $d_2 = \sqrt{50}$, $d_3 = \sqrt{61}$ respectively. Estimate the target's location using the trilateration method.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Trilateration block diagram and explanation [3 marks]:**

*(Draw the system: 3 anchors → 3 ranges → 3 circles → 2 intersection points → 1 unique point → position estimate.)*

Trilateration computes the target's 2D position $(x, y)$ from three (or more) measured distances $d_1, d_2, d_3$ to three anchors with known coordinates $(x_i, y_i)$. Each range defines a circle of radius $d_i$ around anchor $i$. Two circles intersect at two points; the third circle disambiguates the solution.

Mathematically:

$$(x - x_i)^2 + (y - y_i)^2 = d_i^2, \quad i = 1, 2, 3$$

**(ii) Numerical solution [4 marks]:**

Given:
- $A_1 = (0, 0),\; d_1 = \sqrt{50} = 7.071$
- $A_2 = (10, 0),\; d_2 = \sqrt{50} = 7.071$
- $A_3 = (0, 10),\; d_3 = \sqrt{61} = 7.810$

**Step 1 — Write the three circle equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2 + y^2 &= 50 \quad &(\text{from } A_1)\\
(x-10)^2 + y^2 &= 50 \quad &(\text{from } A_2)\\
x^2 + (y-10)^2 &= 61 \quad &(\text{from } A_3)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract equation 1 from equation 2:**

$$(x-10)^2 - x^2 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; -20x + 100 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 5$$

**Step 3 — Subtract equation 1 from equation 3:**

$$(y-10)^2 - y^2 = 11 \;\Rightarrow\; -20y + 100 = 11 \;\Rightarrow\; y = \dfrac{89}{20} = 4.45$$

**Step 4 — Verify by plugging back into equation 1:**

$$\sqrt{5^2 + 4.45^2} = \sqrt{25 + 19.80} = \sqrt{44.80} \approx 6.69 \text{ m}$$

This is close to $d_1 = 7.07$ (small error due to noise in $d_3$).

**Final Answer:** Target is located at $\boxed{(5.00,\; 4.45)}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern, Module 3 ESE]`

**Q (a) [7 marks]: Explain the Angle of Arrival (AoA) localization technique with its geometric principle. State its advantages and limitations compared to ToA-based localization.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) AoA geometric principle [3 marks]:**

AoA measures the **direction** from which the signal arrives at a base station, using **directional antennas** or **antenna arrays**. The angle $\theta_i$ is the angle subtended by the incoming wavefront with respect to a fixed reference direction (usually the +x axis).

For a target at $(x, y)$ and an anchor at $(x_i, y_i)$:

$$\theta_i = \arctan\!\left(\frac{y - y_i}{x - x_i}\right)$$

The locus of points making a fixed angle $\theta_i$ with the anchor is a **straight line** passing through the anchor at the measured angle. With two anchors (each contributing one such line), the **intersection of the two lines** gives the target's location — this is called **triangulation**.

**Hardware methods** for angle estimation:
- **Rotating directional antenna** (mechanical sweep)
- **Phased antenna array** with phase difference measurement between elements
- **MUSIC (MUltiple SIgnal Classification)** algorithm — high-resolution subspace method
- **ESPRIT (Estimation of Signal Parameters via Rotational Invariance Techniques)**

**(ii) Comparison with ToA [4 marks]:**

| Aspect | AoA | ToA |
|---|---|---|
| **Measured quantity** | Angle (radians/degrees) | Time (seconds) |
| **Hardware needed** | Antenna array / direction finding | Precise clock |
| **Clock synchronization** | **Not required** between TX-RX | **Strictly required** |
| **Geometric locus** | Straight line (ray) | Circle |
| **Minimum nodes (2D)** | 2 anchors | 3 anchors |
| **NLOS robustness** | **Very poor** — reflection shifts the apparent angle | Poor — multipath distorts timing |
| **Effect of anchor geometry** | Sensitive — bad geometry (collinear) hurts precision | Less sensitive |
| **Best use case** | mmWave 5G, beamforming | GPS, UWB |

**Limitations of AoA:**
1. **Severe NLOS sensitivity:** A single reflection can change the apparent angle by tens of degrees, producing huge position errors.
2. **Antenna array size:** For high accuracy, array size scales with wavelength — sub-6 GHz needs bulky arrays; mmWave is more compact.
3. **Cost:** Antenna arrays and MUSIC/ESPRIT processors are expensive for low-end IoT devices.

**Advantages over ToA:** No need for precise clock synchronization, and only **2 anchors** are sufficient (vs 3 for ToA).

---

**Q (b) [7 marks]: What is the E-911 mandate? Why is localization accuracy critical for emergency services? Briefly describe two cellular network-based localization techniques standardized by 3GPP.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) E-911 mandate [2 marks]:**

The **Enhanced 911 (E-911)** service is a U.S. Federal Communications Commission (FCC) mandate issued in 1996 and progressively tightened through subsequent orders (1999, 2010, 2015). It requires that all wireless carriers automatically provide the **geographic location of any mobile device placing an emergency call** to the Public Safety Answering Point (PSAP).

- **Phase II accuracy requirements:** location within **50 meters for 67%** of calls and **150 meters for 95%** of calls (network-based), and within **50 meters for 67%** of calls (handset-based).
- **E-112** is the European Union equivalent.

**(ii) Why accuracy is critical [2 marks]:**

- **Time-critical response:** Cardiac arrest, stroke, and trauma survival drop sharply with each minute of delay.
- **Disambiguation:** A 1 km error in a dense city can route an ambulance to the wrong building floor or wrong side of a road.
- **Rural/forest scenarios:** Cellular coverage may be from a distant tower; localization must still pinpoint the caller.
- **Wandering patients / missing persons:** Sub-100 m accuracy is the threshold at which first responders can usefully act.
- **Legal & regulatory:** Non-compliance leads to heavy fines for the carrier.

**(iii) 3GPP-standardized cellular localization techniques [3 marks]:**

**1. E-OTD (Enhanced Observed Time Difference):** The mobile measures the *observed time difference* between signals from nearby BTSs, and reports these to the location server. The server computes the position using hyperbolic multilateration. Accuracy: 50–200 m. Used in GSM.

**2. U-TDOA (Uplink Time Difference of Arrival):** The mobile transmits a known signal; multiple **Location Measurement Units (LMUs)** at different cell sites measure the *time of arrival* of this signal. Differences in arrival times give hyperbolas whose intersection is the mobile's position. Network-based, accurate, but requires LMU infrastructure. Used in 3G/4G.

*(Other relevant methods for additional credit: GPS / A-GPS, OTDOA in LTE — Observed TDOA with reference signal time difference — and 5G NR Multi-RTT.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Localization (Module 3)**

- 🔹 **Localization** = estimating physical coordinates of a device from radio observables. *Not the same as navigation* (which adds routing).
- 🔹 **Four-stage pipeline:** Signal observation → Ranging → Position estimation → Filtering.
- 🔹 **Ranging techniques:** ToA (sync needed), TDoA (BS-only sync), AoA (no sync, needs arrays), RSSI (cheapest, noisiest), Cell-ID (lowest accuracy), Fingerprinting (best for indoor with prior map).
- 🔹 **Key formula — RSSI:** $d = d_0 \cdot 10^{(P_r(d_0) - P_r)/(10n)}$. Path-loss exponent $n = 2$ in free space, **3–6** in real environments.
- 🔹 **Key formula — ToA:** $d = c \cdot \Delta t$, with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s for RF, $c \approx 343$ m/s for acoustic.
- 🔹 **Trilateration:** $\geq 3$ non-collinear anchors. Circles intersect to give unique point. LS solution when over-determined.
- 🔹 **Triangulation:** $\geq 2$ anchors with angle measurements. Lines (rays) intersect.
- 🔹 **CRLB for ToA accuracy:** $\sigma_d^2 \geq c^2 / (8\pi^2 \beta^2 \text{SNR})$. High bandwidth = high accuracy (UWB → cm-level).
- 🔹 **E-911:** Mandate requiring 50 m accuracy for emergency callers.
- 🔹 **Fingerprinting:** Two-phase (offline radio-map building, online ML-based lookup).
- 🔹 **NLOS bias** is the single largest source of error — always increases the apparent distance.
- 🔹 **Kalman filter / Particle filter** are the de facto smoothing stages for time-series localization.
- 🔹 **Exam mantra:** State the **measured quantity**, the **synchronization requirement**, and the **geometric locus** for any ranging technique — these three together fetch full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handover
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handover

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Handover in Wireless Mobile Computing

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Handover** (also called **Handoff**) is the fundamental mechanism in cellular mobile communication systems that enables an active Mobile Station (MS) to seamlessly transfer its ongoing voice call or data session from one Base Transceiver Station (BTS) / Base Station (BS) / Access Point (AP) to another without perceptible interruption to the user. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, handover is a cornerstone of **mobility management** in cellular networks such as **GSM**, **GPRS**, **UMTS**, **LTE**, and **5G NR**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Handover (Handoff):** The procedure by which the radio link between the mobile terminal and the network core is transferred from one cell site (or channel) to another, while preserving service continuity.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine you are talking on your mobile phone while driving from Kozhikode to Kochi. As you cross the coverage boundary of one tower, your call must NOT drop. Think of cellular towers as **lighthouses** on a coastline — when your ship sails past one lighthouse's beam, the next lighthouse takes over the illumination without you noticing. The **handover** is exactly that "handing over the call beam" from one lighthouse (cell tower) to the next — silently, in milliseconds.

### Why Handover Matters (KTU High-Yield Point)

| Reason | Plain-English Meaning |
|--------|------------------------|
| **Signal Degradation** | MS moves away from current BS → RSS drops |
| **Load Balancing** | Current cell is overcrowded → shift MS to a less-loaded neighbor |
| **Interference Mitigation** | Move MS away from a co-channel interferer |
| **Channel Reassignment** | Free up channels or reassign frequency/time slots |
| **User Mobility (Roaming)** | Mobile user changes geographic regions |

---

## Taxonomy of Handover

> [!NOTE]
> Handover classification is a **high-frequency KTU question**. Memorize the entire tree below.

```
                        HANDOVER
                           │
        ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────┐
        │                  │                  │
   HORIZONTAL          VERTICAL          SYSTEM TYPE
        │                  │                  │
   (Same Tech)       (Diff Tech)        ┌─────┴──────┐
        │                  │            │            │
   ┌────┴────┐        ┌────┴────┐    GSM/2G    LTE/5G
   │         │        │         │    
Intra-cell  Inter-cell  Hard VHO   Soft VHO
```

---

## The Two Most Critical Classifications (Exam Favourites)

### 1. Hard Handover ("Break-Before-Make")
- **MS disconnects from the old BS BEFORE connecting to the new BS.**
- Used in: **GSM (2G)**, **LTE**, **5G NR**.
- Analogy: *"Like jumping from one moving boat to another — there is a brief moment in the air."*

### 2. Soft Handover ("Make-Before-Break")
- **MS connects to the new BS BEFORE releasing the old BS link.**
- Temporary simultaneous connection to two or more BSs.
- Used in: **CDMA (IS-95)**, **UMTS (WCDMA)**.
- Analogy: *"Like stepping from one escalator onto the next with both feet on both."*

### 3. Softer Handover
- A special case where the MS is connected to **two sectors of the SAME BS**.
- Intra-BS handover — only the BS internal combiner is involved.

---

## Physical / System Metrics (Bold Per Protocol)

- **RSS (Received Signal Strength)** — measured in **dBm**.
- **C/I Ratio (Carrier-to-Interference Ratio)** — measured in **dB**.
- **BER (Bit Error Rate)** — dimensionless ratio.
- **HO Margin (Hysteresis)** — typically **3–6 dB**.
- **Pilot Channel Power** — continuous beacon signal.
- **MS Speed** — measured in **km/h**.
- **Cell Radius** — typically **0.5 km (urban)** to **35 km (rural macro)**.

---

## Real-World Engineering Significance

Handover is **the lifeline** of cellular mobility. Without it:
- Dropped calls would occur every time a user crosses a cell boundary.
- Mobile telephony as we know it would collapse.
- In **5G NR**, handovers must complete in **< 10 ms** to support URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency) services such as autonomous driving and remote surgery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Three-Phase Handover Process (KTU 2024 Mandatory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every handover goes through **3 sequential phases**. Examiners frequently ask: *"Explain the handover procedure in cellular systems."*

### Phase 1: **Handover Initiation (Measurement / Triggering)**
- **MS continuously monitors** the RSS of the serving BS via the **Downlink (DL) pilot/beacon channel**.
- Simultaneously, MS scans neighbor BSs (up to 6 in GSM, 32 in LTE, up to 504 in 5G NR).
- Reports are sent to the network via **Measurement Reports** (GSM) / **Measurement Gaps** (LTE) / **SS-RSRP** (5G).
- Network Decision Engine (in BSC / RNC / eNodeB / gNodeB) analyzes reports.

### Phase 2: **Handover Decision**
- The decision logic (algorithm) compares metrics against thresholds.
- Decision criteria include:
  - **Relative RSS**: Compare RSS of serving vs neighbor cells.
  - **Absolute RSS Threshold**.
  - **Hysteresis Margin (Δ H)** to prevent **ping-pong effect**.
  - **Load conditions**.
  - **User velocity (Doppler-adaptive handover)**.
- Decision engine decides: **WHEN and WHERE to handover**.

### Phase 3: **Handover Execution**
- Resources are reserved in the target cell.
- **Signaling messages** exchanged (e.g., GSM: Handover Command, Handover Access, Physical Information).
- Channel is switched; path may be updated in core network (VLR / MSC / S-GW / AMF).
- Old radio link is released.

---

## 2. Why Hysteresis Margin is Critical (Ping-Pong Prevention)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ping-Pong Effect:** Without hysteresis, a MS near the cell boundary may rapidly flip-flop between two cells every few seconds, causing excessive signaling load and degraded QoS.

The **Handover Margin (Δ H)** adds a buffer. A handover is triggered ONLY when:

$$ RSS_{neighbor} > RSS_{serving} + \Delta H $$

Typical values: **Δ H = 3 dB to 6 dB** in GSM/LTE.

---

## 3. Time-To-Trigger (TTT) Parameter

To avoid reacting to momentary fading dips, a candidate cell's better RSS must persist for a minimum duration called the **Time-To-Trigger (TTT)**:

- GSM: TTT ≈ **0.5 – 1 s**
- LTE: TTT typically **40 ms – 5120 ms**
- 5G NR: TTT range **0 ms – 5120 ms** (configurable)

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|------------|----------------------|--------|--------------|
| RSS received at MS | $RSS = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL(d)$ | **dBm** | Path-loss model input |
| Free-space path loss | $PL_{FS}(d) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | **dB** | d in km, f in MHz |
| Handover decision condition | $RSS_{nbr} \geq RSS_{srv} + \Delta H$ | **dBm** | Hard handover trigger |
| Hysteresis margin | $\Delta H = RSS_{nbr} - RSS_{srv}$ | **dB** | Ping-pong prevention |
| Cell radius limit (capacity) | $N_{c} = \dfrac{M}{k} = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{(2/3)\cdot C/I}}$ | dimensionless | Frequency reuse planning |
| Co-channel reuse ratio | $Q = \sqrt{3N}$ | dimensionless | Hexagonal cluster design |
| Maximum tolerable path loss | $P_{max} = P_{tx} + G - R_{sens}$ | **dB** | Cell coverage radius |
| Handover failure probability | $P_{HO\_fail} = P(RSS < P_{min})\ \text{during}\ TTT$ | dimensionless | Reliability metric |
| Cell dwell time (mean) | $\bar{T}_{dwell} = \dfrac{R}{\bar{v}}$ | **seconds** | R = cell radius, v = MS velocity |
| Shadow fading margin | $M_{sf} = \sigma \cdot Q^{-1}(P_{outage})$ | **dB** | Link budget (Gaussian σ) |
| Number of HO per call (mean) | $\bar{N}_{HO} = \dfrac{v \cdot T_{call}}{\bar{L}_{cell}}$ | dimensionless | Teletraffic modeling |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** Always quote the **Handover Margin (Δ H)** formula and the **co-channel reuse ratio $Q = \sqrt{3N}$** together — they are KTU favourite two-markers.

---

## 4. Real-World Utility of Handover Algorithms

| Application Area | Engineering Role of Handover |
|------------------|------------------------------|
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Sub-10 ms handover between mmWave small cells for V2X |
| **Mobile Telephony** | VoLTE / VoNR continuity during driving |
| **Satellite-Terrestrial Integration (NTN)** | Handover between LEO satellites & ground cells |
| **Hospital IoT (mHealth)** | Uninterrupted wearable telemetry |
| **Smart City Networks** | Mobility-aware load balancing in HetNets |

---

## 5. Handover in Different Generations (Quick Comparison)

| Generation | Handover Type | Decision Entity | Typical Latency |
|------------|---------------|------------------|------------------|
| GSM (2G) | Hard | BSC | ~ 200 ms |
| GPRS (2.5G) | Hard | BSC / SGSN | ~ 200 ms |
| UMTS (3G) | Soft / Softer | RNC | ~ 100 – 300 ms |
| HSPA+ | Soft | RNC / NodeB | ~ 100 ms |
| LTE (4G) | Hard (X2-based) | eNodeB (flat) | ~ 50 ms |
| 5G NR | Hard (Xn-based) | gNodeB / AMF | **< 10 ms (URLLC)** |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the architectural shift: **decision-making moves closer to the radio edge** (RNC → eNodeB → gNodeB), enabling faster handovers — a key KTU concept for understanding 5G evolution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Handover Decision Boundary (Relative RSS Algorithm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed KTU Part B (14-mark) question**.

We derive the exact **cell boundary locus** where a handover is triggered.

### Given:
- $RSS_{s}$ = Received signal strength from **serving BS** (BS₁) at distance $r_1$.
- $RSS_{n}$ = Received signal strength from **neighbor BS** (BS₂) at distance $r_2$.
- $\alpha$ = Path-loss exponent (typically 2 to 4).
- $\Delta H$ = Hysteresis margin in dB.
- $P_{t1}, P_{t2}$ = Transmit powers of BS₁ and BS₂.

### Step 1: General Log-Distance Path Loss Model
The mean received power at distance $d$ follows the log-distance model:

$$ \overline{PL}(d) = \overline{PL}(d_0) + 10\alpha \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{d}{d_0}\right) $$

This is the **standard log-distance path loss equation**.

### Step 2: RSS from BS₁ at distance $r_1$

$$ RSS_1 = P_{t1} - \overline{PL}(r_1) = P_{t1} - \overline{PL}(d_0) - 10\alpha \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{r_1}{d_0}\right) $$

### Step 3: RSS from BS₂ at distance $r_2$

$$ RSS_2 = P_{t2} - \overline{PL}(r_2) = P_{t2} - \overline{PL}(d_0) - 10\alpha \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{r_2}{d_0}\right) $$

### Step 4: RSS Difference (the comparison the MS performs)

$$ \Delta RSS = RSS_2 - RSS_1 $$

$$ \Delta RSS = (P_{t2} - P_{t1}) + 10\alpha \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}\right) $$

### Step 5: Apply Handover Trigger Condition

A handover is initiated when:

$$ \Delta RSS \geq \Delta H $$

$$ (P_{t2} - P_{t1}) + 10\alpha \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}\right) \geq \Delta H $$

### Step 6: Solve for the Geometric Boundary
For identical power BSs ($P_{t1} = P_{t2}$) and $\alpha = 4$ (typical urban):

$$ 40 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}\right) \geq \Delta H $$

$$ \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{r_1}{r_2}\right) \geq \dfrac{\Delta H}{40} $$

$$ \dfrac{r_1}{r_2} \geq 10^{(\Delta H / 40)} $$

### Step 7: Special Case — Zero Hysteresis ($\Delta H = 0$)
With **no hysteresis**, the boundary reduces to $r_1 = r_2$, which is the **perpendicular bisector** of the line joining the two BSs — exactly what you would expect geometrically for a hexagonal cell-edge.

---

## Derivation 2: Mean Number of Handovers Per Call

### Given:
- MS velocity: $v$ (m/s)
- Average call duration: $\bar{T}_{call}$ (s)
- Average cell-crossing distance: $\bar{L}_{cell} \approx \dfrac{2R}{\pi}$ (random direction model)

### Step 1: Time the MS spends within a single cell

$$ \bar{T}_{dwell} = \dfrac{\bar{L}_{cell}}{v} = \dfrac{2R}{\pi v} $$

### Step 2: Expected number of handovers during a call

$$ \bar{N}_{HO} = \dfrac{\bar{T}_{call}}{\bar{T}_{dwell}} $$

$$ \bar{N}_{HO} = \dfrac{\bar{T}_{call} \cdot \pi v}{2R} $$

### Numerical Example
If $v = 60\ \text{km/h} = 16.67\ \text{m/s}$, $R = 500\ \text{m}$, $T_{call} = 120\ \text{s}$:

$$ \bar{N}_{HO} = \dfrac{120 \times \pi \times 16.67}{2 \times 500} = \dfrac{6282}{1000} \approx 6.28 $$

So the user experiences **≈ 6 handovers** during a 2-minute call while driving at 60 km/h through 500 m cells.

---

## Derivation 3: Handover Failure Probability (Link Budget View)

### Given:
- $P_{min}$ = minimum acceptable received power (sensitivity)
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation of shadow fading (dB)
- TTT = Time-To-Trigger (s)

### Step 1: Outage Probability under Log-Normal Shadowing
The probability that RSS falls below $P_{min}$ is:

$$ P_{outage} = \dfrac{1}{2} \operatorname{erfc}\left(\dfrac{P_{rx} - P_{min}}{\sigma \sqrt{2}}\right) $$

### Step 2: Approximation using Q-function
For wireless engineering, the Q-function approximation is preferred:

$$ P_{outage} \approx Q\left(\dfrac{P_{rx} - P_{min}}{\sigma}\right) $$

### Step 3: Handover Failure Probability
A handover fails if the candidate cell's RSS drops below $P_{min}$ before TTT expires:

$$ P_{HO\_fail} \approx 1 - \left(1 - Q\left(\dfrac{P_{rx} - P_{min}}{\sigma}\right)\right)^{TTT/\tau_c} $$

where $\tau_c$ is the channel coherence time (~ms range).

---

## Analytical Matrix: Handover Decision Algorithms

| Algorithm | Trigger Metric | Strength | Weakness |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|----------|
| **Relative RSS** | $RSS_{nbr} - RSS_{srv}$ | Simple, robust | Ping-pong without hysteresis |
| **Relative RSS + Hysteresis** | $RSS_{nbr} - RSS_{srv} \geq \Delta H$ | Eliminates ping-pong | Slow handover for fast MS |
| **Absolute RSS + Threshold** | $RSS_{serving} < T_{abs}$ | Predictable trigger | Cell-shape distortion |
| **C/I Based** | $C/I \leq T_{C/I}$ | Capacity-aware | Slow under bursty interference |
| **Velocity-Aware (Doppler)** | $\hat{v}_{MS}$ + dynamic TTT | Optimal for high-speed MS | Requires speed estimation |
| **Distance-Based** | $d_{MS-BS}$ | Geometry-based | Needs accurate positioning |
| **AI / ML-Based** | Multi-feature vector | Learns user patterns | Computational complexity |
| **Load-Balancing** | $N_{users}, \text{throughput}$ | Prevents congestion | May cause unnecessary HO |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Three-Phase Handover Flow (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MS in Serving Cell] --> B[Phase 1: Measurement]
    B --> B1[Measure RSS_serving]
    B --> B2[Scan neighbor BSs]
    B --> B3[Send Measurement Report]
    B3 --> C[Phase 2: Decision]
    C --> C1{Compare RSS_nbr vs RSS_srv + Delta H}
    C1 -- Condition not met --> B
    C1 -- Condition met --> C2{TTT timer expired?}
    C2 -- No --> B
    C2 -- Yes --> D[Phase 3: Execution]
    D --> D1[Reserve resources in target cell]
    D1 --> D2[Send Handover Command]
    D2 --> D3[MS tunes to new channel]
    D3 --> D4[Update core network - VLR / AMF]
    D4 --> E[MS Connected to New Cell]
    E --> F[Release old radio link]
```

## Diagram 2: Hard vs Soft vs Softer Handover Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HardHandover[Hard Handover GSM LTE 5G]
        H1[MS linked to BS1] -->|Break first| H2[Brief gap no link]
        H2 -->|Then Make| H3[MS linked to BS2]
    end
    subgraph SoftHandover[Soft Handover UMTS]
        S1[MS linked to BS1] -->|Add link| S2[MS linked to BS1 + BS2]
        S2 -->|Drop old link| S3[MS linked to BS2]
    end
    subgraph SofterHandover[Softer Handover UMTS Sectors]
        O1[MS linked to Sector A] -->|Add link| O2[MS linked to Sector A + Sector B]
        O2 -->|Drop old link| O3[MS linked to Sector B]
    end
```

## Diagram 3: Cellular Handover Topology (Hexagonal Cells)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CellA[Cell 1 - BS1 Coverage]
        MS1[MS at position r1 from BS1]
    end
    subgraph CellB[Cell 2 - BS2 Coverage]
        MS2[MS at position r2 from BS2]
    end
    subgraph HandoverZone[Handover Zone]
        HZ[MS in overlapping region]
    end
    BS1A[BS1] -.->|Serving signal| MS1
    BS2B[BS2] -.->|Neighbor signal| MS1
    MS1 --> HZ
    HZ -->|HO Trigger when RSS_nbr exceeds RSS_srv + Delta H| MS2[Connected to BS2]
```

## Diagram 4: Architecture of Handover Decision Entities Across Generations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GSM[GSM 2G - Decision by BSC] --> RNC[UMTS 3G - Decision by RNC]
    RNC --> eNB[LTE 4G - Decision by eNodeB - Flat Architecture]
    eNB --> gNB[5G NR - Decision by gNodeB + AMF]
    GSM -.->|HLR / VLR / MSC| Core1[Core Network 2G 3G]
    eNB -.->|S-GW / MME| Core2[EPC 4G Core]
    gNB -.->|AMF / UPF| Core3[5GC Service Based Architecture]
```

## Diagram 5: Ping-Pong Effect Visualisation (Why Hysteresis Matters)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[MS near boundary] --> Q1{RSS_nbr greater than RSS_srv}
    Q1 -- Yes no hysteresis --> P2[HO to BS2]
    P2 --> Q2{Still near boundary?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> P3[HO back to BS1 - PING PONG]
    P3 --> P4[Excessive signaling overhead]
    P4 -.->|With Delta H hysteresis| P5[Stable handover only after clear margin]
```

## Diagram 6: Soft Handover Macro-Diversity Combining

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MS[Mobile Station] -->|Uplink signal received by both| BS1[BS1 - Sector Alpha]
    MS -->|Uplink signal received by both| BS2[BS2 - Sector Beta]
    BS1 --> RNC1[RNC Combiner]
    BS2 --> RNC1
    RNC1 --> Selected[Selection Combining Output to Core Network]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** For KTU descriptive answers, redraw Diagram 1 and Diagram 2 on the answer sheet — they collectively cover **6 marks** of the typical 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Handover. Why is it required in cellular mobile communication? **[3 Marks]**
**[Model Answer]**
Handover is the process by which an active Mobile Station (MS) seamlessly transfers its ongoing call or data session from one Base Station (BS) to another without interruption to the user.

**Handover is required because:**
1. **Mobility:** The MS moves out of the coverage area of the current cell.
2. **Signal Degradation:** Received signal strength (RSS) drops below the minimum acceptable threshold.
3. **Load Balancing:** To redistribute traffic from an overloaded cell to a less-congested neighbor.
4. **Interference Management:** To escape a high co-channel interference zone.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Note:** Writing just *"switching from one cell to another"* scores only 1 mark. You must explicitly mention **mobility + load balancing + interference**.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Hard Handover and Soft Handover. **[3 Marks]**
**[Model Answer]**

| Parameter | Hard Handover | Soft Handover |
|------------|---------------|---------------|
| Connection sequence | Break-before-make | Make-before-break |
| Simultaneous links | None | Two or more |
| Technology | GSM, LTE, 5G NR | CDMA, UMTS (WCDMA) |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Handover latency | Short, but with brief gap | Larger macro-diversity window |
| Brief service interruption | Yes (a few ms) | No |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Explain the Handover Procedure in GSM with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of Hysteresis Margin and Time-To-Trigger in avoiding ping-pong effect.**

#### (a) GSM Handover Procedure **[7 Marks]**

**1. Measurement Phase:** The MS continuously monitors the **RSSI** (Received Signal Strength Indicator) and **RXQUAL** (signal quality) of the serving BTS from the slow associated control channel (SACCH) bursts.

**2. Decision Phase:** The BSC (Base Station Controller) compares the serving cell's RSS with up to **6 neighbor cells**. If a neighbor's RSS exceeds the serving cell's RSS by the hysteresis margin ΔH for a duration greater than the Time-To-Trigger, a handover decision is taken.

**3. Execution Phase (GSM Signaling Messages):**
- BSC → Old BTS → MS: **HANDOVER COMMAND** (containing new frequency, timeslot, BSIC, TA).
- MS tunes to new ARFCN.
- MS → New BTS: **HANDOVER ACCESS** (random-access bursts).
- New BTS → MS: **PHYSICAL INFORMATION** (timing advance).
- MS → New BTS: **SABM** (Set Asynchronous Balanced Mode for layer 2 link).
- MS → New BTS → BSC: **HANDOVER COMPLETE**.
- Old BTS releases the channel.
- VLR is updated.

#### (b) Role of Hysteresis Margin and Time-To-Trigger **[7 Marks]**

**Hysteresis Margin (Δ H):**
Without hysteresis, a MS near the boundary of two cells may repeatedly trigger handovers every few seconds as the two RSS values fluctuate around equality. This **ping-pong effect** causes:

- Excessive signaling load on BSC / MSC.
- Increased call-drop probability (HO failures during ping-pong).
- Wasted radio resources.

**Solution:** Add a margin so that handover is triggered only when:

$$ RSS_{nbr} \geq RSS_{srv} + \Delta H $$

Typical GSM value: **Δ H = 3 dB to 6 dB**.

**Time-To-Trigger (TTT):**
Even with hysteresis, instantaneous fades (Rayleigh multipath) can produce false triggers. TTT requires the better RSS condition to **persist for a minimum duration** before a HO is committed.

Typical GSM value: **TTT = 0.5 – 1.0 s**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently write *"TTT is the time for handover to complete"* — this is **WRONG**. TTT is the **wait time before the handover is *triggered***, not the execution time. The execution time is called **Handover Latency**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Compare Soft Handover and Softer Handover in UMTS. Explain how handover decision is taken in 3G using measurement reports.**

#### (a) Soft vs Softer Handover **[7 Marks]**

| Feature | Soft Handover | Softer Handover |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Source cells | Two different NodeBs | Two **sectors of the SAME NodeB** |
| Combining point | RNC (macro-diversity) | NodeB internal combiner |
| Network signaling | Heavy (RNC involved) | Light (intra-NodeB) |
| Active set size | Can be up to 6 cells | At most sectors of one NodeB |
| Latency | Higher | Lower |
| Use case | Inter-cell mobility | Within-cell sector transition |

**Active Set:** The list of cells to which the MS is currently connected in UMTS. Maximum **6 cells** in the active set.

**Active Set Update Procedure:**
1. MS sends **Measurement Report (Event 1A)** when a new pilot crosses add threshold.
2. MS sends **Measurement Report (Event 1B)** when a pilot in active set falls below drop threshold.
3. RNC processes Event 1A/1B and sends **Active Set Update** message.
4. MS adds or removes the pilot.

#### (b) UMTS Handover Decision via Measurement Reports **[7 Marks]**

UMTS uses **CPICH (Common Pilot Indicator Channel)** measurements:

- **CPICH RSCP** = Received Signal Code Power (absolute signal level).
- **CPICH $E_c/N_0$** = Energy-per-chip to noise spectral density ratio.

**Decision Events:**
- **Event 1A:** A primary CPICH enters the reporting range — candidate for addition to active set.
- **Event 1B:** A primary CPICH leaves the reporting range — candidate for removal from active set.
- **Event 1C:** Active set is full and a new stronger pilot replaces a weaker one.
- **Event 1D:** Active set changes — best cell changed.

**Handover Decision Formula:**

$$ (E_c/N_0)_{nbr} \geq (E_c/N_0)_{srv} + \Delta H_{UMTS} $$

The UE reports these measurements on a periodic basis (configurable, typically **200 ms – 1 s**). The RNC runs the **soft handover algorithm** and decides on active set modification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Do not confuse **Handover in GSM (hard, BSC-controlled)** with **Handover in UMTS (soft, RNC-controlled with active set logic)**. Examiners allocate marks based on **correct technology mapping**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Handover** = transferring an active call/data session between cells without user-perceived interruption.
- **Hard handover** = break-before-make (GSM, LTE, 5G NR).
- **Soft handover** = make-before-break (UMTS, CDMA); uses **active set** (max 6 cells).
- **Softer handover** = intra-NodeB sector combining.
- **Hysteresis margin (Δ H)** = prevents ping-pong; typical **3–6 dB**.
- **Time-To-Trigger (TTT)** = minimum duration before HO commitment; GSM ~0.5–1 s.
- **Three phases:** Initiation (Measurement) → Decision → Execution.
- **Decision entity evolution:** BSC (2G) → RNC (3G) → eNodeB (4G) → gNodeB (5G).
- **5G target HO latency:** **< 10 ms** for URLLC services.
- **UMTS measurement events:** **1A** (add), **1B** (drop), **1C** (replace), **1D** (best cell change).
- **Handover trigger formula:** $RSS_{nbr} \geq RSS_{srv} + \Delta H$.
- **Mean HO per call:** $\bar{N}_{HO} = \dfrac{\pi v \cdot T_{call}}{2R}$.
- **Cell dwell time:** $\bar{T}_{dwell} = \dfrac{2R}{\pi v}$.
- **Ping-pong effect** is the #1 reason why hysteresis is mathematically necessary.
- **Vertical Handover** = across different technologies (e.g., Wi-Fi ↔ 5G).
- **Horizontal Handover** = within same technology.
- **Intra-cell HO** = channel change within same cell; **Inter-cell HO** = cell-to-cell.
- **Always remember** to draw a labelled diagram in any 7-mark sub-question — KTU examiners award **up to 2 marks** for neat, correct schematic.
- **Soft handover advantages:** macro-diversity gain, no interruption, smoother QoS.
- **Soft handover disadvantages:** extra uplink noise rise in NodeBs (interference from simultaneous transmission).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telecommunication Systems - Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Telecommunication Systems - Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)** is a **second-generation (2G)** digital cellular communication standard developed by the **European Telecommunications Standards Institute (ETSI)** to provide voice, data, and supplementary services over a unified, pan-European mobile telephony framework. It employs **Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)** combined with **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD)** across **900 MHz** and **1800 MHz** (DCS-1800) bands, employing **Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying (GMSK)** modulation with a spectral efficiency of **1 bit/s/Hz**.

GSM was originally standardized as **Group Special Mobile** and later renamed to *Global System for Mobile Communications*. It is the most widely deployed cellular standard globally, with variants such as **GSM-900**, **GSM-1800 (DCS)**, **GSM-1900 (PCS)**, and **GSM-850**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive, multi-story hotel** (the GSM network) where:

- The **rooms** are radio frequencies (channels).
- Each **floor** is a TDMA frame divided into **8 time slots**.
- A **guest with a reservation slip** (SIM card + authentication key) walks in.
- The **reception desk** (MSC + HLR/VLR) verifies identity and assigns a specific room & slot.
- The **guest** (Mobile Station) is tracked as they move between floors (cells), with **porter assistance** (handover procedures) relocating them seamlessly.

This is precisely what GSM does: it **identifies**, **authenticates**, **allocates radio resources**, and **tracks** subscribers as they roam between cells.

### 1.3 Standard GSM Frequency Bands

| Parameter | GSM-900 | DCS-1800 |
|-----------|---------|----------|
| Uplink (MS → BTS) | **890 – 915 MHz** | **1710 – 1785 MHz** |
| Downlink (BTS → MS) | **935 – 960 MHz** | **1805 – 1880 MHz** |
| Total Bandwidth | **2 × 25 MHz** | **2 × 75 MHz** |
| Duplex Separation | **45 MHz** | **95 MHz** |
| Carrier Spacing | **200 kHz** | **200 kHz** |
| Number of Carriers | 124 | 374 |

### 1.4 Key Technical Constants (Bold Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Carrier Bandwidth (ARFCN spacing):** **200 kHz**
> - **Modulation Scheme:** **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) with BT = **0.3**
> - **Access Technique:** **TDMA / FDMA / FDD** hybrid
> - **Number of Time Slots per Frame:** **8**
> - **Frame Duration:** **4.615 ms**
> - **Time Slot Duration:** **577 µs**
> - **Bit Period:** **3.692 µs**
> - **Gross Bit Rate per Carrier:** **270.833 kbps**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 GSM System Architecture — Operational Logic

The GSM architecture is logically divided into **four major subsystems**, each with a specific role. Let us examine each with operational clarity.

#### A. Mobile Station (MS)

- **Hardware Components:** Mobile Equipment (ME) + Subscriber Identity Module (SIM).
- **Functions:** Radio transmission/reception, speech encoding/decoding, signaling, encryption.
- **Identifiers Stored:**
  - **IMEI** (International Mobile Equipment Identity) — 15-digit device identity.
  - **IMSI** (International Mobile Subscriber Identity) — 15-digit subscriber identity stored in SIM.
  - **MSISDN** — Mobile Station ISDN Number (the dialed phone number).

#### B. Base Station Subsystem (BSS)

The BSS manages the **radio interface** and is the **bridge between MS and the switching network**.

- **Base Transceiver Station (BTS):** Handles radio transmission/reception. Performs modulation/demodulation, channel coding/decoding, ciphering. Each BTS supports one or more cells.
- **Base Station Controller (BSC):** Manages multiple BTSs. Performs **radio resource management** (channel allocation, frequency hopping), handover decisions (intra-BSC), power control, and **Transcoding & Rate Adaptation Unit (TRAU)**.

#### C. Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS) — The Core Network

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The NSS is the **brain** of GSM, handling call routing, subscriber management, and interconnection with the Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN).

- **Mobile Services Switching Center (MSC):** Central switching element. Performs call setup, routing, handoff coordination (inter-BSC).
- **Home Location Register (HLR):** Permanent database of subscribers belonging to its network. Stores IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, supplementary services.
- **Visitor Location Register (VLR):** Temporary database for roaming subscribers currently within the MSC's service area. Stores IMSI, MSISDN, TMSI, location area.
- **Authentication Center (AuC):** Stores **Ki** (subscriber authentication key) and generates **SRES** triplets for authentication.
- **Equipment Identity Register (EIR):** Maintains a list of IMEIs categorized as white/black/grey.

#### D. Operation and Support Subsystem (OSS)

- Performs network monitoring, fault management, performance management, configuration.

### 2.2 GSM Radio Interface — Logical & Physical Channels

GSM uses a **hierarchical channel structure**:
- **Physical Channels** → defined by a specific **ARFCN + Time Slot**.
- **Logical Channels** → mapped onto physical channels depending on traffic type.

#### A. Traffic Channels (TCH)

| Channel Type | Function |
|--------------|----------|
| **TCH/F (Full Rate)** | Carries user speech/data at **13 kbps** (gross 22.8 kbps with coding) |
| **TCH/H (Half Rate)** | Carries speech at **5.6 kbps** (effective) — doubles capacity |
| **TCH/8, TCH/16** | Sub-rates for signaling/small data bursts |
| **TCH/9.6** | Data at 9.6 kbps |
| **TCH/4.8** | Data at 4.8 kbps |

#### B. Signaling (Control) Channels

| Channel | Direction | Purpose |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| **BCCH** | BTS → MS | Broadcasts cell info (LAC, CI, frequency list) |
| **PCH** | BTS → MS | Paging (incoming call notification) |
| **RACH** | MS → BTS | Random access request |
| **AGCH** | BTS → MS | Grants dedicated channel |
| **SDCCH** | Bi-directional | Standalone dedicated control channel (signaling before call) |
| **SACCH** | Bi-directional | Slow associated control (power, timing reports) |
| **FACCH** | Bi-directional | Fast associated control (steals TCH for urgent signaling) |

### 2.3 Frame Hierarchy

The GSM burst structure is hierarchical. The key formula linking all time-domain quantities is:

$$
\text{Frame Duration} = N_{\text{bursts}} \times T_{\text{burst}}
$$

where each burst occupies **one TDMA slot** and the entire frame contains **8 bursts**.

> [!NOTE]
> The "Multiframe" hierarchy exists at two levels:
> - **Traffic Multiframe:** 26 frames (≈ 120 ms) — carries TCH + SACCH.
> - **Control Multiframe:** 51 frames (≈ 235 ms) — carries BCCH, PCH, AGCH, RACH, SDCCH.
> - **Superframe:** 26 × 51 = 1326 frames (≈ 6.12 s).
> - **Hyperframe:** 2048 superframes = **2,715,648 frames** (≈ 3 hrs 28 min 53.76 s). The hyperframe defines the ciphering cycle.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|------|
| 1 | Bit Period $T_b$ | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | **3.692** | $\mu s$ |
| 2 | Gross Bit Rate $R_b$ | $R_b = \dfrac{148 \text{ bits}}{T_{\text{slot}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{8}}$ | **270.833** | kbps |
| 3 | Frame Duration $T_f$ | $T_f = 8 \times T_{\text{burst}}$ | **4.615** | ms |
| 4 | Burst Duration $T_{\text{burst}}$ | $T_{\text{burst}} = 156 \times T_b$ | **0.577** | ms |
| 5 | Time Slot Duration $T_{\text{slot}}$ | $T_{\text{slot}} = T_{\text{burst}}$ | **577** | $\mu s$ |
| 6 | Guard Period $T_{\text{guard}}$ | $T_{\text{guard}} = 8.25 \times T_b$ | **30.46** | $\mu s$ |
| 7 | Users per Carrier | $N_{\text{users}} = 8 \times \tau$ | **8** (full), **16** (half-rate) | — |
| 8 | Carrier Bandwidth | Fixed standard | **200** | kHz |
| 9 | Speech Rate after Coding | $R_{\text{speech}}$ | **13** | kbps |
| 10 | Max Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{\text{BW}}$ | **1.35** | bps/Hz |
| 11 | Total Carriers (GSM-900) | $N_c = \dfrac{25\,\text{MHz}}{200\,\text{kHz}}$ | **124** | — |
| 12 | Cells per Cluster (typical) | $N = i^2 + ij + j^2$ | **3, 4, 7, 12, 13** | — |
| 13 | Frequency Reuse Ratio | $q = \dfrac{D}{R}$ | Cluster-dependent | — |
| 14 | Co-channel Reuse Factor | $Q = \sqrt{3N}$ | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM RULE:** In tables and text, always write absolute value using `\vert` (e.g., $C/I \geq 9\,\text{dB}$ or $\vert x \vert$) rather than the vertical pipe character to avoid markdown table parsing errors.

### 2.5 Speech Coding & Channel Coding

> [!NOTE]
> **GSM Speech Path (Transmission Chain):**
>
> **Speech → 13 kbps PCM → RPE-LTP Encoder → 13 kbps → Channel Coder (1/2 rate convolutional) → 22.8 kbps → Interleaving → Ciphering → 270.833 kbps burst → GMSK Modulation → RF Transmission.**

- **RPE-LTP (Regular Pulse Excitation – Long Term Prediction):** Reduces 64 kbps PCM speech to 13 kbps.
