- **Convolutional Coder (Rate = 1/2, Constraint Length K = 5):** Adds redundancy, producing **22.8 kbps**.
- **Interleaving:** Spreads bursts over 8 time slots to combat Rayleigh fading.
- **Ciphering:** Uses **A5/1** algorithm with 64-bit session key (Kc).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

GSM laid the foundation for **GPRS (2.5G)**, **EDGE (2.75G)**, and the entire 3GPP family (UMTS, LTE, 5G NR). Its **SIM-based authentication model** is still used today. Concepts like **TDMA framing**, **logical/physical channel mapping**, and **roaming via HLR/VLR** remain core to modern cellular networks. Engineers working on **IoT**, **M2M**, **telematics**, and **private LTE/5G** networks frequently reapply GSM-derived principles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bit Rate, Frame Duration & Time Slot

The GSM specification is dimensionally tied: the gross bit rate is fixed by the carrier bandwidth (200 kHz) and GMSK spectral efficiency.

**Step 1 — Gross Bit Rate from Bandwidth and Modulation.**

GMSK with BT = 0.3 yields a spectral efficiency $\eta_s \approx 1.35$ bps/Hz:

$$
\eta_s = \frac{R_b}{B}
$$

Substituting the standard bandwidth:

$$
R_b = \eta_s \cdot B = 1.35 \times 200 \;\text{kHz}
$$

$$
R_b = 270.833 \;\text{kbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Bit Period.**

$$
T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{270{,}833 \;\text{bps}}
$$

$$
T_b = 3.692 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{s} = 3.692 \;\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Burst Duration.**

A normal burst contains 156 bits (148 data + 8 training sequence + 3 tail + 3 stealing flags):

$$
T_{\text{burst}} = 156 \times T_b
$$

$$
T_{\text{burst}} = 156 \times 3.692 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
T_{\text{burst}} = 575.95 \times 10^{-6} \;\text{s} \approx 0.577 \;\text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Frame Duration.**

A TDMA frame carries 8 time slots (bursts):

$$
T_f = 8 \times T_{\text{burst}}
$$

$$
T_f = 8 \times 0.577 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{s}
$$

$$
T_f = 4.615 \;\text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Guard Period per Burst.**

The 156 bits include an 8.25-bit equivalent guard at the end:

$$
T_{\text{guard}} = 8.25 \times T_b = 8.25 \times 3.692 \;\mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{guard}} = 30.46 \;\mu s
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Number of Carriers in GSM-900

**Step 1 — Total Uplink Bandwidth.**

$$
B_{\text{UL}} = 915 - 890 = 25 \;\text{MHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Carriers.**

$$
N_c = \frac{B_{\text{UL}}}{B_{\text{carrier}}} = \frac{25 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
N_c = 125 \;\;\text{(including one guard channel at each edge: 124 usable)}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Capacity per Cell

**Step 1 — Effective voice channels per carrier (full rate):**

$$
N_{\text{channels/carrier}} = 8 \times \text{(frame efficiency)}
$$

Approximately **8 full-rate TCH** per carrier (one TCH occupies 1 slot continuously across frames, with SACCH reusing slot 12 of traffic multiframe).

**Step 2 — Capacity per cell (assume 1 carrier per cell for simplicity):**

$$
N_{\text{users/cell}} = 8
$$

**Step 3 — With Half-Rate Codec:**

$$
N_{\text{users/cell}} = 16
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Cluster Size and Frequency Reuse

The co-channel reuse ratio depends on cluster size $N$:

$$
Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3N}
$$

where $D$ is the co-channel reuse distance and $R$ is the cell radius.

**For N = 7 (typical urban cluster):**

$$
Q = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} = 4.583
$$

**Minimum C/I ratio required for GSM (with slow frequency hopping):**

$$
\frac{C}{I}_{\min} = 9 \;\text{dB}
$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Authentication & Ciphering Triplet Flow

**Step 1 — Authentication Triplet Generation:**

The AuC generates **RAND (128-bit)**, **SRES (32-bit)**, and **Kc (64-bit)** for each subscriber using algorithm A3 (authentication) and A8 (key generation), both keyed by **Ki**.

**Step 2 — Challenge-Response at VLR/SIM:**

$$
\text{SRES} = A3(\text{Ki}, \text{RAND})
$$

$$
\text{Kc} = A8(\text{Ki}, \text{RAND})
$$

**Step 3 — Verification:**

If received SRES matches the locally computed SRES, the user is authenticated.

**Step 4 — Ciphering with A5/1 (or A5/3):**

$$
\text{Ciphertext} = A5(K_c, \text{Frame Number})
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Handover Types in GSM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> GSM defines **four handover types**, classified by who controls the handover decision:

| # | Handover Type | Triggering Node | Description |
|---|---------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Intra-cell handover** | BSC | Change of frequency/time-slot within same cell (e.g., to reduce interference). |
| 2 | **Inter-cell, Intra-BSC** | BSC | Between cells under the same BSC. Most common; fastest. |
| 3 | **Inter-BSC, Intra-MSC** | MSC | Between cells under different BSCs but same MSC. |
| 4 | **Inter-MSC** | New MSC | Between cells in different MSC service areas. Most complex; requires backbone signaling. |

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix: GSM Speech/Channel Coding Rate Path

The following table shows the **bit rate at each stage** in the transmission chain:

| Stage | Bit Rate (kbps) | Description |
|-------|------------------|-------------|
| Analog Speech → PCM | 64 | 8 kHz × 8 bits |
| PCM → RPE-LTP (Speech Coder) | 13 | Block: 260 bits / 20 ms |
| RPE-LTP → Convolutional Coder | 22.8 | Rate 1/2, K = 5; adds 260 bits to 260 data bits over 20 ms |
| Convolutional → Interleaving | 22.8 | Split over 8 bursts |
| Burst (raw) → GMSK | 270.833 | After coding, multiplexing and adding training/guard bits |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GSM System Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS1["MOBILE STATION MS"]
    BTS1["BTS CELL A"]
    BTS2["BTS CELL B"]
    BTS3["BTS CELL C"]
    BSC1["BSC 1"]
    BSC2["BSC 2"]
    TRAU1["TRAU TRANSCODER"]
    MSC1["MSC SWITCHING"]
    GMSC1["GMSC GATEWAY"]
    HLR1["HLR HOME LOCATION REGISTER"]
    VLR1["VLR VISITOR LOCATION REGISTER"]
    AUC1["AUC AUTHENTICATION CENTER"]
    EIR1["EIR EQUIPMENT IDENTITY REGISTER"]
    PSTN1["PSTN FIXED NETWORK"]

    MS1 -- Um Interface --> BTS1
    MS1 -- Um Interface --> BTS2
    MS1 -- Um Interface --> BTS3
    BTS1 -- Abis Interface --> BSC1
    BTS2 -- Abis Interface --> BSC1
    BTS3 -- Abis Interface --> BSC2
    BSC1 -- A Interface --> TRAU1
    TRAU1 -- A Interface --> MSC1
    BSC2 -- A Interface --> MSC1
    MSC1 -- B Interface --> VLR1
    MSC1 -- C Interface --> HLR1
    MSC1 -- D Interface --> HLR1
    MSC1 -- E Interface --> MSC1
    HLR1 -- F Interface --> AUC1
    HLR1 -- MAP Signaling --> GMSC1
    GMSC1 -- PSTN Trunk --> PSTN1
    BSC1 -- Internal Signaling --> EIR1
    VLR1 -- G Interface --> VLR1
```

### 4.2 GSM Burst Structure (Normal Burst)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["GUARD GP 3 BITS"]
    B["TAIL TB 3 BITS"]
    C["DATA 57 BITS"]
    D["TRAINING SEQ 26 BITS"]
    E["DATA 57 BITS"]
    F["TAIL TB 3 BITS"]
    G["STEALING FLAGS 2 BITS"]
    H["GUARD GP 3 BITS"]
    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the burst structure:** Total of **156 bits** = 3 + 57 + 26 + 57 + 3 + 2 + 3 + (guard). This is the standard Normal Burst for traffic channels.

### 4.3 GSM Call Establishment — Signaling Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MobileStation as MS
    participant BaseTransceiver as BTS
    participant BaseController as BSC
    participant MobileSwitch as MSC
    participant HomeRegister as HLR
    participant VisitorRegister as VLR
    participant AuthCenter as AuC

    MobileStation->>BaseTransceiver: RACH Channel Request
    BaseTransceiver->>MobileStation: AGCH Channel Grant SDCCH
    MobileStation->>BaseController: Location Update IMSI
    BaseController->>VisitorRegister: VLR Lookup
    VisitorRegister->>HomeRegister: HLR Fetch Authentication Triplet
    HomeRegister->>AuthCenter: Generate RAND SRES Kc
    AuthCenter-->>HomeRegister: Triplet
    HomeRegister-->>VisitorRegister: Forward Triplet
    VisitorRegister->>MobileStation: Challenge RAND
    MobileStation->>MobileStation: Compute SRES with Ki
    MobileStation->>VisitorRegister: Respond SRES
    VisitorRegister->>MobileStation: Auth OK Ciphering Start
    MobileStation->>BaseTransceiver: TCH Assignment
    BaseTransceiver->>MobileSwitch: Call Setup Request
    MobileSwitch->>MobileStation: Call Connected
```

### 4.4 GSM Frame Hierarchy — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    HYP["HYPERFRAME 2048 SUPERFRAMES 3 H 28 M 53.76 S"]
    SUP["SUPERFRAME 1326 FRAMES 6.12 S"]
    MULTRA["TRAFFIC MULTIFRAME 26 FRAMES 120 MS"]
    MULCTL["CONTROL MULTIFRAME 51 FRAMES 235 MS"]
    FR["TDMA FRAME 8 SLOTS 4.615 MS"]
    SLOT["TIME SLOT BURST 156 BITS 577 US"]
    BIT["BIT 3.692 US"]
    HYP --> SUP
    SUP --> MULTRA
    SUP --> MULCTL
    MULTRA --> FR
    MULCTL --> FR
    FR --> SLOT
    SLOT --> BIT
```

### 4.5 GSM Handover Decision Matrix (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HDET["HANDOVER DECISION TRIGGER"]
    HDET --> MET1["RX LEVEL STRONG TO WEAK"]
    HDET --> MET2["C I BELOW THRESHOLD"]
    HDET --> MET3["DISTANCE ESTIMATE EXCEEDS"]
    HDET --> MET4["POWER BUDGET LIMIT"]

    MET1 --> TYPE1["INTRA CELL CHANGE FREQUENCY"]
    MET1 --> TYPE2["INTER CELL INTRA BSC"]
    MET2 --> TYPE2
    MET1 --> TYPE3["INTER BSC INTRA MSC"]
    MET2 --> TYPE3
    MET1 --> TYPE4["INTER MSC HANDOVER"]
    MET2 --> TYPE4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q:** List the four subsystems of the GSM architecture and briefly state the function of the **Base Station Controller (BSC)**.

**Model Answer:**

The four GSM subsystems are:

1. **Mobile Station (MS)** — User terminal.
2. **Base Station Subsystem (BSS)** — Radio access (BTS + BSC).
3. **Network and Switching Subsystem (NSS)** — Core switching (MSC, HLR, VLR, AuC, EIR).
4. **Operation and Support Subsystem (OSS)** — Network management.

The **BSC** manages a group of BTSs. It performs **radio resource allocation**, **frequency hopping control**, **power control**, **transcoding (via TRAU)**, and **intra-BSC handovers**. It acts as the link between BTSs (over Abis interface) and the MSC (over A interface).

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q:** Differentiate between **HLR** and **VLR** in a GSM network. Mention the identifiers they typically store.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | HLR | VLR |
|--------|-----|-----|
| **Scope** | Permanent record for home subscribers | Temporary record for visiting/roaming subscribers |
| **Location** | One per public mobile network | One per MSC area |
| **Updates** | Subscriber data rarely changes | Data changes on every roam-in/roam-out |
| **Stored Identifiers** | IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, service profile, Ki | IMSI, MSISDN, TMSI, current Location Area Code (LAC) |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 — Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the GSM system**. Describe the functions of **BTS**, **BSC**, and **MSC**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **GSM speech coding and channel coding** process with all intermediate bit rates. How does interleaving help in combating Rayleigh fading? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) GSM Architecture — Block Diagram:**

[Refer to SECTION_4 Diagram 4.1]

**BTS Functions:**
- Radio transmission/reception over the **Um (Air) interface**.
- Modulation/demodulation (GMSK).
- Channel coding/decoding for single-hop radio.
- Ciphering/deciphering on the radio link.
- Frequency hopping (slow).
- Measurement reporting (RXLEV, RXQUAL).

**BSC Functions:**
- Manages multiple BTSs (typically 40–100).
- Radio resource allocation (channel assignment to BTS).
- Handover decisions between BTSs under its control.
- Power control signaling.
- Paging coordination.
- Owns the TRAU for 64 kbps ↔ 16 kbps transcoding.

**MSC Functions:**
- Call switching/routing for mobile-originated and mobile-terminated calls.
- Inter-BSC handover coordination.
- Interworking with PSTN via GMSC.
- Handles subscriber mobility signaling.
- ISDN User Part (ISUP) signaling toward the PSTN.

**(b) Speech and Channel Coding:**

[Refer to Section 3.7 Analytical Matrix for full rate sequence.]

**Detailed Chain:**

- **Step 1:** Analog speech is sampled at 8 kHz, quantized to 8 bits → **64 kbps PCM**.
- **Step 2:** RPE-LTP coder compresses to **13 kbps** by exploiting short-term and long-term correlations in speech. Operates on **20 ms frames = 260 bits**.
- **Step 3:** Convolutional encoder (rate 1/2, K = 5) adds redundancy → **260 data bits → 520 bits over 20 ms = 26 kbps**.
  - However, since TCH/F capacity is **22.8 kbps**, the system uses **puncturing** to reach **456 bits/20 ms ≈ 22.8 kbps**.
- **Step 4:** Interleaving spreads the 456 bits of a 20 ms speech frame across **8 consecutive bursts**, with each burst carrying 57 bits of one speech frame's data.

**Why Interleaving Helps:**

Rayleigh fading causes **burst errors** lasting several milliseconds. By interleaving bits across **8 time slots** (≈ 40 ms separation between two bits of same speech block), the channel sees the errors as **random isolated bit errors**, which the convolutional code's Viterbi decoder can correct efficiently. Without interleaving, a deep fade could obliterate an entire speech frame.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3 — Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a)** Explain the **GSM frame hierarchy** from bit to hyperframe with all durations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **GSM authentication and ciphering** mechanism. List the algorithms A3, A5, and A8 with their purposes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Frame Hierarchy — From Bit to Hyperframe:**

[Refer to SECTION_4 Diagram 4.4]

**Tabular Summary:**

| Level | Bits / Frames | Duration | Number of Items |
|-------|---------------|----------|------------------|
| **Bit Period** | 1 bit | **3.692 µs** | — |
| **Time Slot (Burst)** | 156 bits | **0.577 ms (577 µs)** | 8 per frame |
| **TDMA Frame** | 8 bursts | **4.615 ms** | 26 / 51 in multiframes |
| **Traffic Multiframe** | 26 frames | **120 ms** | 51 in a superframe |
| **Control Multiframe** | 51 frames | **235 ms** | 26 in a superframe |
| **Superframe** | 1326 frames | **6.12 s** | 2048 in a hyperframe |
| **Hyperframe** | 2,715,648 frames | **3 h 28 min 53.76 s** | — |

**Key Derivations:**

$$
T_f = 8 \times 0.577 \;\text{ms} = 4.615 \;\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{TCH multiframe}} = 26 \times 4.615 \;\text{ms} = 120 \;\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CTL multiframe}} = 51 \times 4.615 \;\text{ms} \approx 235 \;\text{ms}
$$

$$
T_{\text{Superframe}} = 1326 \times 4.615 \;\text{ms} \approx 6.12 \;\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{Hyperframe}} = 2048 \times 6.12 \;\text{s} \approx 12{,}533.76 \;\text{s} = 3\,\text{h}\,28\,\text{m}\,53.76\,\text{s}
$$

**Significance:** The hyperframe structure defines the **ciphering sequence cycle (TDMA frame number, FN 0 to 2,715,647)**.

**(b) GSM Authentication and Ciphering:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication verifies subscriber identity. Ciphering protects user data and signaling against eavesdropping.**

**Components Involved:**
- **Ki** — 128-bit authentication key stored in SIM and AuC.
- **RAND** — 128-bit random challenge.
- **SRES** — 32-bit signed response.
- **Kc** — 64-bit session ciphering key.
- **Algorithms:**
  - **A3** — computes SRES from Ki and RAND (one-way hash).
  - **A8** — computes Kc from Ki and RAND.
  - **A5 (A5/1, A5/2, A5/3)** — stream cipher used for ciphering, keyed by Kc.

**Procedure:**

1. AuC generates triplets $\{RAND, SRES, Kc\}$ using A3 and A8 keyed by Ki.
2. HLR forwards triplets to current VLR.
3. VLR sends **RAND** to the MS over the air.
4. SIM computes **SRES** locally using A3 and its **Ki** stored in hardware.
5. MS returns **SRES** to VLR.
6. VLR compares received SRES with stored SRES.
   - If they **match** → authentication **succeeds**.
   - If they **do not match** → authentication **fails** → call rejected.
7. After authentication, both sides hold the same **Kc**.
8. **Ciphering** is enabled: data stream is XORed with keystream generated by **A5(Kc, FN)**, where **FN** is the current TDMA frame number (ensures the cipher stream changes every frame).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Confusing A3/A5/A8** — Students often swap ciphering and authentication. A3 = *authentication*, A8 = *key generation*, A5 = *ciphering*. This is a **2-mark trap**.
> 2. **Forgetting Ki storage** — Ki is stored **inside SIM and AuC only**, never transmitted over the air. Marks lost if you mention Ki being sent to the MS during authentication.
> 3. **Frame number in A5** — Forgetting that **A5 takes both Kc AND the Frame Number** as inputs results in partial answer.
> 4. **Hyperframe arithmetic** — Hyperframe is **2,715,648 frames**, not just "many". Exact value matters.
> 5. **Speech coding rates** — Confusing 13 kbps (RPE-LTP) and 22.8 kbps (channel-coded) is a frequent mistake.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Know Before Exam):**

- **Frequency Bands (GSM-900):** Uplink 890–915 MHz; Downlink 935–960 MHz; Duplex spacing **45 MHz**.
- **Carrier Spacing:** **200 kHz**.
- **Modulation:** **GMSK** with BT = 0.3.
- **Access:** **TDMA/FDMA/FDD**; 8 slots per carrier.
- **Frame Duration:** **4.615 ms**; **156 bits/burst**; **577 µs/slot**.
- **Bit Rate (gross):** **270.833 kbps**.
- **Speech Coding:** **RPE-LTP → 13 kbps**.
- **Channel Coding:** **Rate 1/2 convolutional (K = 5) → 22.8 kbps** (punctured).
- **Interleaving:** 8 bursts (40 ms spread) for fading robustness.
- **Ciphering:** **A5/1** using **Kc + Frame Number**.
- **Authentication:** Triplet (RAND, SRES, Kc) using **A3 + A8** with **Ki**.
- **Three Core Identifiers:** **IMSI** (subscriber), **MSISDN** (number), **IMEI** (device).
- **Architecture Components:** MS → BTS → BSC → MSC ↔ HLR, VLR, AuC, EIR → GMSC → PSTN.
- **Interfaces:** Um (MS–BTS), Abis (BTS–BSC), A (BSC–MSC), MAP (NSS-internal).
- **4 Handover Types:** Intra-cell, Inter-cell intra-BSC, Inter-BSC intra-MSC, Inter-MSC.
- **Number of Carriers (GSM-900):** **124 usable** (125 total, 1 guard).
- **Hyperframe:** **2,715,648 frames**; defines ciphering cycle.
- **Co-channel C/I minimum:** **9 dB** (with frequency hopping).
- **Cluster Sizes:** Common values are **3, 4, 7, 12, 13**.
- **SIM holds:** Ki, IMSI, TMSI, LA identification, ciphering key Kc.
- **Channel Types:** **TCH (traffic)**, **BCCH, PCH, RACH, AGCH, SDCCH, SACCH, FACCH (control)**.
- **Logical Channel Mapping:** TCH/SACCH on traffic multiframe (26 frames); BCCH/PCH/AGCH/RACH/SDCCH on control multiframe (51 frames).
- **First-generation predecessor:** Analog (AMPS, NMT, TACS). GSM is **2G**, digital.
- **Successors:** **GPRS (2.5G, packet-switched)**, **EDGE (2.75G)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for GSM Architecture:** **"MBBN" → MS, BSS, NSS, OSS** *(Mobile Station, Base Station Subsystem, Network Switching Subsystem, Operation Support Subsystem)*.
> **Mnemonic for Cipher Triplet:** **"RSK" → RAND, SRES, Kc**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Network Layer - Mobile Internet Protocol (IP), Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mobile Network Layer - Mobile Internet Protocol (IP), Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)

# Mobile Network Layer: Mobile IP & DHCP

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Mobile Internet Protocol (Mobile IP)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
Mobile IP (defined in **RFC 3344** for IPv4 and **RFC 6275** for IPv6) is a communication protocol (operating at **OSI Layer 3 – Network Layer**) that enables mobile devices to maintain seamless connectivity and a persistent IP address while roaming across different IP networks. It allows a Mobile Node (MN) to receive datagrams destined to its **Home Address** regardless of its current point of attachment in the global Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea (Syllabus Highlight):** Mobile IP separates **identity (who you are)** from **location (where you are now)**. The **Home Address** is identity; the **Care-of Address (CoA)** is location.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Postal Forwarding Address"

Imagine you move to a new city for a job, but you want all your official mail (bank statements, government letters) to keep coming to your permanent hometown address.

| Real-World Analogy | Mobile IP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Your permanent hometown address | **Home Address** |
| A trusted relative (your "Home Agent") who forwards your mail | **Home Agent (HA)** |
| Your temporary address in the new city | **Care-of Address (CoA)** |
| The postal service delivering to the new address | **Foreign Agent (FA)** |
| Mail sent to your hometown address | **Packets from Correspondent Node (CN)** |

Your relative receives the mail, opens it, repackages it, and ships it to your new address — exactly how Mobile IP tunnels packets across networks.

### 1.2 Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)

**Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
DHCP (defined in **RFC 2131** and updated by **RFC 951/1531/2132**) is a client–server **Layer 7 (Application Layer)** protocol built on **UDP** that automatically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, DNS servers, and other network configuration parameters to hosts upon joining a network. It eliminates manual IP configuration via the **DORA** (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledgment) four-message handshake.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DHCP in a Mobile Computing course?** When a Mobile Node enters a Foreign Network, it doesn't know its new Care-of Address — it must **dynamically obtain one** via DHCP (or similar auto-configuration). DHCP is the *plumbing* that makes Mobile IP mobility practical.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Hotel Check-In Counter"

When you arrive at a hotel (foreign network), you don't bring your own room number. You go to the front desk (DHCP Server), show your ID, and the desk clerk **dynamically assigns** you a room number (IP address) for the duration of your stay (lease). You return the room when you leave.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Mobile Node (MN)** | A host or router that changes its point of attachment across networks |
| **Home Network** | The network where the MN's permanent IP address (Home Address) is registered |
| **Home Agent (HA)** | A router on the Home Network that tunnels packets to the MN when it is away |
| **Foreign Network** | Any network, other than the Home Network, where the MN is currently attached |
| **Foreign Agent (FA)** | A router on the Foreign Network that assists the MN and delivers de-tunneled packets |
| **Care-of Address (CoA)** | The temporary IP address used by the MN in the Foreign Network |
| **Correspondent Node (CN)** | Any host communicating with the MN (peer in the conversation) |
| **Home Address** | The permanent IP address assigned to the MN on its Home Network |
| **DHCP Server** | A network device that leases IP addresses from a configured pool |
| **DHCP Relay Agent** | A router that forwards DHCP messages between clients and servers across subnets |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize:**
> - DHCP uses **UDP Port 67** (server) and **UDP Port 68** (client).
> - Mobile IP registration lifetime is typically **3600 seconds (1 hour)**.
> - Mobile IPv6 uses **135** (Binding Update), **137** (Binding Acknowledgment) ICMPv6 codes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mobile IP — Three Functional Entities (Architectural Pillars)

Mobile IP architecture consists of **three mandatory logical components**. Their interplay enables mobility:

### A. Mobile Node (MN)
- Has a **permanent Home Address** (e.g., `192.168.1.10`)
- Uses **two addresses simultaneously**: Home Address (for identity) + CoA (for routing)
- Runs a **Mobile IP daemon** that listens for registration events
- When crossing networks, it detects movement via **Agent Discovery** (ICMP Router Advertisement extensions)

### B. Home Agent (HA)
- Located on the Home Network
- Maintains a **Binding Table** mapping: `<Home Address → Care-of Address, Lifetime>`
- Performs **tunneling** — encapsulates original IP packets and sends them to the CoA
- "Tricks" the rest of the Internet into thinking the MN never left

### C. Foreign Agent (FA)
- Located on the Foreign Network (alternative: Co-located CoA — MN gets its own temporary IP)
- Provides **default router** service to visiting MNs
- **De-tunnels** packets received from HA and delivers to MN
- Sends **Agent Advertisement** messages to attract MNs

### 2.2 Three Core Mechanisms of Mobile IP

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires understanding of these **three mechanisms** in sequence:

**1. Agent Discovery**
- HA and FA periodically broadcast **Agent Advertisement** messages
- MN uses **ICMP Router Solicitation** to actively query
- Messages carry: **Mobility Agent Extension**, **Prefix-Length Extension**, **One-Way Homing Extension**

**2. Registration (Binding)**
- MN sends **Registration Request** to HA (via FA, if FA-CoA is used)
- HA authenticates, creates/updates binding, replies with **Registration Reply** (ACK or NAK)
- Registration lifetime is renewed periodically

**3. Tunneling (Packet Forwarding)**
Three tunneling techniques are mandated by RFC 3344:

| Tunneling Method | Description | Overhead |
|---|---|---|
| **IP-in-IP Encapsulation** (RFC 2003) | Entire original IP packet becomes payload of new IP packet | ~20 bytes |
| **Minimal Encapsulation** (RFC 2004) | Removes duplicate IP header; inserts small forwarding header | ~12 bytes |
| **Generic Routing Encapsulation (GRE)** (RFC 2784) | Adds GRE header for multi-protocol tunneling | ~24 bytes |

### 2.3 The Triangle Routing Problem

When a Correspondent Node (CN) sends packets to the MN's Home Address, they travel:

```
CN → HA → (Tunnel) → FA → MN
```

Packets traverse the **Home Network unnecessarily** — even if CN and MN are geographically adjacent. This is called the **Triangle Routing Problem**.

**Solution — Route Optimization (Mobile IPv6 / RFC 3775/6275):**
CN maintains its own **Binding Cache** and tunnels directly to the CoA using a **Binding Update** message. This eliminates the HA detour.

### 2.4 Mobile IPv4 vs Mobile IPv6 — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | Mobile IPv4 (MIPv4) | Mobile IPv6 (MIPv6) |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Space** | ~4.3 billion | ~3.4 × 10³⁸ |
| **Foreign Agent Required?** | Yes (or Co-located CoA) | No (Co-located only) |
| **Routing Header** | Not used | Uses **Type 2 Routing Header** |
| **Security** | Manual IPSec setup | **IPSec mandatory & built-in** |
| **Route Optimization** | Optional extension | **Inherent in design** |
| **Mobility Header** | Not defined | New MH (defined in RFC 6275) |
| **Home Agent Discovery** | Dynamic (RFC 5026) | Dynamic via DHCPv6 + ND |

### 2.5 DHCP — Architectural Components

DHCP follows a **client–server architecture** with optional relay agents:

```
[DHCPDISCOVER broadcast] → [Relay Agent → Server]
[Solicit (DHCPv6)] → [Relay Agent → Server]
```

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **DHCP Server** | Maintains IP address pool; assigns leases |
| **DHCP Client** | Requests configuration; accepts offers |
| **DHCP Relay Agent** | Forwards messages across subnets (since DHCP is broadcast-based and broadcast doesn't cross routers) |
| **DHCP Snooping** | Layer-2 security feature to prevent rogue servers (not core to syllabus) |

### 2.6 DHCP Message Format (RFC 2131) — KTU High-Yield

Every DHCP message has the same **8-byte fixed header**:

| Field | Bytes | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `op` | 1 | **1 = BOOTREQUEST** (Client→Server), **2 = BOOTREPLY** (Server→Client) |
| `htype` | 1 | Hardware type (**1 = Ethernet**) |
| `hlen` | 1 | Hardware address length (6 for Ethernet MAC) |
| `hops` | 1 | Set to 0 by client; incremented by relay agents |
| `xid` | 4 | **Transaction ID** — random number chosen by client to match requests/replies |
| `secs` | 2 | Seconds elapsed since client began acquisition |
| `flags` | 2 | **Bit 15 = Broadcast flag** (1 = client can't receive unicast yet) |
| `ciaddr` | 4 | Client IP address (only if already configured) |
| `yiaddr` | 4 | **"Your" IP address** — assigned by server |
| `siaddr` | 4 | Server IP address (next-hop in bootstrap) |
| `giaddr` | 4 | **Relay agent IP address** |
| `chaddr` | 16 | Client hardware (MAC) address |
| `sname` | 64 | Optional server hostname |
| `file` | 128 | Boot file name |
| `options` | variable | At minimum: **magic cookie = 0x63825363**, then TLV-encoded options |

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These formulas govern DHCP lease timing and Mobile IP tunneling overhead. Memorize for 14-mark derivations.

$$
T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{DISCOVER}} + T_{\text{OFFER}} + T_{\text{REQUEST}} + T_{\text{ACK}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{lease}} = T_{\text{grace}} + T_{\text{renew}} + T_{\text{rebind}}
$$

$$
\text{Overhead}_{\text{IP-in-IP}} = 20 \text{ bytes (new IP header)}
$$

$$
\text{Overhead}_{\text{Minimal}} = 12 \text{ bytes (Minimal Forwarding Header)}
$$

$$
\text{Overhead}_{\text{GRE}} = 24 \text{ bytes (GRE header + new IP header)}
$$

$$
\text{Path}_{\text{triangle}} = d_{CN \to HA} + d_{HA \to FA} + d_{FA \to MN}
$$

$$
\text{Path}_{\text{optimized}} = d_{CN \to FA} + d_{FA \to MN}
$$

$$
\text{Saving} = \frac{\text{Path}_{\text{triangle}} - \text{Path}_{\text{optimized}}}{\text{Path}_{\text{triangle}}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Registration Lifetime Limit} \leq 65535 \text{ s} \quad (\text{RFC 3344 max field size})
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{\text{total}}$ | Total DHCP acquisition latency | milliseconds |
| $T_{\text{lease}}$ | Total DHCP lease duration | seconds (typ. **86400 s = 24 h**) |
| $T_{\text{grace}}$ | Time before expiry client may not transmit | seconds |
| $T_{\text{renew}}$ | Time when client unicast-renews to server (T1 = **50%** of lease) | seconds |
| $T_{\text{rebind}}$ | Time when client broadcasts to any server (T2 = **87.5%** of lease) | seconds |
| $d_{x \to y}$ | Network distance (hops or latency) | ms or hops |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Where it's Used |
|---|---|
| **Campus Wi-Fi roaming** | Mobile IP ensures ongoing VoIP calls during handover between access points |
| **4G/5G Mobile Networks** | Mobile IPv6 evolved into **PMIPv6 (Proxy MIPv6)** — RFC 5213 — used in LTE core |
| **Smartphone Wi-Fi hotspot** | Device becomes a DHCP server, leasing IPs to laptops/tablets |
| **IoT Device Onboarding** | DHCP auto-configures sensors in smart homes without manual setup |
| **Enterprise Networks** | Centralized DHCP server pools reduce address conflicts and administrative overhead |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Mobile IP Packet Flow with Tunneling

We derive the packet structure at each hop when CN sends a datagram to MN via HA with **IP-in-IP encapsulation**.

**Step 1 — CN creates the original packet:**

$$
P_{\text{orig}} = \big[ \text{IP}_h(\text{CN} \to \text{HA}) \;\big\vert\; \text{TCP}_h(\text{CN} \to \text{MN}) \;\big\vert\; \text{Data} \big]
$$

> *(Note: CN's destination IP is the Home Address of MN, not its CoA. The IP layer of CN does not know MN has moved.)*

**Step 2 — HA intercepts the packet on Home Network** (because destination = Home Address, and MN is currently registered away):

The HA looks up its **Binding Cache**:
$$
\text{Binding: } \langle \text{HomeAddr} = 192.168.1.10, \; \text{CoA} = 10.0.0.45, \; \text{Lifetime} = 3540\text{ s} \rangle
$$

**Step 3 — HA performs IP-in-IP encapsulation:**

$$
P_{\text{encaps}} = \big[ \text{IP}_h(\text{HA} \to \text{CoA}) \;\big\vert\; P_{\text{orig}} \big]
$$

The new outer header has:
- **Source IP** = HA's interface IP (e.g., `192.168.1.1`)
- **Destination IP** = CoA (e.g., `10.0.0.45` for FA-CoA, or directly to MN if Co-located)
- **Protocol field** = **4** (IP-in-IP encapsulation, RFC 2003)

**Step 4 — Packet traverses Foreign Network to FA:**

The outer IP header is used for routing. The inner header is untouched — it is "passenger" data.

**Step 5 — FA de-tunnels the packet:**

FA strips the outer IP header and inspects the inner packet:
$$
P_{\text{decaps}} = \big[ \text{IP}_h(\text{CN} \to 192.168.1.10) \;\big\vert\; \text{TCP}_h \;\big\vert\; \text{Data} \big]
$$

FA then delivers $P_{\text{decaps}}$ to the MN over the link layer.

**Step 6 — MN receives, processes normally:**

The MN's TCP/IP stack sees its Home Address (`192.168.1.10`) as destination — transparent mobility achieved.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: DHCP DORA Timing Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **frequently asked 14-mark analytical problem** in KTU exams.

**Problem Setup (KTU Exam Style):**
A DHCP server assigns a lease with $T_{\text{lease}} = 86400$ seconds. Compute the **T1** (renewal) and **T2** (rebinding) timestamps per RFC 2131.

**Standard Derived Values:**
$$
T_1 = 0.5 \times T_{\text{lease}}
$$
$$
T_2 = 0.875 \times T_{\text{lease}}
$$

**Exhaustive Calculation:**

$$
T_1 = 0.5 \times 86400 = 43200 \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting to hours:
$$
T_1 = \frac{43200}{3600} = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_2 = 0.875 \times 86400 = 75600 \text{ seconds}
$$

Converting to hours:
$$
T_2 = \frac{75600}{3600} = 21 \text{ hours}
$$

**Lease State Diagram (analytical derivation):**

| Time Interval | Client Behavior |
|---|---|
| $[0, T_1)$ | Bound state — normal operation |
| $[T_1, T_2)$ | **RENEW** state — unicast DHCPREQUEST to original server |
| $[T_2, T_{\text{lease}})$ | **REBIND** state — broadcast DHCPREQUEST to any server |
| $[T_{\text{lease}}, \infty)$ | **INIT** state — must begin DORA again from scratch |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mobile IP Entity Functions

| Function | Mobile Node (MN) | Home Agent (HA) | Foreign Agent (FA) | Correspondent Node (CN) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Holds Home Address | ✅ | ✅ (in table) | ❌ | ✅ (sends to it) |
| Holds Care-of Address | ✅ | ✅ (in binding) | ✅ (its own) | Optional (cache for route optimization) |
| Sends Agent Advertisement | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Processes Registration Request | Initiates | ✅ Receives & Replies | Forwards | ❌ |
| Performs Encapsulation | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (only with route optimization) |
| Performs De-capsulation | ✅ (if Co-located) | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ (with route optimization) |
| Maintains Binding Table | Own binding only | All visiting MNs | Visiting MN list | Cached entries |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DHCP Message Fields vs Purpose

| Message Type | `op` Field | Source | Destination IP | `giaddr` | `chaddr` | `yiaddr` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DHCPDISCOVER** | 1 (REQUEST) | Client | `255.255.255.255` (broadcast) | 0 | Client MAC | 0 |
| **DHCPOFFER** | 2 (REPLY) | Server | `255.255.255.255` or client's | If via relay: relay IP | Client MAC | Offered IP |
| **DHCPREQUEST** | 1 (REQUEST) | Client | `255.255.255.255` | 0 | Client MAC | 0 |
| **DHCPACK** | 2 (REPLY) | Server | `255.255.255.255` or client's | If via relay: relay IP | Client MAC | Confirmed IP |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — Triangle Routing Optimization

> A Correspondent Node (CN) is **3 hops** from a Foreign Agent (FA). The Home Agent (HA) is **5 hops** from CN and **4 hops** from FA. The MN is **1 hop** from FA. Compute (a) Triangle Routing path length, (b) Optimized path length, (c) Percentage savings.

**(a) Triangle Path:**

$$
\text{Path}_{\text{triangle}} = d_{CN \to HA} + d_{HA \to FA} + d_{FA \to MN}
$$
$$
\text{Path}_{\text{triangle}} = 5 + 4 + 1 = 10 \text{ hops}
$$

**(b) Optimized Path (Mobile IPv6 Route Optimization):**

$$
\text{Path}_{\text{optimized}} = d_{CN \to FA} + d_{FA \to MN}
$$
$$
\text{Path}_{\text{optimized}} = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ hops}
$$

**(c) Percentage Savings:**

$$
\text{Saving} = \frac{10 - 4}{10} \times 100\% = 60\%
$$

**Interpretation:** Route optimization reduces traversed hops by **60%**, dramatically lowering latency for real-time voice/video over Mobile IP.

---

## 3.6 Exhaustive Comparison: CoA Strategies

| Aspect | Foreign Agent CoA (FA-CoA) | Co-located CoA |
|---|---|---|
| **Who owns the IP?** | FA's IP is shared by many MNs | MN gets a unique temporary IP |
| **DHCP used?** | FA's IP is configured statically or via FA's DHCP | MN calls DHCP directly |
| **De-tunneling** | Done by FA | Done by MN itself |
| **FA required?** | Yes | No |
| **Supported in MIPv6?** | No (deprecated) | Yes (only option) |
| **Address pool requirement** | Small (FA needs one IP) | Large (one IP per MN) |
| **Preferred in** | Legacy IPv4 cellular (2G/3G) | Modern IPv6, 4G/LTE |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mobile IP Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CN["CN Correspondent Node"]
    Internet["Public Internet"]
    HA["Home Agent HA on Home Network 192.168.1.0/24"]
    HN["Home Network"]
    FA["Foreign Agent FA on Foreign Network 10.0.0.0/24"]
    FN["Foreign Network"]
    MN["Mobile Node MN Home Addr 192.168.1.10"]

    CN -->|1 original packet to Home Addr| Internet
    Internet --> HA
    HN --- HA
    HA -->|2 Tunnel encapsulated packet| FA
    FN --- FA
    FA -->|3 De-tunneled packet| MN
    MN -.->|4 Registration Request| FA
    FA -.->|4 forwards| HA
    HA -.->|5 Registration Reply| FA
    FA -.->|5 delivers to MN| MN
```

## 4.2 Mobile IPv6 Binding Update Flow (Route Optimization)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CN as Correspondent Node CN
    participant HA as Home Agent HA
    participant MN as Mobile Node MN

    Note over MN: MN moves to Foreign Network acquires new CoA
    MN->>HA: BU Binding Update with new CoA
    HA-->>MN: BA Binding Acknowledgment
    MN->>CN: BU Binding Update direct to CN
    CN-->>MN: BA Binding Acknowledgment
    Note over CN,CN: CN now caches binding Home Addr to CoA
    CN->>MN: Future packets routed DIRECTLY via optimized path
    CN-->>HA: Legacy triangle path no longer used
```

## 4.3 DHCP DORA Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as DHCP Client MN
    participant Relay as DHCP Relay Agent Router
    participant Server as DHCP Server

    Client->>Client: Generate random xid Transaction ID
    Client->>Relay: DHCPDISCOVER broadcast UDP 68 to 67
    Relay->>Server: DHCPDISCOVER unicast with giaddr set
    Server->>Server: Select IP from pool check lease database
    Server->>Relay: DHCPOFFER unicast with yiaddr offer
    Relay->>Client: DHCPOFFER broadcast back to client
    Client->>Relay: DHCPREQUEST broadcast accept offer
    Relay->>Server: DHCPREQUEST forwarded
    Server->>Server: Commit lease create binding record
    Server->>Relay: DHCPACK unicast with confirmed yiaddr
    Relay->>Client: DHCPACK delivered
    Note over Client: Client now configured bound state until T1
```

## 4.4 DHCP Lease State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> INIT
    INIT --> SELECTING: DHCPDISCOVER sent
    SELECTING --> REQUESTING: DHCPREQUEST after OFFER
    REQUESTING --> BOUND: DHCPACK received
    BOUND --> RENEWING: Timer at T1 50 percent lease
    RENEWING --> BOUND: DHCPACK to unicast renewal
    RENEWING --> REBINDING: No ACK Timer at T2 87.5 percent
    REBINDING --> BOUND: DHCPACK from any server
    REBINDING --> INIT: Lease expires without ACK
    BOUND --> INIT: Lease expires T equals lease
    INIT --> [*]
```

## 4.5 Mobile IP Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Application["Application Layer"]
        APP["User applications VoIP browser streaming"]
    end
    subgraph Transport["Transport Layer"]
        TCP["TCP UDP"]
    end
    subgraph Network["Network Layer Mobile IP"]
        DA["Discovery Agent Solicitation and Advertisement"]
        REG["Registration Binding Table Maintenance"]
        TUN["Tunneling IP in IP GRE Minimal"]
        RO["Route Optimization Binding Cache CN side"]
    end
    subgraph DataLink["Data Link Layer"]
        MAC["MAC Wi Fi LTE Ethernet"]
    end

    APP --> TCP
    TCP --> DA
    TCP --> REG
    TCP --> TUN
    TCP --> RO
    DA --> MAC
    REG --> MAC
    TUN --> MAC
    RO --> MAC
```

## 4.6 DHCP Packet Flow vs Mobile IP Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FN["Foreign Network"]
        FA1["Foreign Agent"]
        DHCP1["DHCP Server in FN"]
    end

    subgraph MN1["Mobile Node"]
        M1["MIP daemon"]
        D1["DHCP client"]
    end

    subgraph HN1["Home Network"]
        HA1["Home Agent"]
        DHCP2["DHCP Server in HN optional"]
    end

    MN1 -->|1 DHCP Discover| DHCP1
    DHCP1 -->|2 DHCP Offer with CoA| MN1
    MN1 -->|3 Registration Req with CoA| FA1
    FA1 -->|4 forwards| HA1
    HA1 -->|5 Reg Reply ACK| FA1
    FA1 -->|6 delivers| MN1
    Note1["MN uses acquired CoA as Care of Address for Mobile IP binding"]
    DHCP1 -.-> Note1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(3 Marks) Define Mobile IP. List and briefly explain any three entities involved in Mobile IP.**

**Model Answer:**

**Mobile IP** is a network-layer protocol standardized in **RFC 3344** that enables a Mobile Node (MN) to maintain continuous connectivity using its permanent IP address (Home Address) while roaming across different IP networks.

**Three Entities:**

1. **Home Agent (HA):** A router on the Home Network that maintains a Binding Cache mapping the MN's Home Address to its current Care-of Address (CoA). It tunnels all incoming packets to the MN's current location.

2. **Foreign Agent (FA):** A router on the Foreign Network that provides default-router services to visiting MNs. It assists in de-tunneling packets received from the HA and forwards them to the MN.

3. **Mobile Node (MN):** A host that moves across networks. It uses its Home Address for identification and a Care-of Address for current routing. It runs a Mobile IP daemon that handles Agent Discovery and Registration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write only definitions without mentioning the *functional roles* (e.g., "HA tunnels packets"). Examiners deduct 1 mark for missing functional context.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(3 Marks) Explain the four-step DORA process used by DHCP for dynamic address allocation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **DORA (Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge)** process is a four-message exchange between a DHCP client and server:

1. **DHCPDISCOVER (Client → Broadcast):** The new client broadcasts a discovery message from UDP port **68** to UDP port **67** on the network, including its MAC address and a random transaction ID (`xid`).

2. **DHCPOFFER (Server → Broadcast/Unicast):** Any available DHCP server responds with an offer containing a tentative IP address (`yiaddr`), lease duration, subnet mask, and default gateway.

3. **DHCPREQUEST (Client → Broadcast):** The client selects one offer (typically the first received) and broadcasts a request confirming the chosen server and parameters. Broadcasting ensures other servers withdraw their offers.

4. **DHCPACK (Server → Broadcast/Unicast):** The selected server sends an acknowledgment confirming the lease. The client is now in the **BOUND** state and may use the IP address until the lease expires (or until $T_1 = 50\%$ of the lease time for renewal).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students forget to specify **UDP ports (67/68)**. Examiners specifically test these for the 1-mark detail. Also do NOT confuse DHCP with BOOTP — DHCP is a *superset* and is *backward compatible*.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions — Internal Choice (1 × 14 Marks)

---

### Question A — Mobile IP Deep Dive **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat diagram, explain the **architecture and operation of Mobile IP**, including Agent Discovery and Registration. Discuss **Triangle Routing** and how **Route Optimization** in Mobile IPv6 solves it.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Explain the three **tunneling techniques** in Mobile IP. Compare **Mobile IPv4** and **Mobile IPv6** in tabular form (minimum six points).

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**Architecture:**
The Mobile IP architecture comprises three functional entities — **Mobile Node (MN)**, **Home Agent (HA)**, and **Foreign Agent (FA)** — connected via the Internet. The MN has a permanent **Home Address** (e.g., `192.168.1.10`) registered on the Home Network. While away, it acquires a **Care-of Address (CoA)** from the Foreign Network.

**Operation — Three Phases:**

**Phase 1 — Agent Discovery:**
- HA and FA periodically broadcast **ICMP Router Advertisement** messages with **Mobility Agent Extension**.
- MN detects movement by observing a change in advertised network prefix or by failing to hear its HA.
- The Agent Advertisement carries the **Mobility Agent Advertisement Extension (Type 16)** containing the FA/HA flags.

**Phase 2 — Registration (Binding):**
- MN sends **Registration Request** to HA (directly, or via FA if FA-CoA is used).
- The request contains: Home Address, Home Agent address, Care-of Address, **Registration Lifetime**, and a **Mobile-Home Authentication Extension**.
- HA validates authentication, creates/updates its **Binding Cache**, and replies with **Registration Reply** (ACK or NAK).

**Phase 3 — Tunneled Packet Delivery:**
- HA intercepts packets destined for the MN's Home Address.
- HA **encapsulates** the original packet inside a new IP packet addressed to the CoA.
- FA receives and **de-encapsulates** the packet, forwarding the inner packet to MN.
- Reverse path: MN sends packets directly to CN (no need for triangular return).

**Triangle Routing Problem:**
When CN → HA → FA → MN, the path is unnecessarily long because all traffic must detour via the Home Network even if CN and MN are geographically close. This increases latency, jitter, and bandwidth waste.

**Solution — Route Optimization (MIPv6):**
- CN maintains its own **Binding Cache** (Home Address → CoA).
- MN sends a **Binding Update (BU)** message to CN via **ICMPv6 Type 135**.
- CN acknowledges with **Binding Acknowledgment (Type 137)**.
- Future packets are tunneled **directly** from CN to CoA using a **Type 2 Routing Header** in MIPv6, bypassing the HA.

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**Three Tunneling Techniques:**

**1. IP-in-IP Encapsulation (RFC 2003):**
The original IP packet becomes the payload of a new outer IP packet. The outer header's Protocol field is set to **4**. Overhead = **20 bytes**. Simple but adds a full IP header.

**2. Minimal Encapsulation (RFC 2004):**
The original IP header is modified — its Destination Address is replaced with the Home Agent's address, and a small **Minimal Forwarding Header (8 bytes)** is inserted. Overhead = **8 + 4 = 12 bytes** (since the original Destination Address field is reused).

**3. Generic Routing Encapsulation — GRE (RFC 2784/2890):**
Adds a **GRE header (4 bytes minimum)** plus a new IP header. The GRE header includes a Protocol Type field, allowing multi-protocol tunneling (not just IP). Overhead = **20 (IP) + 4 (GRE) = 24 bytes**. Used commonly in enterprise VPNs.

**Comparison Table: Mobile IPv4 vs Mobile IPv6**

| Feature | Mobile IPv4 (RFC 3344) | Mobile IPv6 (RFC 6275) |
|---|---|---|
| Address Length | 32-bit | 128-bit |
| Foreign Agent | Required (or Co-located CoA) | **Not required** (Co-located only) |
| Route Optimization | Optional (extension) | **Inherently supported** |
| Security (IPSec) | Optional / manual | **Mandatory & built-in** |
| Mobility Header | Not defined | New **Mobility Header (MH)** defined |
| Autoconfiguration | DHCP | **Stateless Address Autoconfiguration (SLAAC)** + DHCPv6 |
| Triangle Routing | Common problem | Largely solved by design |
| Encapsulation | IP-in-IP / Minimal / GRE | **IPv6-in-IPv6**, Routing Header |
| Deployment | Legacy 2G/3G networks | 4G/5G/LTE, modern Wi-Fi |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Examiners specifically test whether you can **draw the triangle path and contrast it with the optimized direct path**. A diagram is worth 2–3 marks — do NOT skip it.

---

### Question B — DHCP Deep Dive **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a sequence diagram, explain the **complete operation of DHCP** including the four-message DORA process. Discuss the **lease renewal (T1)** and **rebinding (T2)** states with numerical example for a 24-hour lease.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Draw the **DHCP message format** as per RFC 2131. Explain **Agent Discovery** in Mobile IP and how DHCP is used to acquire a Care-of Address in a Foreign Network.

#### Model Solution for Q(a):

**DHCP Operation:**
DHCP automates IP address allocation in four steps:

1. **DHCPDISCOVER** — Client broadcasts a discovery packet from UDP 68 → UDP 67. The packet has `op = 1`, a random `xid`, and `chaddr = MAC address`.

2. **DHCPOFFER** — Server responds with `op = 2`, populating `yiaddr` with the offered IP, and including DHCP options: subnet mask (option 1), router (option 3), DNS (option 6), lease time (option 51).

3. **DHCPREQUEST** — Client broadcasts a request specifying the chosen server's identifier (option 54). Other servers withdraw their offers.

4. **DHCPACK** — Selected server sends final acknowledgment. Client enters **BOUND** state. (If server detects IP conflict, it sends **DHCPNAK** instead — client returns to INIT.)

**Lease Renewal States (RFC 2131):**

For a 24-hour lease ($T_{\text{lease}} = 86400$ seconds):

$$
T_1 = 0.5 \times 86400 = 43200 \text{ s} = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
T_2 = 0.875 \times 86400 = 75600 \text{ s} = 21 \text{ hours}
$$

| Time | State | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 – 12 h | BOUND | Normal operation |
| 12 – 21 h | **RENEWING** | Unicast DHCPREQUEST to original server |
| 21 – 24 h | **REBINDING** | Broadcast DHCPREQUEST to any available server |
| > 24 h | INIT-REBOOT | Re-acquire from scratch |

**Numerical Illustration:**
If a client obtains a lease at **10:00 AM**, then:
- At **10:00 PM** (T1), it tries to renew directly with the original server.
- At **07:00 AM next day** (T2), if renewal failed, it broadcasts to any server.
- At **10:00 AM next day** (lease expiry), if still no ACK, it returns to INIT state.

#### Model Solution for Q(b):

**DHCP Message Format (RFC 2131):**
*(Refer to the message format table in Section 2.6 of these notes.)*

**Field Highlights:**
- `op` (1 byte) — 1 = BOOTREQUEST, 2 = BOOTREPLY
- `htype` (1 byte) — 1 for Ethernet
- `xid` (4 bytes) — Transaction ID
- `flags` (2 bytes) — **Broadcast flag (Bit 15)**
- `chaddr` (16 bytes) — Client MAC
- `options` (variable) — Begins with **magic cookie `0x63825363`**

**Agent Discovery in Mobile IP:**
Agent Discovery allows an MN to determine whether it is on its Home Network or a Foreign Network, and to find available FAs.

Two methods exist:
1. **Passive Discovery:** MN listens for periodic **Agent Advertisements** (ICMP Router Advertisements with mobility extensions).
2. **Active Discovery:** MN broadcasts an **Agent Solicitation** message.

Agent Advertisement contains:
- **Care-of Address** offered
- **Mobility Agent Extension** (type 16)
- **Prefix-Length Extension** (type 19)
- **One-Way Homing Extension** (for reverse tunneling)

**DHCP Acquiring a CoA:**
When an MN moves into a Foreign Network:
1. MN's Mobile IP daemon detects movement (Agent Discovery).
2. The MN's **DHCP client** broadcasts DHCPDISCOVER.
3. The Foreign Network's DHCP server offers an IP.
4. MN DHCPREQUESTS the IP — this becomes its **Co-located Care-of Address**.
5. MN registers this CoA with its Home Agent via Registration Request.

This synergy — DHCP providing the CoA + Mobile IP handling the binding — is precisely how modern smartphones maintain seamless data sessions while moving across Wi-Fi and cellular networks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (DHCP Questions):** Examiners award **2 marks specifically for the numerical derivation of T1 and T2**. Skipping the math (even if the concept is correct) costs easy marks. Always show the working.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam**

### Mobile IP Essentials
- [ ] **RFC 3344** = Mobile IPv4, **RFC 6275** = Mobile IPv6
- [ ] Three entities: **MN, HA, FA**
- [ ] Two addresses simultaneously: **Home Address (identity)** + **Care-of Address (location)**
- [ ] **Two CoA strategies:** FA-CoA (shared) vs Co-located CoA (unique to MN)
- [ ] **Three mechanisms:** Agent Discovery → Registration → Tunneling
- [ ] **Three tunneling methods:** IP-in-IP (20 B), Minimal (12 B), GRE (24 B)
- [ ] **Triangle Routing Problem:** CN → HA → FA → MN (unnecessary HA detour)
- [ ] **Solution:** Route Optimization via Binding Update to CN (MIPv6)
- [ ] **MIPv6 = no FA, mandatory IPSec, inherent route optimization**

### DHCP Essentials
- [ ] **RFC 2131** standard; **UDP ports 67 (server), 68 (client)**
- [ ] **DORA = Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge**
- [ ] **Magic Cookie = `0x63825363`** in options field
- [ ] `op` field: **1 = REQUEST, 2 = REPLY**
- [ ] `xid` = random transaction ID; `chaddr` = client MAC
- [ ] `yiaddr` = "your" IP (the assignment)
- [ ] **T1 = 50% × lease time** → RENEWING state (unicast)
- [ ] **T2 = 87.5% × lease time** → REBINDING state (broadcast)
- [ ] **Lease expires** → INIT state, restart DORA
- [ ] **DHCP Relay Agent** required because DHCP is broadcast-based and broadcasts don't cross routers
- [ ] DHCPv6 → uses **Solicit/Advertise/Request/Reply** messages

### Integration Insight (Exam Favorite!)
- [ ] **DHCP provides the CoA** in Mobile IP's Co-located mode
- [ ] DHCP + Mobile IP = foundation of modern cellular data roaming (3G/4G/5G)
- [ ] Mobile IPv6's Neighbor Discovery + SLAAC reduces dependency on DHCP for autoconfiguration

### Common 14-Mark Blueprint
- [ ] Always **draw a sequence/architecture diagram** — worth 2–3 marks
- [ ] Always **show numerical working** for any timing/lease calculation
- [ ] Always **compare two related concepts** in tabular form (MIPv4 vs MIPv6, FA-CoA vs Co-located)
- [ ] Always **mention security implications** (IPSec in MIPv6, DHCP authentication in enterprise)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mobile Ad-hoc Networks - Routing, Dynamic Source Routing (DSR), Destination Sequenced Distance Vector (DSDV), Ad-hoc Routing Protocols
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST633 - WIRELESS & MOBILE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mobile Ad-hoc Networks - Routing, Dynamic Source Routing (DSR), Destination Sequenced Distance Vector (DSDV), Ad-hoc Routing Protocols

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Mobile Ad-hoc Network (MANET)?

A **Mobile Ad-hoc Network (MANET)** is a self-configuring, infrastructure-less network of mobile devices connected wirelessly, where each node is free to move independently in any direction. Because there is no fixed router/base-station infrastructure, every node acts simultaneously as a **host** (end-system) and a **router** (forwarder), dynamically determining the multi-hop path for traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box (Verbatim from syllabus):**
> *"A Mobile Ad-hoc Network is an autonomous collection of mobile users/devices that communicate over bandwidth-constrained wireless links. Due to nodal mobility, the network topology may change rapidly and unpredictably over time."*

### 1.2 Why Classical Routing Fails in MANETs

Traditional distance-vector (RIP) and link-state (OSPF) protocols assume:
1. A **stable, mostly static topology**.
2. A **trustworthy administrative domain**.
3. **Low node-mobility** (neighbors don't change every few seconds).
4. **Adequate bandwidth and battery** for frequent control packets.

None of these hold in a MANET where:
- Nodes move at walking/driving speeds → links break constantly.
- Power is constrained (battery-operated).
- Bandwidth is shared and limited.
- Routing-table convergence of OSPF is far too slow for rapidly changing topologies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Rescue Team in a Dark Forest"**
> Imagine a group of 10 hikers walking through a forest at night, each holding only a flashlight that illuminates ~20 m. They have no map and no base camp. To send a message from Hiker A to Hiker J, Hiker A shouts "J, are you in range?" — if not, the nearest hiker in A's flashlight range forwards the shout. Every hiker is both a *speaker* and a *relay*. If Hiker C suddenly walks away, the path breaks and a new relay chain must be found on the fly. That dynamic, on-the-spot path-finding **is** MANET routing.

### 1.3 Classification of Ad-hoc Routing Protocols (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

The KTU syllabus groups ad-hoc routing protocols into three families:

| Family | Also Known As | Route Availability | Example Protocols | Philosophy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Proactive (Table-Driven)** | "Always-on" | Routes ready *before* needed | **DSDV**, OLSR, WRP | Maintains fresh routing table at every node via periodic updates |
| **Reactive (On-Demand)** | "Lazy" | Routes discovered *only* when needed | **DSR**, AODV, TORA | Floods a Route Request on demand |
| **Hybrid** | "Zone-based" | Mix of both | ZRP, CBRP | Proactive within a zone, reactive across zones |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> - **P**roactive = **P**re-calculated (think GPS that updates every second)
> - **R**eactive = **R**equests on-demand (think asking a local for directions)
> - **H**ybrid = **H**ome zone + far-zone combination

### 1.4 The Core Design Challenges (Recurring Theme in KTU Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> These **6 challenges** appear in almost every MANET question paper:
> 1. **Mobility-induced route breakage** — links live for seconds.
> 2. **Bandwidth scarcity** — wireless medium is shared.
> 3. **Power limitation** — battery drains with transmissions.
> 4. **Scalability** — control packets grow as $O(n^2)$.
> 5. **Loop-freedom** — no central authority to detect loops.
> 6. **Security** — open wireless medium is exposed to eavesdropping.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Destination Sequenced Distance Vector (DSDV) — Deep Theory

**DSDV** is a **table-driven (proactive)** ad-hoc routing protocol proposed by *Perkins & Bhagwat (1994)*. It is essentially the **Bellman-Ford algorithm** retrofitted with two ad-hoc extensions:

1. **Sequence Numbers** — to guarantee loop-freedom.
2. **Settling Time** — to dampen the "counting-to-infinity" effect.

#### 2.1.1 The DSDV Routing Table

Every node $N_i$ maintains a table with the following columns:

| Destination | Next Hop | Metric (Hops) | Sequence Number | Install Time | Stable Data Pointer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sequence Number Rule (Exam Favourite):**
> Each destination's route is tagged with a **sequence number** originated by the destination itself. Sequence numbers are **even** for normal updates and **odd** for broken-link broadcasts.

#### 2.1.2 Update Mechanism — Two Flavors

DSDV uses two kinds of routing updates:

- **Full Dump (FD)**: Entire routing table advertised. Used rarely, after large topology changes.
- **Incremental Update (IU)**: Only entries that have changed. Sent frequently.

A node may transmit **n** incremental updates before sending one full dump, where $n$ is configurable (typical value = **5**).

#### 2.1.3 The DSDV Route-Selection Tie-Breaking Algorithm

When two advertisements for the same destination arrive, DSDV picks a route using this **two-tier priority** logic:

1. **Primary Criterion — Sequence Number:**
   $$\text{Choose route with } \text{seq}_d^{\text{new}} > \text{seq}_d^{\text{old}}$$

2. **Secondary Criterion — Metric (Hop Count):**
   $$\text{If } \text{seq}_d^{\text{new}} == \text{seq}_d^{\text{old}} \Rightarrow \text{Choose route with smaller metric}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The sequence number is like a "version stamp" of the destination — a fresh stamp ALWAYS wins, even if it advertises a longer path, because it represents more recent information.

#### 2.1.4 Settling Time — Damping Oscillations

Because nodes can move, route metrics may fluctuate between $h$ and $h+1$ in successive updates, causing a "flapping" route. DSDV suppresses a new route advertisement until the route's metric has remained **stable** for a period called the **Settling Time**.

Mathematically, the settling time $T_s$ for a route update is:

$$T_s = \frac{\text{Average Propagation Time} \times \text{Hop Count of Route}}{2}$$

A node delays broadcasting an update by $T_s$ to confirm the new metric is genuinely stable.

### 2.2 Dynamic Source Routing (DSR) — Deep Theory

**DSR** is a **reactive (on-demand)** ad-hoc routing protocol designed by *Johnson, Maltz & Broch (1996)*. It is built on two core mechanisms:

1. **Route Discovery** — find a path on demand.
2. **Route Maintenance** — detect and repair a broken path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unique Feature of DSR:** The **complete route** (every hop) is placed inside **every data packet's header**. Hence the name *Source* Routing. Intermediate nodes do NOT maintain routing tables; they simply look at the header and forward.

#### 2.2.1 Route Discovery (RREQ → RREP)

**Step 1 — Route Request (RREQ):**
The source broadcasts a packet containing: `[Source ID, Request ID, Target ID, Hop List]`. The **Hop List** accumulates the IDs of nodes that have forwarded the RREQ.

**Step 2 — Intermediate Node Behaviour:**
- If a node has already seen this `(Source ID, Request ID)` pair → discard (avoid loop / flood).
- If the node has a cached route to the target → may send a **Route Reply** directly.
- Otherwise → append own ID to Hop List and rebroadcast.

**Step 3 — Route Reply (RREP):**
The target (or an intermediate node with a cached route) unicasts a reply back along the *reverse* of the recorded path, populating the full source route.

#### 2.2.2 Route Maintenance (RERR)

When a node $N_k$ detects that its link to the next hop $N_{k+1}$ has failed (e.g., no MAC-layer ACK after $N$ retries), $N_k$ sends a **Route Error (RERR)** packet back to the source.

The source then:
1. Removes the broken route from its **Route Cache**.
2. Initiates a fresh **Route Discovery** if still needed.

#### 2.2.3 The Route Cache

Every node maintains a **Route Cache** of all source routes it has learned. The cache is consulted before every Route Discovery — if a fresh enough route is already cached, the costly flood is skipped entirely.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Comparison Cheat Sheet

#### Table A: DSDV / DSR Comparison (Most-Frequently-Examined)

| Property | DSDV | DSR |
|---|---|---|
| **Category** | Proactive / Table-driven | Reactive / On-demand |
| **Algorithm Basis** | Enhanced Bellman-Ford | Source Routing |
| **Loop-Free** | Yes (via sequence numbers) | Yes (route in header) |
| **Routing Tables** | Maintained at every node | NOT maintained at intermediate nodes |
| **Packet Header** | No route info (next-hop only) | Full source route in header |
| **Periodic Updates** | Yes (Full Dump + Incremental) | No |
| **Route Discovery Overhead** | None (always known) | High (RREQ flood when needed) |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large networks | Better; cache reduces floods |
| **Mobility Adaptability** | Lower (table churn) | Higher |
| **Memorize** | "P**re**pared, **D**istance-**V**ector" | "**S**ource-listed in packet" |

#### Table B: Mathematical/Algorithmic Summary

| Item | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **DSDV Update Selection** | Choose max $\text{seq}_d$, then min $\text{metric}$ | Two-tier |
| **DSDV Settling Time** | $T_s = (\text{Avg Prop Time} \times \text{Hops}) \div 2$ | Dampens metric oscillation |
| **DSDV Full-Dump Cycle** | $n$ incremental updates $\rightarrow$ 1 full dump | Typical $n = 5$ |
| **DSR Hop List Length** | Grows by 1 per forward | Max $= N-1$ hops |
| **DSR Cache Hit Probability** | $P_{\text{hit}} = 1 - (1 - p_c)^{k}$ | $p_c$ = per-cache-entry success, $k$ = entries |
| **RREQ Flood Complexity** | $O(N)$ messages for $N$ nodes | Worst case |
| **Sequence Number Parity** | Even = stable; Odd = link failure | DSDV trick |

#### Table C: KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Pattern

| Question Type | Marks | Internal Choice | Module 4 Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Part A (Short)** | 3 | No | Typically 1–2 questions |
| **Part B (Long)** | 14 | Yes (a) or (b) | At least 1 full question per module |
| **Total Module Marks** | — | — | ~14–21 marks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **DSDV** is suitable for small, low-mobility tactical networks (military, drone swarms).
> - **DSR** is favoured for highly mobile, sparse networks (VANETs, disaster-response).
> - Modern vehicular standards (DSRC, C-V2X) evolved from DSR/DSR-like on-demand principles.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DSDV Sequence Number Increment Derivation

**Problem Setup:** A node $N_i$ is advertising a route to destination $D$. Show the exact sequence-number evolution across three events: (1) periodic update, (2) topology change, (3) link break.

**Definitions:**
- $S_d^i(t)$ = Sequence number used by $N_i$ for destination $D$ at time $t$.
- Even values → stable, Odd values → unstable / link failure.
- Initial advertisement by $D$ itself: $S_d^D(0) = 0$ (even).

#### Step 1 — Periodic (Time-Triggered) Update by Destination

The destination $D$ periodically increments its own sequence number by 2 to keep it **even**:

$$S_d^D(t_1) = S_d^D(0) + 2 = 0 + 2 = 2$$

#### Step 2 — Neighbour Re-Advertises (Preserve Parity)

When $N_1$ re-advertises to its neighbours, it keeps the same sequence number (does NOT add):

$$S_d^1(t_1) = S_d^D(t_1) = 2$$

**Reasoning:** Sequence numbers are originated ONLY by the destination, then propagated unchanged to ensure global consistency.

#### Step 3 — Topology Change (Mobile Node Moves)

Suppose the link $D \rightarrow N_1$ breaks, but $N_2$ still has a 2-hop route. $D$ advertises an ODD sequence number to mark a broken link:

$$S_d^D(t_2) = S_d^D(t_1) + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \quad (\text{odd, link break})$$

#### Step 4 — Recovery (Odd → Even)

When the route to $D$ stabilises again (via $N_2$), $D$ increments by 1 to make it even:

$$S_d^D(t_3) = S_d^D(t_2) + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \quad (\text{even, recovered})$$

#### Step 5 — General Rule (Compressed)

For any node advertising on behalf of destination $D$ at time $t$:

$$
S_d^{\text{adv}}(t) =
\begin{cases}
S_d^{\text{prev}}(t) + 2, & \text{if advertiser is the destination and route is stable} \\
S_d^{\text{prev}}(t) + 1, & \text{if advertiser is the destination and a link just broke} \\
S_d^{D}(t_{\text{last}}), & \text{if advertiser is an intermediate relay (unchanged)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.2 DSR Hop-List Construction (Route Discovery Trace)

**Network Setup:** $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$ (target). Source $S$ initiates RREQ.

| Step | Action | Hop List at End | Packet |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S$ broadcasts RREQ | $[\,S\,]$ | RREQ:1 |
| 2 | $A$ receives, rebroadcasts | $[\,S, A\,]$ | RREQ:2 |
| 3 | $B$ receives, rebroadcasts | $[\,S, A, B\,]$ | RREQ:3 |
| 4 | $C$ receives, rebroadcasts | $[\,S, A, B, C\,]$ | RREQ:4 |
| 5 | $D$ receives — it's the target | $[\,S, A, B, C, D\,]$ | RREP unicast back |

The RREP from $D$ carries the **same hop list** as the source route. Each data packet from $S$ to $D$ later carries this exact list in its header.

### 3.3 DSDV Route-Update Decision Trace

**Scenario:** Node $S$ receives two advertisements for destination $D$:

| Advertisement | Sequence Number | Metric (hops) |
|---|---|---|
| **Adv-1** (older, from $N_1$) | 12 | 3 |
| **Adv-2** (newer, from $N_2$) | 14 | 4 |

**Decision Procedure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Sequence compare:} \quad & S_d^{\text{Adv-2}} = 14 > S_d^{\text{Adv-1}} = 12 \\[4pt]
& \Rightarrow \text{Adv-2 wins (sequence rule dominates)} \\[4pt]
\text{Step 2 — Metric used:} \quad & \text{Install Adv-2 with metric} = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $S$ selects $N_2$ as next hop with cost 4, even though Adv-1 had a shorter path. **Why?** Sequence 14 is a fresher "version stamp" of $D$, so it is more trustworthy.

### 3.4 DSR Route Maintenance — Failure Trace

**Initial route:** $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D$.

**Event:** Link $B \rightarrow C$ breaks (B's MAC retransmissions exceed threshold).

**Actions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & B \text{ transmits RERR to } S \text{ (unicast, reverse path } S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B) \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & S \text{ removes the route from its Route Cache} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & S \text{ consults cache for alternate route} \\
\text{Step 4a:} \quad & \text{If alternate exists} \Rightarrow \text{use it immediately (no new RREQ)} \\
\text{Step 4b:} \quad & \text{If no alternate} \Rightarrow \text{initiate fresh Route Discovery}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing DSDV vs DSR

| Network Property | DSDV Wins | DSR Wins |
|---|---|---|
| High mobility | ✗ | ✓ |
| Low mobility / quasi-static | ✓ | ✗ |
| Many small, frequent transfers | ✓ (no per-flow setup) | ✗ |
| Few large, bursty transfers | ✗ | ✓ |
| Tight energy budget | ✗ (periodic updates) | ✓ (no idle cost) |
| Large network (100+ nodes) | ✗ (table memory) | ✓ (cache memory) |
| Need QoS guarantees | ✓ (deterministic lookup) | ✗ (route may not exist) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 MANET Topology — Conceptual Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeS[Source Node S]:::host
    nodeA[Relay Node A]:::router
    nodeB[Relay Node B]:::router
    nodeC[Relay Node C]:::router
    nodeD[Target Node D]:::host
    nodeE[Mobile Node E]:::mobile
    nodeF[Mobile Node F]:::mobile

    nodeS --- nodeA
    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeA --- nodeE
    nodeB --- nodeF
    nodeE -.moves.-> nodeF
    nodeF -.breaks link.-> nodeB

    classDef host fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF6B00,color:#000
    classDef router fill:#A0E0FF,stroke:#0066AA,color:#000
    classDef mobile fill:#D0FFD0,stroke:#2E8B57,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Squares = hosts (end devices), blue circles = static relays, green circles = mobile nodes whose movement is shown by dotted arrows. Notice that link `nodeF → nodeB` is broken because $F$ has moved out of $B$'s range — this is the *exact scenario* that triggers DSR's RERR.

### 4.2 DSDV Architecture — Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    evt1[Topology Change or Timer Tick]:::event
    upd[Generate Routing Update]:::proc
    seq1[Assign Sequence Number from Destination]:::proc
    fd{Full Dump or Incremental?}:::decision
    fd1[Full Dump: Entire Table]:::proc
    fd2[Incremental: Changed Entries Only]:::proc
    brd[Broadcast to All Neighbours]:::proc
    rec[Receive Advertisement from Neighbour]:::event
    cmp{Compare Sequence Number}:::decision
    upd1[Update Route Table Entry]:::proc
    ign[Discard Stale Route]:::proc
    stl{Settled for T seconds?}:::decision
    stl1[Advertise Immediately]:::proc
    stl2[Hold for Settling Time T]:::proc

    evt1 --> upd --> seq1 --> fd
    fd -->|every n times| fd1
    fd -->|otherwise| fd2
    fd1 --> brd
    fd2 --> brd
    brd -.-> rec
    rec --> cmp
    cmp -->|newer| stl
    cmp -->|older| ign
    stl -->|yes| stl1 --> brd
    stl -->|no| stl2

    classDef event fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#1E5A8A,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The DSDV loop has two triggers — a periodic timer or a topology change. The decision node `cmp` is the heart of loop-freedom: only newer sequence numbers pass through. The `stl` decision implements the **settling-time** damping described in §2.1.4.

### 4.3 DSR — Route Discovery Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    src[Source S]:::host
    r1[Node A]:::router
    r2[Node B]:::router
    r3[Node C]:::router
    tgt[Target D]:::host

    step1[Step 1: S broadcasts RREQ with Hop List S]:::proc
    step2[Step 2: A rebroadcasts with Hop List S A]:::proc
    step3[Step 3: B rebroadcasts with Hop List S A B]:::proc
    step4[Step 4: C rebroadcasts with Hop List S A B C]:::proc
    step5[Step 5: D receives - it is the target]:::proc
    step6[Step 6: D unicasts RREP back along reverse path]:::proc
    step7[Step 7: S receives RREP - route cached]:::proc
    step8[Step 8: S sends data with source route in header]:::proc

    src --> step1
    step1 --> r1
    r1 --> step2
    step2 --> r2
    r2 --> step3
    step3 --> r3
    r3 --> step4
    step4 --> tgt
    tgt --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8

    classDef host fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF6B00,color:#000
    classDef router fill:#A0E0FF,stroke:#0066AA,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The left-to-right flow is the **RREQ flood** (5 broadcasts), the right-to-left flow is the **RREP unicast** (1 unicast). Total messages for one discovery = **5 broadcasts + 5 unicasts = 10 packets** for a 4-hop path. Note that intermediate nodes do NOT maintain any routing table — they just look at the in-packet hop list and forward.

### 4.4 DSDV vs DSR — Comparative Functional Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph DSDV[PROTOCOL DSDV - PROACTIVE]
        d1[Routing Table at Every Node]:::proc
        d2[Periodic Updates Every X s]:::proc
        d3[Sequence Number Logic]:::proc
        d4[Next Hop Lookup for Data]:::proc
    end
    subgraph DSR[PROTOCOL DSR - REACTIVE]
        s1[Route Cache per Node]:::proc
        s2[Route Discovery on Demand]:::proc
        s3[Source Route in Data Header]:::proc
        s4[No Table Lookup at Relays]:::proc
    end

    d4 -->|lookup| DATA[Data Forwarded]:::event
    s3 -->|in-packet path| DATA

    classDef proc fill:#B0E0FF,stroke:#1E5A8A,color:#000
    classDef event fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The contrast is stark. DSDV spends control overhead *up-front* (always-on tables) so data forwarding is a simple table lookup. DSR spends *zero* overhead when idle, but pays the cost of an RREQ flood the first time data must be sent.
